topics.title: Concept of Networks and Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Networks and Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining a Network and a Circuit

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> A **Network** is an interconnection of various electrical elements such as resistors, inductors, capacitors, and energy sources (both independent and dependent) that may or may not form a closed loop. A **Circuit**, however, is a specific type of network where the elements form at least one closed conducting path, allowing current to flow from the source and return back to it.

In the formal language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** under *PCECT205 – Network Theory*, an **Electric Network** is mathematically represented as a directed graph $\mathcal{G} = (V, E)$, where $V$ represents nodes (junctions) and $E$ represents branches (elements). Every electrical network must satisfy the foundational laws — **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** and **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)**.

### 🔧 Conceptual Analogy — The Water Pipeline Model

Imagine a complex water distribution system in a city:

- **Resistor** $\rightarrow$ A narrow pipe that *resists* water flow (energy dissipates as heat)
- **Inductor** $\rightarrow$ A water tank that *stores* kinetic energy of flowing water (magnetic field $\approx$ momentum)
- **Capacitor** $\rightarrow$ An expandable balloon that *stores* water under pressure (electric field $\approx$ pressure)
- **Voltage Source** $\rightarrow$ A water pump providing a fixed pressure difference
- **Current Source** $\rightarrow$ A pump providing a fixed water flow rate, regardless of pressure

The entire **city's plumbing layout** = **Network**, while a **single loop from the reservoir, through a house, and back** = **Circuit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Every circuit is a network, but every network is NOT necessarily a circuit.**
> A network becomes a circuit only if it has at least one **closed loop** (closed conducting path).

## 1.2 Classification of Network Elements

The KTU Module 1 mandates a strong understanding of how network elements are classified based on their **V-I (Voltage-Current) characteristics** and behavior. Let us lay them out clearly:

| Classification | Element Type | Real-World Implication |
|----------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Active vs Passive** | Sources vs R, L, C | Active elements *generate* energy; passive elements *absorb/store* it |
| **Linear vs Non-Linear** | R, L, C, ideal sources vs Diode, Transistor | Linear obeys superposition; non-linear does not |
| **Bilateral vs Unilateral** | R, L, C, ideal transformer vs Diode, Vacuum Tube | Bilateral: V/I ratio same in both directions |
| **Lumped vs Distributed** | Standard circuit elements vs Transmission Line | Lumped: dimensions negligible vs signal wavelength |
| **Time-Invariant vs Time-Varying** | Constant R vs Rheostat (volume control) | Parameters remain constant over time |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** The classification above is tested every year as a **2 or 3 mark Part-A conceptual question** in both internal assessments and end-semester exams.

## 1.3 Energy Sources — The Driving Force

> [!NOTE]
> **Independent Source:** Delivers a specified voltage/current *irrespective* of any other circuit variable.
> **Dependent Source:** Its output is *controlled* by another voltage or current elsewhere in the circuit (also called **Controlled Source**).

The four types of dependent sources used heavily in **transistor amplifier analysis** (a frequent KTU 2-mark question) are:

1. **VCVS** — Voltage Controlled Voltage Source
2. **VCCS** — Voltage Controlled Current Source
3. **CCVS** — Current Controlled Voltage Source
4. **CCCS** — Current Controlled Current Source
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Key Concepts

### 🔹 A. Passive vs Active Elements (Logical Deduction)

An element is classified as **passive** if and only if it **absorbs net electrical energy over all time** ($t \to \infty$). Mathematically:

$$P(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) \geq 0 \quad \forall t \geq 0$$

- If $P(t) < 0$ for *any* time interval, the element is **active** (it delivers energy).
- All three classical elements **R, L, and C** are passive.
- **Independent and dependent sources** are by definition **active elements**.

### 🔹 B. Linear vs Non-Linear Elements (The Homogeneity Test)

A network element is **linear** if it satisfies both the **Homogeneity (Scaling)** and **Additivity (Superposition)** properties.

For an input $x(t)$ producing response $y(t)$:
$$f(a \cdot x_1(t) + b \cdot x_2(t)) = a \cdot f(x_1(t)) + b \cdot f(x_2(t))$$

If this **Linear Operator Equation** holds, the element is linear. A **diode**, where $i = I_s(e^{v/V_T} - 1)$, is a classic non-linear element that **violates homogeneity**.

### 🔹 C. Lumped vs Distributed (The Wavelength Criterion)

- **Lumped:** Physical size $d \ll \lambda$, where $\lambda$ is the operating signal's wavelength.
- **Distributed:** Physical size $d \approx \lambda$ or $d > \lambda$ (transmission lines, antennas).

> At **50 Hz** power frequency, $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / 50 = \mathbf{6000 \; km}$. Hence, *all practical KTU lab circuits are lumped*.

### 🔹 D. Bilateral vs Unilateral

A **bilateral element** has identical V-I characteristics for both directions of current flow. The resistor, with $v = iR$ (symmetric), is bilateral. A **diode** allows current in only one direction, making it unilateral.

### 🔹 E. Time-Invariant vs Time-Varying

In a **time-invariant** system, the element's parameter does not change with time. Example: a constant 100 $\Omega$ resistor. In a **time-varying** system, parameters like $R(t)$ change explicitly with time, for example, $R(t) = 100(1 + 0.1\sin t) \, \Omega$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles the **mandatory formulas** for Module 1. Memorize these — they appear in nearly every KTU internal and end-semester question paper.

| # | Element | V-I Relationship | Power Absorbed ($P$) | Energy Stored ($W$) |
|---|---------|------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Resistor (R) | $v = iR$ | $i^2 R \; = \; v^2/R$ | $0$ (dissipated as heat) |
| 2 | Inductor (L) | $v = L \, di/dt$ | $L \, i \, di/dt$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ |
| 3 | Capacitor (C) | $i = C \, dv/dt$ | $C \, v \, dv/dt$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} C v^2$ |
| 4 | Ideal Voltage Source | $v = V_s \; (\text{const})$ | $V_s \cdot i$ | $0$ (delivers energy) |
| 5 | Ideal Current Source | $i = I_s \; (\text{const})$ | $v \cdot I_s$ | $0$ (delivers energy) |
| 6 | Total Power Balance | $P_{\text{total}} = \sum P_{\text{absorbed}} + \sum P_{\text{delivered}} = 0$ | — | Tellegen's Theorem |
| 7 | Passive Sign Convention | $P = +ve$ if current enters $\,(+)$ terminal | — | Reference direction rule |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The concept of *active vs passive* elements is foundational in designing **filters, oscillators, and power amplifiers** at the B.Tech level, and later in **VLSI and RFIC design** at the industry level.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Instantaneous Power Delivered to a Network Element

Let us rigorously derive the expression for instantaneous power absorbed by any two-terminal network element under the **passive sign convention**.

### Step 1: Define the Variables

Let a two-terminal element have:
- Voltage $v(t)$ measured across its terminals
- Current $i(t)$ flowing *into* the positive terminal

This is the **passive sign convention**, the standard used universally in KTU examinations.

### Step 2: Define Charge and Work

The charge entering the element in time $dt$ is:
$$dq = i(t) \, dt$$

The work done (energy supplied) in pushing this charge through the potential difference $v(t)$ is:
$$dW = v(t) \, dq$$

### Step 3: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting $dq$ into the work equation:

$$dW = v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$

### Step 4: Define Power as the Rate of Energy Transfer

By definition, power is the time-rate of change of energy:

$$p(t) = \frac{dW}{dt} = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,P(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)\,}$$

**Unit:** Watt (W) in SI base units, with **1 W = 1 J/s**.

If $P(t) > 0$, the element **absorbs** energy (passive). If $P(t) < 0$, the element **delivers** energy (active).

## 3.2 Derivation — Energy Stored in an Inductor

For an inductor, the V-I relationship is:
$$v_L(t) = L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting into the power formula $P = v \cdot i$:

$$P_L(t) = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} \cdot i(t)$$

Energy stored from $i = 0$ to $i = I$:

$$W_L = \int_0^I L \cdot i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L I^2$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2(t)\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Energy Stored in a Capacitor

For a capacitor, the V-I relationship is:
$$i_C(t) = C \cdot \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

Substituting into $P = v \cdot i$:

$$P_C(t) = v(t) \cdot C \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Energy stored from $v = 0$ to $v = V$:

$$W_C = \int_0^V C \cdot v \, dv = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{\,W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C v^2(t)\,}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Concept | Governing Equation | Constant of Proportionality | Type of Element |
|---------|--------------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| Ohm's Law | $v = i \cdot R$ | $R$ (resistance) | Bilateral, Linear, Passive |
| Inductor V-I | $v = L \cdot di/dt$ | $L$ (inductance) | Bilateral, Linear, Passive |
| Capacitor V-I | $i = C \cdot dv/dt$ | $C$ (capacitance) | Bilateral, Linear, Passive |
| Power Absorbed | $P = v \cdot i$ | — | Universal formula |
| Energy (Inductor) | $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2$ | — | Purely stored, not dissipated |
| Energy (Capacitor) | $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C v^2$ | — | Purely stored, not dissipated |
| Energy (Resistor) | $W_R = i^2 R t$ | — | Fully dissipated as heat |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Network Classification

The following Mermaid block-diagram provides a **hierarchical functional map** of how network elements are classified in Module 1 of PCECT205.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Network Elements]:::root
    A0 --> A1[Based on Energy Delivery]
    A0 --> A2[Based on V-I Symmetry]
    A0 --> A3[Based on Time Dependency]
    A0 --> A4[Based on Physical Size]
    A0 --> A5[Based on Linearity]

    A1 --> A1a[Active Elements]
    A1 --> A1b[Passive Elements]
    A1a --> A1a1[Independent Sources]
    A1a --> A1a2[Dependent Sources]
    A1a1 --> A1a1a[Voltage Source]
    A1a1 --> A1a1b[Current Source]
    A1a2 --> A1a2a[VCVS]
    A1a2 --> A1a2b[VCCS]
    A1a2 --> A1a2c[CCVS]
    A1a2 --> A1a2d[CCCS]
    A1b --> A1b1[Resistor R]
    A1b --> A1b2[Inductor L]
    A1b --> A1b3[Capacitor C]

    A2 --> A2a[Bilateral]
    A2 --> A2b[Unilateral]
    A2a --> A2a1[R, L, C, Transformer]
    A2b --> A2b1[Diode, Vacuum Tube, SCR]

    A3 --> A3a[Time Invariant]
    A3 --> A3b[Time Varying]
    A3a --> A3a1[Constant R, Fixed L, Fixed C]
    A3b --> A3b1[Potentiometer, Parametric Amp]

    A4 --> A4a[Lumped]
    A4 --> A4b[Distributed]
    A4a --> A4a1[Low Freq Lab Circuits]
    A4b --> A4b1[Transmission Line, Antenna]

    A5 --> A5a[Linear]
    A5 --> A5b[Non Linear]
    A5a --> A5a1[R, L, C, Ideal Sources]
    A5b --> A5b1[Diode, Transistor, Iron Core Coil]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000;
    classDef pass fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000000;
    classDef act fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000000;
```

## 4.2 Functional Flow — Energy Exchange in a Network

```mermaid
graph LR
    Src[Source Active]:::active --> P[Power Flow v times i]:::flow
    P --> R[Resistor Dissipates Heat]:::passive
    P --> L[Inductor Stores Magnetic Energy]:::passive
    P --> C[Capacitor Stores Electric Energy]:::passive
    R --> Sum[Total Power Balance equals Zero]:::sum
    L --> Sum
    C --> Sum
    Sum --> Result[Tellegens Theorem Validated]:::sum

    classDef active fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF;
    classDef passive fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000000;
    classDef flow fill:#FFDEAD,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000000;
    classDef sum fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#FFFFFF;
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read:** The left diagram maps the **5 standard KTU classifications** of network elements in a tree structure. The right diagram shows how energy from an active source is *partitioned* into heat dissipation and storage, governed by **Tellegen's Theorem** (Total power balance = 0).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1:** Differentiate between a *Network* and a *Circuit*. Provide one real-world example of each.

> **Model Answer:**
> A **network** is an interconnection of various electrical elements which may or may not form a closed loop. A **circuit** is a network that has at least one **closed conducting path** allowing continuous current flow.
>
> **Example of a Network:** An open-circuited voltage source connected to a resistor in series with an open switch — current cannot flow because the loop is broken. This is a network but not a circuit.
>
> **Example of a Circuit:** A simple flashlight circuit — battery, switch, and bulb connected in a closed loop.
>
> *Every circuit is a network, but every network is not a circuit.*

---

### **Question 2:** What are the four types of **dependent (controlled) sources**? State one practical application of each.

> **Model Answer:**
> 1. **VCVS** (Voltage Controlled Voltage Source) — Used in **op-amp non-inverting amplifier** models.
> 2. **VCCS** (Voltage Controlled Current Source) — Used in **FET small-signal amplifier** models.
> 3. **CCVS** (Current Controlled Voltage Source) — Used in **BJT high-frequency hybrid-π model**.
> 4. **CCCS** (Current Controlled Current Source) — Used in **BJT common-emitter small-signal analysis**.
>
> Dependent sources model the *controlled behavior* of active devices like transistors and op-amps.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, the student must answer **one full question** (either Option A OR Option B) from this module. Each option carries two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### ✍️ **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Define the following network properties with one example each: *(7 Marks)*
   (i) Active vs Passive Element
   (ii) Linear vs Non-Linear Element
   (iii) Bilateral vs Unilateral Element

**(b)** For the given circuit, calculate the total power delivered by the source and verify Tellegen's theorem. *(7 Marks)*

> **Given:** A series circuit with a 10 V DC source, a 4 $\Omega$ resistor, and a 6 $\Omega$ resistor. Current is constant throughout.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**(i) Active vs Passive Elements:**
- **Active elements** *generate or deliver* energy to the circuit (e.g., battery, generator, op-amp output stage).
- **Passive elements** *absorb* energy and either dissipate it (R) or store it (L, C).
- **Example:** A 12 V car battery is active; a 100 $\Omega$ resistor connected across it is passive.

**(ii) Linear vs Non-Linear Elements:**
- **Linear elements** obey the **superposition principle** and produce a response directly proportional to the input (V-I graph is a straight line passing through the origin).
- **Non-linear elements** do not obey superposition; their V-I characteristic is a non-linear curve.
- **Example:** A standard carbon-film resistor is linear; a **p-n junction diode** is non-linear.

**(iii) Bilateral vs Unilateral Elements:**
- **Bilateral elements** offer the same V-I characteristic in *both directions* of current flow.
- **Unilateral elements** allow current in only *one direction*.
- **Example:** A 1 k$\Omega$ resistor is bilateral; a **silicon diode** (1N4007) is unilateral.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1:** Identify total series resistance.
$$R_{\text{total}} = 4 \; \Omega + 6 \; \Omega = 10 \; \Omega$$

**Step 2:** Apply Ohm's Law to find circuit current.
$$I = \frac{V}{R_{\text{total}}} = \frac{10 \; \text{V}}{10 \; \Omega} = 1 \; \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate power delivered by the source.
$$P_{\text{source}} = V \cdot I = 10 \cdot 1 = 10 \; \text{W} \quad (\text{delivered})$$

**Step 4:** Calculate power absorbed by each resistor.
$$P_{4\Omega} = I^2 R = (1)^2 \cdot 4 = 4 \; \text{W}$$
$$P_{6\Omega} = I^2 R = (1)^2 \cdot 6 = 6 \; \text{W}$$

**Step 5:** Verify Tellegen's Theorem (Total Power Balance).
$$P_{\text{absorbed}} = 4 + 6 = 10 \; \text{W}$$
$$P_{\text{delivered}} - P_{\text{absorbed}} = 10 - 10 = 0 \; \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** Tellegen's Theorem is satisfied. Total power delivered equals total power absorbed.

---

### ✍️ **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for energy stored in an inductor and a capacitor. State the units. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A 2 H inductor carries a current that varies as $i(t) = 3\sin(100t)$ A. Find: *(7 Marks)*
   (i) The voltage across the inductor.
   (ii) The energy stored at $t = 0$ seconds.
   (iii) The maximum energy stored.

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Inductor Energy Derivation:**

For an inductor, the voltage-current relationship is:
$$v_L(t) = L \cdot \frac{di(t)}{dt}$$

Instantaneous power absorbed:
$$P_L(t) = v_L(t) \cdot i(t) = L \cdot i(t) \cdot \frac{di}{dt}$$

Energy stored from $i = 0$ to $i = I$:
$$W_L = \int_0^I L \cdot i \, di = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_L = \tfrac{1}{2} L i^2 \; \text{Joules}$$

**Capacitor Energy Derivation:**

For a capacitor:
$$i_C(t) = C \cdot \frac{dv(t)}{dt}$$

Instantaneous power absorbed:
$$P_C(t) = v(t) \cdot C \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Energy stored from $v = 0$ to $v = V$:
$$W_C = \int_0^V C \cdot v \, dv = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad W_C = \tfrac{1}{2} C v^2 \; \text{Joules}$$

**Units:** $L$ in Henry (H), $C$ in Farad (F), energy in Joules (J).

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Given:** $L = 2 \; \text{H}$, $i(t) = 3\sin(100t) \; \text{A}$

**(i) Voltage across the inductor:**

$$v_L(t) = L \cdot \frac{di}{dt} = 2 \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\big[3\sin(100t)\big]$$
$$v_L(t) = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 100 \cdot \cos(100t)$$
$$v_L(t) = 600 \cos(100t) \; \text{V}$$

**(ii) Energy stored at $t = 0$ seconds:**

At $t = 0$: $i(0) = 3\sin(0) = 0 \; \text{A}$
$$W(0) = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (0)^2 = 0 \; \text{J}$$

**(iii) Maximum energy stored:**

Maximum current occurs when $\sin(100t) = \pm 1$, i.e., $I_{\max} = 3 \; \text{A}$.
$$W_{\max} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot L \cdot I_{\max}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 2 \cdot (3)^2 = 9 \; \text{J}$$

> **Final Answer:** $v_L(t) = 600\cos(100t) \; \text{V}$, $W(0) = 0 \; \text{J}$, $W_{\max} = 9 \; \text{J}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students most commonly **lose 1–2 marks** in this topic for the following reasons:
>
> 1. **Confusing Network vs Circuit:** Writing *"a network is a closed loop"* is **incorrect**. Many lose the 1-mark definition difference.
> 2. **Ignoring Passive Sign Convention:** When computing power, if current enters the *negative* terminal, students forget to **invert the sign**, leading to wrong answers.
> 3. **Dependent Source Misnaming:** Writing *CCCS* as *Current Controlled Current Source* is correct, but many students skip *"Controlled"* and lose half a mark.
> 4. **Energy Units:** The energy stored in L and C is in **Joules (J)**, not Watt-seconds — be precise with the unit.
> 5. **Tellegen's Theorem Verification:** When asked to verify, students *must compute both sides separately* and show they sum to zero. Writing "verified" without arithmetic fetches 0 marks.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Network** may or may not have a closed loop; a **Circuit** must have at least one closed loop.
- **Active elements** *deliver* energy; **Passive elements** *absorb* energy.
- **Linear elements** obey both **homogeneity** and **superposition**.
- **Bilateral elements** are symmetric in current direction; **Unilateral** elements (e.g., diode) are not.
- **Lumped elements** have physical dimensions $d \ll \lambda$; **distributed** elements do not.
- The **four dependent sources** are: **VCVS, VCCS, CCVS, CCCS** — memorize all four.
- The **passive sign convention** states: current enters the positive terminal ⇒ $P = +vi$ (absorbed).
- **Power formulas:** $P = vi = i^2R = v^2/R$.
- **Energy stored:** $W_L = \tfrac{1}{2}Li^2$ and $W_C = \tfrac{1}{2}Cv^2$.
- **Tellegen's Theorem:** $\sum P_{\text{delivered}} = \sum P_{\text{absorbed}}$ (total power balance = 0).
- At 50 Hz, wavelength $\lambda = 6000 \; \text{km}$, so all standard lab circuits are **lumped**.
- The standard **KTU exam trick** is to swap "active/passive" and "linear/non-linear" definitions in MCQs — read carefully.
- Always **state units** in the final answer (W, J, V, A, $\Omega$, H, F) for full marks in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Circuit Variables # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Introduction to Circuit Variables In the discipline of **Network Theory (PCECT205)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, every electrical circuit — from a simple torchlight to a complex smartphone motherboard — is described using a fundamental set of measurable quantities known as **Circuit Variables**. These variables act as the "language" used by engineers to analyze how energy and information flow through electrical networks. The four primary circuit variables are: 1. **Charge (Q)** — measured in **Coulombs (C)** 2. **Current (I)** — measured in **Amperes (A)** 3. **Voltage (V)** — measured in **Volts (V)** 4. **Power (P)** and **Energy (W)** — measured in **Watts (W)** and **Joules (J)** respectively > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):** The 2024 Scheme explicitly mandates that students must master *Charge, Current, Voltage, Power, Energy, Reference Directions, and the Passive Sign Convention*. These form the bedrock for every subsequent module — mesh analysis, nodal analysis, network theorems, and transient analysis all depend on these variables. --- ## 1.2 Charge (Q) **Formal Definition:** *Charge is the fundamental property of matter (specifically electrons and protons) that causes it to experience a force when placed in an electromagnetic field. In circuit analysis, charge is the quantity of electricity that flows through a conductor.* $$Q = N \cdot e$$ Where: - $Q$ = total charge in **Coulombs (C)** - $N$ = number of electrons - $e$ = charge of a single electron $= -1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ **C** > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constant:** The elementary charge $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (magnitude). This is a **fundamental constant of nature** that KTU examiners often quote in numerical problems. ### Conceptual Analogy Think of charge like the **volume of water in a tank**. Just as you can have 5 liters or 50 liters of water, you can have 5 C or 50 C of charge. Charge is essentially the "stuff" that electrical phenomena are made of. --- ## 1.3 Electric Current (I) **Formal Definition:** *Electric current is the rate of flow of electric charge through a cross-section of a conductor with respect to time.* $$i(t) = \frac{dq(t)}{dt}$$ For a constant (DC) current: $$I = \frac{Q}{t}$$ Where: - $i(t)$ = instantaneous current in **Amperes (A)** - $dq(t)$ = infinitesimal charge in **Coulombs (C)** - $dt$ = infinitesimal time in **seconds (s)** ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a water pipe. The **current** is analogous to the **rate of water flow** — how many liters of water pass through a given point per second. More charge flowing per second = higher current. > [!NOTE] > **Conventional Current vs. Electron Flow:** By KTU convention (and globally adopted), current flows from the **positive (+) terminal to the negative (-) terminal** of a source — opposite to the actual electron motion. This is **Benjamin Franklin's convention** adopted in 1751 and remains the standard in all circuit diagrams. --- ## 1.4 Voltage / Potential Difference (V) **Formal Definition:** *Voltage is the energy required to move a unit positive charge from one point to another in an electric circuit, measured in Volts (V). It represents the potential difference between two nodes.* $$V_{AB} = \frac{dW}{dq}$$ Where: - $V_{AB}$ = voltage from point A to B in **Volts (V)** - $dW$ = energy in **Joules (J)** - $dq$ = charge in **Coulombs (C)** ### Conceptual Analogy Voltage is like **water pressure** in a pipe. Even if the pipe is closed (no current flow), there can still be pressure (voltage). Voltage is the "push" that drives current through a circuit. A 9V battery has more "push" than a 1.5V AA cell. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Voltage is ALWAYS measured between TWO points.** It is a relative (potential difference) quantity, not absolute. Saying "voltage at a node" is meaningless without a reference (typically called "ground" or the negative terminal). --- ## 1.5 Power (P) **Formal Definition:** *Power is the rate at which electrical energy is absorbed or delivered by an element per unit time, measured in Watts (W).* $$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = \frac{dW}{dt}$$ For DC circuits: $$P = V \cdot I = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$$ ### Conceptual Analogy Power is like the **rate of doing work** — a powerful engine consumes fuel faster than a weak one. A 100W bulb consumes energy twice as fast as a 50W bulb. --- ## 1.6 Energy (W) **Formal Definition:** *Electrical energy is the total work done in moving charges through a circuit over a period of time, measured in Joules (J).* $$W = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p(t) \, dt = V \cdot I \cdot t \quad \text{(for DC)}$$ ### Conceptual Analogy If power is the **rate of spending money**, energy is the **total money spent**. Your electricity bill is based on energy (kWh), not power (kW). > [!NOTE] > **Practical Units:** Electricity bills use **kWh (kilowatt-hour)**, where $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J = **3.6 MJ**. This is a frequently tested numerical conversion in KTU exams. --- ## 1.7 Reference Directions — The Critical Convention Since current and voltage are abstract quantities (not physical objects), we must assign **reference directions** before analyzing any circuit. - **Current Reference:** An arrow drawn on the wire indicating the *assumed* direction of positive current flow. - **Voltage Reference:** A "+" and "−" sign across an element indicating the *assumed* higher-potential terminal. If the calculated value turns out **negative**, it simply means the actual direction is **opposite** to the assumed reference. --- ## 1.8 Passive Sign Convention (PSC) This is the **single most important convention** in Network Theory and a guaranteed question in KTU exams. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Passive Sign Convention Rule:** Current enters through the **positive (higher potential) terminal** of an element and exits through the **negative (lower potential) terminal**. Under PSC: > - **Power absorbed** $P = +VI$ (Passive elements like resistors always absorb power) > - **Power delivered** $P = -VI$ (Active elements like batteries deliver power) # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of Circuit Variables The four circuit variables are **interdependent**. They form a closed mathematical framework: 1. **Charge** is the *primordial quantity* — it is the substance being moved. 2. **Current** is *charge in motion* — its time derivative. 3. **Voltage** is *energy per unit charge* — the driving force for current. 4. **Power** is *energy transfer rate* — current × voltage. 5. **Energy** is *integrated power* — power × time. ### Logical Flow of Analysis ``` Charge (Q) → differentiate → Current (I) Current (I) × Voltage (V) → multiply → Power (P) Power (P) → integrate over time → Energy (W) ``` Every step has a precise physical meaning: - Differentiating charge gives the **rate of flow**. - Multiplying current × voltage gives the **rate of energy transfer**. - Integrating power gives the **total energy consumed or delivered**. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Examination of Each Variable ### 2.2.1 Current — The Time Derivative of Charge $$i(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta q}{\Delta t} = \frac{dq}{dt}$$ **Physical Interpretation:** - If $i(t) > 0$ → current flows in the assumed reference direction. - If $i(t) < 0$ → current actually flows opposite to the assumed direction. - If $i(t) = 0$ → no current flows (open circuit). **Types of Current in KTU Syllabus:** - **Direct Current (DC):** Magnitude and direction are constant. $I(t) = I_0$ - **Alternating Current (AC):** Magnitude and direction vary sinusoidally. $i(t) = I_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$ ### 2.2.2 Voltage — The Spatial Derivative of Energy $$V_{AB} = \frac{W_{AB}}{Q}$$ Where $W_{AB}$ is the work done by an external agent in moving charge $Q$ from A to B. **Key Properties:** - Voltage is a **scalar** with a polarity (sign). - It is **path-independent** in conservative electric fields. - $V_{AB} = -V_{BA}$ always. ### 2.2.3 Power and Energy — The Energy Transfer Framework **Instantaneous Power:** $$p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$$ **Sign Convention (Critical for KTU):** | Condition | Sign of P | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---| | $V$ and $I$ in PSC (current enters +) | $P > 0$ | Element **absorbs** power | | $V$ and $I$ in PSC | $P < 0$ | Element **delivers** power | | $V$ and $I$ NOT in PSC | $P > 0$ | Element **delivers** power | | $V$ and $I$ NOT in PSC | $P < 0$ | Element **absorbs** power | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** Marks are frequently lost because students forget to **multiply by -1** when the element is **not** in passive sign convention. Always re-draw the element in PSC before applying $P = VI$. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **SI Unit** | **Practical Unit** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Charge | $Q$ | $Q = N \cdot e$ | Coulomb (C) | Ah (Ampere-hour) | | Current | $I, i(t)$ | $I = \frac{dq}{dt}$ | Ampere (A) | mA, μA | | Voltage | $V, v(t)$ | $V = \frac{dW}{dq}$ | Volt (V) | mV, kV | | Power (DC) | $P$ | $P = V \cdot I$ | Watt (W) | mW, kW, MW | | Power (Resistor) | $P$ | $P = I^2 R = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | Watt (W) | — | | Energy (DC) | $W$ | $W = P \cdot t$ | Joule (J) | kWh | | Energy (AC) | $W$ | $W = \int p(t)\, dt$ | Joule (J) | kWh | | Conversion | — | $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J | — | — | | Elementary Charge | $e$ | $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C | C | — | --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility These circuit variables are **not academic abstractions** — they are the daily tools of every electrical engineer: | **Domain** | **Application of Circuit Variables** | |---|---| | Power Systems | Calculating energy bills using $W = VIt$ | | Electronics Design | Power dissipation in ICs to prevent thermal failure | | Battery Design | Charge capacity rated in Ah (Ampere-hours) | | Electric Vehicles | Power (kW) for performance, Energy (kWh) for range | | PCB Design | Trace current limits determined by $P = I^2R$ heating | | Solar Engineering | Power output = Voltage × Current from PV panels | | Telecom | Signal integrity depends on precise voltage/current levels | > [!IMPORTANT] > Every subsequent chapter of PCECT205 — Ohm's Law, Kirchhoff's Laws, Mesh Analysis, Nodal Analysis, Network Theorems (Thevenin, Norton, Superposition), and Transient Analysis — assumes you are **fluent in circuit variables**. Master this module now, and the rest of the course becomes 10× easier. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current as the Time Derivative of Charge ### Objective Establish the mathematical foundation that current is the rate of change of charge. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Let $\Delta Q$ be the amount of charge passing through a cross-section of a conductor in time $\Delta t$. **Step 2:** The average current over this interval is defined as: $$I_{avg} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}$$ **Step 3:** To obtain the *instantaneous* current (which is what KTU problems and real-world signals deal with), we take the limit as $\Delta t \to 0$: $$i(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta t}$$ **Step 4:** In calculus notation, this limit is the derivative of charge with respect to time: $$\boxed{i(t) = \frac{dq(t)}{dt}}$$ **Step 5:** Inversely, the total charge transferred from time $t_1$ to $t_2$ is obtained by integrating the current: $$Q = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} i(t) \, dt$$ **Physical Interpretation:** This derivation shows that current is fundamentally a *rate quantity*. A charge of 10 C flowing in 2 seconds gives $I = 5$ A. The same 10 C flowing in 0.1 second gives $I = 100$ A — same charge, very different current. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Voltage from the Definition of Electric Potential ### Objective Derive the relationship between voltage, energy, and charge. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Consider a small positive test charge $dq$ located at point A in an electric field. **Step 2:** To move this charge from point A to point B against the electric field, an external agent must perform work $dW$. **Step 3:** The voltage (potential difference) between A and B is defined as the work done **per unit charge**: $$V_{AB} = \frac{dW}{dq}$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging, the work done is: $$dW = V_{AB} \cdot dq$$ **Step 5:** Therefore, the total work done in moving charge $Q$ from A to B (where voltage may vary) is: $$W_{AB} = \int_{A}^{B} V \cdot dq$$ **Step 6:** Equivalently, if voltage is constant: $$\boxed{W = V \cdot Q}$$ This is a heavily tested KTU numerical form: **Work = Voltage × Charge**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Power and Energy Relationship ### Objective Establish that power is the time rate of energy transfer. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Starting from the voltage definition: $V = \frac{dW}{dq}$ **Step 2:** Multiply both sides by current $i = \frac{dq}{dt}$: $$V \cdot i = \frac{dW}{dq} \cdot \frac{dq}{dt}$$ **Step 3:** By chain rule simplification, $dq$ cancels: $$V \cdot i = \frac{dW}{dt}$$ **Step 4:** Define the left side as instantaneous power $p(t)$ and the right side as the time rate of change of energy: $$\boxed{p(t) = \frac{dW}{dt} = v(t) \cdot i(t)}$$ **Step 5:** Integrating both sides from $t_1$ to $t_2$ gives the total energy: $$W = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p(t) \, dt = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$ **Step 6:** For constant voltage and current (DC): $$W = V \cdot I \cdot t$$ This is the basis of every electricity bill calculation worldwide. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Problem-Solving Framework The following worked example demonstrates the **complete analytical pipeline** from raw data to final energy calculation. ### Solved Example (Typical KTU Numerical Type) **Problem:** A current of $i(t) = 4t^2 + 2t + 1$ A flows through an element from $t = 0$ to $t = 3$ seconds. If the voltage across the element is constant at $V = 12$ V, find: (a) The total charge transferred. (b) The total energy delivered. (c) The average power. --- ### Part (a): Total Charge **Step 1:** Use the inverse relation between current and charge: $$Q = \int_{0}^{3} i(t) \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Substitute the expression: $$Q = \int_{0}^{3} (4t^2 + 2t + 1) \, dt$$ **Step 3:** Integrate term-by-term: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \left[ \frac{4t^3}{3} + \frac{2t^2}{2} + t \right]_{0}^{3} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply the limits $t = 3$ and $t = 0$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \left( \frac{4(27)}{3} + (9) + 3 \right) - (0 + 0 + 0) \\ Q &= 36 + 9 + 3 \\ Q &= 48 \text{ C} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{Q = 48 \text{ Coulombs}}$$ --- ### Part (b): Total Energy Delivered **Step 1:** Energy is the integral of power over time: $$W = \int_{0}^{3} p(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{3} V \cdot i(t) \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Since $V = 12$ V is constant: $$W = V \int_{0}^{3} i(t) \, dt = V \cdot Q$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $V = 12$ V and $Q = 48$ C: $$W = 12 \times 48 = 576 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{W = 576 \text{ Joules}}$$ --- ### Part (c): Average Power **Step 1:** Average power is total energy divided by total time: $$P_{avg} = \frac{W}{T} = \frac{576}{3}$$ $$\boxed{P_{avg} = 192 \text{ Watts}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Insight Summary For the KTU exam, the derivation chain you must internalize is: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &\xrightarrow{\text{differentiate}} i(t) = \frac{dq}{dt} \\ i(t), V(t) &\xrightarrow{\text{multiply}} p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) \\ p(t) &\xrightarrow{\text{integrate}} W = \int p(t)\,dt \end{aligned} $$ This three-step ladder (**differentiate → multiply → integrate**) is the conceptual backbone of the entire PCECT205 syllabus. Every numerical problem in this module reduces to one or more of these operations. # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Circuit Variables Interrelationship The following diagram maps the **functional relationships** between all five circuit variables. This is the conceptual map you should mentally carry into the exam hall. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[CHARGE Q
Coulombs] -->|differentiate
dq by dt| B[CURRENT I
Amperes] B -->|integrate
over time| A A -->|multiply by V| C[ENERGY W
Joules] C -->|differentiate
dW by dt| D[POWER P
Watts] D -->|integrate
over time| C B -->|multiply by V| D E[VOLTAGE V
Volts] -->|multiply by I| D F[REFERENCE DIRECTIONS
Arrow for I and plus minus for V] --> A F --> B F --> E G[PASSIVE SIGN CONVENTION
Current enters plus terminal] --> D ``` --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Energy Flow This block diagram illustrates how charge, current, voltage, and power interact in a generic two-terminal circuit element. ```mermaid flowchart LR INP[Input Terminal
Positive Reference] --> CONV[Energy Conversion
Mechanism inside Element] CONV --> OUT[Output Terminal
Negative Reference] QMOD[Charge Q entering] --> CONV VMOD[Voltage V across] --> CONV CONV --> PMOD[Power P delivered or absorbed] CONV --> WMOD[Energy W transferred over time] PSC[Passive Sign Convention Check] --> PMOD PSC --> WMOD ``` --- ## 4.3 ASCII Schematic: Reference Direction Illustration Below is a text-based schematic showing the two scenarios — **Passive Sign Convention (PSC)** and **Active Sign Convention (ASC)** — for a generic two-terminal element. ``` SCENARIO A: PASSIVE SIGN CONVENTION (PSC) + ---/ \--- V (Elem) ----> I (current enters + terminal) - ---\__/--- P = + V * I (Power absorbed if P > 0) SCENARIO B: ACTIVE SIGN CONVENTION (ASC) + ---\__/--- V (Elem) <---- I (current enters - terminal) - ---/ \--- P = - V * I (Sign flip required!) ``` --- ## 4.4 Concept Map: Classification of Circuit Variables ```mermaid graph TD ROOT[CIRCUIT VARIABLES] --> L1[Through Variables] ROOT --> L2[Across Variables] ROOT --> L3[Derived Variables] L1[Through Variables - measured in series] --> L1A[Current I] L1 --> L1B[Charge Q] L2[Across Variables - measured in parallel] --> L2A[Voltage V] L3[Derived Variables - computed quantities] --> L3A[Power P] L3 --> L3B[Energy W] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Conceptual Note:** This classification (through vs. across variables) becomes critical in Module 2 when you study **topological constraints** and **Kirchhoff's Current/Voltage Laws**. A current is a **through variable** (same value through a series element), while voltage is an **across variable** (same across parallel elements). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **Define the following circuit variables with their SI units:** (a) Electric current (b) Voltage (c) Power ### Model Answer **(a) Electric Current (I):** The rate of flow of electric charge through a cross-section of a conductor is called electric current. $$I = \frac{dQ}{dt}$$ **SI Unit: Ampere (A)** — defined as the flow of 1 Coulomb of charge per second. **(b) Voltage (V):** The potential difference between two points is the work done per unit charge in moving a positive test charge from one point to the other. $$V = \frac{dW}{dQ}$$ **SI Unit: Volt (V)** — defined as 1 Joule of work done per Coulomb of charge ($1 \text{ V} = 1 \text{ J/C}$). **(c) Power (P):** The rate at which electrical energy is absorbed or delivered by an element is called power. $$P = V \cdot I$$ **SI Unit: Watt (W)** — defined as 1 Joule of energy transferred per second ($1 \text{ W} = 1 \text{ J/s}$). --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]** **State the Passive Sign Convention. A current of 5 A flows through a 10 Ω resistor from terminal A to B. If $V_A > V_B$ and $V_A - V_B = 50$ V, determine whether the resistor absorbs or delivers power.** ### Model Answer **Passive Sign Convention (PSC):** When the current enters the element through the positive (higher potential) terminal and exits through the negative terminal, the convention is called passive. Under PSC: $P = +V \cdot I$ (absorbs power). **Given Data:** - $I = 5$ A (flows from A to B) - $V_A - V_B = 50$ V → so $V_A > V_B$ - Terminal A is at higher potential (positive terminal) **Analysis:** Since current enters at A (positive terminal) and exits at B (negative terminal), the resistor is in **Passive Sign Convention**. $$P = V \cdot I = 50 \times 5 = 250 \text{ W}$$ Since $P = +250$ W (positive), the resistor **absorbs 250 W** of power. This is consistent with the physical fact that resistors are passive elements that always absorb energy (dissipating it as heat). --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Module Internal Choice: Select EITHER Question A OR Question B --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]** **(a)** Derive the relationship between charge, current, voltage, power, and energy. Define the Passive Sign Convention and explain its importance with two illustrative examples. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A current $i(t) = 10 e^{-2t}$ A flows through a 50 Ω resistor for $t \geq 0$. (i) Find the total charge that flows from $t = 0$ to $t = \infty$. (ii) Find the total energy dissipated in the resistor. (iii) Find the maximum instantaneous power absorbed. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Derivation of Relationships:** The four fundamental circuit variables are mathematically linked through differentiation and integration. **Step 1: Charge → Current** Current is the time derivative of charge: $$i(t) = \frac{dq(t)}{dt}$$ **Step 2: Current → Charge** By integration, the total charge transferred from $t_1$ to $t_2$ is: $$Q = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} i(t) \, dt$$ **Step 3: Voltage Definition** Voltage is the work done per unit charge: $$V = \frac{dW}{dq}$$ **Step 4: Power Derivation** Multiplying $V = \frac{dW}{dq}$ by $i = \frac{dq}{dt}$: $$V \cdot i = \frac{dW}{dq} \cdot \frac{dq}{dt} = \frac{dW}{dt}$$ $$\boxed{p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t) = \frac{dW}{dt}}$$ **Step 5: Energy** Total energy over time interval: $$W = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} p(t) \, dt = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) \cdot i(t) \, dt$$ For DC: $W = V \cdot I \cdot t$ **Passive Sign Convention (PSC):** - **Statement:** Current is assumed to enter the positive terminal of an element and exit from the negative terminal. - **Power Formula:** $P = +VI$ - **Sign Convention:** - $P > 0$ → element absorbs power - $P < 0$ → element delivers power **Example 1 (Resistor in PSC):** Current $I = 2$ A enters the positive terminal. $V = 10$ V across it. $P = VI = 20$ W → Resistor absorbs 20 W (correct, resistors always absorb). **Example 2 (Battery in PSC):** Current $I = 2$ A enters the positive terminal of a battery with $V = 10$ V marked + on that terminal. $P = VI = 20$ W (positive) — this means the battery *absorbs* 20 W, i.e., it is being **charged**, not delivering power. If the battery is delivering power, the current direction must be reversed (ASC). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $i(t) = 10e^{-2t}$ A, $R = 50$ Ω, $t \geq 0$. **(i) Total Charge $Q$ from $0$ to $\infty$:** **Step 1:** Use $Q = \int_{0}^{\infty} i(t) \, dt$: $$Q = \int_{0}^{\infty} 10e^{-2t} \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Integrate: $$Q = 10 \left[ \frac{e^{-2t}}{-2} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$ **Step 3:** Apply limits: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= 10 \left( 0 - \frac{e^{0}}{-2} \right) \\ Q &= 10 \left( \frac{1}{2} \right) \\ Q &= 5 \text{ C} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{Q = 5 \text{ Coulombs}}$$ --- **(ii) Total Energy Dissipated:** **Step 1:** Energy in a resistor is $W = \int_{0}^{\infty} i^2(t) \cdot R \, dt$: $$W = \int_{0}^{\infty} (10e^{-2t})^2 \times 50 \, dt$$ **Step 2:** Simplify: $$W = 50 \times 100 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt = 5000 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t} \, dt$$ **Step 3:** Integrate: $$ \begin{aligned} W &= 5000 \left[ \frac{e^{-4t}}{-4} \right]_{0}^{\infty} \\ W &= 5000 \left( 0 + \frac{1}{4} \right) \\ W &= 1250 \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ $$\boxed{W = 1250 \text{ Joules}}$$ --- **(iii) Maximum Instantaneous Power:** **Step 1:** Instantaneous power absorbed: $$p(t) = i^2(t) \cdot R = (10e^{-2t})^2 \times 50 = 5000 e^{-4t} \text{ W}$$ **Step 2:** Since $e^{-4t}$ is monotonically decreasing, maximum occurs at $t = 0$: $$p_{max} = 5000 \cdot e^{0} = 5000 \text{ W}$$ $$\boxed{p_{max} = 5000 \text{ W} = 5 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of reference directions for current and voltage in circuit elements. A 100 W bulb operates at 220 V for 6 hours daily. (i) Calculate the current drawn by the bulb. (ii) Calculate the energy consumed per day in Joules and kWh. (iii) Calculate the monthly electricity bill at ₹8 per kWh (30 days). **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For the circuit element shown below (assume the current enters the negative terminal), the voltage is $V = -15$ V and the current is $I = 3$ A. (i) Determine whether the element is in PSC or ASC. (ii) Calculate the power absorbed and power delivered. (iii) Is this element active or passive? Justify. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Reference Directions — Concept:** Since current and voltage are abstract quantities, their actual direction in a circuit is often unknown *a priori*. Therefore, we assign: - **Current reference:** An arrow on the wire indicating the *assumed* positive direction of conventional current. - **Voltage reference:** A pair of "+" and "−" signs marking the *assumed* higher-potential terminal. If the calculated value comes out **negative**, the actual direction is opposite to the assumed reference. This approach allows systematic algebraic analysis. **Given Data:** $P = 100$ W, $V = 220$ V, $t = 6$ hours/day. **(i) Current Drawn:** **Step 1:** Using $P = V \cdot I$: $$I = \frac{P}{V} = \frac{100}{220}$$ **Step 2:** Compute: $$\boxed{I = 0.4545 \text{ A} \approx 454.5 \text{ mA}}$$ --- **(ii) Energy per Day:** **Step 1:** $W = P \cdot t = 100 \text{ W} \times 6 \text{ h} = 600 \text{ Wh}$ **Step 2:** Convert to Joules ($1 \text{ Wh} = 3600 \text{ J}$): $$W = 600 \times 3600 = 2{,}160{,}000 \text{ J} = 2.16 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$ **Step 3:** Convert to kWh: $$W = \frac{600}{1000} = 0.6 \text{ kWh}$$ $$\boxed{W = 2.16 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 0.6 \text{ kWh}}$$ --- **(iii) Monthly Bill (30 days):** **Step 1:** Monthly energy: $$W_{month} = 0.6 \text{ kWh/day} \times 30 \text{ days} = 18 \text{ kWh}$$ **Step 2:** Cost at ₹8/kWh: $$\text{Bill} = 18 \times 8 = ₹144$$ $$\boxed{\text{Monthly Electricity Bill} = ₹144}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given Data:** $V = -15$ V, $I = 3$ A, current enters the negative terminal. **(i) PSC or ASC?** **Step 1:** By definition, PSC requires current to enter the **positive** terminal. **Step 2:** Since the current enters the **negative** terminal in this problem, the element is in **Active Sign Convention (ASC)**. $$\boxed{\text{Convention: ASC}}$$ --- **(ii) Power Absorbed and Power Delivered:** **Step 1:** Under ASC, the power formula becomes $P = -VI$: $$P_{ASC} = -V \cdot I = -(-15)(3) = 45 \text{ W}$$ **Step 2:** Interpretation: - $P_{absorbed} = 45$ W (the element absorbs 45 W) - $P_{delivered} = -45$ W in ASC terms, OR equivalently, $P_{delivered}$ = 45 W (delivered to the external circuit from this element's perspective as a source) **Step 3:** Verify using a different method — convert to PSC by flipping the current reference: In PSC: $I_{PSC} = -3$ A, $V = -15$ V $$P_{PSC} = V \cdot I_{PSC} = (-15)(-3) = 45 \text{ W} \text{ (absorbed)}$$ $$\boxed{P_{absorbed} = 45 \text{ W}, \quad P_{delivered} = 45 \text{ W}}$$ --- **(iii) Active or Passive?** **Step 1:** An element is **passive** if it can *only* absorb energy ($P_{absorbed} \geq 0$ for all valid operating conditions). **Step 2:** An element is **active** if it *delivers* energy to the rest of the circuit (acts as a source). **Step 3:** Since this element **delivers 45 W** (it supplies power to the rest of the circuit), it is an **active element**. $$\boxed{\text{The element is ACTIVE (it delivers power)}}$$ **Justification:** Any element capable of delivering net energy over a period — batteries, generators, op-amps in source mode — is classified as active. A pure resistor can never be active because $P = I^2 R \geq 0$ always. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Blindness:** Many students write $P = VI$ mechanically without checking whether the element is in PSC. Always ask: "Does the current enter the + terminal?" If NO, use $P = -VI$. > 2. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing seconds, hours, Joules, and kWh without proper conversion costs 2–3 marks per problem. Remember $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J. > 3. **Improper Integration Limits:** For exponential decay problems like $i(t) = I_0 e^{-t/\tau}$, students often integrate from $0$ to a finite $t$ instead of $\infty$, missing the steady-state convergence. > 4. **Confusing $V_{AB}$ and $V_{BA}$:** $V_{AB} = -V_{BA}$. Always define the subscript order before plugging values. > 5. **Forgetting Passive Sign Convention Definition:** Simply writing $P = VI$ is incomplete. You must state the PSC rule explicitly in definitions for full marks. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Charge (Q)** is the fundamental substance; measured in **Coulombs (C)**; $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C. - **Current (I)** is the rate of charge flow: $i(t) = \frac{dq}{dt}$; measured in **Amperes (A)**. - **Voltage (V)** is energy per unit charge: $V = \frac{dW}{dq}$; measured in **Volts (V)**; always between two points. - **Power (P)** is energy per unit time: $p(t) = v(t) \cdot i(t)$; measured in **Watts (W)**. - **Energy (W)** is the integral of power: $W = \int p(t)\, dt$; measured in **Joules (J)** or **kWh** ($1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6$ J). - **Passive Sign Convention (PSC)** = current enters + terminal. Under PSC: $P = +VI$ (positive = absorbed). - **Active Sign Convention (ASC)** = current enters − terminal. Under ASC: $P = -VI$ (positive = absorbed). - **Reference directions** are assumed directions; a negative answer means the actual direction is opposite. - **Through variables** (current, charge) are measured in series; **Across variables** (voltage) are measured in parallel. - **Conventional current** flows from + to − (Franklin's convention), opposite to electron motion. - The differentiation-integration ladder: $Q \xrightarrow{d/dt} I \xrightarrow{\times V} P \xrightarrow{\int dt} W$. - For resistors: $P = VI = I^2R = \frac{V^2}{R}$ (all equivalent forms). - Resistors are always **passive** ($P \geq 0$); batteries/sources are typically **active** ($P_{delivered} > 0$). - Always re-draw elements in PSC before applying $P = VI$ to avoid sign errors.

topics.title: Ideal and Practical Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ideal and Practical Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Foundations of Network Analysis

## Topic: Ideal and Practical Sources

### 1.1 Core Technical Definition

In electrical network theory, a **source** is an active network element that supplies electrical energy to the circuit. Sources are broadly classified into two major categories based on the quantity they maintain constant:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An **Ideal Voltage Source** is a two-terminal active element that maintains a fixed terminal voltage irrespective of the current drawn by the load.
> An **Ideal Current Source** is a two-terminal active element that delivers a constant current regardless of the voltage developed across its terminals.

In reality, no physical source behaves as an ideal source. Every real-world source possesses some internal resistance (or internal conductance), which causes its terminal characteristics to deviate from the ideal behavior. Such sources are called **Practical Sources** (also termed as **Non-Ideal Sources** or **Real Sources**).

**Standard Metrics & Physical Constants:**

- The internal resistance of a practical voltage source is denoted as **$R_s$** (in **Ohms, $\Omega$**).
- The internal conductance of a practical current source is denoted as **$G_s$** (in **Siemens, S**), where $G_s = \frac{1}{R_s}$.
- The electromotive force (EMF) of an ideal source is denoted as **$V_s$** (in **Volts, V**).
- The short-circuit current of an ideal current source is denoted as **$I_s$** (in **Amperes, A**).

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview & Real-World Analogies

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — The Water Tank Model**
> Imagine a large overhead water tank connected to a tap:
> - An **Ideal Voltage Source** behaves like a tank whose water level (pressure) remains absolutely constant, no matter how fast you open the tap.
> - An **Ideal Current Source** behaves like a special pump that pushes a fixed quantity of water per second, regardless of the height to which the water must be raised.
> - A **Practical Voltage Source** is like the same tank, but with a narrow inlet pipe. When you open the tap, the level (pressure) drops slightly because some water is "lost" overcoming pipe friction (this is the **internal resistance**).

**For a quick engineering perspective:**

- A fresh car battery (12.6 V) acts almost like an ideal voltage source when powering small bulbs.
- A solar PV panel is a practical current source whose output current depends on sunlight intensity.
- A DC laboratory bench power supply (with regulation feedback) is a near-ideal voltage source.
- The current mirror circuit in analog ICs is engineered to behave as an ideal current source.

### 1.3 Types of Sources — Complete Classification

| Source Type | Maintains Constant | Independent/Dependent | Sub-Category |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Ideal Voltage Source | Terminal Voltage $V$ | Independent | DC or AC |
| Practical Voltage Source | Approximately constant $V$ | Independent | Thevenin Equivalent |
| Ideal Current Source | Terminal Current $I$ | Independent | DC or AC |
| Practical Current Source | Approximately constant $I$ | Independent | Norton Equivalent |
| Dependent Voltage Source | $V = k \cdot x$ | Dependent (Controlled) | VCVS, CCVS |
| Dependent Current Source | $I = k \cdot x$ | Dependent (Controlled) | VCCS, CCCS |

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Board Examiner's Insight**
> For the KTU 2024 End Semester Examination (ESE), Module 1 typically focuses on **independent sources**. Dependent sources are generally covered in Module 2 alongside network theorems. However, students should still recognize all four types of dependent sources for analytical questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ideal Voltage Source — Operational Logic

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$V_{terminal} = V_s = \text{constant}$$

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- The terminal voltage is **completely independent** of the load current $I_L$.
- Internal resistance $R_s = 0\ \Omega$ (a perfect conductor inside).
- When load is connected, $I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_L}$.
- **Short-circuit condition:** $R_L = 0 \Rightarrow I_{sc} \to \infty$. The ideal source delivers infinite current — physically impossible, hence "ideal."
- **Open-circuit condition:** $R_L = \infty \Rightarrow I_L = 0$, and $V_{terminal} = V_s$.

**V-I Characteristic (Graphical Representation):**

A straight horizontal line parallel to the current axis. Voltage is fixed; current can be any value.

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Engineering Note**
> In high-voltage power transmission, generator terminals are modeled as near-ideal voltage sources because their internal synchronous reactance (although not zero) is much smaller than the line impedance, so terminal voltage is relatively stiff.

---

## 2.2 The Practical Voltage Source

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$V_{terminal} = V_s - I_L \cdot R_s$$

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- The source is modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_s$ in **series** with an internal resistance $R_s$.
- As the load current $I_L$ increases, the voltage drop $I_L R_s$ across the internal resistance increases.
- Hence, the terminal voltage $V_{terminal}$ **decreases linearly** with load current.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} = V_s$ (when $I_L = 0$).
- **Short-circuit current:** $I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$ (when $V_{terminal} = 0$).

**V-I Characteristic:**

A straight line with a **negative slope** equal to $-R_s$, intersecting the voltage axis at $V_s$ and the current axis at $\frac{V_s}{R_s}$.

---

## 2.3 The Ideal Current Source — Operational Logic

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$I_{terminal} = I_s = \text{constant}$$

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- The terminal current is **completely independent** of the load voltage $V_L$.
- Internal resistance $R_s = \infty\ \Omega$ (an open circuit inside the source).
- The voltage across the source adjusts itself based on the load: $V_{terminal} = I_s \cdot R_L$.
- **Open-circuit condition:** $R_L = \infty \Rightarrow V_{terminal} \to \infty$. The ideal source develops infinite voltage.
- **Short-circuit condition:** $R_L = 0 \Rightarrow V_{terminal} = 0$, and $I_L = I_s$.

**V-I Characteristic:**

A straight vertical line parallel to the voltage axis. Current is fixed; voltage can be any value.

---

## 2.4 The Practical Current Source

**Characteristic Equation:**

$$I_{terminal} = I_s - \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_s} = I_s - V_{terminal} \cdot G_s$$

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- The source is modeled as an ideal current source $I_s$ in **parallel** with an internal resistance $R_s$.
- As the terminal voltage $V_{terminal}$ increases, more current is shunted through $R_s$, leaving less for the load.
- **Short-circuit current:** $I_{sc} = I_s$ (when $V_{terminal} = 0$).
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{oc} = I_s \cdot R_s$ (when $I_{terminal} = 0$).

**V-I Characteristic:**

A straight line with a **negative slope** equal to $-\frac{1}{R_s}$, intersecting the current axis at $I_s$ and the voltage axis at $I_s R_s$.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Boundary Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Ideal Voltage Source | $V = V_s$ | V (Volts) | $R_s = 0\ \Omega$ |
| 2 | Practical Voltage Source Output | $V_L = V_s - I_L R_s$ | V | $R_s > 0$ |
| 3 | Practical Voltage Source Current | $I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$ | A | KVL on the loop |
| 4 | Ideal Current Source | $I = I_s$ | A (Amperes) | $R_s = \infty\ \Omega$ |
| 5 | Practical Current Source Output | $I_L = I_s - \frac{V_L}{R_s}$ | A | $R_s < \infty$ |
| 6 | Source Transformation (V→I) | $I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$ | A | $R_s$ is the same |
| 7 | Source Transformation (I→V) | $V_s = I_s \cdot R_s$ | V | $R_s$ is the same |
| 8 | Open-Circuit Voltage (Practical V) | $V_{oc} = V_s$ | V | $I_L = 0$ |
| 9 | Short-Circuit Current (Practical V) | $I_{sc} = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$ | A | $V_L = 0$ |
| 10 | Open-Circuit Voltage (Practical I) | $V_{oc} = I_s \cdot R_s$ | V | $I_L = 0$ |
| 11 | Short-Circuit Current (Practical I) | $I_{sc} = I_s$ | A | $V_L = 0$ |
| 12 | Maximum Power Transfer | $R_L = R_s$ | $\Omega$ | $P_{max} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$ |
| 13 | Efficiency at Max Power | $\eta = 50\ \%$ | — | For DC sources |
| 14 | Internal Conductance Relation | $G_s = \frac{1}{R_s}$ | S (Siemens) | Used in Norton form |

> [!NOTE] **How to Read the Table Safely**
> When written in plain text outside LaTeX, never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks the table parser. In the formula column above, the pipe $\vert$ is rendered using LaTeX's `\vert` or `\mid` command, or used outside the table structure.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

**Where these models are applied in industry:**

- **Battery Design:** Lithium-ion cell datasheets specify $V_{oc}$ and internal resistance — used to predict runtime under load.
- **Photovoltaic Systems:** Solar panels are modeled as practical current sources in parallel with a diode and series resistance.
- **Power System Protection:** Short-circuit current calculations rely on the Thevenin equivalent (practical voltage source) of the grid at the fault point.
- **Audio Amplifier Output:** The amplifier's output stage is modeled as a practical voltage source to determine loudspeaker damping.
- **Transistor Small-Signal Models:** The BJT and FET in active region are modeled as dependent current sources (covered in Electronics courses).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Terminal Voltage of a Practical Voltage Source

**Given:** A practical voltage source consisting of an ideal EMF $V_s$ in series with internal resistance $R_s$, connected to a load resistance $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the closed loop:**

The sum of voltage rises equals the sum of voltage drops around any closed loop.

$$V_s = V_{R_s} + V_{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Express the voltage drops using Ohm's Law:**

Let the loop current be $I_L$ (the load current). Since the components are in series, the same current flows through $R_s$ and $R_L$.

$$V_{R_s} = I_L \cdot R_s$$

$$V_{R_L} = I_L \cdot R_L$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the KVL equation:**

$$V_s = I_L \cdot R_s + I_L \cdot R_L$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common current $I_L$:**

$$V_s = I_L (R_s + R_L)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the load current $I_L$:**

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $I_L$ back to find the terminal (load) voltage:**

$$V_{terminal} = V_{R_L} = I_L \cdot R_L = \frac{V_s \cdot R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{V_{terminal} = \frac{V_s \cdot R_L}{R_s + R_L}}$$

**Limiting Case Check:**

- If $R_s \to 0$: $V_{terminal} \to V_s$ (becomes ideal).
- If $R_L \to \infty$ (open circuit): $V_{terminal} \to V_s$.
- If $R_L \to 0$ (short circuit): $V_{terminal} \to 0$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Terminal Current of a Practical Current Source

**Given:** A practical current source with ideal current $I_s$ in parallel with internal resistance $R_s$, connected to a load resistance $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the top node:**

The total current from the ideal source splits into the internal resistor branch and the load branch.

$$I_s = I_{R_s} + I_{R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Express each current using Ohm's Law:**

Both $R_s$ and $R_L$ share the same terminal voltage $V_{terminal}$.

$$I_{R_s} = \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_s}$$

$$I_{R_L} = \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the KCL equation:**

$$I_s = \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_s} + \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_L}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out the common voltage $V_{terminal}$:**

$$I_s = V_{terminal} \left( \frac{1}{R_s} + \frac{1}{R_L} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Use conductances for cleaner notation:**

Let $G_s = \frac{1}{R_s}$ and $G_L = \frac{1}{R_L}$. Then:

$$I_s = V_{terminal} (G_s + G_L)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the terminal voltage $V_{terminal}$:**

$$V_{terminal} = \frac{I_s}{G_s + G_L} = \frac{I_s \cdot R_s \cdot R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the load current $I_{R_L}$:**

$$I_{R_L} = \frac{V_{terminal}}{R_L} = \frac{I_s \cdot R_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{I_{load} = \frac{I_s \cdot R_s}{R_s + R_L}}$$

**Limiting Case Check:**

- If $R_s \to \infty$: $I_{load} \to I_s$ (becomes ideal current source).
- If $R_L \to \infty$ (open circuit): $I_{load} \to I_s$.
- If $R_L \to 0$ (short circuit): $I_{load} \to 0$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Source Transformation Equivalence

**Theorem:** A practical voltage source $V_s$ in series with $R_s$ can be replaced by a practical current source $I_s$ in parallel with the **same** $R_s$, where:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$$

**Proof:**

Consider a practical voltage source connected to a load $R_L$. The terminal voltage derived earlier is:

$$V_{terminal} = \frac{V_s \cdot R_L}{R_s + R_L}$$

The current delivered to the load is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Step 1 — Manipulate $I_L$ to introduce a fictitious $I_s$:**

Multiply and divide the numerator and denominator by $\frac{1}{R_s}$:

$$I_L = \frac{\frac{V_s}{R_s}}{\frac{R_s + R_L}{R_s}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$\frac{R_s + R_L}{R_s} = 1 + \frac{R_L}{R_s}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the new form as a current divider:**

$$I_L = \frac{I_s}{1 + \frac{R_L}{R_s}} = I_s \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

where $I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s}$.

**Step 4 — Identify the current-divider rule:**

A practical current source with $I_s$ feeding a parallel combination of $R_s$ and $R_L$ delivers exactly this current $I_L$ to $R_L$. This is precisely the **current divider rule**.

**Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{V_s,\ R_s \text{ (series)} \equiv I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s},\ R_s \text{ (parallel)}}$$

The reverse transformation is similarly obtained:

$$I_s,\ R_s \text{ (parallel)} \equiv V_s = I_s \cdot R_s,\ R_s \text{ (series)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Note**
> The key invariant in source transformation is the **internal resistance $R_s$**, which remains the same numerical value but shifts from series to parallel (or vice versa). The transformation is valid only for the **external behavior** at the terminals; internal power dissipation differs between the two equivalents.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer Theorem

**Given:** A practical voltage source $V_s$ with internal resistance $R_s$ supplying a variable load $R_L$.

**Step 1 — Express the power delivered to the load $R_L$:**

The load current is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

The power absorbed by the load is:

$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = \left( \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_L} \right)^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_s^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_s + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Take the derivative of $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$:**

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{d}{dR_L} \left[ \frac{R_L}{(R_s + R_L)^2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the quotient rule:**

Let $u = R_L$ and $v = (R_s + R_L)^2$.

$$\frac{du}{dR_L} = 1, \quad \frac{dv}{dR_L} = 2(R_s + R_L)$$

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{(R_s + R_L)^2 \cdot 1 - R_L \cdot 2(R_s + R_L)}{(R_s + R_L)^4}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the numerator:**

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{(R_s + R_L) - 2R_L}{(R_s + R_L)^3} = V_s^2 \cdot \frac{R_s - R_L}{(R_s + R_L)^3}$$

**Step 5 — Set the derivative to zero for maxima/minima:**

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0 \implies R_s - R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_s$$

**Step 6 — Confirm this is a maximum (second derivative test or sign analysis):**

For $R_L < R_s$: $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} > 0$ (function increasing).
For $R_L > R_s$: $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} < 0$ (function decreasing).

Hence $R_L = R_s$ gives the **global maximum**.

**Step 7 — Substitute $R_L = R_s$ to find the maximum power:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_s^2 \cdot R_s}{(R_s + R_s)^2} = \frac{V_s^2 \cdot R_s}{4 R_s^2} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{R_L = R_s \quad \text{and} \quad P_{max} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}}$$

**Efficiency at maximum power transfer:**

The total power supplied by the source is:

$$P_{total} = V_s \cdot I_L = V_s \cdot \frac{V_s}{R_s + R_s} = \frac{V_s^2}{2 R_s}$$

The efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{total}} = \frac{V_s^2 / 4 R_s}{V_s^2 / 2 R_s} = \frac{1}{2} = 50\ \%$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Source Classification Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Electrical Sources] --> branchA1[Independent Sources]
    startA --> branchA2[Dependent Sources]
    
    branchA1 --> subA1[Ideal Voltage Source]
    branchA1 --> subA2[Ideal Current Source]
    branchA1 --> subA3[Practical Voltage Source]
    branchA1 --> subA4[Practical Current Source]
    
    subA1 --> featA1[Rs equals 0 ohm]
    subA1 --> featA2[V is constant]
    
    subA2 --> featA3[Rs equals infinity]
    subA2 --> featA4[I is constant]
    
    subA3 --> featA5[Vs in series with Rs]
    subA3 --> featA6[Thevenin Model]
    
    subA4 --> featA7[Is in parallel with Rs]
    subA4 --> featA8[Norton Model]
    
    branchA2 --> depA1[VCVS]
    branchA2 --> depA2[CCVS]
    branchA2 --> depA3[VCCS]
    branchA2 --> depA4[CCCS]
```

## 4.2 Circuit Schematic: Practical Voltage Source with Load

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeV[Vs Source] --> nodeRs[Rs Internal]
    nodeRs --> nodeA[Node A]
    nodeA --> nodeRL[RL Load]
    nodeRL --> nodeB[Node B]
    nodeB --> nodeGND[Ground]
    nodeA --> nodeVT[V terminal across RL]
    nodeVT --> nodeB
    
    style nodeV fill:#FFE4B5
    style nodeRs fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeRL fill:#90EE90
    style nodeVT fill:#87CEEB
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:** The arrow chain `Vs → Rs → Node A → RL → Node B → Ground` represents the series current path. The terminal voltage $V_{terminal}$ is measured between Node A and Node B (across $R_L$). The pink-shaded $R_s$ is the *internal* resistance residing *inside* the source enclosure; the green-shaded $R_L$ is the *external* load.

## 4.3 V-I Characteristic Curves Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    charA[V-I Characteristic Plot] --> typeA1[Ideal Voltage Source]
    charA --> typeA2[Practical Voltage Source]
    charA --> typeA3[Ideal Current Source]
    charA --> typeA4[Practical Current Source]
    
    typeA1 --> descA1[Horizontal line, V equals Vs, slope 0]
    typeA2 --> descA2[Line with negative slope minus Rs, intercepts V axis at Vs]
    typeA3 --> descA3[Vertical line, I equals Is, slope infinity]
    typeA4 --> descA4[Line with negative slope minus 1 over Rs, intercepts I axis at Is]
    
    descA2 --> xintA[Current axis intercept at Vs divided by Rs]
    descA4 --> xintB[Voltage axis intercept at Is times Rs]
```

## 4.4 Source Transformation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sV[Practical Voltage Source: Vs in series with Rs] -->|Transform using Is equals Vs over Rs| sI[Practical Current Source: Is in parallel with Rs]
    sI -->|Transform using Vs equals Is times Rs| sV
    
    sV --> note1[Same Rs value, series to parallel shift]
    sI --> note2[Same Rs value, parallel to series shift]
    sV --> note3[Terminal behavior identical at external ports]
    sI --> note4[Terminal behavior identical at external ports]
```

## 4.5 Functional Block: Maximum Power Transfer Condition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcB[Source: Vs with internal Rs] --> calcB[Calculate RL for max power]
    calcB --> condB[Condition: RL equals Rs]
    condB --> pwrB[Maximum power equals Vs squared divided by 4 Rs]
    pwrB --> effB[Efficiency equals 50 percent]
    effB --> appB[Application: Communication circuits, impedance matching]
    
    calcB --> mathB1[Step 1: Write PL equals IL squared times RL]
    mathB1 --> mathB2[Step 2: Substitute IL from voltage divider]
    mathB2 --> mathB3[Step 3: Differentiate PL with respect to RL]
    mathB3 --> mathB4[Step 4: Set derivative to zero]
    mathB4 --> condB
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the following with neat V-I characteristics:**
**(a)** Ideal voltage source
**(b)** Practical voltage source
**(c)** Ideal current source

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Ideal Voltage Source:** A two-terminal active element that maintains a constant terminal voltage regardless of the magnitude or direction of the current drawn by the load. Its internal resistance is **$R_s = 0\ \Omega$**. The V-I characteristic is a **horizontal straight line** parallel to the current axis, intersecting the voltage axis at $V_s$.

**(b) Practical Voltage Source:** Modeled as an ideal voltage source $V_s$ in **series** with a small internal resistance $R_s$. The terminal voltage decreases as load current increases, governed by $V_{terminal} = V_s - I_L R_s$. The V-I characteristic is a **straight line with a negative slope** of $-R_s$, intercepting the voltage axis at $V_s$ and the current axis at $\frac{V_s}{R_s}$.

**(c) Ideal Current Source:** A two-terminal active element that delivers a constant current $I_s$ regardless of the voltage across its terminals. Its internal resistance is **$R_s = \infty\ \Omega$**. The V-I characteristic is a **vertical straight line** parallel to the voltage axis, intersecting the current axis at $I_s$.

> [!NOTE] Students should **always** draw the V-I characteristic graph in the answer script for full marks, as it forms a 1-mark component of the 3-mark question.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**State and explain the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem for a practical DC source. Mention the efficiency at the maximum power condition.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** For a linear bilateral network containing a practical DC voltage source $V_s$ and a fixed internal resistance $R_s$, the power delivered to a variable load resistance $R_L$ is **maximum** when the load resistance equals the internal (Thevenin) resistance of the source:

$$R_L = R_s$$

**Maximum Power Value:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$$

**Efficiency:** At the maximum power transfer condition, the efficiency of power transfer from source to load is exactly **$50\ \%$** (since half the total generated power is dissipated as heat inside $R_s$).

**Explanation:** When $R_L = R_s$, the load current is $I_L = \frac{V_s}{2 R_s}$, and the load voltage is $V_L = \frac{V_s}{2}$. The power delivered to the load is therefore $V_L \cdot I_L = \frac{V_s^2}{4 R_s}$. Any deviation of $R_L$ from $R_s$ (either lower or higher) results in lower load power.

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common student error is to write $R_L = R_s$ without showing the differentiation step. Always show at least the **derivative condition** $\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$ to score full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the terminal voltage of a practical voltage source with EMF $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$ and internal resistance $R_s = 2\ \Omega$ when connected to a load of $R_L = 10\ \Omega$. Also calculate the power delivered to the load and the power lost inside the source.

**(b)** A practical current source of $I_s = 5\ \text{A}$ in parallel with $R_s = 4\ \Omega$ is connected to a load of $R_L = 6\ \Omega$. Find (i) the load current, (ii) the load voltage, and (iii) the equivalent Thevenin voltage and resistance of this practical current source.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit configuration:**

The practical voltage source is a series combination of $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$ and $R_s = 2\ \Omega$, connected to a load $R_L = 10\ \Omega$.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL around the loop:**

$$V_s = I_L R_s + I_L R_L = I_L (R_s + R_L)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$24 = I_L (2 + 10) = 12 \cdot I_L$$

**Step 4 — Solve for load current:**

$$I_L = \frac{24}{12} = 2\ \text{A}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the terminal voltage (across the load):**

$$V_{terminal} = I_L \cdot R_L = 2 \times 10 = 20\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the power delivered to the load:**

$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = (2)^2 \times 10 = 4 \times 10 = 40\ \text{W}$$

Alternatively, $P_L = V_{terminal} \cdot I_L = 20 \times 2 = 40\ \text{W}$.

**Step 7 — Calculate the power lost inside the source (in $R_s$):**

$$P_{loss} = I_L^2 \cdot R_s = (2)^2 \times 2 = 4 \times 2 = 8\ \text{W}$$

**Step 8 — Verify total power balance:**

$$P_{total} = V_s \cdot I_L = 24 \times 2 = 48\ \text{W}$$

$$P_L + P_{loss} = 40 + 8 = 48\ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers for (a):** $V_{terminal} = 20\ \text{V}$, $P_L = 40\ \text{W}$, $P_{loss} = 8\ \text{W}$.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the practical current source parameters:**

$I_s = 5\ \text{A}$, $R_s = 4\ \Omega$ (in parallel with $I_s$), $R_L = 6\ \Omega$.

**Step 2 — Apply the current divider rule to find the load current:**

The current divider formula for the load is:

$$I_L = I_s \cdot \frac{R_s}{R_s + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$I_L = 5 \cdot \frac{4}{4 + 6} = 5 \cdot \frac{4}{10} = 2\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the load voltage (same as terminal voltage):**

$$V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = 2 \times 6 = 12\ \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Find the equivalent Thevenin voltage $V_{th}$:**

The Thevenin voltage is the open-circuit voltage at the terminals. With the load removed, all of $I_s$ flows through $R_s$:

$$V_{th} = I_s \cdot R_s = 5 \times 4 = 20\ \text{V}$$

**Step 6 — Find the equivalent Thevenin resistance $R_{th}$:**

The Thevenin resistance is the internal resistance seen from the terminals (with the ideal current source opened):

$$R_{th} = R_s = 4\ \Omega$$

**Step 7 — Verify using Thevenin model:**

With $V_{th} = 20\ \text{V}$ and $R_{th} = 4\ \Omega$ connected to $R_L = 6\ \Omega$:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{R_{th} + R_L} = \frac{20}{4 + 6} = \frac{20}{10} = 2\ \text{A} \quad \checkmark$$

**Final Answers for (b):** $I_L = 2\ \text{A}$, $V_L = 12\ \text{V}$, $V_{th} = 20\ \text{V}$, $R_{th} = 4\ \Omega$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of source transformation with a suitable example. Using source transformation, find the current through the $6\ \Omega$ resistor in the following circuit: A $12\ \text{V}$ practical voltage source with $R_s = 4\ \Omega$ in series is connected to a parallel combination of a $6\ \Omega$ resistor and a $2\ \Omega$ resistor.

**(b)** State and prove the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem. A practical voltage source of $V_s = 50\ \text{V}$ and $R_s = 5\ \Omega$ feeds a load $R_L$. Find (i) the value of $R_L$ for maximum power transfer, (ii) the maximum power delivered, and (iii) the efficiency at this condition.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Conceptual Explanation:**

Source transformation is a circuit analysis technique that allows a practical voltage source ($V_s$ in series with $R_s$) to be replaced by an equivalent practical current source ($I_s$ in parallel with $R_s$), and vice versa, **without changing the terminal behavior** at any external load. The transformation relations are:

$$I_s = \frac{V_s}{R_s} \quad \text{and} \quad V_s = I_s \cdot R_s$$

**Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the original circuit:**

$V_s = 12\ \text{V}$, $R_s = 4\ \Omega$ in series, feeding a parallel combination of $R_1 = 6\ \Omega$ and $R_2 = 2\ \Omega$.
