```mermaid
    stepI -->|Yes| stepC
    stepI -->|No| stepJ[Step 4: Algebraically Sum All Partial Responses]
    stepJ --> stepK[Step 5: Compute Final Voltage or Current]
    stepK --> stepL{Power Required?}
    stepL -->|Yes| stepM[Apply P equals V times I on Total Response]
    stepL -->|No| stepN[Output Final Answer]
    stepM --> stepN
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Source Deactivation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph beforeA[Original Circuit]
        v1A[Vs Source Active]:::active
        i1A[Is Source Active]:::active
    end

    subgraph deactA[Step 1: Vs Active Only]
        v2A[Vs Retained]:::active
        i2A[Is Replaced by Open]:::deactivated
    end

    subgraph deactB[Step 2: Is Active Only]
        v3A[Vs Replaced by Short]:::deactivated
        i3A[Is Retained]:::active
    end

    beforeA --> deactA
    beforeA --> deactB
    deactA --> sumA[Compute X1]
    deactB --> sumB[Compute X2]
    sumA --> finalA[X total equals X1 plus X2]
    sumB --> finalA

    classDef active fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deactivated fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Pitfall Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startP[Find Power in Element] --> branchP{Method Selection}
    branchP -->|WRONG Method| wrongP[Sum Individual Powers Pi]
    wrongP --> wrongR[Pi equals Vi squared divided by R]
    wrongR --> wrongO[Answer: Incorrect Higher Value]:::deactivated

    branchP -->|CORRECT Method| corrP[Step 1: Find Total V or I via Superposition]:::active
    corrP --> corrC[Step 2: Apply P equals I squared R on Total]
    corrC --> corrO[Answer: Correct Single Value]:::active

    classDef active fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef deactivated fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Superposition in Power Grid Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sourcePool[Source Pool in Grid]
        gen1A[Generator 1: 100 MW]:::active
        gen2A[Generator 2: 150 MW]:::active
        gen3A[Generator 3: 80 MW]:::active
    end

    sourcePool --> busA[Common Bus Bar]
    busA --> loadA[Load Network]

    subgraph analysisA[Superposition Analysis]
        isol1A[Isolate Gen 1 Contribution to Load]:::active
        isol2A[Isolate Gen 2 Contribution to Load]:::active
        isol3A[Isolate Gen 3 Contribution to Load]:::active
    end

    loadA --> isol1A
    loadA --> isol2A
    loadA --> isol3A

    isol1A --> resultA[Total Load Current]
    isol2A --> resultA
    isol3A --> resultA

    classDef active fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the Superposition Theorem. Mention one condition under which it is not applicable.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement:** The Superposition Theorem states that in any linear bilateral network containing multiple independent sources, the response (voltage or current) in any element is equal to the algebraic sum of the responses caused by each independent source acting alone, with all other independent sources replaced by their internal impedances.
>
> **Condition of non-applicability:** The theorem is **not applicable to non-linear networks** (e.g., circuits containing diodes, transistors operating in non-linear regions, saturable inductors). It is also not directly valid for **power calculations**, since power is a non-linear (quadratic) function of current/voltage. **(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for deactivation rule, 1 mark for limitation)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**While applying the Superposition Theorem, how are ideal voltage sources and ideal current sources treated when they are deactivated?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Source Type | Deactivation Treatment | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Ideal Voltage Source | Replaced by a **short circuit** | Setting $V = 0$ while keeping $R_{int} = 0$ is equivalent to a short |
| Ideal Current Source | Replaced by an **open circuit** | Setting $I = 0$ while keeping $R_{int} = \infty$ is equivalent to an open |

**(1 mark per row for treatment + 1 mark for the reasoning column)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Define the Superposition Theorem. State its **two essential conditions** for applicability. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For the circuit shown, determine the current through the **$10\ \Omega$ resistor** using the Superposition Theorem. **Given:** $V_1 = 30\ \text{V}$, $V_2 = 20\ \text{V}$, $R_1 = 5\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 10\ \Omega$ (target), $R_3 = 15\ \Omega$. The two voltage sources are connected in two adjacent loops sharing the $R_2$ branch. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Definition (3 Marks):** The Superposition Theorem is a network analysis principle stating that in any **linear, bilateral** network with multiple independent sources, the voltage across or current through any element is the **algebraic sum** of the voltages/currents produced by each independent source acting **independently** (with all other independent sources deactivated).

**Essential Conditions (4 Marks):**

1. **Linearity:** The network must be linear — all elements must obey Ohm's Law ($V = IR$ linearly). Diode, transistor, and saturable-core inductor circuits are excluded.
2. **Bilateral Nature:** The element's $V\text{-}I$ characteristic must be the same in both directions (e.g., standard resistors, inductors, capacitors qualify; diodes do not).
3. **Independent vs Dependent Sources:** The theorem applies to **independent** sources only. Dependent sources **must never be deactivated** — they are mathematical constructs dependent on circuit variables.
4. **Power Exception:** Superposition is valid for voltages and currents only, not for power directly.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Step 1: Activate $V_1 = 30\ \text{V}$, short $V_2$**

$$R_{eq,1} = R_1 + (R_2 + R_3) = 5 + (10 + 15) = 30\ \Omega$$

$$I'_{R_2} = \frac{V_1}{R_{eq,1}} = \frac{30}{30} = 1\ \text{A} \quad \text{(flowing left to right through } R_2 \text{)}$$

**Step 2: Activate $V_2 = 20\ \text{V}$, short $V_1$**

$$R_{eq,2} = R_3 + (R_1 + R_2) = 15 + (5 + 10) = 30\ \Omega$$

$$I''_{R_2} = \frac{V_2}{R_{eq,2}} = \frac{20}{30} = \frac{2}{3}\ \text{A} \quad \text{(flowing right to left through } R_2 \text{)}$$

**Step 3: Superposition**

$$I_{R_2} = I'_{R_2} - I''_{R_2} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}\ \text{A} \approx 0.333\ \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{R_2} = \frac{1}{3}\ \text{A, directed from left to right}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure to apply the Superposition Theorem for a circuit with **one voltage source and one current source**. What happens to the dependent sources during the analysis? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** In a two-loop network, $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$ (left loop), $I_s = 3\ \text{A}$ (right loop), $R_A = 8\ \Omega$ (left branch), $R_B = 12\ \Omega$ (middle/shared branch), and $R_C = 4\ \Omega$ (right branch). Calculate the **voltage across the shared branch** using Superposition. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Procedure (5 Marks):**

1. **Identify sources:** Recognize the one voltage source ($V_s$) and one current source ($I_s$).
2. **Case 1 — Voltage source active:** Replace the current source with an **open circuit** (since $I_s = 0$). Solve the resulting circuit using mesh/nodal analysis to get $V_1$ (partial voltage across the shared branch).
3. **Case 2 — Current source active:** Replace the voltage source with a **short circuit** (since $V_s = 0$). Solve the resulting circuit to get $V_2$ (partial voltage across the shared branch).
4. **Superpose:** $V_{total} = V_1 + V_2$ (with sign convention respected).

**Dependent Source Treatment (2 Marks):** Dependent sources are **mathematical functions** of circuit variables (like $V_x$ or $I_x$). They **must be retained** in the circuit during both sub-analyses. Deactivating them would change the network equations and lead to incorrect results. This is a critical distinction from independent sources.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Step 1: Activate $V_s = 24\ \text{V}$, open $I_s$**

With $I_s$ open, the $4\ \Omega$ branch is broken. The circuit reduces to $V_s$ in series with $R_A$ and $R_B$:

$$R_{eq,1} = R_A + R_B = 8 + 12 = 20\ \Omega$$

$$I_1 = \frac{V_s}{R_{eq,1}} = \frac{24}{20} = 1.2\ \text{A}$$

$$V_1 = I_1 \cdot R_B = 1.2 \times 12 = 14.4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Activate $I_s = 3\ \text{A}$, short $V_s$**

With $V_s$ shorted, $R_A$ and $R_B$ are in **parallel** (since the left end of $R_A$ is now grounded):

$$R_{parallel} = \frac{R_A \cdot R_B}{R_A + R_B} = \frac{8 \times 12}{8 + 12} = \frac{96}{20} = 4.8\ \Omega$$

The voltage across this parallel combination (which is also the voltage across $R_B$):

$$V_2 = I_s \cdot R_{parallel} = 3 \times 4.8 = 14.4\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3: Superposition**

$$V_{R_B} = V_1 + V_2 = 14.4 + 14.4 = 28.8\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{R_B} = 28.8\ \text{V}}$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Direction sign convention error** (–2 marks): Students often forget to assign a reference direction and add currents algebraically. Always **draw an arrow** on the target branch and stick to it.
> 2. **Deactivating a dependent source** (–2 to –3 marks): Dependent sources are NEVER turned off. This is a fatal error costing heavy marks.
> 3. **Power superposition** (–3 marks): Do not add powers directly. Compute total $I$ or $V$ first, then square to find power.
> 4. **Forgetting units** (–0.5 mark): Always state final answer in **V** or **A** explicitly.
> 5. **Incomplete sub-circuit diagrams** (–1 mark): Redraw the circuit for **each** sub-analysis. Don't just describe it in words.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP] **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print Before Exam)**

- **Superposition Theorem** is valid only for **linear, bilateral** networks.
- **Voltage Source → Short Circuit** when deactivated (since $V = 0$).
- **Current Source → Open Circuit** when deactivated (since $I = 0$).
- **Dependent sources are NEVER deactivated** — they stay in all sub-circuits.
- The theorem applies to **voltages and currents only**, **not power**.
- For $n$ independent sources, perform **$n$ separate sub-circuit analyses**.
- **Sign convention is critical** — always fix a reference direction for the target branch current/voltage before summing.
- **Power formula after superposition:** $P = I_{total}^2 \cdot R$ or $P = V_{total}^2 / R$.
- The theorem is a consequence of the **linearity property** (homogeneity + additivity).
- The constants in the superposition equation ($a_i, b_j$) depend only on the **passive network elements**, not on the source magnitudes.
- Common KTU problem types: **two-source DC networks**, **mixed voltage-current source circuits**, and **bridge networks with dependent sources**.
- **Practical utility:** Load flow in power grids, small-signal amplifier analysis, fault analysis, filter design, and noise superposition in communication systems.
- **Negative answers** in superposition are valid — they indicate the actual response is opposite to the assumed reference direction.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reciprocity Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reciprocity Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Reciprocity Theorem (KTU 2024 Standard Statement):** 
> *"In any linear, bilateral, passive, single-source network, the ratio of excitation (input) to response (output) remains constant when the positions of the excitation and response are interchanged."*

In more rigorous mathematical terms, the Reciprocity Theorem states that **if a voltage source $V_s$ connected in branch $ab$ produces a current $I_{cd}$ in branch $cd$ of a linear bilateral network $N$ containing only passive elements and no independent sources, then interchanging the source and the responding branch — i.e., placing the same voltage source $V_s$ in branch $cd$ — will produce the same current $I_{cd}$ in branch $ab$."*

The mathematical compact form is expressed as:

$$
\frac{V_s}{I_{cd}}\bigg|_{\text{original}} \;=\; \frac{V_s}{I_{ab}}\bigg|_{\text{interchanged}}
$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Two-Way Mirror of Networks:**
> 
> Imagine you stand at one end of a long, empty hallway and shout. The person at the far end hears you. Now, that person shouts back with the **same loudness**, and you hear them with the **same volume**. The hallway doesn't care who speaks and who listens — it is *reciprocal*. The path is the same; only the role of speaker and listener has swapped.
> 
> A **linear, bilateral, passive** electrical network behaves in exactly this manner. If a voltage source at Port 1 produces a current at Port 2, then that *same* source, when shifted to Port 2, will produce the *same* current back at Port 1. The network "doesn't remember" which side is the source and which is the load — it just sees a linear relationship.

### 1.3 Critical Pre-Conditions (Validity Requirements)

For the theorem to hold, the network **must** simultaneously satisfy ALL of the following:

| # | Condition | Why It Matters |
|---|-----------|----------------|
| 1 | **Linearity** | Superposition must hold; the response is proportional to the excitation. |
| 2 | **Bilateralism** | Each element's behavior is identical in both directions (e.g., a standard resistor). |
| 3 | **Passivity** | No dependent or independent energy sources *inside* the network — only the test source. |
| 4 | **Time-Invariance** | Parameters ($R$, $L$, $C$) do not change with time. |
| 5 | **Single Independent Source** | Only ONE source may exist in the network at the time of measurement. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Non-Reciprocal Elements:** Common KTU pitfalls include the inclusion of **dependent sources** or asymmetric elements such as **diodes, transistors (in active mode), and gyrators**. The theorem is **invalid** for such networks. A classic non-reciprocal example is an **op-amp circuit** containing a controlled source.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Units of Source: **Volts (V)** for voltage sources; **Amperes (A)** for current sources.
- Units of Response: **Amperes (A)** for current response; **Volts (V)** for voltage response.
- Standard SI Symbol for voltage: $V$ or $v(t)$; for current: $I$ or $i(t)$.
- The theorem applies to both **DC (steady-state)** and **AC (sinusoidal steady-state)** networks.

### 1.5 The Three Canonical Forms of Reciprocity

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners expect you to know **all three** manifestations of the Reciprocity Theorem:

| Form | Excitation (Source) | Response (Measured) | Network Topology |
|------|---------------------|----------------------|------------------|
| **Form I** | Voltage source $V_s$ | Current $I$ | Single-loop / two-port |
| **Form II** | Current source $I_s$ | Voltage $V$ | Single-node-pair / two-port |
| **Form III** | Voltage source $V_s$ | Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ | Specific impedance-matching condition |

Form I and Form II are the most commonly tested in KTU examinations. Form III is typically covered as a corollary under Two-Port Network Parameters (Y/Z/h parameters) where it equates the **transfer impedances** and **transfer admittances**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Logic Chain

The Reciprocity Theorem is best understood by deconstructing it into a four-step logical flow:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Excitation Branch:**
  Locate the branch where the *original* independent source (either voltage or current) is connected. Call this Branch $A$ (with terminals $a$–$b$).

- **Step 2 — Identify the Response Branch:**
  Locate the branch where the *original* response (current through, or voltage across) is to be measured. Call this Branch $B$ (with terminals $c$–$d$).

- **Step 3 — Apply KVL / KCL in the Original Configuration:**
  Using Mesh Analysis (for Form I) or Nodal Analysis (for Form II), solve for the response variable. The relationship is inherently *symmetric* because the impedance matrix of a bilateral network is symmetric ($Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$).

- **Step 4 — Interchange Source and Response:**
  Physically swap the source to the response branch and the measurement instrument to the source branch. **Re-analyze the network.** The response magnitude will be identical to the original, with the *only* possible difference being a sign change due to the choice of reference direction.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Reciprocity — A Deeper Look

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The mathematical **Why** of Reciprocity lies in the **symmetry of the network's impedance (or admittance) matrix** when expressed in standard form.

For a linear bilateral network, the off-diagonal elements of the mesh impedance matrix are symmetric:

$$
Z_{12} \;=\; Z_{21}
$$

And for the admittance matrix:

$$
Y_{12} \;=\; Y_{21}
$$

This **transposition symmetry** is the algebraic fingerprint of the Reciprocity Theorem. It is the direct consequence of the fact that the underlying energy storage (inductors, capacitors) and dissipation (resistors) elements all obey *symmetric* constitutive relations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Memorize this table** — it covers 95% of all KTU 2024 numerical problems on Reciprocity.

| # | Form | Original Setup | Interchanged Setup | Reciprocity Condition | Common Use |
|---|------|----------------|--------------------|------------------------|------------|
| 1 | **Voltage–Current** | $V_s$ in branch $ab$ $\rightarrow$ $I$ in branch $cd$ | $V_s$ in branch $cd$ $\rightarrow$ $I$ in branch $ab$ | $V_{s(1)}/I_{(1)} = V_{s(2)}/I_{(2)}$ | Mesh Analysis problems |
| 2 | **Current–Voltage** | $I_s$ entering node $a$ $\rightarrow$ $V$ across $cd$ | $I_s$ entering node $c$ $\rightarrow$ $V$ across $ab$ | $I_{s(1)}/V_{(1)} = I_{s(2)}/V_{(2)}$ | Nodal Analysis problems |
| 3 | **Voltage–Voltage (with $R_0$)** | $V_s$ with internal $R_0$ $\rightarrow$ $V_{oc}$ at far end | $V_s$ with internal $R_0$ $\rightarrow$ $V_{oc}$ at original end | Both open-circuit voltages equal if load $R_L = R_0$ | Matching networks |

### 2.4 Form I Detailed — Voltage Source & Current Response

This is the **most frequently tested** form in KTU Module 2.

**Original Configuration:** A voltage source $V_s$ is connected between nodes $a$ and $b$ of a passive linear network. A short circuit is placed between nodes $c$ and $d$, and the current $I_{cd}$ flowing through this short is measured.

$$
I_{cd} \;=\; \frac{V_s}{Z_{ab,cd}^{\text{transfer}}}
$$

**Interchanged Configuration:** The voltage source is now placed between $c$ and $d$, and a short circuit is placed between $a$ and $b$. The current $I_{ab}$ through this new short is measured.

$$
I_{ab} \;=\; \frac{V_s}{Z_{cd,ab}^{\text{transfer}}}
$$

By the Reciprocity Theorem:

$$
I_{cd} \;=\; I_{ab} \quad \text{(magnitudes equal; sign may invert based on reference)}
$$

### 2.5 Form II Detailed — Current Source & Voltage Response

**Original Configuration:** A current source $I_s$ is injected at node $a$ and extracted at node $b$. The open-circuit voltage $V_{cd}$ between nodes $c$ and $d$ is measured.

$$
V_{cd} \;=\; I_s \cdot Z_{ab,cd}^{\text{transfer}}
$$

**Interchanged Configuration:** The current source is moved to nodes $c$–$d$, and the open-circuit voltage $V_{ab}$ is measured.

$$
V_{ab} \;=\; I_s \cdot Z_{cd,ab}^{\text{transfer}}
$$

Reciprocity ensures:

$$
V_{cd} \;=\; V_{ab}
$$

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?**
> 
> - **Antenna Engineering:** The Reciprocity Theorem is the *theoretical foundation* of antenna theory. The **transmitting and receiving patterns of any antenna are identical** — this is why your Wi-Fi router's antenna works the same way regardless of whether it's transmitting or receiving.
> - **PCB Trace Design:** Engineers use reciprocity to simplify crosstalk analysis — the coupling between two traces is the same in both directions.
> - **Two-Port Network Parameters:** The Reciprocity Theorem is equivalent to saying the **$Z$-parameters**, **$Y$-parameters**, and **$h$-parameters** (in some forms) are symmetric. This dramatically simplifies the parameter matrix.
> - **Filter Design:** Reciprocal networks (LC ladders) form the backbone of all classical filter topologies.

### 2.7 Sign Convention — The Hidden Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **Reference Direction Trap:** KTU examiners routinely set up the same problem with **opposite reference directions** in the interchanged configuration. You must state the assumption: *"Assuming the current reference direction is the same in both cases."* The theorem holds for **magnitudes**; the sign is purely conventional.

### 2.8 Zero-Response Networks (Open/Short Special Case)

If the original response is **zero** (e.g., due to a bridge balance condition or an open circuit), then by reciprocity, the interchanged response is also **zero**. This is the principle behind the **Wheatstone Bridge** null detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Rigorous Mathematical Proof of Reciprocity Theorem (Form I)

#### 3.1.1 Network Setup (Two-Mesh Representation)

Consider a general linear, bilateral, passive network. The most general case can be reduced to a **two-mesh equivalent** by Thevenin's theorem or by superposition, where:

- **Mesh 1** contains the voltage source $V_s$ and a self-impedance $Z_{11}$.
- **Mesh 2** contains the short-circuit branch (the response branch) and a self-impedance $Z_{22}$.
- The two meshes share a **mutual impedance** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (the bilateral property).

We can represent this as a **two-port $\pi$-equivalent** or by directly writing the mesh equations.

#### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Original Configuration (Source in Mesh 1)

In the original network, the voltage source $V_s$ is in **Mesh 1** and we are measuring the current $I_2$ in **Mesh 2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mesh 1 KVL:} \quad & V_s \;=\; Z_{11} \cdot I_1 \;-\; Z_{12} \cdot I_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Mesh 2 KVL:} \quad & 0 \;=\; -Z_{12} \cdot I_1 \;+\; Z_{22} \cdot I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

The negative signs appear because the mesh currents are defined to circulate in the same direction (clockwise), making the mutual impedance terms opposite in sign.

#### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Solve for $I_2$ (Response Current) in Original

From Mesh 2 equation:

$$
Z_{12} \cdot I_1 \;=\; Z_{22} \cdot I_2
$$

Substitute $I_1$ into Mesh 1 equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s \;&=\; Z_{11} \cdot \left(\frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{12}}\right) I_2 \;-\; Z_{12} \cdot I_2 \\[6pt]
V_s \;&=\; \left(\frac{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22} - Z_{12}^{\,2}}{Z_{12}}\right) I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the response current in the original configuration is:

$$
I_2 \;=\; \frac{V_s \cdot Z_{12}}{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22} - Z_{12}^{\,2}}
$$

#### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Interchanged Configuration (Source in Mesh 2)

Now we swap the source and the response. The voltage source $V_s$ is placed in **Mesh 2** and we measure the current $I_1'$ in **Mesh 1**. The new mesh equations are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mesh 1 KVL:} \quad & 0 \;=\; Z_{11} \cdot I_1' \;-\; Z_{12} \cdot I_2' \\[6pt]
\text{Mesh 2 KVL:} \quad & V_s \;=\; -Z_{12} \cdot I_1' \;+\; Z_{22} \cdot I_2'
\end{aligned}
$$

#### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Solve for $I_1'$ (Response Current) After Interchange

From Mesh 1 equation:

$$
Z_{12} \cdot I_2' \;=\; Z_{11} \cdot I_1'
$$

Substitute into Mesh 2 equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_s \;&=\; -Z_{12} \cdot I_1' \;+\; Z_{22} \cdot \left(\frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{12}}\right) I_1' \\[6pt]
V_s \;&=\; \left(\frac{-Z_{12}^{\,2} + Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22}}{Z_{12}}\right) I_1'
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, the response current in the interchanged configuration is:

$$
I_1' \;=\; \frac{V_s \cdot Z_{12}}{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22} - Z_{12}^{\,2}}
$$

#### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Comparison and Conclusion

Comparing the two results:

$$
I_2 \;=\; \frac{V_s \cdot Z_{12}}{Z_{11} \cdot Z_{22} - Z_{12}^{\,2}} \;=\; I_1'
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The response current is **numerically identical** in both configurations. The reciprocity condition $I_2 = I_1'$ holds **if and only if** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, which is the mathematical statement of **bilateral symmetry** of the network. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Rigorous Mathematical Proof of Reciprocity Theorem (Form II)

#### 3.2.1 Nodal Analysis Setup

Let the passive linear bilateral network have two principal nodes $a$ and $b$ where the current source $I_s$ is connected. The voltage $V_{cd}$ is measured between two other nodes $c$ and $d$.

The nodal admittance matrix for a bilateral network is **symmetric**:

$$
Y \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{with} \quad Y_{12} \;=\; Y_{21}
$$

The relationship between the nodal voltages and the source current is given by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{s(1)} \;&=\; Y_{11} \cdot V_a \;-\; Y_{12} \cdot V_c \\[6pt]
0 \;&=\; -Y_{12} \cdot V_a \;+\; Y_{22} \cdot V_c
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $V_c$ (which equals $V_{cd}$ in this two-node case):

$$
V_{cd} \;=\; \frac{Y_{12}}{Y_{11} \cdot Y_{22} - Y_{12}^{\,2}} \cdot I_{s(1)}
$$

#### 3.2.2 Interchanged Source Position

Moving the current source to nodes $c$–$d$ and measuring $V_{ab}$:

$$
V_{ab} \;=\; \frac{Y_{12}}{Y_{11} \cdot Y_{22} - Y_{12}^{\,2}} \cdot I_{s(2)}
$$

If $I_{s(1)} = I_{s(2)}$, then:

$$
V_{cd} \;=\; V_{ab} \quad \blacksquare
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Illustration — Worked Example (Form I)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 3-Mark Format Problem**

**Problem:** A linear bilateral passive network has a voltage source of **$V_s = 10 \text{ V}$** in branch $ab$. A short circuit in branch $cd$ carries a current of **$I_{cd} = 2 \text{ mA}$**. Using the Reciprocity Theorem, find the current that will flow in branch $ab$ when the **same source** is transferred to branch $cd$.

**Solution Using Reciprocity:**

By the Reciprocity Theorem (Form I), interchanging the source and the response branch yields the **same magnitude** of current:

$$
I_{ab} \;=\; I_{cd} \;=\; 2 \text{ mA}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> Students often overcomplicate this problem by trying to solve the network from scratch. Recognize that if the network is *given* as linear, bilateral, and passive, reciprocity directly applies. **Save time for the harder sub-parts.**

### 3.4 Numerical Illustration — Complex Mesh Example

**Problem:** For the network shown (2 meshes, 1 source), verify the Reciprocity Theorem by computing the current through the 2 $\Omega$ resistor (response branch) for both source positions.

Assume: $V_s = 12 \text{ V}$, $R_1 = 4 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 2 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 6 \, \Omega$, with $R_2$ being the shared branch (mutual impedance).

**Configuration A:** Source in left mesh; measure current through $R_2$ in right mesh.

Mesh equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
12 \;&=\; (R_1 + R_2) I_1 - R_2 I_2 = 6 I_1 - 2 I_2 \\[6pt]
0 \;&=\; -R_2 I_1 + (R_2 + R_3) I_2 = -2 I_1 + 8 I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

From the second equation:

$$
I_1 \;=\; 4 I_2
$$

Substituting into the first:

$$
\begin{aligned}
12 \;&=\; 6(4 I_2) - 2 I_2 \;=\; 24 I_2 - 2 I_2 \;=\; 22 I_2 \\[6pt]
I_2 \;&=\; \frac{12}{22} \;=\; 0.5454 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Configuration B:** Source in right mesh; measure current through $R_2$ in left mesh.

By symmetry of the mesh impedance matrix ($Z_{12} = Z_{21} = R_2 = 2 \, \Omega$), the response is identical:

$$
I_1' \;=\; 0.5454 \text{ A} \quad \text{(verified)}
$$

### 3.5 Special Case — Reciprocity Failure with Dependent Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Proof of Non-Reciprocity in Active Networks:**

Consider a simple VCVS (Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source) amplifier with gain $A$. The network is **NOT reciprocal** because the controlled source is an active, directional element. Placing the source at the input produces an amplified output, but placing it at the output does not produce an amplified input — the asymmetry is intrinsic to the active element.

This is why **operational amplifiers, transistors in common-emitter mode, and gyrators** violate reciprocity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reciprocity Process Flow

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the **logical workflow** for applying the Reciprocity Theorem to a KTU-style problem. It maps the decision flow from problem identification to final solution.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Linear Bilateral Passive Network] --> B{Is the network passive and single-source?}
    B -- No --> C[STOP: Theorem does NOT apply. Use Mesh or Nodal analysis directly.]
    B -- Yes --> D[Step 1: Identify Source Branch ab]
    D --> E[Step 2: Identify Response Branch cd]
    E --> F[Step 3: Apply Mesh or Nodal Analysis to Original Network]
    F --> G[Step 4: Compute Original Response Icd or Vcd]
    G --> H[Step 5: Interchange Source and Response Branches]
    H --> I[Step 6: Apply Mesh or Nodal Analysis to Interchanged Network]
    I --> J[Step 7: Compute New Response Iab or Vab]
    J --> K[Step 8: Compare Original and Interchanged Responses]
    K --> L{Are the magnitudes equal?}
    L -- Yes --> M[SUCCESS: Reciprocity Theorem Verified]
    L -- No --> N[CHECK: Re-examine assumptions, network symmetry, or reference directions]
    N --> O[Re-verify Z matrix is symmetric]
    O --> F
    M --> P[End: Final Answer Reported]
    C --> P
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Diagram — Reciprocity in a Two-Port Network

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OriginalConfig["ORIGINAL CONFIGURATION"]
        direction LR
        Vs1[Voltage Source Vs] -->|Terminal a| NodeA1((Node a))
        NodeA1 --> Z11[Passive Network N]
        Z11 --> NodeB1((Node b))
        NodeB1 -->|Back to Source| Vs1
        NodeA1 -.->|Short Circuit| NodeC1((Node c))
        NodeC1 -->|Measured Current Icd| NodeD1((Node d))
        NodeD1 -.->|Back| NodeA1
    end

    subgraph InterchangedConfig["INTERCHANGED CONFIGURATION"]
        direction LR
        Vs2[Voltage Source Vs] -->|Terminal c| NodeC2((Node c))
        NodeC2 --> Z22[Same Passive Network N]
        Z22 --> NodeD2((Node d))
        NodeD2 -->|Back to Source| Vs2
        NodeC2 -.->|Short Circuit| NodeA2((Node a))
        NodeA2 -->|Measured Current Iab| NodeB2((Node b))
        NodeB2 -.->|Back| NodeC2
    end
```

### 4.3 Decision Matrix — When to Apply Reciprocity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New KTU Problem on Reciprocity] --> Q1{Network contains dependent source?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> NA1[Reciprocity DOES NOT apply]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Network contains diodes or transistors?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> NA2[Reciprocity DOES NOT apply]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Number of independent sources?}
    Q3 -- More than 1 --> NA3[Apply Superposition first, then Reciprocity to each]
    Q3 -- Exactly 1 --> Q4{All elements linear and bilateral?}
    Q4 -- No --> NA4[Reciprocity DOES NOT apply]
    Q4 -- Yes --> APPLY[Reciprocity APPLIES: Swap source and response, response magnitude is invariant]
```

### 4.4 Visualization of Transfer Impedance Symmetry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Z_Matrix["Mesh Impedance Matrix Z = Z transpose Property"]
        direction TB
        Z11[Z11: Self-impedance of Mesh 1] --- Z12[Z12: Mutual impedance 1 to 2]
        Z12 --- Z21[Z21: Mutual impedance 2 to 1]
        Z21 --- Z22[Z22: Self-impedance of Mesh 2]
        SYM[Z12 = Z21 is the SYMMETRY that GUARANTEES Reciprocity]
    end
    Z_Matrix --> RESULT[Response Current I2 in Mesh 2 when source is in Mesh 1]
    Z_Matrix --> RESULT2[Response Current I1 in Mesh 1 when source is in Mesh 2]
    RESULT --- EQUAL[Equal in magnitude]
    RESULT2 --- EQUAL
```

### 4.5 Application Map — Where Reciprocity Shows Up in Engineering

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Reciprocity Theorem Applications))
    Antennas
      Transmit Receive Pattern Equality
      Radar Cross Section
    Two Port Networks
      Z Parameter Symmetry
      Y Parameter Symmetry
      ABCD Parameter Relations
    Circuit Analysis
      Mesh Analysis Simplification
      Bridge Balance Detection
    PCB Design
      Crosstalk Analysis
      Trace Coupling
    Filter Design
      LC Ladder Networks
      Lossless Matching
    Power Systems
      Network Equivalents
      Fault Analysis
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*State the Reciprocity Theorem. Mention the conditions under which it is applicable.*

**Model Answer:**

> The Reciprocity Theorem states that in any linear, bilateral, passive network containing a single independent source, the ratio of excitation to response remains unchanged when the source and the point of measurement are interchanged.
>
> **Conditions of Applicability:**
> 1. The network must be **linear** (obeys Ohm's law and superposition).
> 2. The network must be **bilateral** ($Z_{ij} = Z_{ji}$ for all $i, j$).
> 3. The network must be **passive** (no internal energy sources, no dependent sources).
> 4. Only **one independent source** should be present at a time.
> 5. The network must be **time-invariant**.
>
> **Significance:** It allows simplification of complex network analysis by interchanging source and response positions, often avoiding repeated mesh/nodal computations.

**Mark Allocation Tip (KTU Pattern):**
- Statement: **1 mark**
- 4–5 conditions: **2 marks** (0.4–0.5 each)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Differentiate between the three forms of the Reciprocity Theorem. Give one practical example where the theorem fails.**

**Model Answer:**

| Form | Excitation | Response | Key Equation |
|------|-----------|----------|--------------|
| **Form I** | Voltage source $V_s$ | Current $I$ in another branch | $V_s / I_{cd} = V_s / I_{ab}$ |
| **Form II** | Current source $I_s$ | Open-circuit voltage $V$ | $I_s / V_{cd} = I_s / V_{ab}$ |
| **Form III** | Voltage source $V_s$ with $R_0$ | Open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ | $V_{oc1} = V_{oc2}$ under matching |

> **Example where Reciprocity Fails:**
> An **operational amplifier (op-amp) circuit** in inverting or non-inverting configuration **fails reciprocity** because it contains a **Voltage-Controlled Voltage Source (VCVS)** — a dependent source that breaks the bilateral symmetry ($Z_{12} \neq Z_{21}$). Similarly, a network containing a **gyrator**, **diode**, or **transistor** in active mode is non-reciprocal.

**Mark Allocation Tip:**
- Three forms with descriptions: **2 marks**
- Failure example with explanation: **1 mark**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) = 7 marks** and **(b) = 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE of the two alternatives** (Q1 OR Q2).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** For the network shown in Figure 1, a **$V_s = 20 \text{ V}$** source is connected in branch $ab$ (left mesh), and a short circuit is placed in branch $cd$ (right mesh). The network has $R_1 = 5 \, \Omega$ in the left loop, $R_2 = 10 \, \Omega$ in the right loop, and a shared branch resistor $R_3 = 4 \, \Omega$.
*(i) Compute the current $I_{cd}$ in the short circuit.* [4 marks]
*(ii) Verify the Reciprocity Theorem by interchanging the source and the short circuit.* [3 marks]

**(b)** With the **same** network as in (a), but now using a **current source $I_s = 2 \text{ A}$** in branch $ab$, compute the open-circuit voltage $V_{cd}$ across branch $cd$. Then verify reciprocity by transferring the current source to branch $cd$ and measuring $V_{ab}$. [7 marks]

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Part (a)(i):** Original Configuration (Source in Branch $ab$, Response $I_{cd}$)

Applying KVL to both meshes using mesh currents $I_1$ (left, containing $V_s$) and $I_2$ (right, containing the short):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Left Mesh:} \quad & V_s \;=\; (R_1 + R_3) I_1 - R_3 I_2 \\[6pt]
\text{Right Mesh:} \quad & 0 \;=\; -R_3 I_1 + (R_2 + R_3) I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \;&=\; (5 + 4) I_1 - 4 I_2 \;=\; 9 I_1 - 4 I_2 \\[6pt]
0 \;&=\; -4 I_1 + (10 + 4) I_2 \;=\; -4 I_1 + 14 I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

From the second equation:

$$
I_1 \;=\; \frac{14}{4} I_2 \;=\; 3.5 \, I_2
$$

Substituting into the first equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \;&=\; 9(3.5 I_2) - 4 I_2 \;=\; 31.5 I_2 - 4 I_2 \;=\; 27.5 I_2 \\[6pt]
I_2 \;&=\; \frac{20}{27.5} \;=\; 0.7273 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

The short-circuit current in the original configuration is:

$$
\boxed{I_{cd} \;=\; I_2 \;=\; 0.7273 \text{ A}}
$$

**Part (a)(ii):** Interchanged Configuration (Source in Branch $cd$, Response $I_{ab}$)

Now swap the source and the short. New mesh equations:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Left Mesh:} \quad & 0 \;=\; 9 I_1' - 4 I_2' \\[6pt]
\text{Right Mesh:} \quad & 20 \;=\; -4 I_1' + 14 I_2'
\end{aligned}
$$

From the first equation: $I_1' = (4/9) I_2'$

Substituting into the second:

$$
\begin{aligned}
20 \;&=\; -4 \cdot \frac{4}{9} I_2' + 14 I_2' \;=\; -\frac{16}{9} I_2' + 14 I_2' \\[6pt]
20 \;&=\; \left(14 - \frac{16}{9}\right) I_2' \;=\; \frac{126 - 16}{9} I_2' \;=\; \frac{110}{9} I_2' \\[6pt]
I_2' \;&=\; \frac{20 \cdot 9}{110} \;=\; \frac{180}{110} \;=\; 1.6364 \text{ A}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I_1' \;=\; \frac{4}{9} \times 1.6364 \;=\; 0.7273 \text{ A}
$$

The current in the short circuit (now in branch $ab$) is:

$$
\boxed{I_{ab} \;=\; I_1' \;=\; 0.7273 \text{ A}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** $I_{cd} = I_{ab} = 0.7273 \text{ A}$. The Reciprocity Theorem is **verified**. $\checkmark$

**Part (b):** Reciprocity with Current Source (Form II)

Replace the voltage source in branch $ab$ with a **current source $I_s = 2 \text{ A}$** (injected at node $a$, extracted at node $b$). Branch $cd$ is **open-circuited** and we measure $V_{cd}$.

The open-circuit voltage $V_{cd}$ can be found using the transfer resistance. From the previous voltage-source problem, we know:

$$
I_{cd} \;=\; \frac{V_s}{R_{tr, ab \to cd}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{tr} \;=\; \frac{V_s}{I_{cd}} \;=\; \frac{20}{0.7273} \;=\; 27.5 \, \Omega
$$

(Note: $R_{tr}$ is the transfer resistance, i.e., $V_{ab}/I_{cd}$ — and equals the denominator in our earlier formula, $R_1 R_2 + R_1 R_3 + R_2 R_3 - R_3^2$ over $R_3$, but here we use the computed numerical value.)

By the **linear relationship** between excitation and response in a linear network:

$$
V_{cd} \;=\; I_s \cdot R_{tr} \;=\; 2 \text{ A} \times 27.5 \, \Omega \;=\; 55 \text{ V}
$$

**Interchanged Configuration:** Place the $2 \text{ A}$ current source in branch $cd$ and measure $V_{ab}$ (with branch $ab$ open).

By the **Reciprocity Theorem (Form II)**:

$$
V_{ab} \;=\; V_{cd} \;=\; 55 \text{ V}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification confirmed.** The two-port transfer resistance is symmetric: $R_{ab,cd} = R_{cd,ab} = 27.5 \, \Omega$. This is the Form II manifestation of reciprocity. $\checkmark$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Define the Reciprocity Theorem. For the network in Figure 2 (a Wheatstone bridge-like configuration with $R_1 = 10 \, \Omega$, $R_2 = 20 \, \Omega$, $R_3 = 30 \, \Omega$, $R_4 = 60 \, \Omega$, and a galvanometer arm $R_g = 50 \, \Omega$ connecting the midpoints), apply a **$V_s = 12 \text{ V}$** source across one diagonal (nodes $a$–$b$) and determine the **galvanometer current $I_g$** through the mid-branch. Then, by reciprocity, find the current in the original source branch when the source is moved to the galvanometer branch (with the original branch shorted). [7 marks]

**(b)** Discuss with a neat functional block diagram why a **non-reciprocal device** like an **op-amp (VCVS)** violates the Reciprocity Theorem. State **two engineering applications** where the Reciprocity Theorem is *directly used* in practice. [7 marks]

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Part (a):** Wheatstone Bridge with Source in Diagonal $ab$

In the balanced bridge configuration, $R_1/R_2 = R_3/R_4 = 1/2$. Since the bridge is **balanced**, the galvanometer current is **zero**.

By the Reciprocity Theorem, if we transfer the source to the galvanometer branch (with the original diagonal $ab$ shorted), the current through the short in branch $ab$ will **also be zero**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Conceptual Pearl:** A zero response in one configuration guarantees zero response in the interchanged configuration. This is the principle underlying all **null-detection bridge measurements**.

**Detailed Mesh Analysis Verification:**

Set up four mesh currents $I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4$ corresponding to the four loops. With $V_s$ in the $ab$ diagonal:

The mesh equations for a balanced bridge:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mesh 1:} \quad & V_s = (R_1 + R_3 + R_g) I_1 - R_3 I_3 - R_g I_4 \\[6pt]
\text{Mesh 2:} \quad & 0 = -R_3 I_1 + (R_2 + R_3 + R_g) I_2 - R_g I_4 \\[6pt]
\text{Mesh 3:} \quad & 0 = \cdots \\[6pt]
\text{Mesh 4:} \quad & 0 = \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

By the **balance condition** $R_1 R_4 = R_2 R_3$ (here $10 \times 60 = 600 = 20 \times 30$), the galvanometer branch carries **no current**. By reciprocity, $I_{ab} = 0$ when the source is in the galvanometer arm.

**Part (b):** Non-Reciprocity of Op-Amp and Practical Applications

**Op-Amp Non-Reciprocity — Functional Block Diagram:**

```
    [Input Signal vin] --> [Differential Amplifier Stage] --> [Gain Stage A] --> [Output vout]
                                                                 |
                                                                 v
                                                    [Feedback Network β]
                                                                 |
                                                                 +------> Back to input
```

The op-amp has an **internal VCVS with gain $A$** that amplifies only in the **forward direction** (input to output). The feedback path is a *separate* physical route, not the same network. Mathematically:

$$
Z_{12} \;=\; \frac{\partial V_2}{\partial I_1}\bigg|_{I_2 = 0} \;\neq\; Z_{21} \;=\; \frac{\partial V_1}{\partial I_2}\bigg|_{I_1 = 0}
$$

The asymmetry of the active VCVS means the **transfer matrix is not symmetric**, breaking reciprocity.

> [!WARNING]
> **Two Engineering Applications of Reciprocity:**
> 1. **Antenna Reciprocity:** A single antenna used for transmission has **identical radiation pattern** when used for reception. This is why the same antenna serves both TX and RX modes in a radar or radio system.
> 2. **Two-Port Parameter Simplification:** The Reciprocity Theorem forces the **$Z$-parameter matrix** of any reciprocal two-port to be **symmetric** ($Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ properties hold under specific loading, and $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ always). This reduces the number of independent parameters from four to three, simplifying filter and matching network design.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Reciprocity Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the source value change** — If the original source is $V_s$ and the new source is $V_s'$, students often forget to scale. Always state: *"For the same source value, the response is identical."*
> 2. **Sign confusion** — The theorem guarantees **equal magnitudes**, not necessarily equal signs. Reference directions matter. State your assumption clearly.
> 3. **Applying reciprocity to active networks** — Op-amps, transistors (CE/CB configurations), and gyrators are **non-reciprocal**. KTU examiners frequently set traps here. Always check: *"Are there any dependent sources?"*
> 4. **Ignoring the implicit conditions** — Don't just state the theorem; explicitly list linearity, bilateralism, passivity, and single-source conditions. Examiners award marks for the *complete statement*.
> 5. **Mesh equation sign errors** — When $Z_{12}$ and $Z_{21}$ terms have opposite signs in the two mesh equations (due to mesh current direction conventions), students often drop the negative sign. This leads to **wrong answer but right method** — a partial credit trap.
> 6. **Confusing Form I with Form II** — Voltage source ↔ Current response is Form I. Current source ↔ Voltage response is Form II. Don't mix them up in 3-mark questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Sheet for Reciprocity Theorem — KTU 2024**

- **Core Statement:** *"In a linear, bilateral, passive, single-source network, the ratio of excitation to response is invariant under interchange of source and response."*
- **Three Mandatory Conditions:** Linear, Bilateral, Passive.
- **Single-Source Rule:** Only one independent source at a time.
- **Three Forms:**
  - **Form I:** $V_s \to I$ (voltage source → current response)
  - **Form II:** $I_s \to V$ (current source → voltage response)
  - **Form III:** $V_s$ with $R_0 \to V_{oc}$ (matched case)
- **Mathematical Foundation:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (mesh analysis) and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (nodal analysis).
- **Magnitude Equality, Not Sign:** Reciprocity guarantees **equal magnitudes**; the sign depends on reference direction.
- **Zero-Response Invariant:** If the original response is zero, the interchanged response is **also zero** (bridge principle).
- **Non-Reciprocal Devices:** Op-amps, transistors, diodes, gyrators, VCVS, CCCS, VCCS, CCVS.
- **Reciprocal Devices:** Resistors, capacitors, inductors, transformers (ideal), LC ladder networks, antennas.
- **Antenna Reciprocity:** TX pattern = RX pattern (theoretical cornerstone of antenna engineering).
- **Two-Port Link:** Reciprocity $\equiv$ symmetric $Z$-matrix $\equiv$ symmetric $Y$-matrix $\equiv$ symmetric $h$-matrix (under specific conditions).
- **Step-by-Step Application Recipe:**
  1. Verify pre-conditions.
  2. Identify source and response branches.
  3. Compute original response via mesh/nodal.
  4. Swap and recompute.
  5. Compare magnitudes.
- **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Using the theorem with dependent sources.
- **Engineering Use Cases:** Antenna design, PCB crosstalk, two-port parameter reduction, bridge null detection, LC filter design.
- **Unit Reminders:** $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amperes, $Z$ in Ohms, $Y$ in Siemens.
- **Famous Application:** **Wheatstone Bridge** balance detection is a direct corollary of reciprocity (zero response remains zero upon interchange).
- **KTU-Specific:** In ESE (End Semester Exam), always draw the **circuit diagram in both configurations** to secure full marks on Part B questions.
- **Mnemonic for Conditions:** **"L-B-P-S-T"** — **L**inear, **B**ilateral, **P**assive, **S**ingle source, **T**ime-invariant.
- **Mnemonic for Three Forms:** **"V-I, I-V, V-V"** — Voltage-Current, Current-Voltage, Voltage-Voltage.

---

> **END OF NOTES — RECIPROCITY THEOREM (PCECT205, MODULE 2)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thevenin's Theorem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thevenin's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thevenin's Theorem (KTU 2024 Module 2 Definition):**
> *Any linear bilateral network containing active and passive elements, when viewed from any two specified output terminals (a-b), can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single voltage source **V_Th** (Thevenin Voltage / Open-Circuit Voltage) in series with a single resistance **R_Th** (Thevenin Resistance / Equivalent Resistance).*

The theorem was first stated by French telegraph engineer **Léon Charles Thévenin** in **1883** (published in *Comptes Rendus Hebdomadaires des Séances de l'Académie des Sciences*), and is one of the most heavily tested theorems in the KTU Network Theory (PCECT205) End Semester Examination.

### Key Terminology Mapping (KTU Notation Standard)

| KTU Symbol | Technical Name | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **V_Th** | Thevenin Equivalent Voltage | **Volt (V)** |
| **R_Th** | Thevenin Equivalent Resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** |
| **V_oc** | Open-Circuit Voltage across a-b | **Volt (V)** |
| **I_sc** | Short-Circuit Current across a-b | **Ampere (A)** |
| **Z_L** or **R_L** | Load Impedance / Resistance | **Ohm (Ω)** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank & Pipe" Intuition

Imagine a complex network of pipes, water tanks, pumps, and valves as your **linear bilateral network**. The load (a resistor) is like a **turbine** at the output terminals. Calculating the exact water flow through the turbine from the entire pipe network is messy.

**Thevenin's idea (1883):** Forget the entire pipe network! You can replace *everything* with a single **equivalent pump (V_Th)** connected to a single **equivalent pipe of one resistance (R_Th)**. The turbine (load) will see the exact same flow, pressure, and power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in engineering:** Thevenin's Theorem lets engineers design complex circuits (like amplifier stages, sensor interfaces, and power distribution networks) by simplifying only the *source side* while keeping the load untouched — crucial for **maximum power transfer analysis**, **load line analysis**, and **op-amp circuit design** in later semesters.

---

## 1.3 Conditions & Limitations (Syllabus-Mandated)

> [!WARNING]
> **Strict Validity Conditions (Common Exam Trap):**
> 1. The network **must be linear** (obeys Ohm's Law: $V = IR$).
> 2. The network **must be bilateral** (same current for same voltage regardless of polarity — resistors, inductors, capacitors qualify; diodes do not).
> 3. Active elements must remain **independent** of the load terminals being analyzed.
> 4. The theorem applies to **AC networks** as well, where R_Th is replaced by **Z_Th** (complex impedance).

---

## 1.4 Visual Schematic — Thevenin Equivalent Concept

```
   LINEAR BILATERAL NETWORK          THEVENIN EQUIVALENT
  ┌─────────────────────────┐      ┌──────────────────────┐
  │   ┌──[R1]──┐            │      │                      │
  │  [V1]    [R2]  ┌─ a ────┼──────┼─ (+) V_Th ──[R_Th]─┐├─ a
  │   │      │    │        │      │                      │
  │   └──────┴────┘  b ─────┼──────┼──────────────────────┘├─ b
  │                         │      │                      │
  │   [R3]   [V2]           │      │                      │
  └─────────────────────────┘      └──────────────────────┘
       (COMPLEX ORIGINAL)             (SIMPLIFIED)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Theorem Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 ESE valuation scheme explicitly awards marks for **systematic procedure**. Memorize this 4-step protocol:

### **STEP 1: Identify and Mark the Load Terminals (a-b)**
- Remove the load resistor **R_L** (the one across which we need to find the behavior).
- Clearly mark the two terminals as **'a'** (positive reference) and **'b'** (reference/ground).

### **STEP 2: Calculate the Open-Circuit Voltage V_oc = V_Th**
- With R_L removed, no current flows out of the terminals a-b (since the circuit is open).
- Apply any standard analysis method:
  - **Mesh Analysis** (preferred for planar circuits)
  - **Nodal Analysis** (preferred when many parallel branches exist)
  - **Source Transformation** (elegant for series-parallel reductions)
  - **Superposition Theorem** (valid alternative — covered in next module)
- The voltage measured between a and b is **V_Th**.

### **STEP 3: Calculate the Thevenin Resistance R_Th**
Three KTU-approved methods exist (memorize all three — examiner awards method choice marks):

| Method | Procedure | When to Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **R_Th Method A** | Short-circuit all **independent voltage sources** (V = 0) and open-circuit all **independent current sources** (I = 0). Compute equivalent resistance looking into a-b. | **Preferred** for circuits with only independent sources. |
| **R_Th Method B** | Apply a **test voltage V_test** at a-b, measure I_test, then R_Th = V_test / I_test. | Used when circuit has **dependent sources**. |
| **R_Th Method C** | Compute **V_oc** (Step 2) and **I_sc** (short-circuit current from a to b). Then R_Th = V_oc / I_sc. | Quick cross-check method. |

### **STEP 4: Draw the Thevenin Equivalent Circuit**
- Redraw: A single voltage source **V_Th** in series with **R_Th**, with the load **R_L** reconnected across a-b.
- Any load current/voltage can now be computed using simple **Ohm's Law**:
$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} \quad \text{and} \quad V_L = I_L \cdot R_L$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they cover ~90% of KTU 2024 Thevenin problems:**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_{Th} = V_{oc}\big\vert_{R_L \to \infty}$ | Thevenin Voltage = Open-circuit voltage | **V** |
| 2 | $R_{Th} = R_{ab}\big\vert_{\text{sources killed}}$ | Thevenin Resistance (independent sources only) | **Ω** |
| 3 | $R_{Th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$ | R_Th via short-circuit test | **Ω** |
| 4 | $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | Load current in Thevenin equivalent | **A** |
| 5 | $V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = V_{Th} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L}$ | Load voltage (voltage divider rule) | **V** |
| 6 | $P_{L,\max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4R_{Th}}$ at $R_L = R_{Th}$ | **Maximum Power Transfer** (KTU favorite!) | **W** |
| 7 | $\eta_{\max} = 50\%$ | Efficiency at maximum power transfer condition | **%** |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Design:** Finding bias point and small-signal output impedance of BJT/JFET amplifier stages.
- **Power Systems:** Simplifying vast interconnected grids to compute fault currents at a single bus.
- **Battery Modelling:** Real batteries are modelled as ideal EMF + internal resistance (R_Th) — this *is* a Thevenin equivalent.
- **Sensor Interfacing:** Thevenin equivalent is the standard way to interface sensors with high-impedance ADCs.
- **Communication Networks:** Antenna impedance matching relies on R_Th calculations.

---

## 2.4 Relation to Norton's Theorem (Dual Theorem — KTU Cross-Question)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently pairs Thevenin with Norton in Part A. They are **dual**:
> - $V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N$ (Norton current × Norton resistance = Thevenin voltage)
> - $R_{Th} = R_N$ (Resistances are identical)
> - Source transformation: any **V–R series** pair $\equiv$ **I–R parallel** pair where $I = V/R$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Full Thevenin Derivation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU-Style Problem (Model Question Pattern):**
> *In the circuit shown, find the Thevenin equivalent across terminals a-b.*
> - V₁ = 12 V (left source, +ve terminal up)
> - V₂ = 6 V (middle source, +ve terminal up)
> - R₁ = 2 Ω (in series with V₁)
> - R₂ = 4 Ω (in series with V₂)
> - R₃ = 4 Ω (between node A and terminal a)
> - R_L is removed; find V_Th and R_Th.

```
         R₁=2Ω         R₂=4Ω
    ┌──[====]──┬──[====]──┐
    │          │          │
  ┌─┴─┐      ┌─┴─┐      ┌─┴─┐
  │+  │      │+  │      │   │
  │V₁ │      │V₂ │      R₃=4Ω
  │12V│      │6V │      │   │
  │-  │      │-  │      │   │
  └─┬─┘      └─┬─┘      └─┬─┘
    │          │          │
    │          │          a (terminal)
    │          │          │
    └──────────┴──────────┘
                  b (terminal, grounded)
```

---

### **PART A: Derivation of V_Th (Open-Circuit Voltage)**

Since R_L is removed, the terminals a-b are **open**. Therefore, **no current flows through R₃** (current has nowhere to go from terminal a).

**Key Realization:**
$$I_{R_3} = 0 \text{ A} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{R_3} = I_{R_3} \cdot R_3 = 0 \text{ V}$$

So the voltage at terminal a equals the voltage at node A (call it $V_A$).

By inspection: $V_{Th} = V_{ab} = V_A - V_b = V_A - 0 = V_A$

We now find $V_A$ using **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at node A.

The two source branches feed current *into* node A. Currents leaving node A through R₁ and R₂:

$$I_1 = \frac{V_A - V_1}{R_1} = \frac{V_A - 12}{2}$$

$$I_2 = \frac{V_A - V_2}{R_2} = \frac{V_A - 6}{4}$$

Setting sum of currents leaving node A to zero (KCL):

$$I_1 + I_2 = 0$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A - 6}{4} = 0$$

Multiplying through by the LCM (= 4):

$$2(V_A - 12) + (V_A - 6) = 0$$

$$2V_A - 24 + V_A - 6 = 0$$

$$3V_A - 30 = 0$$

$$3V_A = 30$$

$$\boxed{V_A = 10 \text{ V} = V_{Th}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Marks are awarded here for the *statement* "Since terminals a-b are open, no current flows through R₃" — never skip this logical justification.

---

### **PART B: Derivation of R_Th (Equivalent Resistance)**

**Method A (KTU Recommended):** Deactivate all independent sources.
- Replace V₁ (12 V) with a **short circuit** (since it's an ideal voltage source).
- Replace V₂ (6 V) with a **short circuit**.
- Look into terminals a-b and find the equivalent resistance.

```
   R₁=2Ω            R₂=4Ω
   [====]            [====]
   ┌──┴──┬──────┬────┴──┐
   │     │      │       │
   ─     ─      R₃=4Ω   a
   │     │      │       │
   └──┬──┴──────┴───────┘
      │                  
      b                  
```

**Step B.1:** With both sources shorted, R₁ and R₂ are now in **parallel** (both connect node A to the bottom rail).

$$R_{1\parallel 2} = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3} \; \Omega$$

**Step B.2:** R₃ is in **series** with this parallel combination (from terminal a → R₃ → node A → R₁∥R₂ → bottom rail → terminal b).

$$R_{Th} = R_3 + R_{1\parallel 2} = 4 + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{12 + 4}{3} = \frac{16}{3} \; \Omega$$

$$\boxed{R_{Th} = \frac{16}{3} \; \Omega \approx 5.33 \; \Omega}$$

---

### **PART C: Verification Using Method C (V_oc / I_sc)**

This method serves as a **cross-check** that earns partial credit even if Method A had a sign error.

**Compute I_sc** (current from a to b when a-b is short-circuited):
With a-b shorted, R₃ now carries current. Apply KCL at node A:

$$\frac{V_A - 12}{2} + \frac{V_A - 6}{4} + \frac{V_A - 0}{4} = 0$$

The third term represents the current through R₃ to the shorted terminal a (= 0 V).

Multiplying by 4:

$$2(V_A - 12) + (V_A - 6) + V_A = 0$$

$$2V_A - 24 + V_A - 6 + V_A = 0$$

$$4V_A = 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A = 7.5 \text{ V}$$

The short-circuit current flows through R₃:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{V_A - 0}{R_3} = \frac{7.5}{4} = 1.875 \text{ A}$$

Now verify R_Th:

$$R_{Th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{10}{1.875} = \frac{10}{1.875} = \frac{16}{3} \; \Omega \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Marking Insight:** Whenever a problem gives a numerical Thevenin equivalent, the cross-check via V_oc / I_sc demonstrates rigorous understanding and earns **bonus partial credit** if the main method has a minor arithmetic error.

---

### **PART D: Load Analysis (if R_L = 8 Ω is reconnected)**

Using the Thevenin equivalent:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{10}{\frac{16}{3} + 8} = \frac{10}{\frac{16 + 24}{3}} = \frac{10}{\frac{40}{3}} = \frac{10 \cdot 3}{40} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 \text{ A}$$

$$V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = 0.75 \times 8 = 6 \text{ V}$$

$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = (0.75)^2 \times 8 = 0.5625 \times 8 = 4.5 \text{ W}$$

### 3.2 Summary Result Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | SI Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Thevenin Voltage | $V_{Th}$ | $10$ | **V** |
| Thevenin Resistance | $R_{Th}$ | $\dfrac{16}{3} \approx 5.33$ | **Ω** |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | $1.875$ | **A** |
| Load Current (R_L = 8Ω) | $I_L$ | $0.75$ | **A** |
| Load Voltage (R_L = 8Ω) | $V_L$ | $6$ | **V** |
| Load Power (R_L = 8Ω) | $P_L$ | $4.5$ | **W** |
| Max Power (R_L = R_Th) | $P_{L,\max}$ | $\dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4R_{Th}} = \dfrac{100 \cdot 3}{64} \approx 4.69$ | **W** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thevenin's Theorem — Algorithmic Flow (Block Architecture)

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **decision flow** a student must follow when solving any KTU Thevenin problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Given Linear Network] --> B[Step 1: Identify Load Terminals a-b]
    B --> C[Step 2: Remove Load R_L]
    C --> D[Step 3: Find Open-Circuit Voltage V_oc]
    D --> D1{Choose Method}
    D1 --> D2[Mesh Analysis]
    D1 --> D3[Nodal Analysis]
    D1 --> D4[Source Transformation]
    D2 --> E[Record V_Th = V_oc]
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Step 4: Find R_Th]
    F --> F1{Source Type?}
    F1 -->|Independent Only| F2[Method A: Kill Sources, Find R_ab]
    F1 -->|Dependent Sources| F3[Method B: Apply V_test, Find I_test]
    F1 -->|Cross-Check Needed| F4[Method C: Compute I_sc, R_Th = V_oc / I_sc]
    F2 --> G[Step 5: Draw Equivalent]
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Draw V_Th in series with R_Th, reconnect R_L]
    H --> I[Step 6: Compute Load Quantities]
    I --> I1[I_L = V_Th / R_Th + R_L]
    I --> I2[V_L = I_L times R_L]
    I --> I3[P_L = I_L squared times R_L]
    I1 --> J[END: Final Answer Verified]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
```

## 4.2 Thevenin Equivalent — Functional Block Representation

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ORIGINAL_NETWORK [Original Linear Network]
        N1[V1 = 12V] --- R1A[2 ohm]
        N2[V2 = 6V] --- R2A[4 ohm]
        R1A --- NODEA[Node A]
        R2A --- NODEA
        NODEA --- R3A[4 ohm]
        R3A --- TERMA[Terminal a]
    end

    subgraph THEVENIN_EQUIV [Thevenin Equivalent]
        VTH[V Th = 10V] --- RTH[R Th = 16/3 ohm]
        RTH --- TERMA2[Terminal a]
        VTH --- TERMB2[Terminal b]
    end

    TERMA2 --- RL[Load R L]
    RL --- TERMB2
```

## 4.3 Conceptual State Diagram — Source Killing Rules

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> ActiveState: Original Circuit
    ActiveState --> VoltageKilled: Short Voltage Sources V=0
    ActiveState --> CurrentKilled: Open Current Sources I=0
    VoltageKilled --> RThCalculation
    CurrentKilled --> RThCalculation
    RThCalculation --> RThResult: R Th = R ab
    RThResult --> [*]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. State Thevenin's Theorem. Mention any two conditions for its validity.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** *Any linear bilateral network containing independent and/or dependent sources, when viewed from any two specified output terminals a-b, can be replaced by an equivalent circuit comprising a single voltage source V_Th in series with a resistance R_Th, where V_Th is the open-circuit voltage across a-b and R_Th is the equivalent resistance looking into a-b with all independent sources deactivated.*

**Conditions:**
1. The network must consist of **linear** elements (resistors, inductors, capacitors that obey $V=IR$ relationship).
2. The network must be **bilateral** (current is identical for the same magnitude of voltage regardless of direction of application).
3. Active elements should be **independent** of the terminals under consideration.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### **Q2. How is the Thevenin resistance R_Th determined when the network contains (i) only independent sources, and (ii) dependent sources? Mention the formula used.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Network with only independent sources:**
Replace each ideal **voltage source with a short circuit** (V = 0) and each ideal **current source with an open circuit** (I = 0). Then compute the equivalent resistance looking into terminals a-b using series-parallel reduction or Y-Δ transformation. This resistance is R_Th.

**(ii) Network with dependent sources:**
Dependent sources **cannot be deactivated** (they are functions of circuit variables). Apply a **test voltage V_test** (typically 1 V) at terminals a-b, compute the resulting **test current I_test** flowing in, and calculate:

$$R_{Th} = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$$

Alternatively, compute $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ separately (with sources active) and use $R_{Th} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Module 2, Model Paper Style]**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Sub-parts (a) and (b)**

#### **Q.A(a)** Find the Thevenin equivalent circuit across terminals a-b for the network shown below. Compute the load current if R_L = 6 Ω is connected across a-b. `[7 Marks]`

**Given Network:**
- V₁ = 20 V source in series with R₁ = 5 Ω (left branch)
- V₂ = 10 V source in series with R₂ = 10 Ω (right branch)
- Both branches in parallel between node A and ground
- R₃ = 4 Ω between node A and terminal a
- Terminal b is grounded

---

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Find V_Th (Open-Circuit Voltage)**

With R_L removed, terminals a-b are open ⇒ no current through R₃ ⇒ no voltage drop across R₃.

Apply **KCL at Node A** (currents leaving node A):

$$\frac{V_A - 20}{5} + \frac{V_A - 10}{10} = 0$$

Multiply by LCM = 10:

$$2(V_A - 20) + (V_A - 10) = 0$$

$$2V_A - 40 + V_A - 10 = 0$$

$$3V_A = 50 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_A = \frac{50}{3} \text{ V}$$

Since no current flows through R₃:
$$V_{Th} = V_A = \frac{50}{3} \approx 16.67 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Find R_Th (Equivalent Resistance)**

Deactivate both voltage sources (short them). R₁ and R₂ are now in parallel:

$$R_{1\parallel 2} = \frac{5 \times 10}{5 + 10} = \frac{50}{15} = \frac{10}{3} \; \Omega$$

R₃ is in series:
$$R_{Th} = R_3 + R_{1\parallel 2} = 4 + \frac{10}{3} = \frac{22}{3} \approx 7.33 \; \Omega$$

**Step 3: Load Current Computation**

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{50/3}{22/3 + 6} = \frac{50/3}{(22 + 18)/3} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$V_{Th} = 16.67 \text{ V}, \quad R_{Th} = 7.33 \; \Omega, \quad I_L = 1.25 \text{ A}$$

---

#### **Q.A(b)** For the Thevenin equivalent derived in part (a), determine the value of R_L for **maximum power transfer** and compute the maximum power delivered. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Condition for Maximum Power Transfer (MPT):**

$$R_L = R_{Th} = \frac{22}{3} \; \Omega \approx 7.33 \; \Omega$$

**Maximum Power Formula:**

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$

Substituting values:

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{(50/3)^2}{4 \times (22/3)} = \frac{2500/9}{88/3} = \frac{2500}{9} \times \frac{3}{88} = \frac{7500}{792} = \frac{625}{66} \approx 9.47 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$R_L = 7.33 \; \Omega, \quad P_{L,\max} \approx 9.47 \text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in $P_{max} = V_{Th}^2 / 4R_{Th}$ — this is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Not stating the MPT condition explicitly** ($R_L = R_{Th}$) before substituting — deduct 0.5 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up R_Th and R_L** in the final load current equation — verify $R_{Th} + R_L$ is in the denominator of $I_L$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B(a)** Using **source transformation method**, find the Thevenin equivalent across terminals a-b for the circuit where a 12 V source is in series with a 3 Ω resistor, this combination in parallel with a 2 A current source, and the entire network connected to terminals a-b through a 4 Ω series resistor. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Source Transformation of Current Source**

The 2 A current source in parallel with the 3 Ω resistor (from the voltage source branch) can be transformed. First convert the 12 V series combination to a current source:

$$I_{eq} = \frac{V}{R} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ A}$$

The 12 V source in series with 3 Ω is equivalent to a **4 A source in parallel with 3 Ω**.

**Step 2: Combine Parallel Current Sources**

The 4 A source (transformed) and the original 2 A source are now in parallel. Both assumed in same direction:
$$I_{total} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ A}$$

The parallel resistance remains 3 Ω.

**Step 3: Source Transformation Back to Voltage Source**

Convert the 6 A ∥ 3 Ω back to a voltage source in series with 3 Ω:
$$V_{eq} = I_{total} \times R = 6 \times 3 = 18 \text{ V}$$

So the circuit reduces to: **18 V source in series with 3 Ω, then 4 Ω in series**, terminating at a-b.

**Step 4: Open-Circuit Voltage V_Th**

With a-b open, no current flows, hence no drop across resistors:
$$V_{Th} = 18 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Thevenin Resistance R_Th**

Kill the 18 V source (short it). Looking into a-b:
$$R_{Th} = 3 + 4 = 7 \; \Omega$$

**Final Answer:**
$$V_{Th} = 18 \text{ V}, \quad R_{Th} = 7 \; \Omega$$

---

#### **Q.B(b)** A practical voltage source has an EMF of 15 V and internal resistance of 1.5 Ω. It is connected to a variable load R_L. Using Thevenin's theorem, plot the **load line** and determine: (i) the condition for maximum power transfer, (ii) the efficiency at MPT condition. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

Here, the practical source **is** a Thevenin equivalent with $V_{Th} = 15$ V and $R_{Th} = 1.5 \; \Omega$.

**(i) Maximum Power Transfer Condition:**
$$R_L = R_{Th} = 1.5 \; \Omega$$

Maximum power:
$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(15)^2}{4 \times 1.5} = \frac{225}{6} = 37.5 \text{ W}$$

**(ii) Efficiency at MPT:**

Total power delivered by source:
$$P_{total} = V_{Th} \cdot I_L = 15 \times \frac{15}{1.5 + 1.5} = 15 \times 5 = 75 \text{ W}$$

Load power at MPT: $P_L = 37.5$ W

Efficiency:
$$\eta = \frac{P_L}{P_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{37.5}{75} \times 100\% = 50\%$$

**Verification using MPT efficiency formula:**
$$\eta_{max} = \frac{R_L}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{1.5}{1.5 + 1.5} = 0.5 = 50\% \; \checkmark$$

**Final Answer:**
$$R_L = 1.5 \; \Omega, \quad P_{L,\max} = 37.5 \text{ W}, \quad \eta = 50\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark Deductions for Q.B(b):**
> 1. **Conflating the practical source with an "unknown" circuit** — recognize immediately that a real-world battery/source with internal resistance *is* a Thevenin equivalent. Time lost identifying this costs ~1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong formula for efficiency** — some students use $\eta = V_L / V_{Th}$. Correct form is $\eta = P_L / P_{total} = (I_L^2 R_L) / (V_{Th} \cdot I_L) = I_L R_L / V_{Th}$.
> 3. **Not commenting that 50% is the theoretical maximum efficiency** for Thevenin-driven MPT — this conceptual remark earns 0.5 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute KTU Revision Sheet):**

- **Thevenin's Theorem** replaces a complex linear network with a single **V_Th–R_Th series** combination as seen from output terminals.
- **V_Th = V_oc** (open-circuit voltage across a-b, with R_L removed).
- **R_Th calculation rules:**
  - Independent sources only → **kill sources** (short V-sources, open I-sources) and find R_ab.
  - Dependent sources present → use **V-test / I-test** method or **V_oc / I_sc** method.
- **Load analysis** in Thevenin equivalent uses simple **Ohm's Law**:
  $I_L = V_{Th} / (R_{Th} + R_L)$, $V_L = I_L R_L$, $P_L = I_L^2 R_L$.
- **Maximum Power Transfer** condition: $R_L = R_{Th}$, giving $P_{L,\max} = V_{Th}^2 / 4R_{Th}$.
- **Efficiency at MPT** is always exactly **50%** for an ideal Thevenin source.
- **Thevenin ↔ Norton duality:** $V_{Th} = I_N R_N$, $R_{Th} = R_N$ — use this to verify answers.
- **Sign convention matters:** Mark terminal **'a'** as the assumed positive terminal; ensure V_Th is computed with correct polarity.
- **Independent vs Dependent sources:** Only **independent** sources are killed for R_Th calculation. Dependent sources remain active.
- **Limitation:** The theorem is valid only for **linear bilateral** networks — not applicable to circuits with diodes, transistors (as nonlinear elements), or saturable inductors.
- **Common exam trap:** When the load is reconnected, students forget to add $R_L$ to $R_{Th}$ in the denominator. Always write the **total resistance** explicitly.
- **Cross-verification strategy:** Use both Method A (kill sources) and Method C (V_oc / I_sc) to check R_Th — this is a hallmark of KTU toppers' answer sheets.
- **KTU 2024 Module 2 weightage:** Thevenin's Theorem carries **~14-21 marks** (1 full Part-B question) in the End Semester Examination.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Norton's Theorem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Norton's Theorem (IEC/IS Standard Definition)**
> *Norton's Theorem states that any linear bilateral active network containing independent and/or dependent sources, when viewed from two specified output terminals, can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a single current source $I_N$ in **parallel** with a single resistance $R_N$, where $I_N$ is the short-circuit current through the shorted terminals and $R_N$ is the equivalent resistance looking back into the network with all independent sources deactivated (voltage sources short-circuited and current sources open-circuited).*

The two key parameters of the Norton equivalent circuit are:
- **Norton Current ($I_N$)** — measured in **Amperes (A)**
- **Norton Resistance ($R_N$)** — measured in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Norton's Theorem is the *dual* of Thevenin's Theorem. Where Thevenin uses a **voltage source in series with resistance**, Norton uses a **current source in parallel with resistance**. Both describe the *exact same* external behavior of a linear network from the perspective of the load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Pipe Model"

Imagine a complex plumbing system (a network of pipes, pumps, and valves) connected to your kitchen tap (the load). You do not need to understand the entire plumbing layout to predict how the tap will behave. You only need to know two things:

1. **How much water pressure the system can push out when the tap is open-circuited (no water flowing out)** — analogous to $V_{Th}$.
2. **How much water current flows when the tap is replaced by a short pipe (water rushes out freely)** — analogous to $I_N$.

Norton's Theorem simplifies this plumbing system into a single equivalent model: **one ideal water pump (current source $I_N$) connected in parallel with a single pipe resistance ($R_N$)**, which is the simplest possible model that replicates the exact tap behavior.

In electrical terms: **Any complicated linear circuit, no matter how many loops or nodes it has, looks identical to a single current source with one parallel resistor when observed from two specific terminals.**

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Standard Symbol |
|---|---|---|---|
| Norton Current | $I_N$ | Ampere | A |
| Norton Resistance | $R_N$ | Ohm | $\Omega$ |
| Norton Conductance | $G_N = 1/R_N$ | Siemens | S |
| Load Current | $I_L$ | Ampere | A |
| Load Voltage | $V_L$ | Volt | V |
| Load Resistance | $R_L$ | Ohm | $\Omega$ |

## 1.4 Where Norton's Theorem Is Used in Real Engineering

- **Power Systems:** Analysing fault currents at a busbar without solving the full grid.
- **Electronics:** Simplifying bias networks in transistor amplifiers to find load-line behaviour.
- **Signal Processing:** Modelling transducer outputs (current-output sensors) feeding amplifier stages.
- **VLSI Design:** Reducing large passive networks to a Norton equivalent for pin-level analysis.
- **KTU 2024 Relevance:** Mandatory part of Module 2 (Network Theorems), forming the basis for subsequent theorems (Maximum Power Transfer, Superposition, Reciprocity).
