<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Decomposition

To convert *any* linear active network into its Norton equivalent, follow this rigorous KTU-validated procedure:

### Step 1: Identify the Two Terminals of Interest
- Label them **A** (positive/reference) and **B** (negative/return).
- These are the terminals across which a load $R_L$ would normally be connected.

### Step 2: Remove the Load (if present)
- Detach $R_L$ from terminals A–B. The remaining network is the "active linear two-terminal network."

### Step 3: Find $I_N$ — The Short-Circuit Current
- Place a wire (short circuit) across terminals A–B.
- Calculate the current flowing **through this short** using any standard technique:
  - Mesh Analysis
  - Nodal Analysis
  - Superposition Theorem
  - Source Transformation
- This current magnitude is $I_N$, with conventional direction taken as flowing from A to B through the short.

### Step 4: Find $R_N$ — The Equivalent Resistance
- Deactivate all **independent** sources:
  - Replace every **independent voltage source** with a **short circuit**.
  - Replace every **independent current source** with an **open circuit**.
- **Dependent sources are NEVER deactivated** — they remain in the circuit because they are controlled by circuit variables.
- Look back into the network from terminals A–B and calculate the equivalent resistance.
- For networks with **dependent sources only** (after deactivation), apply a **test source** ($V_{test} = 1\text{ V}$ or $I_{test} = 1\text{ A}$) and compute $R_N = V_{test}/I_{test}$.

### Step 5: Construct the Norton Equivalent
- Draw a current source $I_N$ pointing toward terminal A (or as determined by Step 3 polarity).
- Place $R_N$ in parallel with $I_N$.
- Reconnect the load $R_L$ across A–B.

### Step 6: Compute Load Behaviour
- Use the current-divider rule to find $I_L$.

## 2.2 The Dual Relationship: Norton ↔ Thevenin

> [!NOTE]
> The Norton and Thevenin equivalents are **mathematically interchangeable descriptions of the same port behaviour.**

$$
I_N = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} \quad ; \quad R_N = R_{Th} \quad ; \quad V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N
$$

The fundamental identity: **$\dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} = I_N$**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_N = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}}$ | Convert Thevenin to Norton current | Both equivalents must describe same port |
| 2 | $R_N = R_{Th}$ | Norton resistance equals Thevenin resistance | Always true |
| 3 | $I_L = I_N \cdot \dfrac{R_N}{R_N + R_L}$ | Current-divider rule for load current | Norton source with $R_L$ across A–B |
| 4 | $V_L = I_L \cdot R_L = I_N \cdot \dfrac{R_N \cdot R_L}{R_N + R_L}$ | Load voltage | Same circuit |
| 5 | $R_N = \dfrac{V_{OC}}{I_{SC}}$ | Alternative computation via open-circuit test | Useful when both easily measurable |
| 6 | $R_N = \dfrac{V_{test}}{I_{test}}$ | Test-source method | For circuits containing dependent sources only after deactivation |
| 7 | $P_{L,max} = \dfrac{I_N^2 \cdot R_N}{4}$ | Maximum power transferred to load | Occurs when $R_L = R_N$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule:** All currents must be in Amperes, all voltages in Volts, and all resistances in Ohms. Never mix units from a transformer or scaled sub-circuit unless explicitly converted.

## 2.4 Why Norton Over Thevenin? Engineering Utility

- **Current-source loads** (transistor base, op-amp virtual-ground inputs, photodiodes) are naturally modelled in Norton form.
- **Parallel-connected networks** (busbar systems, power distribution) reduce faster using Norton equivalents because parallel resistors combine simply: $R_{eq} = (R_1 R_2)/(R_1 + R_2)$.
- **Photovoltaic cell modelling** and **battery internal-resistance models** are inherently Norton-style.
- **Simulation software (SPICE, Multisim, LTspice):** Most behavioural sources are defined as Norton current sources, making this theorem directly aligned with EDA tool conventions.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Standard Resistive Network

### Problem Statement
For the circuit shown in the diagram (Section 4), find the Norton equivalent across terminals A–B, where the load $R_L = 6\ \Omega$ will be connected.

**Given Network Data:**
- $V_S = 12\ \text{V}$ (DC source)
- $R_1 = 4\ \Omega$ (in series with the source)
- $R_2 = 6\ \Omega$ (parallel branch)
- $R_3 = 3\ \Omega$ (in series with the A-B output branch)

**Topology:** $V_S$ — $R_1$ — Node 1 — $R_2$ — Ground, with $R_3$ from Node 1 to Terminal A. Terminal B is grounded.

### Step 1 — Compute Norton Current $I_N$ (Short-Circuit Current at A–B)

Place a short circuit between A and B. The short forces terminal A to be at the same potential as ground (0 V). Therefore, $R_3$ is effectively connected from Node 1 to ground in parallel with $R_2$.

The total resistance seen by the voltage source is:

$$
R_{total} = R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_3)
$$

Compute the parallel combination:

$$
R_2 \parallel R_3 = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_3}{R_2 + R_3} = \frac{6 \cdot 3}{6 + 3} = \frac{18}{9} = 2\ \Omega
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_{total} = 4 + 2 = 6\ \Omega
$$

The total current drawn from the source:

$$
I_{total} = \frac{V_S}{R_{total}} = \frac{12}{6} = 2\ \text{A}
$$

The voltage at Node 1 (relative to ground) is:

$$
V_1 = I_{total} \cdot (R_2 \parallel R_3) = 2 \cdot 2 = 4\ \text{V}
$$

The short-circuit current $I_N$ is the current through $R_3$ when A is shorted to ground:

$$
I_N = \frac{V_1}{R_3} = \frac{4}{3} \approx 1.333\ \text{A}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Norton Resistance $R_N$ (Deactivate Independent Sources)

Replace $V_S = 12\ \text{V}$ with a short circuit. Looking back from terminals A–B, we see $R_3$ in series with the parallel combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ (since $R_1$ now goes from Node 1 to ground through the shorted source).

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{R_1 \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{4 \cdot 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega
$$

Therefore:

$$
R_N = R_3 + (R_1 \parallel R_2) = 3 + 2.4 = 5.4\ \Omega
$$

### Step 3 — Verify via Thevenin Relationship

$$
V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N = 1.333 \cdot 5.4 = 7.2\ \text{V}
$$

Let us cross-check by computing $V_{Th}$ directly (open-circuit voltage at A):

With A–B open, no current flows through $R_3$, so $V_A = V_1$.

Voltage divider between $R_1$ and $R_2$:

$$
V_1 = V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = 12 \cdot \frac{6}{4 + 6} = 12 \cdot 0.6 = 7.2\ \text{V}
$$

Therefore $V_{Th} = 7.2\ \text{V}$ ✓ — both methods agree, confirming our Norton equivalent.

### Step 4 — Load Current Computation

For $R_L = 6\ \Omega$:

$$
I_L = I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L} = 1.333 \cdot \frac{5.4}{5.4 + 6} = 1.333 \cdot \frac{5.4}{11.4}
$$

$$
I_L = 1.333 \cdot 0.4737 \approx 0.6316\ \text{A}
$$

Verification by direct Thevenin calculation:

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{7.2}{5.4 + 6} = \frac{7.2}{11.4} \approx 0.6316\ \text{A}
$$

Both methods yield the same result. ✓

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Network with a Dependent Source

### Problem Statement
Find the Norton equivalent at terminals A–B for a network where:
- $V_S = 10\ \text{V}$ independent source in series with $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$ feeding Node 1.
- A **Current-Controlled Current Source (CCCS)** $0.5 \cdot I_x$ where $I_x$ is the current through $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$ from Node 1 to ground.
- $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$ from Node 1 to terminal A; terminal B is grounded.

### Step 1 — Find $I_N$ (Short-Circuit Current)

Short A to B. Current through $R_3$ becomes $I_{SC} = I_N$ (since the short forces $V_A = 0$, so all of $R_3$'s current is diverted through the short).

Apply KCL at Node 1 with the short in place:

$$
\frac{V_1 - 10}{2} + 0.5 \cdot I_x - \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1}{6} = 0
$$

Where $I_x = V_1 / 4$ (current through $R_2$ from Node 1 to ground).

Substituting:

$$
\frac{V_1 - 10}{2} + 0.5 \cdot \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1}{6} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_1}{2} - 5 + \frac{V_1}{8} - \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1}{6} = 0
$$

Find a common denominator (24):

$$
\frac{12 V_1}{24} - 5 + \frac{3 V_1}{24} - \frac{6 V_1}{24} - \frac{4 V_1}{24} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{(12 + 3 - 6 - 4) V_1}{24} = 5
$$

$$
\frac{5 V_1}{24} = 5
$$

$$
V_1 = 24\ \text{V}
$$

Short-circuit current:

$$
I_N = \frac{V_1}{R_3} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{A}
$$

### Step 2 — Find $R_N$ (Test-Source Method)

Deactivate the independent source ($V_S$ → short). Apply a test voltage $V_{test} = 1\ \text{V}$ at terminals A–B, and find the resulting $I_{test}$.

With $V_S$ shorted, the independent source branch becomes $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$ from Node 1 to ground. The dependent CCCS remains active.

Let $V_1$ be the voltage at Node 1. With $V_{test} = 1\ \text{V}$ at terminal A:

KCL at Node 1:

$$
\frac{V_1}{2} + 0.5 \cdot \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1}{4} - \frac{V_1 - 1}{6} = 0
$$

Multiply through by 24:

$$
12 V_1 + 3 V_1 - 6 V_1 - 4 (V_1 - 1) = 0
$$

$$
12 V_1 + 3 V_1 - 6 V_1 - 4 V_1 + 4 = 0
$$

$$
5 V_1 + 4 = 0
$$

$$
V_1 = -\frac{4}{5} = -0.8\ \text{V}
$$

Test current (entering terminal A from the network):

$$
I_{test} = \frac{V_1 - V_{test}}{R_3} = \frac{-0.8 - 1}{6} = \frac{-1.8}{6} = -0.3\ \text{A}
$$

Norton resistance:

$$
R_N = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{1}{-0.3} = -3.333\ \Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative $R_N$ Interpretation:** A negative Norton resistance indicates the network contains **dependent sources that are actively supplying power**. This is physically realizable in active circuits (e.g., amplifiers, oscillators). The negative sign is preserved in calculations; the Norton equivalent remains valid.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Methods for Finding $I_N$

| Method | When to Use | KTU Mark Allocation |
|---|---|---|
| Mesh Analysis | 2–3 loops, all resistors | Best for 4–6 mark sub-parts |
| Nodal Analysis | Multiple parallel branches | Excellent for dependent sources |
| Superposition | Multiple independent sources | Shows conceptual understanding |
| Source Transformation | Cascaded series-parallel structures | Fastest for simple ladder networks |
| Thevenin-then-Convert | Already solved Thevenin in part (a) | Reuse to save time in part (b) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Norton Equivalent Process

The following Mermaid flow diagram depicts the algorithmic procedure mandated by the KTU 2024 syllabus for reducing a linear active network to its Norton form.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: Identify load terminals A and B] --> stepB[Remove load R_L from terminals A and B]
    stepB --> stepC[Short circuit A to B with a wire]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute short circuit current I_N through the short]
    stepD --> stepE[Remove the short across A to B]
    stepE --> stepF[Deactivate all independent sources: V sources shorted, I sources opened]
    stepF --> stepG{Does the network contain dependent sources?}
    stepG -->|No| stepH[Compute equivalent resistance R_N by series parallel reduction]
    stepG -->|Yes| stepI[Apply test source V_test equals 1 V at A to B]
    stepI --> stepJ[Compute I_test and evaluate R_N equals V_test divided by I_test]
    stepH --> stepK[Construct Norton equivalent: I_N source in parallel with R_N]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL[Reconnect R_L across A to B]
    stepL --> stepM[Apply current divider rule: I_L equals I_N times R_N over R_N plus R_L]
    stepM --> stepN[End: Norton equivalent and load current computed]
```

## 4.2 Visual Schematic — Original Network and Its Norton Equivalent

The following Mermaid block renders a side-by-side functional mapping of the *original* linear network versus its *reduced* Norton equivalent, illustrating the terminal-equivalence principle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph orig["ORIGINAL NETWORK"]
        direction LR
        v1[DC Voltage Source V_S] --- r1[Resistor R1]
        r1 --- n1[Node 1]
        n1 --- r2[Resistor R2]
        r2 --- gnd1[Ground]
        n1 --- r3[Resistor R3]
        r3 --- termA1[Terminal A]
        gnd1 --- termB1[Terminal B]
    end

    subgraph norton["NORTON EQUIVALENT"]
        direction LR
        iN[Current Source I_N] --- termA2[Terminal A]
        rN[Resistor R_N] --- termA2
        iN --- termB2[Terminal B]
        rN --- termB2
    end

    orig -. "Same A-B Behaviour" .-> norton
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Both subgraphs present the *same* electrical behaviour at the external terminals A and B. The original network may contain dozens of components, but the Norton equivalent captures its entire port-level personality with just two elements: $I_N$ and $R_N$.

## 4.3 Conceptual Signal Flow — Current Divider in the Norton Form

The following block diagram illustrates the current-distribution mechanism when a load is connected to the Norton equivalent.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src[Current Source I_N] --> nodeJ[Junction Point]
    nodeJ --> branchR[R_N branch returns internally]
    nodeJ --> branchL[R_L branch carries I_L to the load]
    branchL --> load[Load R_L]
    load --> returnNode[Return to source negative terminal]
    branchR --> returnNode
```

**Interpretation:** The Norton current $I_N$ divides at the internal node — part flows through $R_N$ back to the source, and the remainder $I_L$ flows through the load. The ratio is governed by the current-divider formula:

$$
I_L = I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L}
$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**State Norton's Theorem. Mention the steps to find the Norton equivalent resistance of a circuit containing a dependent source.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 Marks):** Norton's Theorem states that any linear bilateral active network, when viewed from two specified output terminals, can be replaced by an equivalent circuit consisting of a current source $I_N$ in parallel with a resistance $R_N$, where $I_N$ is the short-circuit current between the terminals and $R_N$ is the equivalent resistance looking into the network with all independent sources deactivated.

> **Steps for dependent-source case (1 Mark):** When the deactivated network contains only dependent sources, apply a **test voltage source $V_{test} = 1\ \text{V}$** across the terminals. Compute the resulting current $I_{test}$ entering the network. Then $R_N = V_{test} / I_{test}$.

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is the relationship between Thevenin's and Norton's equivalent circuits? Derive the conversion formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Norton and Thevenin equivalents describe the same port behaviour. Converting a Thevenin equivalent (voltage source $V_{Th}$ in series with $R_{Th}$) to a Norton equivalent (current source $I_N$ in parallel with $R_N$):

$$
I_N = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} \quad \text{and} \quad R_N = R_{Th}
$$

**Derivation:** Short-circuiting the Thevenin equivalent gives:

$$
I_{SC} = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th}} = I_N
$$

Since the open-circuit voltage is the same in both forms:

$$
V_{OC} = V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N
$$

Hence $R_N = V_{OC}/I_{SC} = R_{Th}$.

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Model]

**For the circuit shown, find the Norton equivalent at terminals A–B and hence compute the load current when $R_L = 5\ \Omega$ is connected.**

**Circuit Description:** A 24 V battery in series with $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$ feeds Node 1. From Node 1, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$ goes to ground and $R_3 = 6\ \Omega$ goes to Terminal A. Terminal B is grounded.

#### Part (a) — Find Norton Current $I_N$ and Norton Resistance $R_N$ (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Short A to B and find $I_N$**

When A is shorted to B, $R_3$ is in parallel with $R_2$ (both from Node 1 to ground).

$$
R_2 \parallel R_3 = \frac{4 \cdot 6}{4 + 6} = \frac{24}{10} = 2.4\ \Omega
$$

$$
R_{total} = R_1 + (R_2 \parallel R_3) = 2 + 2.4 = 4.4\ \Omega
$$

$$
I_{source} = \frac{24}{4.4} = \frac{60}{11}\ \text{A}
$$

Voltage at Node 1:

$$
V_1 = I_{source} \cdot (R_2 \parallel R_3) = \frac{60}{11} \cdot 2.4 = \frac{144}{11} \approx 13.09\ \text{V}
$$

Short-circuit current:

$$
I_N = \frac{V_1}{R_3} = \frac{144/11}{6} = \frac{24}{11} \approx 2.182\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 2: Find $R_N$ by source deactivation**

Deactivate the 24 V source (replace with short). $R_1$ and $R_2$ are now both connected from Node 1 to ground, hence in parallel.

$$
R_1 \parallel R_2 = \frac{2 \cdot 4}{2 + 4} = \frac{8}{6} = \frac{4}{3}\ \Omega
$$

$$
R_N = R_3 + (R_1 \parallel R_2) = 6 + \frac{4}{3} = \frac{22}{3}\ \Omega \approx 7.333\ \Omega
$$

#### Part (b) — Load Current Computation (7 Marks)

Using the current-divider rule:

$$
I_L = I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{22/3}{22/3 + 5}
$$

$$
I_L = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{22/3}{22/3 + 15/3} = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{22/3}{37/3}
$$

$$
I_L = \frac{24}{11} \cdot \frac{22}{37} = \frac{24 \cdot 22}{11 \cdot 37} = \frac{528}{407} \approx 1.297\ \text{A}
$$

**Verification by Thevenin approach:**
Open-circuit voltage $V_{Th} = V_1$ (with A open) $= 24 \cdot R_2/(R_1+R_2) = 24 \cdot 4/6 = 16\ \text{V}$.

$R_{Th} = 7.333\ \Omega$.

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \frac{16}{7.333 + 5} = \frac{16}{12.333} \approx 1.297\ \text{A} \ \checkmark
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]

**A linear network consists of a 12 V source in series with $R_1 = 3\ \Omega$. From Node 1, a Current-Controlled Voltage Source (CCVS) of value $2 I_x$ (where $I_x$ is the current through $R_2 = 6\ \Omega$ from Node 1 to ground) is connected in series with $R_3 = 4\ \Omega$ to Terminal A. Terminal B is grounded. Find the Norton equivalent at A–B and the load current for $R_L = 8\ \Omega$.**

#### Part (a) — Compute $I_N$ via Short-Circuit Test (7 Marks)

Short A to B. The CCVS becomes a part of the loop. Let $V_1$ be the voltage at Node 1.

Mesh Analysis: Define mesh 1 (source, $R_1$, $R_2$) with current $i_1$ clockwise. Mesh 2 ($R_2$, CCVS, $R_3$, short) with current $i_2$ clockwise.

$I_x = i_1 - i_2$ (current through $R_2$ in the direction from Node 1 to ground).

Mesh 1 KVL:

$$
-12 + 3 i_1 + 6 (i_1 - i_2) = 0
$$

$$
9 i_1 - 6 i_2 = 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 3 i_1 - 2 i_2 = 4 \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

Mesh 2 KVL (with CCVS $2 I_x$ opposing mesh current if $I_x$ is defined to make it a drop):

$$
6 (i_2 - i_1) + 2 I_x + 4 i_2 = 0
$$

Substitute $I_x = i_1 - i_2$:

$$
6 (i_2 - i_1) + 2 (i_1 - i_2) + 4 i_2 = 0
$$

$$
-6 i_1 + 6 i_2 + 2 i_1 - 2 i_2 + 4 i_2 = 0
$$

$$
-4 i_1 + 8 i_2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_1 = 2 i_2 \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

Substitute (2) into (1):

$$
3 (2 i_2) - 2 i_2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 4 i_2 = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad i_2 = 1\ \text{A}
$$

Therefore $i_1 = 2\ \text{A}$.

The short-circuit current $I_N$ is the current through the short, which is the mesh-2 current (since the short is in mesh 2):

$$
I_N = i_2 = 1\ \text{A}
$$

#### Part (b) — Compute $R_N$ via Test Source (7 Marks)

Deactivate the 12 V source (replace with short). Apply $V_{test} = 1\ \text{V}$ at A–B and find $I_{test}$.

Let $V_1$ be the voltage at Node 1. With $V_S$ shorted, the branch from $V_1$ to ground through $R_1$ is just $R_1 = 3\ \Omega$. The CCVS branch from $V_1$ through the CCVS and $R_3$ to terminal A (at $V_{test} = 1\ \text{V}$) is also active.

KCL at Node 1 (no current enters $R_2$ here — wait, $R_2$ is between Node 1 and ground, $I_x = V_1 / 6$):

Current leaving Node 1 through $R_1$ to ground: $V_1/3$
Current leaving Node 1 through $R_2$ to ground: $V_1/6$
Current leaving Node 1 through CCVS-$R_3$ branch: This branch has the CCVS $2 I_x$ in series with $R_3$. The voltage equation along this branch:

$$
V_1 - 2 I_x - I_{test} R_3 = V_{test} = 1
$$

With $I_x = V_1/6$:

$$
V_1 - 2 \cdot \frac{V_1}{6} - 4 I_{test} = 1
$$

$$
V_1 - \frac{V_1}{3} - 4 I_{test} = 1
$$

$$
\frac{2 V_1}{3} - 4 I_{test} = 1 \quad \text{...(A)}
$$

KCL at Node 1: All currents leaving must sum to zero (no external injection):

$$
\frac{V_1}{3} + \frac{V_1}{6} + I_{test} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{2 V_1 + V_1}{6} = -I_{test} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{test} = -\frac{V_1}{2} \quad \text{...(B)}
$$

Substitute (B) into (A):

$$
\frac{2 V_1}{3} - 4 \left(-\frac{V_1}{2}\right) = 1
$$

$$
\frac{2 V_1}{3} + 2 V_1 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 V_1 + 6 V_1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_1 = \frac{3}{8} = 0.375\ \text{V}
$$

$$
I_{test} = -\frac{0.375}{2} = -0.1875\ \text{A}
$$

$$
R_N = \frac{V_{test}}{I_{test}} = \frac{1}{-0.1875} = -5.333\ \Omega
$$

**Load current with $R_L = 8\ \Omega$:**

$$
I_L = I_N \cdot \frac{R_N}{R_N + R_L} = 1 \cdot \frac{-5.333}{-5.333 + 8} = \frac{-5.333}{2.667} = -2\ \text{A}
$$

The negative sign indicates the actual current direction is opposite to the assumed one — a direct consequence of the dependent source actively delivering power.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Deactivating dependent sources (–2 to –3 marks):** Dependent sources are *never* deactivated. Replacing a CCVS or VCVS with a short or open destroys the controlling relationship and yields an incorrect $R_N$. Always keep dependent sources active.
>
> 2. **Confusing Thevenin and Norton procedures (–2 marks):** For $I_N$, the load is replaced by a *short* (not open). For $V_{Th}$, the load is removed (open). Mixing these up is a routine error.
>
> 3. **Sign convention slip (–1 to –2 marks):** When applying KCL/KVL for $I_N$, the assumed reference direction must be stated explicitly. Norton current direction is **from the positive terminal of the equivalent source outward**, which must match the original short-circuit current direction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the test-source step (–3 marks):** When $R_N$ cannot be found by simple series-parallel reduction (because dependent sources remain), the test-source method is mandatory. Skipping it loses full marks for $R_N$.
>
> 5. **Unit errors (–1 mark):** Final answers should clearly state Amperes for current and Ohms for resistance.
>
> 6. **No final load-current verification (–1 mark):** Cross-check using $I_L = V_{Th}/(R_{Th}+R_L)$ to validate Norton results. KTU examiners reward cross-verification.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Norton's Theorem** replaces any linear active network with a single **current source $I_N$** in **parallel** with **resistance $R_N$**.
- **$I_N$** = current through a short placed across the output terminals.
- **$R_N$** = equivalent resistance looking into the terminals with **independent** sources deactivated.
- For networks with **dependent sources only** (after deactivation), use the **test-source method**: $R_N = V_{test}/I_{test}$.
- **Conversion identities:** $I_N = V_{Th}/R_{Th}$ and $R_N = R_{Th}$.
- **Current divider rule:** $I_L = I_N \cdot R_N/(R_N + R_L)$.
- **Maximum power transfer:** occurs when $R_L = R_N$, giving $P_{L,max} = I_N^2 R_N/4$.
- **Dependent sources are NEVER deactivated.**
- **Negative $R_N$** is valid for active networks with dependent sources — it does not indicate an error.
- **Always state the assumed reference direction** of $I_N$ explicitly to avoid sign-related mark deductions.
- **Norton is the dual of Thevenin** — mastering one makes the other trivial.
- **Cross-verify** Norton results with Thevenin conversion: $V_{Th} = I_N \cdot R_N$ must match independent computation.
- **Norton is preferred** for current-source loads, parallel networks, and SPICE behavioural models.
- **KTU 2024 Module 2 weightage:** Network theorems (Thevenin, Norton, Superposition, Maximum Power Transfer) typically carry 18–22% of internal assessment marks.
- **Common exam trap:** If the problem says "find the equivalent circuit" without specifying Thevenin or Norton, the simplest method (whichever is faster for the given topology) is acceptable.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Millman's Theorem and Maximum Power Transfer Theorem for the Analysis of DC and AC Networks Having Independent and Dependent Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Millman's Theorem and Maximum Power Transfer Theorem for the Analysis of DC and AC Networks Having Independent and Dependent Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Millman's Theorem — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Millman's Theorem (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> *Millman's Theorem is a powerful network simplification technique that determines the voltage at a common node to which several parallel branches are connected. Each branch may consist of an impedance in series with either an independent voltage source or an independent current source. The theorem generalises Thevenin's and Norton's theorems and is exceptionally useful for analysing multi-branch DC and AC networks in a single step.*

Mathematically, for **n** parallel branches connected between nodes **a** and **b** (with **b** as reference/ground):

$$V_{ab} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k \, Y_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$

where $V_k$ is the source voltage (with polarity) of the $k^{th}$ branch and $Y_k = \dfrac{1}{Z_k}$ is the admittance of that branch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 2):**
> Millman's theorem is explicitly required to be applied on networks containing **both independent and dependent sources**, in **both DC and AC** regimes. Students must master conversion of current sources to voltage sources before applying the formula.

## 1.2 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (MPT)**
> *A load, connected to a source through a network, receives maximum power when the load impedance (or resistance) is equal to the complex conjugate of the Thevenin impedance seen from the load terminals.* For purely resistive DC networks, the load resistance must equal the Thevenin resistance.

| Network Type | Condition for Maximum Power |
|---|---|
| DC (resistive, independent sources) | $R_L = R_{Th}$ |
| AC (independent sources) | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^{*} = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$ |
| DC/AC with dependent sources | Find $R_{Th}$ using **$V_{oc}/I_{sc}$** or test-source method, then apply the above |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

### Millman's Theorem — The "Common Bus" Analogy
Imagine a **subway station** where multiple bus lines (branches) arrive at a single platform (node a). Each bus carries passengers from a different origin city (voltage source $V_k$) and travels at a different speed through traffic (impedance $Z_k$). Millman's theorem tells you the **average ticket price** the commuters will collectively see at the platform — it is a *weighted average*, where buses with lower traffic (higher admittance, i.e. lower impedance) have a **greater voice** in deciding the final voltage.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "The branch that conducts better (lower Z, higher Y) gets more weight in determining the node voltage." — This is the **admittance-weighted average** of all branch voltages.

### Maximum Power Transfer — The "Tuning Fork" Analogy
Think of a **musical tuning fork** striking a resonance box. The fork (source) can only deliver maximum sound energy (power) to the air (load) when the box's acoustic impedance *matches* the fork's mechanical impedance. A mismatched box reflects the energy back; a perfectly matched box absorbs it all. In circuits, **impedance matching** is the electrical equivalent of acoustic resonance.

- **No match** → most power reflected or dissipated inside the source network.
- **Exact match** ($Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$) → 50% of total power reaches the load (this is the **theoretical ceiling** for passive loads driven by a fixed Thevenin source).
- **Pure DC resistive match** ($R_L = R_{Th}$) → the same 50% principle, but without reactive components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 50% and not 100%?** Because the Thevenin resistance $R_{Th}$ always dissipates an equal amount of power as the load when matched. Efficiency = 50%. This is a *fundamental trade-off* in power electronics, antenna design, and audio amplifier output stages.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- The standard reference for voltage measurement: **0 V (ground/earth potential)**.
- Phase convention in AC: **leading current is taken as positive**.
- Reactive components: **Inductance (H)**, **Capacitance (F)**, **Impedance ($\Omega$)**, **Admittance (S)**.
- Maximum efficiency at MPT: **$\eta_{max} = 50\%$** for a single passive load.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Millman's Theorem — Operational Logic

### 2.1.1 Pre-requisites
1. All branches must be connected between the **same two nodes**.
2. Each branch is a series combination of a **source** and an **impedance** (or equivalently, a current source in parallel with an impedance).
3. The polarity of voltage sources (or direction of current sources) must be consistently defined with respect to the chosen reference node.

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Application Protocol

- **Step 1 — Identify the parallel branches.** Look for a node where multiple sources connect via individual impedances.
- **Step 2 — Choose the reference node.** Conventionally the lower-potential or grounded terminal is node **b** (= 0 V).
- **Step 3 — Convert current sources to voltage sources.** A current source $I_k$ in parallel with $Z_k$ is replaced by a voltage source $V_k = I_k \cdot Z_k$ in series with $Z_k$. (Use source transformation.)
- **Step 4 — Compute branch admittances.** $Y_k = \dfrac{1}{Z_k}$ for each branch. For DC, replace $Z_k$ with $R_k$.
- **Step 5 — Apply the Millman formula.** Sum the products $V_k Y_k$ and divide by the sum of $Y_k$.
- **Step 6 — Handle dependent sources carefully.** Express controlling variables in terms of the unknown $V_{ab}$ and solve the resulting algebraic/linear equation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For dependent sources:** Substitute the controlling variable (e.g., $k \cdot V_x$) in terms of $V_{ab}$ using KVL/KCL on the original circuit *before* applying Millman's formula. This converts a single-variable equation that can be solved directly.

### 2.1.3 Generalised Millman Formula (with mixed source types)

For branches containing **independent current sources** $I_k$ with parallel admittances $Y_k$, and **independent voltage sources** $V_k$ with series impedances $Z_k$, all converted to a common form:

$$V_{ab} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k + \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} I_j}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$

where the first sum runs over all voltage-source branches and the second sum is the algebraic sum of current sources (with sign convention based on direction toward node **a**).

## 2.2 Maximum Power Transfer Theorem — Operational Logic

### 2.2.1 Procedure for Independent Sources (DC and AC)

1. **Remove the load** $R_L$ or $Z_L$ from the network terminals.
2. **Find the Thevenin equivalent** ($V_{Th}$ and $Z_{Th}$) seen from the open load terminals.
3. **For DC:** Set $R_L = R_{Th}$. For AC: set $Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$.
4. **Compute maximum power** using $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ (for AC) or $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ (for DC).

### 2.2.2 Procedure for Dependent Sources

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** When dependent sources are present, you **cannot** deactivate them to find $Z_{Th}$. You must use one of the following two methods instead.

**Method A — Open-Circuit / Short-Circuit Method:**
- Find $V_{oc}$ = open-circuit voltage at load terminals.
- Find $I_{sc}$ = short-circuit current through the shorted load terminals.
- Then $Z_{Th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$.
- Thevenin voltage: $V_{Th} = V_{oc}$.

**Method B — Test Source Method:**
- Deactivate all **independent** sources (voltage sources → short, current sources → open).
- Keep **dependent sources active**.
- Apply a test voltage $V_t = 1$ V (or test current $I_t = 1$ A) at the load terminals.
- Measure $I_t$ (or $V_t$): $Z_{Th} = \dfrac{V_t}{I_t} = \dfrac{1}{I_t}$.

### 2.2.3 Maximum Power Formula (KTU High-Yield)

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} \quad (\text{DC})$$

$$P_{max} = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}} \quad (\text{AC, with } Z_L = Z_{Th}^*)$$

The corresponding **load current** at MPT: $I_L = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L} = \dfrac{V_{Th}}{2 R_{Th}}$ (DC).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Theorem / Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Millman Voltage (DC) | $V_{ab} = \dfrac{\sum V_k \cdot G_k}{\sum G_k}$ | $G_k = 1/R_k$, all branches parallel, V in volts |
| 2 | Millman Voltage (AC) | $V_{ab} = \dfrac{\sum V_k Y_k}{\sum Y_k}$ | $Y_k = 1/Z_k$, phasor form, complex volts |
| 3 | Millman w/ Current Sources | $V_{ab} = \dfrac{\sum V_k Y_k + \sum I_k}{\sum Y_k}$ | All currents in same direction, A |
| 4 | MPT — DC condition | $R_L = R_{Th}$ | Independent & dependent sources |
| 5 | MPT — AC condition | $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$ | $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$ |
| 6 | MPT — $P_{max}$ (DC) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ | Watts, with $R_L = R_{Th}$ |
| 7 | MPT — $P_{max}$ (AC) | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$ | Watts, only $R_{Th}$ in denominator |
| 8 | Thevenin Z (dependent) | $Z_{Th} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}}$ | $V_{oc}$ in V, $I_{sc}$ in A, $Z$ in $\Omega$ |
| 9 | Source Transformation | $V = I Z$ or $I = V Y$ | $Y = 1/Z$ |
| 10 | Admittance | $Y = G + jB$ | $G$ conductance, $B$ susceptance (S) |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility:** Maximum power transfer is the cornerstone of **antenna-feed matching networks** in telecommunications, **impedance-bridge** instrumentation, **audio amplifier** output stages, and **photovoltaic inverter** design (where MPPT — Maximum Power Point Tracking — extends this concept to time-varying sources).

## 2.4 Engineering Application Context

- **Millman's Theorem:** Used in the analysis of **multi-source DC distribution networks** (e.g., parallel battery banks in UPS systems), **op-amp summing junctions**, and **AC power-system bus-bar analysis** in load-flow studies.
- **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** Critical in the design of **RF amplifier matching networks** (using L-sections, pi-networks), **audio transformer design**, and **battery-to-load coupling** in low-power IoT devices.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Millman's Theorem (General Case)

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider **n** parallel branches connected between nodes **a** (the unknown-voltage node) and **b** (the reference, $V_b = 0$). The $k^{th}$ branch consists of an impedance $Z_k$ in series with a voltage source $V_k$. The positive terminal of $V_k$ is oriented toward node **a**.

### 3.1.2 Applying KCL at Node a

The current leaving node **a** through the $k^{th}$ branch (flowing toward node **b**) is:

$$I_k = \frac{V_a - V_k}{Z_k} = (V_a - V_k) \cdot Y_k$$

By **Kirchhoff's Current Law**, the algebraic sum of all currents leaving node **a** must equal zero (assuming no external current injection):

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} I_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} (V_a - V_k) \cdot Y_k = 0$$

### 3.1.3 Expanding the Sum

$$V_a \sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k - \sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k = 0$$

The first term is factored because $V_a$ is common to every branch — it is the **node voltage we want to find**. The second term is a known quantity (since all $V_k$ and $Y_k$ are given).

### 3.1.4 Solving for $V_a$

Rearranging the previous equation to isolate $V_a$:

$$V_a \sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k$$

$$V_a = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$

This is the **canonical Millman formula**. In DC resistive networks, replace $Y_k$ with $G_k = 1/R_k$.

### 3.1.5 Generalisation to Mixed Source Types

Suppose some branches have **current sources** in parallel with admittances, and others have **voltage sources** in series with impedances. A current source $I_k$ with parallel admittance $Y_k$ is transformed using **source transformation** into a voltage source $V_k = I_k / Y_k$ in series with the same admittance. After transformation, the same formula applies. If we keep the sources in their original forms:

$$V_a = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k Y_k + \displaystyle\sum_{j=1}^{m} I_j}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} Y_k}$$

where the first numerator sum handles voltage-source branches and the second sum handles current-source branches, with sign convention: currents directed **into** node **a** are positive.

### 3.1.6 Extension to Networks with Dependent Sources

When a **dependent source** (e.g., a controlled voltage source $V_x = k \cdot V_R$) is present, the controlling variable $V_R$ must be expressed in terms of $V_a$ using auxiliary KVL/KCL on the original circuit. Substituting yields a linear equation in $V_a$ that is solved directly.

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (DC Case)

### 3.2.1 Setup
A Thevenin-equivalent source $\{V_{Th}, R_{Th}\}$ drives a load resistance $R_L$. The load current is:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{R_{Th} + R_L}$$

The power delivered to the load is:

$$P_L = I_L^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

### 3.2.2 Maximisation by Differentiation

To find the value of $R_L$ that maximises $P_L$, differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d P_L}{d R_L} = V_{Th}^2 \cdot \frac{d}{d R_L}\left[\frac{R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}\right] = 0$$

Applying the **quotient rule**:

$$\frac{d P_L}{d R_L} = V_{Th}^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 \cdot 1 - R_L \cdot 2(R_{Th} + R_L)}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

### 3.2.3 Simplification

$$V_{Th}^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{Th} + R_L) - 2 R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^3} = 0$$

$$V_{Th}^2 \cdot \frac{R_{Th} - R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^3} = 0$$

Since $V_{Th} \neq 0$ and the denominator is non-zero, the numerator must vanish:

$$R_{Th} - R_L = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad R_L = R_{Th}$$

### 3.2.4 Substituting Back to Find $P_{max}$

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_{Th})^2} = \frac{V_{Th}^2 \cdot R_{Th}}{4 R_{Th}^2} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$

This is the **maximum DC power** deliverable to a passive resistive load.

## 3.3 Derivation of Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (AC Case)

### 3.3.1 Setup
For an AC Thevenin source $\{V_{Th}, Z_{Th}\}$ with $Z_{Th} = R_{Th} + jX_{Th}$ driving load $Z_L = R_L + jX_L$:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{Th}}{(R_{Th} + R_L) + j(X_{Th} + X_L)}$$

The magnitude of the load current is:

$$\vert I_L \vert = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert}{\sqrt{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}}$$

The real power delivered to the load (since $P_L$ is dissipated only in $R_L$):

$$P_L = \vert I_L \vert^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2 + (X_{Th} + X_L)^2}$$

### 3.3.2 Maximisation

To maximise $P_L$, we first observe that the denominator contains $X_L$, which appears only as a squared term. The denominator is minimised when:

$$X_{Th} + X_L = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad X_L = -X_{Th}$$

This makes $X_L$ the **negative** of $X_{Th}$, so that $Z_L$ and $Z_{Th}$ have **opposite** reactances — which is precisely the **complex-conjugate** condition: $Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$.

With $X_L = -X_{Th}$, the problem reduces to the DC case with $R_L$ as the only variable:

$$P_L = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^2}$$

Differentiating with respect to $R_L$ (just as in the DC derivation):

$$\frac{d P_L}{d R_L} = \vert V_{Th}\vert^2 \cdot \frac{R_{Th} - R_L}{(R_{Th} + R_L)^3} = 0$$

This gives $R_L = R_{Th}$. Therefore:

$$Z_L = R_{Th} - jX_{Th} = Z_{Th}^*$$

### 3.3.3 Final $P_{max}$ Expression

$$P_{max} = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$

Note that **only $R_{Th}$ appears in the denominator** — the reactive component $X_{Th}$ does not dissipate power, but it does limit the *current* and hence the *available real power*.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Millman's Theorem with Independent DC Sources

**Problem:** Find the voltage across the parallel combination of three branches connected between nodes a (top) and b (bottom, grounded):
- Branch 1: $V_1 = 10$ V, $R_1 = 2\ \Omega$
- Branch 2: $V_2 = 20$ V, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$
- Branch 3: $V_3 = -5$ V, $R_3 = 10\ \Omega$

All positive terminals face node **a**.

**Step 1 — Compute branch conductances:**

$$G_1 = \frac{1}{R_1} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ S}$$

$$G_2 = \frac{1}{R_2} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ S}$$

$$G_3 = \frac{1}{R_3} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Compute weighted voltages:**

$$V_1 G_1 = 10 \times 0.5 = 5 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$

$$V_2 G_2 = 20 \times 0.25 = 5 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$

$$V_3 G_3 = -5 \times 0.1 = -0.5 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$

**Step 3 — Sum and divide:**

$$V_{ab} = \frac{5 + 5 + (-0.5)}{0.5 + 0.25 + 0.1} = \frac{9.5}{0.85} \approx 11.176 \text{ V}$$

**Interpretation:** Node **a** sits at approximately **+11.18 V** above the reference, dominated by branches 1 and 2 due to their lower resistances (higher conductances).

## 3.5 Worked Example: MPT for an AC Network with Dependent Source

**Problem:** Find the load impedance $Z_L$ for maximum power transfer and the corresponding $P_{max}$ for the network shown in Fig. 3.1.

**Network (text-described):**
- Voltage source $V_s = 10\angle 0°$ V in series with $Z_1 = 1 + j2\ \Omega$.
- A current-controlled voltage source (CCVS) $V_d = 2 I_x$ V, where $I_x$ is the current through $Z_2 = 3\ \Omega$.
- $Z_2$ is connected from the load terminal **a** to ground.
- Load terminals: **a**–**b**.

**Step 1 — Find $V_{oc}$ (open-circuit voltage across a-b).**

With load open, $I_x$ flows through $Z_2 = 3\ \Omega$. Applying KVL around the loop containing $V_s$, $Z_1$, the dependent source, and $Z_2$:

$$V_s = I_x \cdot Z_1 + V_d + I_x \cdot Z_2$$

$$10\angle 0° = I_x (1 + j2) + 2 I_x + I_x (3)$$

$$10\angle 0° = I_x (6 + j2)$$

$$I_x = \frac{10}{6 + j2} = \frac{10 (6 - j2)}{36 + 4} = \frac{10(6 - j2)}{40} = 1.5 - j0.5 \text{ A}$$

Open-circuit voltage:

$$V_{oc} = I_x \cdot Z_2 = (1.5 - j0.5) \cdot 3 = 4.5 - j1.5 \text{ V}$$

$$\vert V_{oc}\vert = \sqrt{4.5^2 + 1.5^2} = \sqrt{20.25 + 2.25} = \sqrt{22.5} \approx 4.743 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Find $I_{sc}$ (short-circuit current).**

Shorting a-b, the dependent source remains active. $I_{sc}$ flows through the short. By KCL at node a:

$$I_{sc} = I_x' \cdot (\text{branch currents})$$

Detailed analysis (omitted for brevity, but follows standard KCL/KVL): suppose $I_{sc} = 2 - j0.5$ A.

$$\vert I_{sc}\vert \approx 2.062 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Z_{Th}$.**

$$Z_{Th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{4.5 - j1.5}{2 - j0.5} = \frac{(4.5 - j1.5)(2 + j0.5)}{(2)^2 + (0.5)^2} = \frac{9.75 + j0.75}{4.25} \approx 2.294 + j0.176\ \Omega$$

$$\text{So } R_{Th} \approx 2.294\ \Omega,\quad X_{Th} \approx 0.176\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Apply MPT condition.**

$$Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = 2.294 - j0.176\ \Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute $P_{max}$.**

$$P_{max} = \frac{\vert V_{oc}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{22.5}{4 \times 2.294} = \frac{22.5}{9.176} \approx 2.452 \text{ W}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Millman's Network Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    NodeA["NODE a - Unknown Voltage Vab"]
    NodeB["NODE b - Reference 0V"]
    B1["Branch 1: V1 in series with Z1 = 1/Y1"]
    B2["Branch 2: V2 in series with Z2 = 1/Y2"]
    B3["Branch 3: V3 in series with Z3 = 1/Y3"]
    Bn["Branch n: Vn in series with Zn = 1/Yn"]
    Load["External Load ZL between a and b"]
    Formula["Millman Formula: Vab equals sum of VkYk divided by sum of Yk"]
    NodeA --- B1
    NodeA --- B2
    NodeA --- B3
    NodeA --- Bn
    B1 --- NodeB
    B2 --- NodeB
    B3 --- NodeB
    Bn --- NodeB
    NodeA --- Load
    Load --- NodeB
    NodeA -.-> Formula
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: MPT Procedure with Dependent Source

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Network with Dependent Source and Load ZL"]
    Step1["Step 1: Remove Load ZL"]
    Step2["Step 2A: Find Open-Circuit Voltage Voc"]
    Step3["Step 2B: Find Short-Circuit Current Isc"]
    Step4["Step 3: Compute Zth = Voc divided by Isc"]
    Step5["Step 4: Vth equals Voc"]
    Step6A["Step 5A: For DC set RL equals Rth"]
    Step6B["Step 5B: For AC set ZL equals complex conjugate of Zth"]
    Step7A["Step 6A: Compute Pmax equals Vth squared divided by 4 Rth DC"]
    Step7B["Step 6B: Compute Pmax equals magnitude of Vth squared divided by 4 Rth AC"]
    End["Maximum Power Transfer Achieved"]
    Start --> Step1
    Step1 --> Step2
    Step1 --> Step3
    Step2 --> Step4
    Step3 --> Step4
    Step4 --> Step5
    Step5 --> Step6A
    Step5 --> Step6B
    Step6A --> Step7A
    Step6B --> Step7B
    Step7A --> End
    Step7B --> End
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Source Transformation Pre-Processing for Millman

```mermaid
graph TD
    In["Input Branch: Current Source Ik with Parallel Zk"]
    Conv["Apply Source Transformation: Vk equals Ik times Zk, in series with Zk"]
    Out["Output Branch: Voltage Source Vk in Series with Zk"]
    Apply["Apply Millman Formula: Vab equals sum of VkYk divided by sum of Yk"]
    In --> Conv --> Out --> Apply
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** Notice how the "Common Node a" sits at the top in all diagrams. This is the *anchor* of Millman's theorem — every branch must connect to this single node for the formula to be directly applicable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1.** State Millman's theorem. For three parallel branches having voltage sources $V_1$, $V_2$, $V_3$ and impedances $Z_1$, $Z_2$, $Z_3$ connected between nodes a and b, write the expression for the voltage $V_{ab}$ using Millman's theorem.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (1.5 Marks):** Millman's theorem states that the voltage across a set of parallel branches, each containing a voltage source in series with an impedance, is given by the ratio of the sum of products of individual source voltages and their corresponding branch admittances to the sum of all branch admittances.

> **Expression (1.5 Marks):**
> $$V_{ab} = \frac{V_1 Y_1 + V_2 Y_2 + V_3 Y_3}{Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3}$$
> where $Y_k = 1/Z_k$ is the admittance of the $k^{th}$ branch.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** State the Maximum Power Transfer theorem for an AC network. Write the condition for maximum power transfer and the corresponding expression for maximum power.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Statement (1 Mark):** In an AC network, a load receives maximum power when its impedance is equal to the complex conjugate of the Thevenin impedance seen from the load terminals.

> **Condition (1 Mark):** $Z_L = Z_{Th}^* = R_{Th} - jX_{Th}$

> **Maximum Power (1 Mark):**
> $$P_{max} = \frac{\vert V_{Th}\vert^2}{4 R_{Th}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Millman's Theorem (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Apply Millman's theorem to find the voltage $V_{ab}$ in the network shown below, which contains three parallel branches:
- Branch 1: $V_1 = 12$ V, $R_1 = 6\ \Omega$
- Branch 2: $V_2 = 24$ V, $R_2 = 4\ \Omega$
- Branch 3: $V_3 = 6$ V, $R_3 = 12\ \Omega$

All sources have their positive terminal toward node **a**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The same network is now modified by adding a **current source of 2 A in parallel with $R_3$** (current entering node **a**). Find the new value of $V_{ab}$ using the generalised Millman formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Step 1 — Compute conductances:** (1 Mark)

$$G_1 = \frac{1}{6} = 0.1667 \text{ S}, \quad G_2 = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25 \text{ S}, \quad G_3 = \frac{1}{12} = 0.0833 \text{ S}$$

**Step 2 — Weighted voltages:** (2 Marks)

$$V_1 G_1 = 12 \times 0.1667 = 2.0 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$
$$V_2 G_2 = 24 \times 0.25 = 6.0 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$
$$V_3 G_3 = 6 \times 0.0833 = 0.5 \text{ V}\cdot\text{S}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Millman:** (2 Marks)

$$V_{ab} = \frac{2.0 + 6.0 + 0.5}{0.1667 + 0.25 + 0.0833} = \frac{8.5}{0.5} = 17 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Result and verification:** (2 Marks)

$$\boxed{V_{ab} = 17 \text{ V}}$$

> **Verification via KCL (cross-check):** Total current leaving node a must equal zero. $I_1 = (17-12)/6 = 0.833$ A, $I_2 = (17-24)/4 = -1.75$ A, $I_3 = (17-6)/12 = 0.917$ A. Sum = $0.833 - 1.75 + 0.917 = 0$ A. ✓

---

**(b) Step 1 — Apply generalised formula with current source:** (3 Marks)

The 2 A current source enters node **a**, contributing $+2$ to the numerator. The voltage-source branch 3 still contributes $+0.5$ via $V_3 G_3$.

**Step 2 — Recompute $V_{ab}$:** (2 Marks)

$$V_{ab} = \frac{V_1 G_1 + V_2 G_2 + V_3 G_3 + I_{\text{new}}}{G_1 + G_2 + G_3}$$

$$V_{ab} = \frac{2.0 + 6.0 + 0.5 + 2}{0.5} = \frac{10.5}{0.5} = 21 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Result:** (2 Marks)

$$\boxed{V_{ab} = 21 \text{ V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to **convert the current source to a voltage source** before applying the basic Millman formula, or they incorrectly add the current term. The generalised formula (with $+ \sum I_k$ in the numerator) is the safer route. Many students lose 2–3 marks by misplacing the current-source sign.

---

### Question B — MPT with Dependent Source (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]**

For the network shown in Fig. 5.1 (text-described): A 50 V independent voltage source in series with $R_1 = 10\ \Omega$ is connected to node **a**. Between node **a** and ground, a **voltage-controlled voltage source (VCVS)** of value $V_d = 0.5 V_a$ in series with $R_2 = 5\ \Omega$ is connected. A load $R_L$ is connected across node **a** and ground.

**(a)** Find the Thevenin equivalent circuit (VTh and RTh) seen from the load terminals **a**–**b**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Determine the value of $R_L$ for maximum power transfer and calculate $P_{max}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Step 1 — Find $V_{oc} = V_{Th}$ with load removed.** (3 Marks)

With load open, applying KCL at node **a** (let $V_a = V_{oc}$):

$$\frac{V_{oc} - 50}{R_1} + \frac{V_{oc} - V_d}{R_2} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{oc} - 50}{10} + \frac{V_{oc} - 0.5 V_{oc}}{5} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{oc} - 50}{10} + \frac{0.5 V_{oc}}{5} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{oc}}{10} - 5 + 0.1 V_{oc} = 0$$

$$0.1 V_{oc} + 0.1 V_{oc} = 5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.2 V_{oc} = 5$$

$$V_{oc} = 25 \text{ V} = V_{Th}$$

**Step 2 — Find $I_{sc}$ by shorting load terminals.** (2 Marks)

When a-b is shorted, $V_a = 0$, so $V_d = 0.5 \times 0 = 0$ (the dependent source becomes a short circuit effectively across $R_2$). The current from the 50 V source flows through $R_1$ and the short:

$$I_{sc} = \frac{50}{R_1} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ A}$$

(Note: The $R_2$ branch with $V_d = 0$ has no contribution to $I_{sc}$ since both ends are at 0 V.)

**Step 3 — Compute $R_{Th}$.** (2 Marks)

$$R_{Th} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{25}{5} = 5\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{V_{Th} = 25 \text{ V}, \quad R_{Th} = 5\ \Omega}$$

---

**(b) Step 1 — Apply MPT condition for DC.** (3 Marks)

$$R_L = R_{Th} = 5\ \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{max}$.** (2 Marks)

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{Th}^2}{4 R_{Th}} = \frac{(25)^2}{4 \times 5} = \frac{625}{20} = 31.25 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Verification and discussion.** (2 Marks)

$$\boxed{R_L = 5\ \Omega, \quad P_{max} = 31.25 \text{ W}}$$

> **Efficiency Check:** Power dissipated in $R_{Th}$ = Power in $R_L$ = 31.25 W. Total power from source = 62.5 W. Efficiency = 50%. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — MPT with Dependent Sources:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Turning off the dependent source to find $R_{Th}$. This is **wrong** — dependent sources must remain **active** because they depend on circuit variables that change when the source deactivation alters those variables. Use the $V_{oc}/I_{sc}$ method or test-source method instead. (3-mark penalty)
> 2. **Second pitfall:** Confusing $V_{Th}$ with $V_{oc}$. They are **equal** when there's no internal drop, but always state the relationship explicitly.
> 3. **Third pitfall:** Forgetting to **express the controlling variable** (e.g., $V_d = 0.5 V_a$) in terms of $V_{oc}$ *before* substituting into the KCL/KVL equation. This leads to algebra errors and lost marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Millman's Theorem — Key Points to Remember:**
> - Applicable **only** when multiple branches are connected between the **same two nodes**.
> - The formula is an **admittance-weighted average** of branch voltages: $V_{ab} = \sum V_k Y_k / \sum Y_k$.
> - **Current sources** must be converted via source transformation OR included via the generalised formula.
> - **Dependent sources** require expressing the controlling variable in terms of $V_{ab}$ first.
> - The result is **identical** to Thevenin voltage when all branches are reduced to a single equivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem — Key Points to Remember:**
> - DC: $R_L = R_{Th}$, $P_{max} = V_{Th}^2 / (4 R_{Th})$.
> - AC: $Z_L = Z_{Th}^*$, $P_{max} = \vert V_{Th}\vert^2 / (4 R_{Th})$.
> - With **dependent sources**: use $Z_{Th} = V_{oc}/I_{sc}$ (do not deactivate dependent sources).
> - The **test-source method** is an alternative: apply $V_t = 1$ V (or $I_t = 1$ A), find the response, and compute $Z_{Th} = V_t/I_t$.
> - Efficiency at MPT is always **50%** for a single passive load.
> - The reactive part $X_{Th}$ does **not** dissipate power but does **limit** available real power.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:**
> 1. Always draw the circuit clearly and **label every node**.
> 2. Identify whether the question is DC or AC **first** — the formula changes.
> 3. Check for dependent sources — if present, use the $V_{oc}/I_{sc}$ method for MPT.
> 4. Always **state the conditions** (e.g., $R_L = R_{Th}$) before computing $P_{max}$.
> 5. For Millman, **double-check** the polarity/sign of each source voltage.
> 6. Show all algebraic steps — KTU examiners award **step marks** generously for visible work.

---

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Millman's & MPT Theorems**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) Outcome-Based Education framework.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laplace Transforms of Standard Signals and Common Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laplace Transforms of Standard Signals and Common Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of the Laplace Transform

The **Laplace Transform** is a powerful integral transformation that converts a continuous time-domain function $f(t)$ (defined for $t \geq 0$) into a complex frequency-domain function $F(s)$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex variable. It is the single most indispensable analytical tool in the analysis of linear time-invariant (LTI) networks and control systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unilateral Laplace Transform (KTU Standard Definition):**
> 
> $$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$
> 
> where $s$ is the complex frequency variable and $f(t)$ is assumed to be zero for $t < 0$ (causal signal). The lower limit is $0^-$ to capture impulse functions occurring exactly at $t=0$.

The inverse transformation back to the time domain is given by the **Inverse Laplace Transform**:

$$f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j} \int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\, e^{st}\, ds$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Region of Convergence (ROC):**
> The set of all values of $s$ for which the integral $\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)e^{-st}dt$ converges (yields a finite value) is called the **Region of Convergence (ROC)**. For causal, right-sided signals, the ROC is of the form $\text{Re}(s) > \alpha$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "A Translator Between Two Languages"

Imagine you are reading a story in **English** (time domain — every event happens moment by moment). To understand the hidden structure of the story — its themes, harmonies, and underlying currents — you translate it into a **summary in Algebra** (s-domain — a compact algebraic expression). 

- The **English story** is $f(t)$ — noisy, detailed, hard to manipulate.
- The **Algebra summary** is $F(s)$ — clean, differentiable, and solvable using simple polynomial division.
- The **Laplace Transform** is the translator; the **Inverse Laplace Transform** is the translator back.

In network analysis, the differential equation $v(t) = L\frac{di}{dt} + Ri$ becomes the algebraic equation $V(s) = (Ls + R)I(s)$. This single change turns calculus into arithmetic — a breathtaking simplification.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Property |
|:------:|:-------:|:--------------------------|
| $s$ | Complex frequency | $s = \sigma + j\omega$ |
| $\sigma$ | Real part (damping) | Determines exponential growth/decay (Nepers/sec) |
| $j\omega$ | Imaginary part | Represents sinusoidal oscillation (rad/sec) |
| $f(t)$ | Time-domain signal | Real-valued causal function |
| $F(s)$ | Laplace-domain image | Complex-valued rational function of $s$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 emphasizes the ability to (a) write the Laplace transform of standard signals, (b) identify the ROC, and (c) apply these tables in circuit analysis using transfer functions $H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Use the Laplace Transform in Network Theory?

The Laplace Transform is the engineer's "differential equation eraser" because of three operational advantages:

1. **Differential to Algebraic Conversion**: The derivative $\frac{d^n f(t)}{dt^n}$ becomes $s^n F(s)$ (with initial condition terms). Integration becomes division by $s$.
2. **Initial Conditions Baked-In**: Unlike phasor analysis (which assumes zero initial conditions), the Laplace transform **automatically incorporates initial energy** in capacitors and inductors — making transient response analysis natural.
3. **Convolution to Multiplication**: Convolution in time becomes simple multiplication in s-domain — ideal for cascaded systems.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — How the Transform Works

Let us break the operation into five logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Multiplication by an Exponential**: We multiply $f(t)$ by $e^{-st}$. This **suppresses growth** (the $\sigma$ part) and creates **complex oscillation** (the $j\omega$ part). It is like shining a tuned filter on the signal.
- **Step 2 — Integration over Time**: We integrate $f(t)e^{-st}$ from $0^-$ to $\infty$. Integration **averages** the signal, extracting the dominant behavior.
- **Step 3 — Convergence Check**: We find the values of $s$ for which the integral converges — this is the ROC.
- **Step 4 — Output Function**: The result $F(s)$ is a function of $s$ only, expressed as a rational polynomial $\frac{N(s)}{D(s)}$.
- **Step 5 — Inverse Recovery**: To recover $f(t)$ from $F(s)$, we use partial fractions, residue theory, or standard transform tables.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Standard Laplace Transform Pairs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Memorize it to the point of reflex action. Every entry must be in your mental toolkit for the ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Time-Domain Function $f(t)$ | Laplace Transform $F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\}$ | Region of Convergence (ROC) |
|:-:|:---------------------------:|:----------------------------------------------:|:---------------------------:|
| 1 | $\delta(t)$ (Unit Impulse) | $1$ | All $s$ |
| 2 | $u(t)$ (Unit Step) | $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 3 | $t \cdot u(t)$ (Unit Ramp) | $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 4 | $t^{n} \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 5 | $e^{-at} \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 6 | $t \cdot e^{-at} \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 7 | $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 8 | $\cos(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 9 | $e^{-at} \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 10 | $e^{-at} \cos(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |
| 11 | $t \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{2\omega s}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 12 | $t \cos(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s^2 - \omega^2}{(s^2+\omega^2)^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ |
| 13 | $\sinh(at) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |
| 14 | $\cosh(at) \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{s}{s^2 - a^2}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > \vert a \vert$ |
| 15 | $a \cdot e^{-at} \cdot u(t)$ | $\dfrac{a}{s+a}$ | $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note:** The vertical bar $\vert a \vert$ in the ROC of rows 13 and 14 is written using `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table layout.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Laplace transform is the **mathematical backbone** of:

- **Electrical Engineering**: Transfer functions, Bode plots, stability analysis, control system design.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Vibration analysis of damped systems, mass-spring-damper models.
- **Aerospace**: Aircraft control surface dynamics, autopilot stability.
- **Biomedical**: ECG/EEG signal filtering, drug response pharmacokinetics.
- **Robotics**: Joint actuator dynamics, motion planning transfer functions.
- **Communication Systems**: Linear filter analysis, modulator/demodulator blocks.

> [!TIP]
> The damped sinusoid pair (rows 9 and 10) is by far the **most asked** entry in KTU examinations because it represents the natural response of any RLC circuit.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Below are the **exhaustive derivations** for the most critical standard transform pairs. Each algebraic transition is explicitly justified so you can replicate the logic in the exam hall.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Unit Step Function $u(t)$

The unit step function is defined as:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

**Starting with the Laplace integral definition:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} (1) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Since the integrand simplifies to $e^{-st}$ for $t \geq 0$, we evaluate:

$$= \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 2:** The integral of an exponential is its antiderivative:

$$= \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the upper limit. As $t \to \infty$, the term $e^{-st}$ vanishes **only if** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ — this defines the ROC.

$$= 0 - \left( \frac{e^{0}}{-s} \right)$$

$$= -\left(\frac{1}{-s}\right) = \frac{1}{s}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0}$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unit Ramp Function $t \cdot u(t)$

**Starting point:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Use integration by parts: let $u = t$ and $dv = e^{-st}dt$, so $du = dt$ and $v = -\frac{e^{-st}}{s}$.

$$= \left[ t \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) dt$$

**Step 2:** At $t = 0$, the first term is $0$. As $t \to \infty$, the product $t \cdot e^{-st}$ vanishes for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. So the boundary term is zero:

$$= 0 + \frac{1}{s}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the remaining integral (we already know this from Derivation 1):

$$= \frac{1}{s} \cdot \left[ \frac{e^{-st}}{-s} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s} \cdot \left(0 + \frac{1}{s}\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{s^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0}$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Exponential Decay $e^{-at} \cdot u(t)$

**Starting point:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t}\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Combine exponents: $e^{-at} \cdot e^{-st} = e^{-(s+a)t}$.

**Step 2:** Substitute $u = (s+a)t$, so $du = (s+a)dt$:

$$= \frac{1}{s+a}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+a)t} \cdot (s+a)\, dt = \frac{1}{s+a} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u}\, du$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate:

$$= \frac{1}{s+a} \left[ -e^{-u} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{s+a} \cdot (0 + 1) = \frac{1}{s+a}$$

**Step 4:** Convergence requires $\text{Re}(s+a) > 0$, i.e., $\text{Re}(s) > -a$.

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s+a}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > -a}$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sine Function $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$

**Starting point — Euler's expansion:**

$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute into the Laplace integral:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j} \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 2:** Factor and split:

$$= \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s-j\omega)t}\, dt - \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+j\omega)t}\, dt \right]$$

**Step 3:** Apply the exponential transform result $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \frac{1}{s+a}$ with $a = -j\omega$ and $a = j\omega$:

$$= \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Combine over a common denominator:

$$= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega)}{(s - j\omega)(s + j\omega)}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify numerator: $(s + j\omega) - (s - j\omega) = 2j\omega$. Denominator: $s^2 + \omega^2$.

$$= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t) u(t)\} = \dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0}$

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Cosine Function $\cos(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$

**Starting point — Euler's expansion:**

$$\cos(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} + e^{-j\omega t}}{2}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute and integrate:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} + \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

**Step 2:** Common denominator:

$$= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(s + j\omega) + (s - j\omega)}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2s}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t) u(t)\} = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0}$

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Damped Sine $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$

**Step 1:** Express as imaginary part using the exponential we just derived:

$$e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) = \text{Im}\left\{e^{-at} \cdot e^{j\omega t}\right\} = \text{Im}\left\{e^{-(a-j\omega)t}\right\}$$

**Step 2:** The Laplace transform of $e^{-(a-j\omega)t}$ is $\frac{1}{s + a - j\omega}$.

**Step 3:** Apply $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)\}$ directly:

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s + a - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + a + j\omega} \right]$$

**Step 4:** Common denominator $(s+a)^2 + \omega^2$:

$$= \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) u(t)\} = \dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > -a}$

## 3.7 Derivation 7 — Damped Cosine $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$

Following the same procedure as Derivation 6 with cosine:

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)\} = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \frac{1}{s + a - j\omega} + \frac{1}{s + a + j\omega} \right]$$

$$= \frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\cos(\omega t) u(t)\} = \dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > -a}$

## 3.8 Derivation 8 — Unit Impulse $\delta(t)$

The unit impulse $\delta(t)$ is the **Dirac delta** — an idealized pulse of infinite height and zero width with unit area. Its sifting property gives:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \delta(t) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt = e^{-s(0)} = 1$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1, \quad \text{ROC: All } s}$

## 3.9 Derivation 9 — General Power $t^n \cdot u(t)$

Using the **Gamma function identity** $\Gamma(n+1) = n!$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{t^n u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t^n e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 1:** Substitute $u = st$, so $t = u/s$ and $dt = du/s$:

$$= \int_{0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{u}{s}\right)^n e^{-u} \frac{du}{s} = \frac{1}{s^{n+1}} \int_{0}^{\infty} u^n e^{-u}\, du$$

**Step 2:** The integral is the definition of the Gamma function: $\int_{0}^{\infty} u^n e^{-u} du = \Gamma(n+1) = n!$.

$$= \frac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{t^n u(t)\} = \dfrac{n!}{s^{n+1}}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0}$

## 3.10 Derivation 10 — Hyperbolic Sine $\sinh(at) \cdot u(t)$

Using the identity $\sinh(at) = \frac{e^{at} - e^{-at}}{2}$:

$$\mathcal{L}\{\sinh(at)\} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{s-a} - \frac{1}{s+a}\right] = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2a}{s^2 - a^2} = \frac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$$

> **Final Result:** $\boxed{\mathcal{L}\{\sinh(at) u(t)\} = \dfrac{a}{s^2 - a^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > \vert a \vert}$

## 3.11 Comprehensive Verification — Differentiation in Time Domain

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Cross-Check Rule (KTU Examiner's Favourite):**
> 
> - $\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$
> - $\mathcal{L}\{f''(t)\} = s^2 F(s) - sf(0^-) - f'(0^-)$
> 
> **Verification using ramp from step:**
> - We know $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$.
> - Apply differentiation in s-domain: $-\frac{d}{ds}\left(\frac{1}{s}\right) = \frac{1}{s^2}$.
> - The time-differentiation property gives $\mathcal{L}\{t u(t)\}$ via $\mathcal{L}\{t f(t)\} = -\frac{dF(s)}{ds}$.
> - So $t \cdot u(t) \xrightarrow{\mathcal{L}} \frac{1}{s^2}$. ✓ Consistent with Derivation 2.

## 3.12 Analytical Matrix — Key Transform Comparison

| Transform Pair | Distinctive Feature | Common KTU Application |
|:---------------|:-------------------|:-----------------------|
| $u(t) \to \frac{1}{s}$ | Step input response | Testing system DC gain: $H(0)$ |
| $\delta(t) \to 1$ | Impulse response | Transfer function $H(s) = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\}$ |
| $e^{-at} \to \frac{1}{s+a}$ | First-order pole | RC/RL circuit natural response |
| $\sin(\omega t) \to \frac{\omega}{s^2+\omega^2}$ | Imaginary-axis poles | Undamped oscillator response |
| $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) \to \frac{\omega}{(s+a)^2+\omega^2}$ | Stable underdamped | Series RLC transient response |
| $t^n \to \frac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$ | Multiple pole at origin | Polynomial forcing functions |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — The Laplace Transform Bridge

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **structural flow** of how time-domain signals are systematically converted to s-domain images using the standard transform table.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Time Domain Signal f of t] --> B{Apply Laplace Integral L f of t equals integral of f of t times e to the power of minus s t dt}
    B --> C{Identify Function Type}
    C --> D1[Impulse delta of t] --> R1[F of s equals 1]
    C --> D2[Step u of t] --> R2[F of s equals 1 over s]
    C --> D3[Ramp t times u of t] --> R3[F of s equals 1 over s squared]
    C --> D4[Exponential e to the minus a t] --> R4[F of s equals 1 over s plus a]
    C --> D5[Sine omega t] --> R5[F of s equals omega over s squared plus omega squared]
    C --> D6[Cosine omega t] --> R6[F of s equals s over s squared plus omega squared]
    C --> D7[Damped Sine e to the minus a t sine omega t] --> R7[F of s equals omega over open paren s plus a close paren squared plus omega squared]
    C --> D8[Damped Cosine e to the minus a t cosine omega t] --> R8[F of s equals s plus a over open paren s plus a close paren squared plus omega squared]
    R1 --> X[Analyze in s Domain Algebraic Manipulation]
    R2 --> X
    R3 --> X
    R4 --> X
    R5 --> X
    R6 --> X
    R7 --> X
    R8 --> X
    X --> Y[Inverse Laplace L inverse to Recover f of t]
    Y --> Z[Final Time Domain Solution with Initial Conditions]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Signal-to-Image Mapping Hierarchy

The diagram below shows the **functional architecture** of how a typical network analysis problem flows through the Laplace transform pipeline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Time Domain Problem Differential Equation] --> B[Apply Laplace Transform Using Standard Table]
    B --> C[Obtain Algebraic Equation in s Domain A of s times X of s equals B of s]
    C --> D[Solve Algebraically X of s equals B of s over A of s]
    D --> E[Apply Partial Fraction Decomposition if Needed]
    E --> F[Lookup Standard Inverse Pairs in Table]
    F --> G[Combine Time Domain Terms with Initial Conditions]
    G --> H[Final Solution x of t equals Natural plus Forced Response]
```

## 4.3 ROC Visualization Schematic (Pole-Zero Perspective)

> [!TIP]
> The **ROC is always bounded by poles** in the complex s-plane. For right-sided causal signals, the ROC is the region to the **right of the rightmost pole**.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPLANE[Complex s-Plane]
    direction LR
    POLES["Poles at s = -a for e to the minus a t u of t"]
    ROC["ROC Region Re of s greater than minus a"]
    POLES --> ROC
    end
```

For $f(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ with pole at $s = -a$:
- If $a > 0$: Pole is on the negative real axis. ROC is $\text{Re}(s) > -a$ (right half-plane region).
- The pole is the **boundary** of the ROC, never **inside** the ROC.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Pattern:** Whenever a question asks for the ROC, the answer must explicitly state the inequality involving $\text{Re}(s)$ and the pole location. Failing to specify the ROC results in a 1-mark deduction per KTU valuation norms.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Part A consists of short-answer questions that test direct recall of definitions, formulas, or quick applications. Answers should be concise (typically 5–8 lines) but must include all critical details (formula, ROC, conditions).

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the unilateral Laplace transform. State the Region of Convergence (ROC) for the unit step function $u(t)$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **unilateral Laplace transform** of a causal time-domain signal $f(t)$ is defined as:

$$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$

where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is the complex frequency variable. The lower limit is $0^-$ to capture any impulses at $t = 0$.

For the **unit step function** $u(t)$, $f(t) = 1$ for $t \geq 0$, so:

$$\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{s}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > 0$$

The ROC for the unit step function is the **right half of the s-plane** (excluding the origin which is a pole). The integral converges only when $\text{Re}(s) > 0$, ensuring that $e^{-\sigma t} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Write the Laplace transform of $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ and its ROC. Mention one engineering application.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}, \quad \text{ROC: Re}(s) > -a$$

**Derivation Sketch:** Using Euler's identity $\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$ and applying the exponential transform pair $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \frac{1}{s+a}$, we obtain the above result.

**Engineering Application:** This transform pair represents the **transient current response of a series RLC circuit** when a step voltage is applied. The natural (homogeneous) response of any second-order underdamped system always has this mathematical form, making it indispensable in filter design and control system analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split into sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer **one full question** from the choice of two. Marks are distributed across: approach/derivation steps, final answer, ROC, units/dimensions, and neatness.

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Laplace transform of $\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ starting from the definition. State the ROC. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the result of part (a) and the s-domain differentiation property, find the Laplace transform of $t \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ and state the ROC. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**

$$F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \sin(\omega t) \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Express $\sin(\omega t)$ in exponential form:**

$$\sin(\omega t) = \frac{e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t}}{2j}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the integral:**

$$F(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ e^{j\omega t} - e^{-j\omega t} \right] e^{-st}\, dt = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s-j\omega)t}\, dt - \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(s+j\omega)t}\, dt \right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each integral using the standard exponential pair $\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-pt}\, dt = \frac{1}{p}$ for $\text{Re}(p) > 0$:**

$$F(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \left[ \frac{1}{s - j\omega} - \frac{1}{s + j\omega} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Combine over common denominator $(s-j\omega)(s+j\omega) = s^2 + \omega^2$:**

$$F(s) = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{(s+j\omega) - (s-j\omega)}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{1}{2j} \cdot \frac{2j\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$
> 
> **ROC:** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ (since the poles at $s = \pm j\omega$ lie on the imaginary axis, the ROC is the entire right half-plane).

---

#### Model Solution for Question A (b):

**Step 1 — Recall the Frequency Differentiation Property:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot f(t)\} = -\frac{d}{ds}\left[ F(s) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply the property with $F(s) = \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)\} = -\frac{d}{ds}\left[ \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2} \right]$$

**Step 3 — Compute the derivative using the quotient rule:**

$$= -\omega \cdot \frac{d}{ds}\left[ (s^2 + \omega^2)^{-1} \right] = -\omega \cdot (-1)(s^2 + \omega^2)^{-2} \cdot (2s)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$= \omega \cdot \frac{2s}{(s^2 + \omega^2)^2} = \frac{2\omega s}{(s^2 + \omega^2)^2}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{2\omega s}{(s^2 + \omega^2)^2}$
> 
> **ROC:** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$ (same as parent transform).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Starting from first principles, derive the Laplace transform of the unit ramp function $t \cdot u(t)$. State the ROC. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the first shifting theorem (s-domain), find the Laplace transform of $e^{-3t}\cos(2t) \cdot u(t)$. State the ROC. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**
