$$\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply integration by parts** with $u = t$, $dv = e^{-st}dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -\frac{e^{-st}}{s}$:

$$= \left[ t \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} (1) \cdot \left(-\frac{e^{-st}}{s}\right) dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the boundary term.** At $t = 0$: the term is $0 \cdot (-1/s) = 0$. As $t \to \infty$: $t \cdot e^{-st} \to 0$ for $\text{Re}(s) > 0$. So the boundary term vanishes:

$$= 0 + \frac{1}{s}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st}\, dt$$

**Step 4 — The remaining integral is the unit step transform:**

$$= \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{1}{s} = \frac{1}{s^2}$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathcal{L}\{t \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s^2}$
> 
> **ROC:** $\text{Re}(s) > 0$

---

#### Model Solution for Question B (b):

**Step 1 — Recall the First Shifting Theorem (Frequency Shifting):**

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} f(t)\} = F(s+a)$$

In other words, replace every occurrence of $s$ in $F(s)$ with $(s+a)$.

**Step 2 — Base transform — Laplace of $\cos(2t) \cdot u(t)$:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{\cos(2t) \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{s}{s^2 + 4}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the shifting theorem with $a = 3$ — replace $s$ with $(s+3)$:**

$$\mathcal{L}\{e^{-3t}\cos(2t) \cdot u(t)\} = \frac{s+3}{(s+3)^2 + 4}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator for clarity:**

$$(s+3)^2 + 4 = s^2 + 6s + 9 + 4 = s^2 + 6s + 13$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-3t}\cos(2t) \cdot u(t)\} = \dfrac{s+3}{s^2 + 6s + 13}$
> 
> **ROC:** $\text{Re}(s) > -3$ (the rightmost pole is at $s = -3 + j2$ and $s = -3 - j2$, so the ROC extends to the right of $s = -3$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Laplace Transform questions (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the ROC**: A student correctly derives $F(s) = \frac{1}{s}$ for the unit step but writes "ROC = all s" instead of "$\text{Re}(s) > 0$." This results in a **1-mark deduction**. The ROC is **mandatory** in KTU boards.
> 
> 2. **Wrong denominator for damped sinusoid**: Confusing $\frac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ with $\frac{\omega}{s^2 + (a^2 + \omega^2)}$ — a classic algebra slip. Memorize: the **$a$ shifts the entire $(s^2+\omega^2)$ block**, not just the $s$ term.
> 
> 3. **Lower limit confusion**: Writing $0^+$ instead of $0^-$ in the integral definition. The $0^-$ is critical for capturing impulses.
> 
> 4. **Missing the unity in step**: Writing $\mathcal{L}\{\text{step}\} = \frac{1}{s-a}$ instead of $\frac{1}{s}$. The unit step has **no exponential factor**.
> 
> 5. **Poles vs. ROC confusion**: Marking the ROC **inside** the poles. The ROC is always **outside** the rightmost pole for causal right-sided signals.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the substitution step**: In Derivation 4 (sine), students often skip the Euler expansion and write the final formula directly, losing process marks. Always show **at least 3–4 intermediate steps**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 (Laplace Transforms of Standard Signals)**

- **Core Definition**: $\mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-st}\, dt$, where $s = \sigma + j\omega$.
- **Region of Convergence (ROC)**: The set of $s$ for which the transform integral converges. For causal right-sided signals, ROC is to the right of the rightmost pole.
- **Most Critical Transforms** (in order of KTU importance):
  1. $u(t) \to \frac{1}{s}$
  2. $\delta(t) \to 1$
  3. $e^{-at}u(t) \to \frac{1}{s+a}$
  4. $\sin(\omega t)u(t) \to \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$
  5. $\cos(\omega t)u(t) \to \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$
  6. $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)u(t) \to \frac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$
  7. $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)u(t) \to \frac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$
  8. $t \cdot u(t) \to \frac{1}{s^2}$
  9. $t^n \cdot u(t) \to \frac{n!}{s^{n+1}}$
  10. $\sinh(at) \to \frac{a}{s^2 - a^2}$, $\cosh(at) \to \frac{s}{s^2 - a^2}$
- **Frequency Shifting Theorem**: $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at} f(t)\} = F(s+a)$ — replace $s$ with $(s+a)$.
- **Frequency Differentiation**: $\mathcal{L}\{t f(t)\} = -\frac{dF(s)}{ds}$ — useful for $t \cdot \sin$ and $t \cdot \cos$ pairs.
- **Time Differentiation**: $\mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$ — incorporates initial capacitor voltages and inductor currents.
- **Time Integration**: $\mathcal{L}\left\{\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)d\tau\right\} = \frac{F(s)}{s}$.
- **Pole-Zero Insight**: The denominator of $F(s)$ reveals the **natural response** modes of the system. Repeated roots cause $t^n$ terms; complex roots cause damped sinusoids.
- **Engineering Significance**: Laplace transforms convert differential equations of RLC circuits into algebraic equations — making transient and steady-state analysis tractable in a single unified framework.
- **Exam Strategy**: Always state the ROC explicitly, even if the question doesn't ask for it. It costs you nothing and signals mastery to the examiner.
- **Numerical Sanity Check**: For $F(s) = \frac{1}{s}$, the value $F(0) = \infty$ (pole at origin), corresponding to the unit step **growing forever**. For $F(s) = \frac{1}{s+1}$, the inverse $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = e^{-t}u(t)$ which decays — consistent with $\text{Re}(-1) < 0$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laplace Transform Theorems (Proof Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Laplace Transform Theorems (Proof Not Required) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of Laplace Transform Theorems The **Laplace Transform Theorems** constitute a powerful set of operational rules that govern how the Laplace Transform behaves when the time-domain function $f(t)$ undergoes common mathematical operations such as differentiation, integration, shifting, scaling, and convolution. Since the proofs of these theorems are explicitly excluded from the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, the focus is entirely on **statement, application, and problem-solving**. Mathematically, the Laplace Transform of a time-domain function $f(t)$ is defined as: $$L\{f(t)\} = F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ where $s = \sigma + j\omega$ is a complex frequency variable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** For Module 3 of PCECT205, students are required to memorize the theorem statements and apply them directly to network analysis problems (s-domain circuit analysis, transfer functions, transient analysis). Proof derivations will **NOT** appear in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are a **translator** working between two languages — **English (Time Domain)** and **French (s-Domain / Frequency Domain)**. A translation dictionary alone (basic Laplace Transform table) is not enough. You need **grammar rules** (theorems) to handle tricky sentences (functions) that involve: - **Tenses changing** → Differentiation/Integration theorems - **Words shifting in time** → Time Shifting theorem - **Words being whispered louder or softer** → Scaling theorem - **Combining two conversations** → Convolution theorem **Real-world analogy:** Consider a **musical equalizer** in a sound system. - The **time domain** is the raw audio signal (voltage/current waveform). - The **s-domain** is the frequency representation (bass, mid, treble). - The Laplace Transform Theorems are the **knobs and switches** on the equalizer — they tell you precisely how a change in the waveform (volume spike, delay, echo) will modify the frequency content. > [!NOTE] > **Why Theorems Matter in Network Theory:** > 1. They convert differential equations (hard) into algebraic equations (easy). > 2. They handle initial conditions automatically — a major advantage over classical methods. > 3. They allow piecewise/periodic function analysis using shifting theorems. > 4. They form the mathematical backbone of **transfer functions** and **Bode plots**. ## 1.3 Classification of Laplace Transform Theorems (KTU Taxonomy) | Category | Theorems | Engineering Use | |----------|----------|-----------------| | **Operational Theorems** | Linearity, Scaling | Combining circuit elements | | **Shifting Theorems** | Time Shifting, Frequency Shifting | Switched circuits, modulated signals | | **Differential Theorems** | Time Differentiation, s-Differentiation | Inductors, capacitors with initial conditions | | **Integral Theorems** | Time Integration, s-Integration | Charge accumulation, energy functions | | **Limit Theorems** | Initial Value Theorem (IVT), Final Value Theorem (FVT) | Steady-state and transient analysis | | **Composite Theorem** | Convolution Theorem | Cascade systems, impulse response | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Detailed Theorem Breakdown (Logic Steps) ### **Theorem 1: Linearity Theorem** - **Statement:** The Laplace Transform of a linear combination of functions equals the linear combination of their individual Laplace Transforms. - **Logic:** $L\{a f(t) + b g(t)\} = aF(s) + bG(s)$ - **Why it works:** Integration is a linear operator; constants $a$ and $b$ pass through the integral. - **KTU Application:** Superposition principle in s-domain circuits. ### **Theorem 2: Frequency Shifting Theorem (s-Shifting / First Translation)** - **Statement:** Multiplying a time-domain function by $e^{at}$ shifts the s-domain function to $s - a$. - **Logic:** $L\{e^{at} f(t)\} = F(s - a)$ - **Why it works:** Substitution $s \rightarrow s - a$ in the defining integral. - **KTU Application:** Analysis of exponentially growing/decaying sinusoids, damped oscillations. ### **Theorem 3: Time Shifting Theorem (Second Translation)** - **Statement:** A delay of $a$ seconds in time corresponds to multiplication by $e^{-as}$ in the s-domain. - **Logic:** $L\{f(t - a)\, u(t - a)\} = e^{-as} F(s)$, where $u$ is the unit step. - **Why it works:** Changing the lower limit of integration from $0$ to $a$. - **KTU Application:** Switched circuits, delay lines, propagation delays in transmission lines. ### **Theorem 4: Time Reversal Theorem** - **Statement:** Time reversal corresponds to sign change in $s$ in the s-domain. - **Logic:** $L\{f(-t)\} = F(-s)$ (with modification for causal functions). - **KTU Application:** Reciprocal networks, bilateral Laplace analysis. ### **Theorem 5: Time Scaling Theorem** - **Statement:** Compressing/expanding time by factor $a$ scales $s$ inversely. - **Logic:** $L\{f(at)\} = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} F\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)$ - **KTU Application:** Time-base scaling in oscilloscopes, frequency conversion. ### **Theorem 6: Differentiation in Time Domain** - **First Order:** $L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$ - **Second Order:** $L\{f''(t)\} = s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$ - **General (n-th Order):** $L\{f^{(n)}(t)\} = s^n F(s) - \sum_{k=1}^{n} s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0^-)$ - **Why it works:** Integration by parts on the defining integral. - **KTU Application:** Converting KVL/KVL differential equations of RLC circuits to algebraic s-domain equations. **The single most used theorem in Network Theory.** ### **Theorem 7: Integration in Time Domain** - **Statement:** Integration in time corresponds to division by $s$ in the s-domain. - **Logic:** $L\!\left\{\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ - **KTU Application:** Analysis of circuits with integrators (op-amp integrators, capacitor charge). ### **Theorem 8: Differentiation in s-Domain** - **Statement:** Differentiation in s-domain corresponds to multiplication by $-t$ in time domain. - **Logic:** $L\{-t\, f(t)\} = \dfrac{dF(s)}{ds}$ - **General:** $L\{(-t)^n f(t)\} = \dfrac{d^n F(s)}{ds^n}$ - **KTU Application:** Generating transforms of $t\, f(t)$, $t^2 f(t)$ type functions. ### **Theorem 9: Integration in s-Domain** - **Statement:** Division by $t$ in time corresponds to integration over $s$ in s-domain. - **Logic:** $L\!\left\{\dfrac{f(t)}{t}\right\} = \displaystyle\int_{s}^{\infty} F(u)\, du$ - **KTU Application:** Finding inverse transforms of $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ type expressions. ### **Theorem 10: Initial Value Theorem (IVT)** - **Statement:** The initial value of a time-domain function can be obtained directly from its s-domain representation. - **Logic:** $f(0^+) = \lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$ - **Condition:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie in the **left half-plane (LHP)** strictly. - **KTU Application:** Verifying transient response, checking initial conditions in switched circuits. ### **Theorem 11: Final Value Theorem (FVT)** - **Statement:** The steady-state (final) value of a time-domain function can be obtained from its s-domain representation. - **Logic:** $f(\infty) = \lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$ - **Condition:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie in the **strict LHP** except possibly a simple pole at $s = 0$. - **KTU Application:** Finding steady-state error in control systems, DC operating point of circuits. ### **Theorem 12: Convolution Theorem** - **Statement:** Convolution in time domain corresponds to multiplication in s-domain. - **Logic:** $L\{f(t) * g(t)\} = F(s) \cdot G(s)$ - **Where:** $f(t) * g(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau$ - **KTU Application:** Cascade of LTI systems, finding output when impulse responses are known. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Table for KTU 2024 ESE — Memorize This Verbatim** | # | Theorem Name | Time Domain $f(t)$ | s-Domain $F(s)$ | Key Condition | |---|--------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------| | 1 | Linearity | $a f(t) + b g(t)$ | $aF(s) + bG(s)$ | Always valid | | 2 | Frequency Shifting | $e^{at} f(t)$ | $F(s - a)$ | Always valid | | 3 | Time Shifting | $f(t - a)\, u(t - a)$ | $e^{-as} F(s)$ | $a \geq 0$ | | 4 | Time Reversal | $f(-t)$ | $F(-s)$ | For $t > 0$ | | 5 | Time Scaling | $f(at)$ | $\dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} F\!\left(\dfrac{s}{a}\right)$ | $a > 0$ | | 6 | First Derivative | $f'(t)$ | $sF(s) - f(0^-)$ | Always valid | | 7 | Second Derivative | $f''(t)$ | $s^2 F(s) - s f(0^-) - f'(0^-)$ | Always valid | | 8 | n-th Derivative | $f^{(n)}(t)$ | $s^n F(s) - \sum s^{n-k} f^{(k-1)}(0^-)$ | Always valid | | 9 | Time Integration | $\int_0^t f(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $\dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ | Always valid | | 10 | s-Differentiation | $t\, f(t)$ | $-\dfrac{dF(s)}{ds}$ | Always valid | | 11 | s-Integration | $\dfrac{f(t)}{t}$ | $\int_s^{\infty} F(u)\, du$ | Always valid | | 12 | Initial Value (IVT) | $f(0^+)$ | $\lim\limits_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$ | All poles in strict LHP | | 13 | Final Value (FVT) | $f(\infty)$ | $\lim\limits_{s \to 0} sF(s)$ | All poles in strict LHP (or simple at origin) | | 14 | Convolution | $f(t) * g(t)$ | $F(s) \cdot G(s)$ | For causal LTI systems | ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Domain | Theorem Application | |--------------------|---------------------| | **Power Systems** | IVT/FVT for fault analysis, switching transients | | **Control Systems** | Convolution theorem for cascade compensator design | | **Signal Processing** | Frequency shifting for AM modulation/demodulation | | **Communications** | Time shifting for echo cancellation in telephony | | **VLSI Design** | s-Differentiation for parasitic delay modeling | | **Biomedical Engineering** | Convolution for ECG signal filtering | | **Aerospace** | FVT for steady-state response of autopilot systems | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: First Differentiation Theorem **Starting from the Laplace Transform definition:** $$F(s) = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 1:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ inside the integral and use integration by parts on: $$I = \int_{0}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Apply integration by parts with $u = e^{-st}$ and $dv = f'(t)\, dt$: $$I = \left[ f(t)\, e^{-st} \right]_{0}^{\infty} - \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, (-s e^{-st})\, dt$$ **Step 3:** Simplify the boundary term. Since $f(t)$ is assumed to be of exponential order, $f(t)\, e^{-st} \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$: $$I = \left[ 0 - f(0) \right] + s \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the remaining integral as $F(s)$: $$L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why $0^-$ and not $0^+$?** KTU examiners prefer the lower limit notation $0^-$ because it captures initial conditions **just before** a switching event (e.g., current through an inductor, voltage across a capacitor). This is the convention used in modern Network Theory texts. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Initial Value Theorem (IVT) **Starting Point:** Apply the first differentiation theorem: $$L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-) = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 1:** Take the limit as $s \to \infty$ on both sides: $$\lim_{s \to \infty} \left[ sF(s) - f(0^-) \right] = \lim_{s \to \infty} \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Evaluate the right-hand side. As $s \to \infty$, the exponential $e^{-st}$ forces the integrand to zero everywhere except at $t = 0$: $$\lim_{s \to \infty} \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = 0$$ **Step 3:** This gives: $$\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) - f(0^-) = 0$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging: $$\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = f(0^-) \approx f(0^+)$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Final Value Theorem (FVT) **Starting Point:** Apply the first differentiation theorem: $$\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = sF(s) - f(0^-)$$ **Step 1:** Take the limit as $s \to 0$ on both sides: $$\lim_{s \to 0} \int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, e^{-st}\, dt = \lim_{s \to 0} \left[ sF(s) - f(0^-) \right]$$ **Step 2:** When $s \to 0$, $e^{-st} \to 1$, so the left-hand side becomes: $$\int_{0^-}^{\infty} f'(t)\, dt = f(\infty) - f(0^-)$$ **Step 3:** Equating both sides: $$f(\infty) - f(0^-) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) - f(0^-)$$ **Step 4:** The $f(0^-)$ terms cancel: **Final Result:** $$\boxed{f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)}$$ > [!WARNING] > **FVT Validity Condition (Frequently Tested in KTU):** > The poles of $sF(s)$ **must** all lie in the open left half-plane. If there is a pole on the imaginary axis (other than origin) or in the right half-plane, FVT gives a **wrong answer**. Always check the pole locations before applying FVT. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Convolution Theorem **Starting Point:** Define the convolution integral: $$f(t) * g(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$ **Step 1:** Take the Laplace Transform: $$L\{f(t) * g(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau \right] e^{-st}\, dt$$ **Step 2:** Change the order of integration. The region of integration in the $(\tau, t)$ plane is $0 \leq \tau \leq t < \infty$, which is equivalent to $0 \leq \tau < \infty$ and $\tau \leq t < \infty$: $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau) \left[ \int_{\tau}^{\infty} g(t - \tau)\, e^{-st}\, dt \right] d\tau$$ **Step 3:** Substitute $u = t - \tau$ in the inner integral, so $du = dt$ and $e^{-st} = e^{-s(u + \tau)}$: $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau) \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} g(u)\, e^{-s(u + \tau)}\, du \right] d\tau$$ **Step 4:** Separate the exponentials: $$= \int_{0}^{\infty} f(\tau)\, e^{-s\tau}\, d\tau \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} g(u)\, e^{-su}\, du$$ **Step 5:** Recognize both integrals as Laplace Transforms: **Final Result:** $$\boxed{L\{f(t) * g(t)\} = F(s) \cdot G(s)}$$ ## 3.5 Worked Example: Applying Multiple Theorems (KTU Pattern) **Problem:** Find the inverse Laplace Transform of: $$F(s) = \frac{s}{(s+2)(s+3)^2}$$ **Step 1:** Apply partial fraction decomposition: $$F(s) = \frac{A}{s+2} + \frac{B}{s+3} + \frac{C}{(s+3)^2}$$ **Step 2:** Compute residues. For $A$, multiply by $(s+2)$ and set $s = -2$: $$A = \frac{-2}{(-2+3)^2} = \frac{-2}{1} = -2$$ For $C$, multiply by $(s+3)^2$ and set $s = -3$: $$C = \frac{-3}{(-3+2)} = \frac{-3}{-1} = 3$$ For $B$, differentiate or use cover-up. Equate coefficients of $s^2$: $$0 = A + B \implies B = -A = 2$$ **Step 3:** Therefore: $$F(s) = \frac{-2}{s+2} + \frac{2}{s+3} + \frac{3}{(s+3)^2}$$ **Step 4:** Apply inverse Laplace using standard pairs and the **frequency shifting theorem**: $$L^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{s+a}\right\} = e^{-at} u(t)$$ $$L^{-1}\left\{\frac{1}{(s+a)^2}\right\} = t\, e^{-at} u(t)$$ **Step 5:** Combine the results using the **linearity theorem**: **Final Result:** $$f(t) = \left[ -2 e^{-2t} + 2 e^{-3t} + 3 t\, e^{-3t} \right] u(t)$$ **Step 6 (Verification using IVT):** $$\lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{s^2}{(s+2)(s+3)^2} = 1$$ At $t = 0^+$: $f(0^+) = -2 + 2 + 0 = 0$. The result $0$ should equal $\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = 1$. > [!WARNING] > **Trap Alert:** The IVT result of $1$ does not match $f(0^+) = 0$? Let's recheck. As $t \to 0^+$, the dominant term in $f(t)$ is the $3t e^{-3t}$ term. For very small $t$, $3t e^{-3t} \approx 3t$, so $f(0^+) = 0$ indeed. Re-checking IVT: $\lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = 1$. This suggests an arithmetic check — let's verify the decomposition: > $\frac{-2}{s+2} + \frac{2}{s+3} + \frac{3}{(s+3)^2}$: combine over common denominator gives $\frac{s^2(1) + \ldots}{(s+2)(s+3)^2}$. The correct IVT value should be the coefficient of $s^0 / s^1$ ratio. For **proper** $F(s)$ with denominator degree $3$ and numerator degree $1$, $\lim sF(s) = 0$. We made an IVT arithmetic slip — the correct IVT gives $0$, matching $f(0^+) = 0$. ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Taxonomy of Laplace Transform Theorems ```mermaid graph TD A[Laplace Transform Theorems] --> B[Operational] A --> C[Shifting] A --> D[Differential] A --> E[Integral] A --> F[Limit] A --> G[Composite] B --> B1[Linearity] B --> B2[Scaling] C --> C1[Frequency Shift s-a] C --> C2[Time Shift e-as] D --> D1[Time Diff snF s] D --> D2[s-Diff dF ds] E --> E1[Time Int F s / s] E --> E2[s-Int Integral F u du] F --> F1[IVT lim s to infty] F --> F2[FVT lim s to 0] G --> G1[Convolution F s times G s] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Functional Flow — From Time Domain to s-Domain Application ```mermaid graph LR A[Time Domain Function f t] --> B[Apply Laplace Theorems] B --> C[s-Domain F s] C --> D[Algebraic Manipulation] D --> E[Partial Fractions] E --> F[Inverse Laplace Transform] F --> G[Time Domain Solution y t] H[Initial Conditions f 0] --> B I[Circuit Elements R L C] --> A J[KVL KCL Equations] --> A ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Theorem Selection Decision Tree (KTU Exam Strategy) ```mermaid graph TD Start{Type of Operation on f t} -->|Add/Subtract functions| Op1[Linearity Theorem] Start -->|Multiply by exponential| Op2[Frequency Shifting] Start -->|Delay by a seconds| Op3[Time Shifting] Start -->|Derivative wrt t| Op4[Time Differentiation] Start -->|Integral wrt t| Op5[Time Integration] Start -->|Multiply by t to power n| Op6[s-Domain Differentiation] Start -->|Need f 0 plus| Op7[Initial Value Theorem] Start -->|Need f infinity| Op8[Final Value Theorem] Start -->|Cascade of systems| Op9[Convolution Theorem] Op4 --> Check{Initial Conditions?} Check -->|Given| Apply1[Use full formula with f 0] Check -->|Zero| Apply2[Use simplified sn F s] Op7 --> PoleCheck1{Poles of sF s in LHP?} PoleCheck1 -->|Yes| Valid1[IVT is Valid] PoleCheck1 -->|No| Invalid1[IVT NOT Valid] Op8 --> PoleCheck2{Poles of sF s in strict LHP?} PoleCheck2 -->|Yes| Valid2[FVT is Valid] PoleCheck2 -->|No| Invalid2[FVT NOT Valid] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: s-Domain Circuit Element Equivalents (Block View) ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Time Domain A1[Resistor R] --- A2[Inductor L] A2 --- A3[Capacitor C] end subgraph s-Domain B1[Impedance R] --- B2[Impedance sL with Initial Condition Li 0] B2 --- B3[Impedance 1 sC with Initial Condition v 0 over s] end A1 -->|Laplace| B1 A2 -->|Laplace| B2 A3 -->|Laplace| B3 Note[Time Differentiation Theorem
is the Key Bridge] -.-> A2 Note -.-> A3 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) ### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]** **State and explain the Initial Value Theorem and Final Value Theorem of Laplace Transform. Mention the conditions for their validity.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Initial Value Theorem (IVT):** $$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s F(s)$$ It gives the value of $f(t)$ at $t = 0^+$ directly from the s-domain function $F(s)$, without performing inverse Laplace Transform. **Final Value Theorem (FVT):** $$f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s F(s)$$ It gives the steady-state value of $f(t)$ as $t \to \infty$ directly from $F(s)$. **Conditions of Validity:** - **IVT:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie strictly in the **left half-plane (LHP)**. - **FVT:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie strictly in the **LHP** except possibly a simple pole at the origin ($s = 0$). --- ### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]** **State the Convolution Theorem. Mention its significance in network analysis.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Convolution Theorem:** The Laplace Transform of the convolution of two time-domain functions equals the product of their individual Laplace Transforms. $$L\{f(t) * g(t)\} = F(s) \cdot G(s)$$ where $f(t) * g(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\, g(t - \tau)\, d\tau$. **Significance in Network Analysis:** 1. The output of a **cascade of LTI systems** can be obtained by simply multiplying the transfer functions in the s-domain. 2. It provides a direct way to compute the **zero-state response** of an LTI system from its impulse response. 3. It eliminates the need to perform **convolution integration in the time domain**, replacing it with simple multiplication in the s-domain. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (Module Internal Choice: 14 Marks) ### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Pattern]** **Q. (a)** State the Laplace Transform theorems for: **(i)** First-order differentiation in time domain **(ii)** Integration in time domain **(iii)** Frequency shifting Use these theorems to find the Laplace Transform of: $$f(t) = t^2 e^{-3t} \cos(5t)\, u(t)$$ **[(i), (ii), (iii): 3 Marks; Main Problem: 4 Marks = Total 7 Marks]** **Model Solution:** **Theorems (3 Marks):** - **(i) First-Order Differentiation:** $L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$ - **(ii) Time Integration:** $L\!\left\{\int_0^t f(\tau)\, d\tau\right\} = \dfrac{F(s)}{s}$ - **(iii) Frequency Shifting:** $L\{e^{at} f(t)\} = F(s - a)$ **Main Problem (4 Marks):** **Step 1:** Start with the basic function $g(t) = \cos(5t)\, u(t)$. Its Laplace Transform is: $$G(s) = \frac{s}{s^2 + 25}$$ **Step 2:** To get $t^2 \cos(5t)$, apply the **s-differentiation theorem** twice: $$L\{t\, g(t)\} = -\frac{dG(s)}{ds} = -\frac{(s^2 + 25) - s(2s)}{(s^2 + 25)^2} = \frac{25 - s^2}{(s^2 + 25)^2}$$ **Step 3:** Apply s-differentiation again for $t^2 g(t)$: $$L\{t^2\, g(t)\} = -\frac{d}{ds}\left[\frac{25 - s^2}{(s^2 + 25)^2}\right]$$ Using the quotient rule: $$= -\frac{(-2s)(s^2 + 25)^2 - (25 - s^2) \cdot 2(s^2 + 25)(2s)}{(s^2 + 25)^4}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify the numerator: $$= -\frac{2s(s^2 + 25) \left[ -(s^2 + 25) - 2(25 - s^2) \right]}{(s^2 + 25)^4}$$ $$= -\frac{2s \left[ -s^2 - 25 - 50 + 2s^2 \right]}{(s^2 + 25)^3}$$ $$= -\frac{2s(s^2 - 75)}{(s^2 + 25)^3}$$ **Step 5:** Apply the **frequency shifting theorem** with $a = -3$ (i.e., substitute $s \to s + 3$): **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{F(s) = L\{t^2 e^{-3t} \cos(5t)\} = -\frac{2(s+3)\left[(s+3)^2 - 75\right]}{\left[(s+3)^2 + 25\right]^3}}$$ --- **Q. (b)** For a system with transfer function: $$H(s) = \frac{10(s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)}$$ Determine: **(i)** The initial value $y(0^+)$ **(ii)** The final value $y(\infty)$ **(iii)** Comment on the stability of the system. **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the poles of $H(s)$.** Poles are at $s = 0$, $s = -1$, $s = -3$. All poles have non-positive real parts, with a simple pole at origin. **Step 2: Compute $y(0^+)$ using IVT.** $$y(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} s H(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10 s (s+2)}{s(s+1)(s+3)} = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$$ $$y(0^+) = \frac{10 \cdot \infty \text{ (leading)}}{1 \cdot \infty^2 \text{ (denom)}} = 0$$ **Step 3: Compute $y(\infty)$ using FVT.** Check validity: Poles of $sH(s) = \dfrac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)}$ are at $s = -1, -3$, both in LHP. FVT is **valid**. $$y(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} s H(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s+2)}{(s+1)(s+3)} = \frac{10 \times 2}{1 \times 3} = \frac{20}{3}$$ **Step 4: Stability comment.** Pole at $s = 0$ (origin) indicates the system is **marginally stable** (neither strictly stable nor unstable). The output settles to a constant value (confirmed by FVT), indicating bounded output for bounded input, but is not asymptotically stable. **Final Answers:** - $y(0^+) = 0$ - $y(\infty) = \dfrac{20}{3}$ - System is **marginally stable**. --- ### **Question B: [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]** **Q. (a)** State and explain the **Time Shifting Theorem** and **Frequency Shifting Theorem** of Laplace Transform. Using these theorems, find the Laplace Transform of the following waveform described as: $$f(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 2 \\ e^{-(t-2)}, & t \geq 2 \end{cases}$$ **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Theorems (2 Marks).** **Time Shifting Theorem:** $$L\{f(t - a)\, u(t - a)\} = e^{-as} F(s)$$ **Frequency Shifting Theorem:** $$L\{e^{at} f(t)\} = F(s - a)$$ **Step 2: Rewrite $f(t)$ using unit step (1 Mark).** The function $f(t) = e^{-(t-2)}$ is the standard exponential $e^{-t}$ delayed by $a = 2$ seconds: $$f(t) = e^{-(t-2)}\, u(t - 2)$$ **Step 3: Apply Time Shifting Theorem (2 Marks).** We know $L\{e^{-t}\, u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s + 1}$, so $F(s) = \dfrac{1}{s+1}$. Using the Time Shifting Theorem with $a = 2$: $$L\{f(t)\} = e^{-2s} \cdot \frac{1}{s+1}$$ **Final Answer (2 Marks):** $$\boxed{F(s) = \frac{e^{-2s}}{s+1}}$$ --- **Q. (b)** Find the inverse Laplace Transform of: $$F(s) = \frac{1}{s^2(s+2)} + \frac{3}{s^2 + 4s + 13}$$ **[(7 Marks)]** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Decompose into two simpler terms.** $$F(s) = F_1(s) + F_2(s)$$ where $F_1(s) = \dfrac{1}{s^2(s+2)}$ and $F_2(s) = \dfrac{3}{s^2 + 4s + 13}$. **Step 2: Partial fractions for $F_1(s)$ (2 Marks).** $$\frac{1}{s^2(s+2)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s^2} + \frac{C}{s+2}$$ Multiplying by $s^2(s+2)$: $$1 = A s(s+2) + B(s+2) + C s^2$$ - Set $s = 0$: $1 = 2B \implies B = \dfrac{1}{2}$ - Set $s = -2$: $1 = 4C \implies C = \dfrac{1}{4}$ - Compare coefficient of $s^2$: $0 = A + C \implies A = -\dfrac{1}{4}$ $$F_1(s) = -\frac{1}{4s} + \frac{1}{2s^2} + \frac{1}{4(s+2)}$$ **Step 3: Complete the square for $F_2(s)$ (1 Mark).** $$s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s+2)^2 + 9$$ $$F_2(s) = \frac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}$$ **Step 4: Apply inverse Laplace (3 Marks).** - $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s}\right\} = u(t)$ - $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s^2}\right\} = t\, u(t)$ - $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s+2}\right\} = e^{-2t} u(t)$ - $L^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{3}{(s+2)^2 + 3^2}\right\} = e^{-2t} \sin(3t)\, u(t)$ (using frequency shift) **Final Answer:** $$\boxed{f(t) = \left[ -\frac{1}{4} + \frac{t}{2} + \frac{e^{-2t}}{4} + e^{-2t} \sin(3t) \right] u(t)}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 ESE:** > 1. **Forgetting the unit step $u(t)$** in final answers: For a causal function, $f(t)$ should always be multiplied by $u(t)$. (-1 Mark) > 2. **IVT/FVT applied without checking pole locations**: Always verify that poles of $sF(s)$ are in the LHP before applying these theorems. (-2 Marks if ignored) > 3. **Wrong sign in time differentiation theorem**: $L\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0^-)$, NOT $f(0^+) - sF(s)$. Sign error is the #1 cause of full-mark loss. (-7 Marks potential) > 4. **Confusing Time Shifting with Frequency Shifting**: $f(t-a)$ uses $e^{-as}$, while $e^{at}f(t)$ uses $F(s-a)$. Mixing these up loses 3-4 marks. > 5. **Forgetting the modulus in scaling theorem**: $L\{f(at)\} = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert} F(s/a)$. Always include $\vert a \vert$ in the coefficient. > 6. **Convolution domain error**: Convolution in time is multiplication in s-domain, NOT the other way around. > 7. **Neglecting initial conditions in s-domain impedances**: For inductors use $sL$ with a voltage source $Li(0^-)$, and for capacitors use $1/(sC)$ with a voltage source $v(0^-)/s$. Missing this loses 2-3 marks per circuit. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE** - **12 Core Theorems** to master: Linearity, Scaling, Time Shift, Frequency Shift, Time Differentiation (1st, 2nd, n-th), Time Integration, s-Differentiation, s-Integration, IVT, FVT, Convolution. - **Most-used theorem in Network Theory:** First-order **Time Differentiation Theorem** — converts inductor/capacitor equations to algebraic form. - **Time Differentiation General Formula:** Always write $L\{f^{(n)}(t)\} = s^n F(s) - s^{n-1}f(0^-) - s^{n-2}f'(0^-) - \ldots - f^{(n-1)}(0^-)$. - **IVT formula:** $f(0^+) = \lim\limits_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$. **Pole condition:** All poles of $sF(s)$ in strict LHP. - **FVT formula:** $f(\infty) = \lim\limits_{s \to 0} sF(s)$. **Pole condition:** All poles of $sF(s)$ in strict LHP, or at most a simple pole at origin. - **Time Shifting:** Replace $t \to t-a$ in $f(t)$ → multiply $F(s)$ by $e^{-as}$. - **Frequency Shifting:** Multiply $f(t)$ by $e^{at}$ → replace $s$ with $s-a$ in $F(s)$. - **Convolution:** $L\{f * g\} = F(s) G(s)$ — the cascade of two LTI systems. - **s-Differentiation shortcut:** Multiplying by $t$ in time domain = $-\dfrac{d}{ds}$ in s-domain. - **s-Integration:** Dividing by $t$ in time domain = $\int_s^{\infty} F(u)\, du$ in s-domain. - **Initial conditions:** Always use $0^-$ (just before switching) for the differentiation theorem, and $0^+$ for the IVT. - **Stability check using FVT:** If FVT exists and is finite, the system is **stable**. - **Quick mnemonic — "TISE-TIFT"**: **T**ime diff, **I**ntegration time, **S**hift time, **E**valuation (IVT/FVT) — **T**ime convolution, **I**nverse, **F**req diff, **T**ransform.

topics.title: Inverse Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Inverse Laplace Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is a mathematical operator that recovers a time-domain function $f(t)$ from its corresponding frequency-domain (or s-domain) representation $F(s)$. It is the reverse operation of the forward (direct) Laplace Transform. The standard mathematical notation used by KTU examiners is:

$$\boxed{\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = f(t)}$$

The **Bromwich integral** (complex inversion formula) is the rigorous mathematical definition:

$$f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi j}\int_{\sigma - j\infty}^{\sigma + j\infty} F(s)\,e^{st}\,ds, \quad t \geq 0$$

where $\sigma$ is a real number chosen such that the contour of integration lies entirely to the right of all singularities (poles) of $F(s)$ in the complex s-plane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The inverse Laplace transform is the unique time-domain signal $f(t)$ defined for $t \geq 0$ whose unilateral Laplace transform equals $F(s)$. The **uniqueness theorem** guarantees that if two functions have the same $F(s)$, they are identical for $t \geq 0$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Laplace Transform as a **linguistic translator** that converts a function from "Time-Language" (e.g., $f(t) = e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$) into "Frequency-Language" (e.g., $F(s) = \dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$). The frequency-language is often much easier to manipulate, especially when solving differential equations of RLC circuits.

The **Inverse Laplace Transform** is simply the **reverse translator** that takes the solved frequency-domain answer and brings it back into time-domain so engineers can observe *what physically happens* to voltages and currents in the real world.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering context:** In network analysis, you convert an RLC circuit's integro-differential equation into a simple algebraic equation in s-domain, solve for $I(s)$ or $V(s)$, and then apply $\mathcal{L}^{-1}$ to find $i(t)$ or $v(t)$ — the actual current/voltage flowing at time $t$.

## 1.3 The Standard Inverse Transform Pairs (Recall Table)

These are the **fundamental building blocks** every KTU student must memorize:

| $F(s)$ | $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $\dfrac{1}{s}$ | $1$ (unit step) |
| $\dfrac{1}{s+a}$ | $e^{-at}$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{s^2}$ | $t$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{s^n}$ | $\dfrac{t^{n-1}}{(n-1)!}$ |
| $\dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\sin(\omega t)$ |
| $\dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$ | $\cos(\omega t)$ |
| $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$ |
| $\dfrac{s+a}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2}$ | $e^{-at}\cos(\omega t)$ |
| $\dfrac{1}{(s+a)^2}$ | $t\,e^{-at}$ |
| $\dfrac{2\omega s}{(s^2 + \omega^2)^2}$ | $t\sin(\omega t)$ |
| $\dfrac{s^2 - \omega^2}{(s^2 + \omega^2)^2}$ | $t\cos(\omega t)$ |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Finding Inverse Laplace Transforms

There are three primary techniques taught in KTU Module 3:

### Method 1: Direct Table Lookup (with Linear Combination)
If $F(s)$ can be expressed as a linear combination of standard entries, use the **Linearity Property**:

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{aF_1(s) + bF_2(s)\} = a\,f_1(t) + b\,f_2(t)$$

### Method 2: Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE) — The Most Important Method for KTU
Most $F(s)$ arising from RLC circuits are **rational functions**:

$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{b_m s^m + b_{m-1}s^{m-1} + \dots + b_0}{a_n s^n + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \dots + a_0}$$

The PFE splits $F(s)$ into a sum of simpler terms whose inverse transforms are in the table.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proper vs. Improper Fractions:**
> - If $m < n$: $F(s)$ is **proper** — proceed directly with PFE.
> - If $m \geq n$: $F(s)$ is **improper** — first perform polynomial long division to obtain $Q(s) + \dfrac{R(s)}{D(s)}$, then apply PFE only to the remainder $R(s)/D(s)$.

### Method 3: Use of Theorems (Shifting, Multiplication/Division by $t$, etc.)

## 2.2 Classification of Poles and Corresponding PFE Terms

| Pole Type | Factor in $D(s)$ | PFE Term | Inverse Transform |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simple real pole** | $(s + a)$ | $\dfrac{A}{s+a}$ | $A\,e^{-at}$ |
| **Repeated real pole** $(r$ times$)$ | $(s + a)^r$ | $\dfrac{A_1}{s+a} + \dfrac{A_2}{(s+a)^2} + \dots + \dfrac{A_r}{(s+a)^r}$ | $e^{-at}\left(A_1 + A_2 t + \dots + \dfrac{A_r\, t^{r-1}}{(r-1)!}\right)$ |
| **Simple complex pair** | $s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2$ | $\dfrac{As + B}{s^2 + 2\zeta\omega_n s + \omega_n^2}$ | Exponential sinusoid |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Inverse Transform Theorems

> [!NOTE]
> The following theorems are **favorite KTU Part B question areas**. Master each one.

### Theorem 1: First Shifting Theorem (Frequency Shifting / s-Shifting)

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s - a)\} = e^{at}\,f(t)$$

where $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}$ and $a$ is a real constant.

### Theorem 2: Second Shifting Theorem (Time Shifting / t-Shifting)

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{e^{-as}F(s)\} = f(t - a)\,u(t - a)$$

where $u(t - a)$ is the unit step function shifted to the right by $a$ seconds.

### Theorem 3: Differentiation in s-Domain

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\frac{d^n F(s)}{ds^n}\right\} = (-1)^n t^n f(t)$$

Equivalently: $\mathcal{L}\{t^n f(t)\} = (-1)^n \dfrac{d^n F(s)}{ds^n}$.

### Theorem 4: Integration in s-Domain

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\int_s^\infty F(u)\,du\right\} = \frac{f(t)}{t}$$

### Theorem 5: Multiplication by $t^n$ (Time Multiplication)

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{(-1)^n \frac{d^n F(s)}{ds^n}\right\} = t^n f(t)$$

### Theorem 6: Division by $t$ (Frequency Integration)

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\int_s^\infty F(\sigma)\,d\sigma\right\} = \frac{f(t)}{t}$$

### Theorem 7: Convolution Theorem (Real-Product Form)

$$\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s)\} = f_1(t) * f_2(t) = \int_0^t f_1(\tau)\,f_2(t - \tau)\,d\tau$$

## 2.4 Initial and Final Value Theorems (Highly Tested)

> [!CAUTION]
> These theorems allow you to find $f(0^+)$ and $f(\infty)$ directly from $F(s)$ — without performing the full inverse transform. KTU examiners love questions that test the **limit conditions** under which these theorems are valid.

### Initial Value Theorem (IVT)

$$f(0^+) = \lim_{t \to 0^+} f(t) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s)$$

**Validity Condition:** $F(s)$ must be a **strictly proper** rational function (i.e., degree of numerator $<$ degree of denominator) and must have **no poles on or to the right of the $j\omega$ axis** (no poles with $\text{Re}(s) \geq 0$, except possibly a single pole at the origin).

### Final Value Theorem (FVT)

$$f(\infty) = \lim_{t \to \infty} f(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s)$$

**Validity Condition:** All poles of $sF(s)$ must lie in the **open left-half plane** (i.e., $\text{Re}(s) < 0$). If $F(s)$ has any pole on the $j\omega$ axis (e.g., $\frac{1}{s}$), the FVT **fails**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application of $\mathcal{L}^{-1}$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Power Systems** | Recovering fault currents and transient over-voltages from s-domain network equations |
| **Control Systems** | Converting transfer functions $T(s)$ back to step/impulse responses $c(t)$ |
| **Signal Processing** | Recovering filtered signals from s-domain filter transfer functions |
| **Communication** | Solving for transient response of transmission lines and matching networks |
| **Analog Electronics** | Time-domain analysis of op-amp circuits with RLC loads |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Partial Fraction Expansion Coefficients

Consider a strictly proper rational function:

$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{(s - p_1)(s - p_2)\cdots(s - p_n)}$$

where $p_1, p_2, \dots, p_n$ are distinct poles (no repetitions).

### Derivation Step 1: Set Up the PFE Form

$$F(s) = \frac{N(s)}{D(s)} = \frac{K_1}{s - p_1} + \frac{K_2}{s - p_2} + \cdots + \frac{K_n}{s - p_n}$$

### Derivation Step 2: Multiply Both Sides by $D(s)$

$$N(s) = K_1(s - p_2)(s - p_3)\cdots(s - p_n) + K_2(s - p_1)(s - p_3)\cdots(s - p_n) + \cdots + K_n(s - p_1)(s - p_2)\cdots(s - p_{n-1})$$

### Derivation Step 3: Apply the "Cover-Up" Method

Evaluate at $s = p_k$ to isolate $K_k$ (since all other terms vanish):

$$K_k = \left[(s - p_k) F(s)\right]_{s = p_k} = \left.\frac{N(s)}{D(s)/(s - p_k)}\right|_{s = p_k}$$

### Derivation Step 4: For Repeated Roots

For a pole of order $r$ at $s = p$, the coefficient of $\dfrac{1}{(s - p)^j}$ is:

$$K_{p,j} = \frac{1}{(r - j)!} \lim_{s \to p} \frac{d^{r-j}}{ds^{r-j}}\left[(s - p)^r F(s)\right]$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: PFE with Distinct Real Poles

> **Problem:** Find $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{2s + 5}{s^2 + 5s + 6}\right\}$

### Solution

**Step 1: Factor the denominator.**

$$s^2 + 5s + 6 = (s + 2)(s + 3)$$

**Step 2: Verify proper fraction.** Degree of numerator (1) $<$ Degree of denominator (2). Proceed.

**Step 3: Write the PFE form.**

$$\frac{2s + 5}{(s + 2)(s + 3)} = \frac{A}{s + 2} + \frac{B}{s + 3}$$

**Step 4: Apply cover-up method for $A$.**

$$A = \left[(s + 2) \cdot \frac{2s + 5}{(s + 2)(s + 3)}\right]_{s = -2} = \left.\frac{2s + 5}{s + 3}\right|_{s = -2} = \frac{2(-2) + 5}{-2 + 3} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 5: Apply cover-up method for $B$.**

$$B = \left[(s + 3) \cdot \frac{2s + 5}{(s + 2)(s + 3)}\right]_{s = -3} = \left.\frac{2s + 5}{s + 2}\right|_{s = -3} = \frac{2(-3) + 5}{-3 + 2} = \frac{-1}{-1} = 1$$

**Step 6: Combine and take inverse transform term-by-term.**

$$F(s) = \frac{1}{s + 2} + \frac{1}{s + 3}$$

**Step 7: Apply standard pair $\dfrac{1}{s+a} \leftrightarrow e^{-at}$.**

$$f(t) = e^{-2t} + e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: PFE with Repeated Real Poles

> **Problem:** Find $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{s + 4}{(s + 2)^2 (s + 3)}\right\}$

### Solution

**Step 1: Write the PFE form for repeated pole.**

$$F(s) = \frac{A}{s + 2} + \frac{B}{(s + 2)^2} + \frac{C}{s + 3}$$

**Step 2: Find $B$ using cover-up at $s = -2$ (only $(s+2)^2$ remains in denominator).**

$$B = \left[(s + 2)^2 \cdot F(s)\right]_{s = -2} = \left.\frac{s + 4}{s + 3}\right|_{s = -2} = \frac{-2 + 4}{-2 + 3} = \frac{2}{1} = 2$$

**Step 3: Find $C$ using cover-up at $s = -3$.**

$$C = \left[(s + 3) \cdot F(s)\right]_{s = -3} = \left.\frac{s + 4}{(s + 2)^2}\right|_{s = -3} = \frac{-3 + 4}{(-3 + 2)^2} = \frac{1}{1} = 1$$

**Step 4: Find $A$ by equating coefficients or substituting a convenient value.**

Multiply out:

$$s + 4 = A(s + 2)(s + 3) + B(s + 3) + C(s + 2)^2$$

Substitute $s = -2$:

$$-2 + 4 = 0 + B(-2 + 3) + C(0) \Rightarrow 2 = B(1) = 2 \checkmark$$

Substitute $s = 0$:

$$0 + 4 = A(2)(3) + B(3) + C(2)^2 = 6A + 3(2) + 4(1)$$

$$4 = 6A + 6 + 4 \Rightarrow 6A = -6 \Rightarrow A = -1$$

**Step 5: Combine.**

$$F(s) = \frac{-1}{s + 2} + \frac{2}{(s + 2)^2} + \frac{1}{s + 3}$$

**Step 6: Take inverse transform.**

$$f(t) = -e^{-2t} + 2t\,e^{-2t} + e^{-3t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: First Shifting Theorem Application

> **Problem:** Find $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{3}{s^2 + 4s + 13}\right\}$

### Solution

**Step 1: Complete the square in the denominator.**

$$s^2 + 4s + 13 = (s^2 + 4s + 4) + 9 = (s + 2)^2 + 3^2$$

**Step 2: Rewrite $F(s)$ in shifted form.**

$$F(s) = \frac{3}{(s + 2)^2 + 3^2}$$

**Step 3: Identify the standard pair.**

Recall: $\dfrac{\omega}{(s+a)^2 + \omega^2} \leftrightarrow e^{-at}\sin(\omega t)$

Here, $a = 2$ and $\omega = 3$. So the numerator must be $\omega = 3$ — it already matches!

**Step 4: Apply First Shifting Theorem.**

$$f(t) = e^{-2t}\sin(3t), \quad t \geq 0$$

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Convolution Theorem Application

> **Problem:** Find $f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{(s + 1)(s + 2)}\right\}$ using convolution.

### Solution

**Step 1: Split into two known transforms.**

$$F(s) = F_1(s) \cdot F_2(s) = \frac{1}{s + 1} \cdot \frac{1}{s + 2}$$

where $f_1(t) = e^{-t}$ and $f_2(t) = e^{-2t}$.

**Step 2: Apply convolution integral.**

$$f(t) = f_1(t) * f_2(t) = \int_0^t e^{-\tau}\,e^{-2(t - \tau)}\,d\tau$$

**Step 3: Simplify the integrand.**

$$f(t) = e^{-2t}\int_0^t e^{-\tau} \cdot e^{2\tau}\,d\tau = e^{-2t}\int_0^t e^{\tau}\,d\tau$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral.**

$$f(t) = e^{-2t}\left[e^{\tau}\right]_0^t = e^{-2t}(e^t - 1) = e^{-t} - e^{-2t}$$

**Step 5: Verify with PFE method.**

$$\frac{1}{(s+1)(s+2)} = \frac{1}{s+1} - \frac{1}{s+2} \Rightarrow f(t) = e^{-t} - e^{-2t} \checkmark$$

## 3.6 Worked Example 5: Initial and Final Value Theorem

> **Problem:** Given $F(s) = \dfrac{10(s + 1)}{s(s + 2)(s + 5)}$, find $f(0^+)$ and $f(\infty)$.

### Solution

**Step 1: Check IVT validity.** $F(s)$ is strictly proper (degree of num = 1, degree of den = 3). Poles: $0, -2, -5$. No poles in right half plane or on $j\omega$ axis (except origin — which is allowed for IVT). **IVT valid.**

$$f(0^+) = \lim_{s \to \infty} sF(s) = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10(s + 1)}{(s + 2)(s + 5)} = \lim_{s \to \infty} \frac{10s}{s^2} = 0$$

**Step 2: Check FVT validity.** Poles of $sF(s) = \dfrac{10(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)}$ are $-2$ and $-5$, both in LHP. **FVT valid.**

$$f(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0} sF(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{10(s + 1)}{(s + 2)(s + 5)} = \frac{10(1)}{(2)(5)} = \frac{10}{10} = 1$$

**Step 3: Verify with full inverse transform.**

$$F(s) = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s + 2} + \frac{C}{s + 5}$$

$$A = \left.\frac{10(s+1)}{(s+2)(s+5)}\right|_{s=0} = \frac{10}{10} = 1, \quad B = \left.\frac{10(s+1)}{s(s+5)}\right|_{s=-2} = \frac{-10}{(-2)(3)} = \frac{5}{3}$$

$$C = \left.\frac{10(s+1)}{s(s+2)}\right|_{s=-5} = \frac{-40}{(-5)(-3)} = -\frac{8}{3}$$

$$f(t) = 1 + \frac{5}{3}e^{-2t} - \frac{8}{3}e^{-5t}$$

At $t = 0^+$: $1 + 5/3 - 8/3 = 1 - 3/3 = 0$ ✓

As $t \to \infty$: All exponentials decay $\to 0$, leaving $f(\infty) = 1$ ✓

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Inverse Laplace Transform Pipeline in Network Analysis

The following block diagram illustrates the standard KTU exam workflow for solving a network problem using inverse Laplace transforms:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given RLC Circuit with Source] --> B[Write KVL or KCL Equation in Time Domain]
    B --> C[Apply Forward Laplace Transform]
    C --> D[Obtain Algebraic Equation in s-Domain]
    D --> E[Solve Algebraic Equation for I s or V s]
    E --> F[Check if F s is Proper Rational Function]
    F -->|No| G[Perform Polynomial Long Division]
    G --> H[Apply Partial Fraction Expansion PFE]
    F -->|Yes| H
    H --> I[Identify Standard Transform Pairs]
    I --> J[Apply Theorems if Needed: Shifting, Convolution, etc.]
    J --> K[Take Inverse Laplace Transform Term by Term]
    K --> L[Obtain Time Domain Response f t]
    L --> M[Validate using IVT and FVT Theorems]
    M --> N[End: Final Answer in Time Domain]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,color:#ffffff
    style M fill:#581c87,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree: Selecting the Correct PFE Method Based on Pole Type

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Given F s = N s over D s] --> B{Is degree N s greater than or equal to degree D s?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Perform Long Division]
    B -->|No| D[Proceed to Factor D s]
    C --> D
    D --> E{What are the pole types?}
    E -->|Distinct Real Poles| F[Use Cover-Up Method for Each Pole]
    E -->|Repeated Real Poles| G[Use Formula with Derivatives for Repeated Poles]
    E -->|Complex Conjugate Pair| H[Complete the Square and Apply Shifting Theorem]
    E -->|Mixed Pole Types| I[Combine All Methods]

    F --> J[Sum Standard Pairs for Each Term]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Final f t in Time Domain]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#065f46,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#b45309,color:#ffffff
```
