# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the time constant of an RC circuit. What is its significance in transient analysis?**

**Model Answer:**

The **time constant** $\tau$ of a first-order RC circuit is defined as the product of the resistance and capacitance:
$$\tau = RC \quad \text{(in seconds)}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is the time required for the capacitor voltage (or current) to reach approximately **63.2%** of its final steady-state value during charging (or to decay to **36.8%** of its initial value during discharging).
2. After a time $t = 5\tau$, the transient is considered to have **decayed to less than 1%** of its initial value, marking practical steady state.
3. It quantifies the **speed of response** of the network — a smaller $\tau$ means a faster circuit.

Mathematically, at $t = \tau$: $e^{-1} = 0.368$, so the remaining transient fraction is $36.8\%$.

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Distinguish between overdamped, critically damped, and underdamped responses in a series RLC circuit.**

**Model Answer:**

| Damping Type | Condition | Nature of Roots | Response Shape |
|--------------|-----------|-----------------|----------------|
| Overdamped | $\alpha > \omega_0$ | Two distinct, real, negative | Slow non-oscillatory exponential approach to steady state |
| Critically damped | $\alpha = \omega_0$ | Repeated real root $s_1 = s_2 = -\alpha$ | Fastest non-oscillatory response |
| Underdamped | $\alpha < \omega_0$ | Complex conjugate $-\alpha \pm j\omega_d$ | Decaying sinusoidal oscillation about steady state |

Here, $\alpha = R/2L$ is the **damping coefficient** and $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$ is the **undamped natural frequency**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** In the RC circuit shown, a switch is closed at $t = 0$, connecting a 50 V DC source to a $5\ \text{k}\Omega$ resistor in series with a $20\ \mu\text{F}$ capacitor. The capacitor is initially uncharged. Determine:
(i) The time constant $\tau$.
(ii) The initial and final values of capacitor voltage and circuit current.
(iii) The expressions for $v_C(t)$ and $i(t)$ for $t \geq 0$.
(iv) The time required for the capacitor to charge to 40 V.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same circuit, find the time at which the energy stored in the capacitor equals 60% of its maximum value. Also, calculate the power dissipated in the resistor at that instant.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Time constant:**
$$\tau = RC = (5 \times 10^3)(20 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.1\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Initial and final values:**
- $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-) = 0$ V (capacitor uncharged)
- $v_C(\infty) = V_s = 50$ V (capacitor open in steady state)
- $i(0^+) = \dfrac{V_s}{R} = \dfrac{50}{5 \times 10^3} = 10$ mA (capacitor is a short initially)
- $i(\infty) = 0$ A (capacitor blocks DC in steady state)

**Step 3 — Expressions:**
$$v_C(t) = 50\,(1 - e^{-t/0.1}) = 50\,(1 - e^{-10t})\ \text{V}, \quad t \geq 0$$

$$i(t) = 10\,e^{-10t}\ \text{mA}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 4 — Time to charge to 40 V:**
$$40 = 50\,(1 - e^{-10t})$$
$$e^{-10t} = 0.2$$
$$t = \frac{\ln(5)}{10} = \frac{1.6094}{10} \approx 0.161\ \text{s}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum energy in capacitor** (at $t = \infty$):
$$W_{\max} = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}(20 \times 10^{-6})(50)^2 = 25\ \text{mJ}$$

**Step 2 — Set energy to 60% of maximum:**
$$\frac{1}{2}C\,v_C^2 = 0.6 \times W_{\max}$$
$$v_C^2 = 0.6 \times 2500 = 1500 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_C = \sqrt{1500} \approx 38.73\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Find the corresponding time:**
$$38.73 = 50\,(1 - e^{-10t})$$
$$e^{-10t} = 1 - 0.7746 = 0.2254$$
$$t = \frac{-\ln(0.2254)}{10} = \frac{1.4901}{10} \approx 0.149\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Current at that instant:**
$$i = 10\,e^{-10 \times 0.149} = 10 \times e^{-1.49} = 10 \times 0.2254 \approx 2.254\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Power dissipated in R:**
$$P_R = i^2 R = (2.254 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times (5 \times 10^3) = 25.4\ \text{mW}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A series RLC circuit has $R = 20\ \Omega$, $L = 0.5$ H, and $C = 100\ \mu$F. A 100 V DC source is applied at $t = 0$ with zero initial conditions. Determine:
(i) Whether the circuit is overdamped, critically damped, or underdamped.
(ii) The roots of the characteristic equation.
(iii) The complete expression for $i(t)$ and $v_C(t)$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the same circuit, calculate the maximum value of the capacitor voltage and the time at which it occurs.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$:**
$$\alpha = \frac{R}{2L} = \frac{20}{2 \times 0.5} = 20\ \text{s}^{-1}$$
$$\omega_0 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{5 \times 10^{-5}}} \approx 141.42\ \text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damping classification:**
Since $\alpha = 20 < \omega_0 = 141.42$, the circuit is **underdamped**.

**Step 3 — Characteristic roots:**
$$\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2} = \sqrt{20000 - 400} = \sqrt{19600} = 140\ \text{rad/s}$$
$$s_{1,2} = -20 \pm j140$$

**Step 4 — General form:**
$$v_C(t) = 100 + e^{-20t}\big[B_1 \cos(140 t) + B_2 \sin(140 t)\big]$$

**Step 5 — Apply initial conditions:**

$v_C(0^+) = 0 \Rightarrow 0 = 100 + B_1 \Rightarrow B_1 = -100$

$\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}\bigg|_{0^+} = \dfrac{i(0^+)}{C} = 0 \Rightarrow 0 = -\alpha B_1 + \omega_d B_2 = -20(-100) + 140 B_2 = 2000 + 140 B_2$
$$B_2 = -\frac{2000}{140} = -\frac{100}{7} \approx -14.286$$

**Step 6 — Final expression:**
$$v_C(t) = 100 - e^{-20t}\left[100 \cos(140 t) + \frac{100}{7}\sin(140 t)\right]\ \text{V}$$

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_C}{dt}$$

Differentiating:
$$\frac{dv_C}{dt} = e^{-20t}\left[2000\cos(140t) + \frac{2000}{7}\sin(140t)\right] - e^{-20t}\left[-14000\sin(140t) + \frac{14000}{7}\cos(140t)\right]$$

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_C}{dt} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{-20t}\left[2000\cos(140t) + \frac{2000}{7}\sin(140t) + 14000\sin(140t) - 2000\cos(140t)\right]$$

$$i(t) = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{-20t}\left[\frac{2000 + 98000}{7}\sin(140t)\right] = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times e^{-20t}\left[\frac{100000}{7}\sin(140t)\right]$$

$$i(t) = \frac{10}{7}\,e^{-20t}\sin(140 t) \approx 1.429\,e^{-20t}\sin(140 t)\ \text{A}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Find the time of maximum $v_C$** by setting $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} = 0$.

Since $i(t) = C\,\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$, the maximum of $v_C$ corresponds to $i(t) = 0$, i.e., $\sin(140 t) = 0$:
$$140 t = n\pi,\ n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The first maximum occurs at $t = \dfrac{\pi}{140} \approx 0.0224$ s.

**Step 2 — Compute $v_C$ at this time:**

At $t = \pi/140$: $\cos(140 t) = \cos(\pi) = -1$, $\sin(140 t) = \sin(\pi) = 0$.

$$v_C\left(\frac{\pi}{140}\right) = 100 - e^{-20 \pi/140}(-100) = 100 + 100\,e^{-\pi/7} \approx 100 + 100 \times 0.6382 \approx 163.82\ \text{V}$$

**Maximum overshoot:** $163.82 - 100 = 63.82$ V (about 63.82% above the final value).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B — 14-Mark Type):**
> 1. **Sign error in $\alpha$:** Some students write $\alpha = R/L$ instead of $R/2L$. This makes the entire damping classification wrong. *Always* derive $\alpha$ from the standard form $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by $LC$:** After writing $LC\,\ddot{v_C} + RC\,\dot{v_C} + v_C = V_s$, students often read $\alpha = R$ and $\omega_0^2 = 1$ instead of the correct $\alpha = R/2L$ and $\omega_0^2 = 1/LC$.
> 3. **Incorrect initial condition for $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$:** The initial derivative is $\dfrac{i(0^+)}{C}$, not $\dfrac{V_s}{L}$ or $0$ (unless the problem states so).
> 4. **Mixing up $v_C$ and $i$ units:** $v_C$ is in volts, $i$ is in amperes. The factor $C$ must multiply $\dfrac{dv_C}{dt}$ to obtain $i(t)$.
> 5. **Missing the "+ $V_s$" steady-state term:** A common oversight is writing the total response as only the transient part. Always include the forced response (particular integral).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **First-order RC ODE:** $RC\,\dfrac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = V_s$ → solution $v_C(t) = V_s + A\,e^{-t/RC}$.
- **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (RC) or $\tau = L/R$ (RL). After $5\tau$, transient is essentially gone.
- **Continuity laws:** $v_C(0^+) = v_C(0^-)$ and $i_L(0^+) = i_L(0^-)$.
- **Second-order characteristic equation:** $s^2 + 2\alpha s + \omega_0^2 = 0$ with $\alpha = R/2L$ and $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{LC}$.
- **Damping cases:**
  - Overdamped: $\alpha > \omega_0$ → sum of two real exponentials.
  - Critically damped: $\alpha = \omega_0$ → $(A_1 + A_2 t)e^{-\alpha t}$.
  - Underdamped: $\alpha < \omega_0$ → $e^{-\alpha t}(B_1 \cos\omega_d t + B_2 \sin\omega_d t)$.
- **Damped frequency:** $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \alpha^2}$ (underdamped only).
- **Laplace-domain tricks:** Replace $C$ with $1/sC$ + initial voltage source $v_C(0^-)/s$; replace $L$ with $sL$ + initial current source $Li_L(0^-)$.
- **Initial and Final Value Theorems:** Powerful shortcuts but require the s-domain function's poles to lie in the left half-plane for the Final Value Theorem.
- **Maximum overshoot** in underdamped RLC occurs when $i(t) = 0$ (i.e., $dv_C/dt = 0$).
- **KTU exam pattern:** Always state the differential equation, identify $\alpha$ and $\omega_0$, classify damping, and then write the general solution with constants determined from $v_C(0^+)$ and $dv_C/dt(0^+)$.
- **Common pitfalls:** wrong $\alpha$ formula, missing $V_s$ in total response, wrong initial derivative sign, ignoring second-order effects, units mismatch.
- **Real-world context:** RC networks = filters, timers, integrators; RLC networks = resonant filters, power-system transients, mechanical analogs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impulse

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Unit Impulse Function** (also called the **Dirac Delta Function**), denoted by **δ(t)**, is a mathematical singularity function that models an idealized, infinitesimally narrow pulse of infinite amplitude but with a **finite, unit area**. In the context of KTU Network Theory (Module 3 — Transient Analysis and Signal Transforms), the impulse is treated as the *time derivative of the unit step function* and serves as the fundamental **excitation function** for determining the **Impulse Response** of linear time-invariant (LTI) networks.

Mathematically, the unit impulse is defined by the following two conditions:

$$
\delta(t) = \begin{cases} \infty & \text{for } t = 0 \\ 0 & \text{for } t \neq 0 \end{cases}
$$

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t)\, dt = 1
$$

The shifted (or delayed) impulse δ(t − t₀) is centered at t = t₀, with the area concentrated entirely at that instant.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **cricket ball being struck perfectly by a bat**. The contact between bat and ball lasts for an extremely short duration (say, a millisecond), but during that brief instant, an enormous force is delivered — enough to send the ball flying. If you plotted the force versus time, you would get a tall, narrow spike. Now, if you could imagine the strike happening in **zero time** with **infinite force** but a **finite impulse** (force × time), that is exactly the **unit impulse δ(t)**.

Another intuitive way: think of **δ(t) as the limit of a rectangular pulse** whose width shrinks to zero while its height grows to infinity, but the area (height × width) remains **exactly equal to 1**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The unit impulse is a *singularity function*. It is **not physically realizable** by any real source. However, it is the most powerful *mathematical tool* in network analysis because the response of any LTI network to δ(t) — called the **impulse response h(t)** — completely characterizes the network. From h(t), the response to **any** arbitrary input can be obtained via the **Convolution Integral**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Laplace Transform of δ(t) is unity** (i.e., 1). This is one of the most heavily tested identities in KTU exams. The Laplace transform of δ(t − t₀) is **e^(−s·t₀)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The area of δ(t) is always **1** (for unit impulse).
- The sifting / sampling property carries the dimensionless constant **1** at the sampled point.
- The Laplace transform pair: **δ(t) ↔ 1** and **δ(t − t₀) ↔ e^(−s·t₀)** — fundamental for KTU derivations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Impulse

The impulse function is best understood as the derivative of the unit step function u(t). Let us break this down step-by-step:

- **Step 1 — Define the rectangular pulse approximation:** A rectangular pulse p_ε(t) of width ε and height 1/ε, centered at t = 0, satisfies the area = 1 condition. As ε → 0, p_ε(t) becomes an infinitely tall, infinitely narrow spike — the impulse.

- **Step 2 — Recognize δ(t) as the limit:**
  $$
  \delta(t) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} p_\varepsilon(t)
  $$
  This is the limit definition KTU examiners expect in derivations.

- **Step 3 — Connect to the step function:** Since δ(t) is the derivative of u(t), we have:
  $$
  \delta(t) = \frac{d\, u(t)}{dt}
  $$
  Conversely, the step function is the integral of the impulse:
  $$
  u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\, d\tau
  $$

- **Step 4 — Identify the network role:** When δ(t) is applied as the source excitation to an LTI network, the output is the **impulse response h(t)**, given by the inverse Laplace transform of the network's transfer function H(s):
  $$
  h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\}
  $$
  where **H(s)** is the network's transfer function (also called network function or driving-point/transfer function).

- **Step 5 — Realize the importance:** The convolution integral expresses the response y(t) to *any* input x(t) as:
  $$
  y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, h(t-\tau)\, d\tau = x(t) * h(t)
  $$
  Hence, knowing h(t) is equivalent to knowing the **complete behavior** of the network.

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind the Impulse

- **Why δ(t)?** In real life, we deal with sudden shocks — lightning strikes, circuit switching, hammer impacts. Although δ(t) is a mathematical idealization, it provides a **clean, closed-form** way to analyze how networks react to such abrupt events.
- **How is it used?** Through its **Laplace transform (= 1)**, the impulse lets us probe a network's natural behavior without worrying about the forcing function's form. The transfer function H(s) is the system's signature.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for the Impulse topic. **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Scheme exam favorites.** Note: vertical bars for absolute value are written as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Property | Statement | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|--------------------|-----------|---------------------|
| 1 | Definition (compact) | $\delta(t) = 0$ for $t \neq 0$; $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t) dt = 1$ | ★★★ |
| 2 | Derivative of step | $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d\,u(t)}{dt}$ | ★★★ |
| 3 | Integral to step | $u(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$ | ★★ |
| 4 | Shifted impulse area | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0)\,dt = 1$ | ★★ |
| 5 | Sampling / Sifting property | $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)$ | ★★★ |
| 6 | Time-scaling property | $\delta(a\,t) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,\delta(t)$ | ★★ |
| 7 | Even function | $\delta(-t) = \delta(t)$ | ★ |
| 8 | Product with function | $f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0) = f(t_0)\,\delta(t - t_0)$ | ★★★ |
| 9 | Laplace transform pair | $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ | ★★★ |
| 10 | Laplace of shifted impulse | $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - t_0)\} = e^{-s t_0}$ for $t_0 > 0$ | ★★★ |
| 11 | Impulse response | $h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\}$ | ★★★ |
| 12 | Convolution integral | $y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$ | ★★★ |
| 13 | Network function | $H(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{X(s)}\bigg\vert_{X(s)=1} = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\}$ | ★★ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The impulse response h(t) is the **fingerprint of an LTI system**. In practical engineering:

- **Filter design:** The frequency response H(jω) is obtained by taking the Fourier transform of h(t), letting engineers design low-pass, high-pass, and band-pass filters.
- **Control systems:** The impulse response reveals stability, settling time, and natural frequencies of feedback control loops.
- **Communication channels:** The impulse response of a channel models multipath propagation, intersymbol interference, and equalization.
- **Structural engineering:** Impulse response testing (e.g., with a hammer strike) identifies natural frequencies of bridges and buildings.
- **Biomedical signal processing:** EEG and ECG systems are characterized by their impulse responses.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Impulse as the Limit of a Rectangular Pulse

We start with a rectangular pulse p_ε(t) of width ε, height 1/ε, and unit area:

$$
p_\varepsilon(t) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon} & \text{for } -\dfrac{\varepsilon}{2} < t < +\dfrac{\varepsilon}{2} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The total area is the height times the width:

$$
\text{Area} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon} \times \varepsilon = 1
$$

As ε → 0, the width shrinks to zero and the height grows to infinity, yet the area remains 1. Hence, the impulse is defined as:

$$
\delta(t) = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0}\, p_\varepsilon(t)
$$

**Textual logic:** The rectangular pulse approximates a real-world "kick" (finite force, finite duration). The mathematical impulse is its idealized limit — an infinitely brief, infinitely strong "perfect kick" that delivers one unit of impulse.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Impulse as the Derivative of the Unit Step

Consider the unit step function:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} 0 & t < 0 \\ 1 & t \geq 0 \end{cases}
$$

This step can be approximated by a ramped function u_ε(t) that transitions from 0 to 1 over a small interval of width ε. Differentiating u_ε(t) with respect to time yields a pulse of height 1/ε over the transition interval. As ε → 0, the derivative becomes infinite at t = 0 and zero elsewhere — which is precisely δ(t):

$$
\delta(t) = \frac{d\,u(t)}{dt} = \lim_{\varepsilon \to 0} \frac{d\,u_\varepsilon(t)}{dt}
$$

Integrating δ(t) over all time recovers the step function:

$$
u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Proof of the Sifting (Sampling) Property

We need to show that:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)
$$

Starting from the definition, δ(t − t₀) is non-zero only at t = t₀. By the product rule for impulses:

$$
f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0) = f(t_0)\,\delta(t - t_0)
$$

This is because δ(t − t₀) is zero everywhere except at t = t₀, where f(t) effectively equals f(t₀). Now integrate:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t_0)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt
$$

$$
= f(t_0) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0)\,dt
$$

$$
= f(t_0) \times 1 = f(t_0)
$$

**Textual logic:** The impulse "picks out" the value of the function f(t) exactly at the location where the impulse is centered. Hence the name **sifting property** — the impulse sifts out one specific value of f.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Laplace Transform of the Unit Impulse

The Laplace transform is defined as:

$$
F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} f(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt
$$

For f(t) = δ(t):

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt
$$

By the sifting property (with t₀ = 0), this evaluates the exponential at t = 0:

$$
= e^{-s \times 0} = 1
$$

Hence:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1
$$

For the shifted impulse δ(t − t₀) with t₀ > 0:

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - t_0)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0)\,e^{-st}\,dt = e^{-s t_0}
$$

**Textual logic:** Because the impulse is a "spike" of area 1 at a specific point, the Laplace integral collapses to a single evaluation of e^(−st) at that point.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Impulse Response of a Series RC Circuit

Consider a series RC circuit driven by an impulse voltage source δ(t). The KVL equation is:

$$
v_R(t) + v_C(t) = \delta(t)
$$

Using the relations v_R(t) = R·i(t) and i(t) = C·dv_C/dt:

$$
RC\,\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = \delta(t)
$$

Taking the Laplace transform (with zero initial conditions, since the impulse acts at t = 0 and capacitor is initially uncharged):

$$
RC\,s\,V_C(s) + V_C(s) = 1
$$

$$
V_C(s)\,(RC\,s + 1) = 1
$$

$$
V_C(s) = \frac{1}{RC\,s + 1} = \frac{1}{RC} \cdot \frac{1}{s + \dfrac{1}{RC}}
$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform:

$$
v_C(t) = \frac{1}{RC}\,e^{-t/RC}\,u(t)
$$

This is the **impulse response** of the RC network (in terms of capacitor voltage). Note that v_C(t) is a *decaying exponential* — the capacitor absorbs the impulse's energy and then releases it gradually through the resistor.

**Textual logic:** When struck by an impulse, the RC circuit does not respond instantly. Instead, the energy is integrated by the capacitor, and the voltage decays exponentially with time constant τ = RC.

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Impulse Response of a Series RL Circuit

For a series RL circuit with current i(t) as the response to impulse voltage:

$$
L\,\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = \delta(t)
$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial current):

$$
L\,s\,I(s) + R\,I(s) = 1
$$

$$
I(s) = \frac{1}{L\,s + R} = \frac{1}{L} \cdot \frac{1}{s + \dfrac{R}{L}}
$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$
i(t) = \frac{1}{L}\,e^{-Rt/L}\,u(t)
$$

The time constant is τ = L/R. Again, the response is a decaying exponential.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Step vs. Impulse Response

| Circuit | Step Response (input = u(t)) | Impulse Response (input = δ(t)) | Time Constant |
|---------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|---------------|
| RC (series) | $v_C(t) = (1 - e^{-t/RC})\,u(t)$ | $v_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC}\,e^{-t/RC}\,u(t)$ | $\tau = RC$ |
| RL (series) | $i(t) = \dfrac{1}{R}\,(1 - e^{-Rt/L})\,u(t)$ | $i(t) = \dfrac{1}{L}\,e^{-Rt/L}\,u(t)$ | $\tau = L/R$ |
| RLC (series) | Underdamped: oscillatory decay | Underdamped: derivative of step response | $\tau = 2L/R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The impulse response h(t) is the **time derivative** of the step response s(t). This is a direct consequence of δ(t) = du(t)/dt and the linearity of LTI systems. So if you know the step response, the impulse response is **one differentiation away**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Impulse as Part of the Singularity Function Family

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the impulse function relates to other singularity functions commonly tested in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Singularity Functions] --> B[Unit Impulse delta of t]
    A --> C[Unit Step u of t]
    A --> D[Unit Ramp r of t]
    A --> E[Unit Parabola]

    B --> F[Laplace Transform: 1]
    C --> G[Laplace Transform: 1 over s]
    D --> H[Laplace Transform: 1 over s squared]
    E --> I[Laplace Transform: 1 over s cubed]

    B --> J[Derivative of Step]
    C --> K[Integral of Impulse]
    D --> L[Integral of Step]
    E --> M[Integral of Ramp]

    F --> N[Network Analysis Tool]
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> N

    N --> O[Impulse Response h of t]
    N --> P[Step Response s of t]
    N --> Q[Transfer Function H of s]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Network Function & Impulse Response Relationship

The following Mermaid diagram shows the flow of signals from an impulse input through an LTI network to the impulse response, and from there to the convolution with arbitrary input.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impulse Input delta of t] -->|Laplace Transform| B[X of s equals 1]
    B --> C[LTI Network H of s]
    C --> D[Output Y of s equals H of s]
    D -->|Inverse Laplace| E[Impulse Response h of t]
    E --> F[Convolution with x of t]
    F --> G[Response y of t to any input]

    subgraph FT [Frequency Domain]
        B
        C
        D
    end

    subgraph TD [Time Domain]
        A
        E
        F
        G
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Derivation of Impulse Response from Step Response

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Apply u of t to Network] --> B[Obtain Step Response s of t]
    A2[Apply delta of t to Network] --> C[Obtain Impulse Response h of t]

    B -->|Differentiate with respect to t| D[ds by dt]
    C -->|Integrate with respect to t| E[Integral from 0 to t of h of tau d tau]

    D --> F[Equals h of t]
    E --> G[Equals s of t]
    F --> H[Equivalence: h of t = d s of t by d t]
    G --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to find the impulse response of a network, you have **two routes**:
> 1. Apply δ(t) directly and solve the differential equation in the s-domain.
> 2. First find the step response, then **differentiate** it with respect to time.
> Route 2 is often easier if the step response is already known.

## 4.4 Schematic — RC Circuit Under Impulse Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impulse Voltage Source delta of t] -->|v in equals delta of t| B[Resistor R]
    B --> C[Capacitor C]
    C --> D[Ground]

    subgraph EQN [Circuit Equation]
        E[RC dvC by dt + vC = delta of t]
    end

    A -.-> E
    B -.-> E
    C -.-> E

    E --> F[In s domain: RC s VC of s + VC of s = 1]
    F --> G[VC of s = 1 over RC s + 1]
    G --> H[vC of t = 1 over RC e to power minus t by RC u of t]
```

## 4.5 Schematic — RL Circuit Under Impulse Excitation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Impulse Voltage Source delta of t] -->|v in equals delta of t| B[Inductor L]
    B --> C[Resistor R]
    C --> D[Ground]

    subgraph EQN [Circuit Equation]
        E[L di by dt + R i = delta of t]
    end

    A -.-> E
    B -.-> E
    C -.-> E

    E --> F[In s domain: L s I of s + R I of s = 1]
    F --> G[I of s = 1 over L s + R]
    G --> H[i of t = 1 over L e to power minus R t by L u of t]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**Q: Define the unit impulse function. State and prove its sifting property.**

**Model Answer:**

The unit impulse function δ(t) is a singularity function defined by:

$$
\delta(t) = 0 \text{ for } t \neq 0; \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1
$$

**Sifting Property:**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)
$$

**Proof:** Since δ(t − t₀) is non-zero only at t = t₀, we have f(t)·δ(t − t₀) = f(t₀)·δ(t − t₀). Integrating:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t_0)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)\cdot 1 = f(t_0)
$$

Hence, the impulse "sifts out" the value of f(t) at t = t₀. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**Q: Find the Laplace transform of δ(t) and δ(t − 2).**

**Model Answer:**

For δ(t):

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt = e^{-s(0)} = 1
$$

For δ(t − 2) (assuming 2 > 0):

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - 2)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \delta(t - 2)\,e^{-st}\,dt = e^{-s(2)} = e^{-2s}
$$

**Result:** $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ and $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t-2)\} = e^{-2s}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — ESE Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU End Semester Examination – Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the unit impulse function. Derive its Laplace transform and explain its physical significance in network analysis.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A series RC circuit with R = 2 Ω and C = 0.5 F is excited by a unit impulse voltage source δ(t). Find the current i(t) flowing through the circuit. Assume zero initial conditions.

#### Model Solution to Part A(a):

**Step 1 — Definition:**

The unit impulse δ(t) is a singularity function defined as:

$$
\delta(t) = 0 \text{ for } t \neq 0; \quad \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1
$$

It can be viewed as the limit of a rectangular pulse of width ε and height 1/ε as ε → 0, or equivalently as the derivative of the unit step function u(t):

$$
\delta(t) = \frac{d\,u(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation of Laplace Transform:**

$$
\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = \int_{0^-}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,e^{-st}\,dt
$$

Applying the sifting property with t₀ = 0:

$$
= e^{-s(0)} = 1
$$

Hence, $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$. **(3 marks for derivation)**

**Step 3 — Physical Significance:**

- The impulse represents an idealized **instantaneous shock** or **kick** delivered to the network at t = 0.
- Its Laplace transform being **unity** means that when δ(t) is applied, the input is spectrally "flat" — every frequency component is excited with equal strength.
- The output of the network, h(t) = ℒ⁻¹{H(s)}, is the **impulse response**, which is the network's complete signature.
- The impulse is used to characterize filters, control systems, and communication channels. **(4 marks for significance)**

#### Model Solution to Part A(b):

**Step 1 — KVL Equation:**

For the series RC circuit with input v_in = δ(t):

$$
v_R(t) + v_C(t) = \delta(t)
$$

With v_R = R·i and i = C·dv_C/dt:

$$
RC\,\frac{dv_C}{dt} + v_C = \delta(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Transform:**

With zero initial conditions:

$$
RC\,s\,V_C(s) + V_C(s) = 1
$$

$$
V_C(s) = \frac{1}{RC\,s + 1}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Values R = 2 Ω, C = 0.5 F:**

RC = 2 × 0.5 = 1, so:

$$
V_C(s) = \frac{1}{s + 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Find Current I(s):**

Since i(t) = C·dv_C/dt, in the s-domain:

$$
I(s) = C\,s\,V_C(s) = 0.5 \times s \times \frac{1}{s + 1} = \frac{0.5\,s}{s + 1}
$$

Rewriting for inverse Laplace:

$$
I(s) = \frac{0.5\,(s+1) - 0.5}{s + 1} = 0.5 - \frac{0.5}{s + 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Laplace Transform:**

$$
i(t) = 0.5\,\delta(t) - 0.5\,e^{-t}\,u(t)
$$

**Result:** $i(t) = 0.5\,\delta(t) - 0.5\,e^{-t}\,u(t)$ A. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** [7 Marks] State and prove the time-scaling and time-shifting properties of the unit impulse function δ(t).

**(b)** [7 Marks] A series RL circuit with R = 4 Ω and L = 2 H is excited by a unit impulse voltage source δ(t). Determine the voltage across the inductor v_L(t) using Laplace transform. Assume zero initial current.

#### Model Solution to Part B(a):

**Step 1 — Time-Shifting Property:**

$$
\delta(t - t_0) = \begin{cases} \infty & t = t_0 \\ 0 & t \neq t_0 \end{cases}
$$

The area under δ(t − t₀) is still 1, and:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - t_0)\,dt = 1
$$

**Proof of shifted sifting property:**

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)
$$

This is because δ(t − t₀) is non-zero only at t = t₀, so f(t) effectively equals f(t₀) at the impulse location, and integration gives f(t₀). **(3 marks)**

**Step 2 — Time-Scaling Property:**

$$
\delta(a\,t) = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,\delta(t)
$$

**Proof:** Let τ = a·t, so dτ = a·dt. Then:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(a\,t)\,dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(\tau)\,\frac{d\tau}{\vert a \vert} = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}
$$

Since this must equal $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$ scaled, the relation $\delta(a\,t) = \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,\delta(t)$ follows. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Consequence (Even Function):**

Setting a = −1 in the time-scaling property:

$$
\delta(-t) = \frac{1}{\vert -1 \vert}\,\delta(t) = \delta(t)
$$

This shows δ(t) is an **even function** of t. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part B(b):

**Step 1 — KVL for Series RL Circuit:**

$$
L\,\frac{di}{dt} + R\,i = \delta(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Laplace Transform (zero initial current):**

$$
L\,s\,I(s) + R\,I(s) = 1
$$

$$
I(s) = \frac{1}{L\,s + R}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute R = 4 Ω, L = 2 H:**

$$
I(s) = \frac{1}{2\,s + 4} = \frac{0.5}{s + 2}
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse Laplace to find i(t):**

$$
i(t) = 0.5\,e^{-2t}\,u(t) \text{ A}
$$

**Step 5 — Find v_L(t):**

Since v_L = L·(di/dt):

$$
v_L(t) = L\,\frac{di}{dt} = 2 \cdot \frac{d}{dt}\left[0.5\,e^{-2t}\,u(t)\right]
$$

Using the product rule (and noting d(u(t))/dt = δ(t)):

$$
v_L(t) = 2 \cdot \left[0.5 \cdot (-2)\,e^{-2t}\,u(t) + 0.5\,e^{0}\cdot\delta(t)\right]
$$

$$
v_L(t) = -2\,e^{-2t}\,u(t) + 1\cdot\delta(t)
$$

**Alternative method (s-domain):**

$$
V_L(s) = L\,s\,I(s) - L\,i(0^-) = 2\,s \cdot \frac{0.5}{s + 2} = \frac{s}{s + 2}
$$

Rewriting:

$$
V_L(s) = \frac{(s+2) - 2}{s + 2} = 1 - \frac{2}{s + 2}
$$

Inverse Laplace:

$$
v_L(t) = \delta(t) - 2\,e^{-2t}\,u(t) \text{ V}
$$

**Result:** $v_L(t) = \delta(t) - 2\,e^{-2t}\,u(t)$ V. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the δ(t) component in inductor voltage:** When finding v_L(t) via v_L = L·(di/dt), students often miss the impulsive spike at t = 0 that arises from differentiating u(t). The result v_L(t) = δ(t) − 2e^(−2t)u(t) is the **only correct answer** — the δ(t) term is non-negotiable.
> 2. **Misapplying initial conditions:** The impulse acts at t = 0, so initial conditions are taken at t = 0⁻ (just before the impulse). Always use the **0⁻** convention in s-domain derivations involving impulses.
> 3. **Sifting property sign errors:** When integrating f(t)·δ(t − t₀), the result is f(t₀) only if the limits of integration bracket t₀. If the limits are (0, ∞) and t₀ > 0, the sifting still works; if t₀ < 0, the integral is zero.
> 4. **Confusing impulse with step:** Many students mix up δ(t) and u(t) in s-domain. Remember: ℒ{δ(t)} = 1, ℒ{u(t)} = 1/s. This is the single most common error in KTU Module 3.
> 5. **Dropping the area condition:** The defining condition $\int \delta(t)\,dt = 1$ is what makes the impulse "unit." Forgetting it loses easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Impulse in Network Theory**

- ✅ **Definition:** δ(t) = 0 for t ≠ 0, with $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$.
- ✅ **Derivative of step:** $\delta(t) = \dfrac{d\,u(t)}{dt}$ — the single most important relation.
- ✅ **Integral of impulse:** $u(t) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\,d\tau$.
- ✅ **Sifting property:** $\displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0)\,dt = f(t_0)$.
- ✅ **Product rule:** $f(t)\,\delta(t - t_0) = f(t_0)\,\delta(t - t_0)$.
- ✅ **Time-scaling:** $\delta(a\,t) = \dfrac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,\delta(t)$.
- ✅ **Even function:** $\delta(-t) = \delta(t)$.
- ✅ **Laplace transform:** $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ and $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t - t_0)\} = e^{-s t_0}$.
- ✅ **Impulse response:** $h(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{H(s)\}$, the network's "fingerprint."
- ✅ **Convolution:** Any output $y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$.
- ✅ **RC impulse response (voltage across C):** $v_C(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC}\,e^{-t/RC}\,u(t)$.
- ✅ **RL impulse response (current):** $i(t) = \dfrac{1}{L}\,e^{-Rt/L}\,u(t)$.
- ✅ **Step vs. Impulse:** Impulse response h(t) = d(step response)/dt.
- ✅ **Initial condition convention:** Use 0⁻ (just before impulse) for accurate s-domain modeling.
- ✅ **Physical realizability:** δ(t) is a **mathematical idealization**, never physically realizable — but indispensable as a probing tool.
- ✅ **Energy:** Impulse contains **infinite energy** but **finite unit area** (a "bounded impulse").
- ✅ **KTU 2024 focus areas:** (1) Derive impulse response of RC/RL/RLC using s-domain. (2) Apply sifting property. (3) Compute Laplace transforms. (4) Convert step response to impulse response and vice versa.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Step and Sinusoidal Inputs, Analysis of Low Pass and High Pass RC Circuits Using Laplace Transforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Step and Sinusoidal Inputs, Analysis of Low Pass and High Pass RC Circuits Using Laplace Transforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Step and Sinusoidal Inputs – Formal Definition

In network analysis, **inputs** (also called **excitations** or **forcing functions**) are the electrical signals applied to a circuit. The two most fundamental inputs analyzed in the KTU 2024 Network Theory syllabus are:

**(a) Step Input:** A signal that abruptly changes from one constant value to another at a specific instant of time (usually $t = 0$). Mathematically, the unit step function is:

$$u(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1, & t \geq 0 \end{cases}$$

A scaled and shifted step input of amplitude $V$ is $v_i(t) = V \cdot u(t)$, with its Laplace transform being $V/s$.

**(b) Sinusoidal Input:** A continuous, periodic signal of the form:

$$v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi) \quad \text{or} \quad V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi)$$

where $V_m$ is the peak amplitude, $\omega$ is the angular frequency in **rad/s**, and $\phi$ is the phase angle in **radians**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCECT205 - Module 3):** The focus is on analyzing **first-order RC circuits** subjected to step and sinusoidal excitations using the **Laplace Transform** method, which converts integro-differential equations in time-domain into algebraic equations in the **s-domain**.

## 1.2 Low Pass and High Pass RC Circuits – Definition

- **Low Pass (LP) RC Circuit:** A circuit that allows low-frequency signals to pass through to the output while attenuating high-frequency signals. The output is taken across the **capacitor**.
- **High Pass (HP) RC Circuit:** A circuit that allows high-frequency signals to pass through to the output while attenuating low-frequency signals. The output is taken across the **resistor**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The Water Bucket Model:**
> 
> Imagine a **bucket (capacitor)** with a small inlet pipe controlled by a **valve (resistor)**. 
> - When you suddenly open the valve (a **step input** of water pressure), the bucket fills up **gradually** — not instantly — because of the resistance to flow. The water level (voltage across capacitor) follows an **exponential rise**: $v(t) = V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.
> - In the **Low Pass** configuration, you measure the water **level in the bucket** (capacitor voltage). Sudden changes (high frequencies) are smoothed out, so this circuit "passes" slow changes.
> - In the **High Pass** configuration, you measure the **flow rate through the valve** (resistor voltage). At the moment of sudden change, the flow is maximum, and it decays as the bucket fills. This circuit "passes" sudden changes (high frequencies).

## 1.4 Why Use Laplace Transforms?

Laplace transforms convert the differential equations of circuits into **algebraic equations** in the **s-domain**, making it easier to:
- Solve for transient and steady-state responses simultaneously.
- Handle initial conditions (initial capacitor voltage $V_0$) systematically.
- Obtain the **transfer function** $H(s)$ directly, which characterizes the circuit's behavior for **any input**.

The standard Laplace transform pairs used here are:
- $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{s}$
- $\mathcal{L}\{\sin(\omega t)\} = \dfrac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$
- $\mathcal{L}\{\cos(\omega t)\} = \dfrac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$
- $\mathcal{L}\{e^{-at}\} = \dfrac{1}{s + a}$
- $\mathcal{L}\{1 - e^{-t/\tau}\} = \dfrac{1}{s(1 + s\tau)}$

## 1.5 Standard Circuit Topologies

**Series RC Circuit (used for both LP and HP analysis):**
A resistor $R$ and capacitor $C$ connected in series, driven by source $v_i(t)$, with output $v_o(t)$.

For a given input, depending on the output node:
- **Low Pass:** Output across capacitor $C$ → $v_o(t) = v_C(t)$.
- **High Pass:** Output across resistor $R$ → $v_o(t) = v_R(t)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Derivation of Transfer Functions Using Laplace Transforms

For a series RC circuit in the s-domain, the impedances are:
- Resistor: $Z_R = R$
- Capacitor (with initial voltage $V_0$): $Z_C = \dfrac{1}{sC}$ (assuming **zero initial condition** for transfer function derivation).

Using the **voltage divider rule** in the s-domain:

**Low Pass (LP) Transfer Function:**

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{1/sC}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{1}{1 + sRC}$$

**High Pass (HP) Transfer Function:**

$$H_{HP}(s) = \frac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} = \frac{R}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

Letting $\tau = RC$ (the **time constant** of the circuit):

$$H_{LP}(s) = \frac{1}{1 + s\tau}, \quad H_{HP}(s) = \frac{s\tau}{1 + s\tau}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Observation:** Notice that $H_{HP}(s) = 1 - H_{LP}(s)$. This complementary relationship is a direct consequence of Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL): the sum of voltages across R and C equals the input voltage.

## 2.2 Frequency Response (Sinusoidal Steady-State Analysis)

To find the steady-state response to a sinusoidal input, substitute $s = j\omega$ into the transfer function:

**Low Pass:**
$$H_{LP}(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Magnitude: $\vert H_{LP}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$

Phase: $\angle H_{LP}(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$

**High Pass:**
$$H_{HP}(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega RC}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

Magnitude: $\vert H_{HP}(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$

Phase: $\angle H_{HP}(j\omega) = 90° - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$

## 2.3 Cutoff Frequency (-3 dB Frequency)

The **cutoff frequency** $\omega_c$ is the frequency at which the output power drops to half (or voltage to $1/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707$) of its maximum value.

Setting $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \vert H \vert_{max}$:

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} = \frac{1}{\tau}$$

In Hertz: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$

## 2.4 Step Response Analysis

For a step input $v_i(t) = V \cdot u(t)$, $V_i(s) = V/s$.

**LP Circuit Step Response:**

$$V_o(s) = H_{LP}(s) \cdot V_i(s) = \frac{V}{s(1 + sRC)}$$

Performing partial fractions and inverse Laplace transform:

$$v_o(t) = V\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \cdot u(t)$$

- At $t = 0^+$: $v_o(0) = 0$ (capacitor acts as short circuit).
- At $t = \infty$: $v_o(\infty) = V$ (capacitor acts as open circuit).
- At $t = \tau = RC$: $v_o(\tau) = 0.632 V$ (63.2% of final value).

**HP Circuit Step Response:**

$$V_o(s) = H_{HP}(s) \cdot V_i(s) = \frac{V \cdot RC}{1 + sRC} = \frac{V}{s + 1/RC}$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$v_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \cdot u(t)$$

- At $t = 0^+$: $v_o(0^+) = V$ (capacitor acts as short circuit, all voltage across R).
- At $t = \infty$: $v_o(\infty) = 0$ (capacitor fully charged, no current flows).

## 2.5 Sinusoidal Response (Complete Response)

For input $v_i(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t)$, $V_i(s) = \dfrac{V_m \cdot s}{s^2 + \omega^2}$.

**LP Circuit Sinusoidal Response:**

$$V_o(s) = \frac{V_m \cdot s}{(s^2 + \omega^2)(1 + sRC)}$$

$$v_o(t) = \underbrace{\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \cos(\omega t + \phi)}_{\text{Steady State (Forced)}} - \underbrace{\frac{V_m \omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} e^{-t/RC}}_{\text{Transient (Natural)}}$$

where $\phi = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$.

**HP Circuit Sinusoidal Response:**

$$v_o(t) = \underbrace{\frac{V_m \omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \cos(\omega t + \phi')}_{\text{Steady State}} + \underbrace{\frac{V_m \omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} e^{-t/RC}}_{\text{Transient}}$$

where $\phi' = \tan^{-1}(1/\omega RC)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Applications:**
> - **LP RC Circuits:** Audio bass filters, anti-aliasing filters in ADCs, smoothing circuits in power supplies (DC power supplies use large RC to remove high-frequency ripple).
> - **HP RC Circuits:** DC blocking circuits, high-frequency coupling in amplifiers, treble boost in audio systems, noise filtering where low-frequency hum (50 Hz) needs removal.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Low Pass (LP) RC Circuit** | **High Pass (HP) RC Circuit** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output Node** | Across Capacitor $C$ | Across Resistor $R$ |
| **Transfer Function $H(s)$** | $\dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ | $\dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ |
| **Time Constant $\tau$** | $RC$ (seconds) | $RC$ (seconds) |
| **Cutoff Frequency $\omega_c$** | $\dfrac{1}{RC}$ rad/s | $\dfrac{1}{RC}$ rad/s |
| **DC Gain (s = 0)** | $1$ (passes DC) | $0$ (blocks DC) |
| **High Freq. Gain (s → ∞)** | $0$ (blocks HF) | $1$ (passes HF) |
| **Step Response $v_o(t)$** | $V(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ | $V \cdot e^{-t/RC}$ |
| **Initial Value $v_o(0^+)$** | $0$ | $V$ |
| **Final Value $v_o(\infty)$** | $V$ | $0$ |
| **Magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$** | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| **Phase $\angle H(j\omega)$** | $-\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$ | $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$ |
| **-3 dB Frequency** | $\omega_c = 1/RC$ | $\omega_c = 1/RC$ |
| **Passband** | $0 \leq \omega < \omega_c$ | $\omega > \omega_c$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Relationship:** The product of LP and HP transfer functions equals $sRC/(1 + sRC)^2$ — they are **complementary** but **not inverses**. However, $H_{LP}(s) + H_{HP}(s) = 1$ (KVL property).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Step Response of Low Pass RC Circuit

**Problem Setup:** A series RC circuit with $R$ ohms and $C$ farads, initially at rest (zero initial capacitor voltage), is excited by a DC step source $v_i(t) = V \cdot u(t)$. Find the output voltage across the capacitor.

**Step 1: Write KVL in Time Domain**

$$v_i(t) = v_R(t) + v_C(t) = R \cdot i(t) + v_C(t)$$

The current through the capacitor is $i(t) = C \dfrac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$.

Substituting:

$$v_i(t) = RC \cdot \frac{dv_C(t)}{dt} + v_C(t)$$

**Step 2: Apply Laplace Transform to Both Sides**

Using $\mathcal{L}\{v_i(t)\} = V_i(s) = V/s$ and $\mathcal{L}\{v_C(t)\} = V_C(s)$, and applying the derivative property $\mathcal{L}\{dv_C/dt\} = sV_C(s) - v_C(0^-)$:

With zero initial condition $v_C(0^-) = 0$:

$$\frac{V}{s} = RC \cdot s V_C(s) + V_C(s)$$

**Step 3: Factor Out $V_C(s)$**

$$\frac{V}{s} = V_C(s) \cdot (sRC + 1)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $V_C(s)$**

$$V_C(s) = \frac{V}{s(1 + sRC)}$$

**Step 5: Partial Fraction Expansion**

Let $V_C(s) = \dfrac{A}{s} + \dfrac{B}{1 + sRC}$

Multiplying both sides by $s(1 + sRC)$:

$$V = A(1 + sRC) + B \cdot s$$

Equating coefficients:
- Constant term: $V = A \Rightarrow A = V$
- Coefficient of $s$: $0 = ARC + B \Rightarrow B = -VRC$

Therefore:

$$V_C(s) = \frac{V}{s} - \frac{VRC}{1 + sRC} = \frac{V}{s} - \frac{V}{s + 1/RC}$$

(Using $\dfrac{VRC}{1 + sRC} = \dfrac{V}{s + 1/RC}$)

**Step 6: Apply Inverse Laplace Transform**

$$v_C(t) = V \cdot u(t) - V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \cdot u(t)$$

$$\boxed{v_C(t) = V\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right) \cdot u(t)}$$

**Step 7: Verify Initial and Final Values**

$$v_C(0^+) = V(1 - e^0) = V(1 - 1) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

$$v_C(\infty) = V(1 - e^{-\infty}) = V(1 - 0) = V \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Step Response of High Pass RC Circuit

**Problem Setup:** Same series RC circuit, but output is taken across the resistor.

**Step 1: Laplace Domain Representation**

$$V_i(s) = \frac{V}{s}, \quad H_{HP}(s) = \frac{R}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$$

**Step 2: Output in s-domain**

$$V_o(s) = H_{HP}(s) \cdot V_i(s) = \frac{sRC}{1 + sRC} \cdot \frac{V}{s} = \frac{V \cdot RC}{1 + sRC} = \frac{V}{s + 1/RC}$$

**Step 3: Inverse Laplace Transform**

Using $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{s + a}\right\} = e^{-at}$:

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \cdot u(t)}$$

**Step 4: Verification**

At $t = 0^+$: capacitor is uncharged (short circuit), so entire source voltage appears across R: $v_o(0^+) = V$. ✓

At $t = \infty$: capacitor is fully charged (open circuit), no current flows, so $v_o(\infty) = 0$. ✓

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Sinusoidal Response of Low Pass RC Circuit

**Problem Setup:** Find the complete response of an LP RC circuit to $v_i(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t) \cdot u(t)$ with zero initial conditions.

**Step 1: Laplace Transform of Input**

$$V_i(s) = \frac{V_m \cdot \omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 2: Output in s-domain**

$$V_o(s) = H_{LP}(s) \cdot V_i(s) = \frac{1}{1 + sRC} \cdot \frac{V_m \omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

Let $a = 1/RC$ for simplicity:

$$V_o(s) = \frac{V_m \omega}{(s + a)(s^2 + \omega^2)}$$

**Step 3: Partial Fraction Expansion**

$$V_o(s) = \frac{A}{s + a} + \frac{Bs + C}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

Multiplying both sides by $(s + a)(s^2 + \omega^2)$:

$$V_m \omega = A(s^2 + \omega^2) + (Bs + C)(s + a)$$

Expanding the right side:

$$V_m \omega = As^2 + A\omega^2 + Bs^2 + Bsa + Cs + Ca$$

Collecting terms:
- $s^2$ coefficient: $A + B = 0 \Rightarrow B = -A$
- $s^1$ coefficient: $Ba + C = 0 \Rightarrow C = aA$
- $s^0$ coefficient: $A\omega^2 + Ca = V_m \omega$

Substituting $C = aA$:

$$A\omega^2 + a^2 A = V_m \omega \Rightarrow A = \frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2}$$

Therefore:
- $A = \dfrac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2}$
- $B = -\dfrac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2}$
- $C = \dfrac{a V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2}$

**Step 4: Separate into Transient and Steady-State Parts**

$$V_o(s) = \frac{V_m \omega/(\omega^2 + a^2)}{s + a} + \frac{(-V_m \omega/(\omega^2 + a^2))s + (a V_m \omega/(\omega^2 + a^2))}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

The second term can be written as:

$$-\frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} \cdot \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2} + \frac{a V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} \cdot \frac{1}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

$$= -\frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} \cdot \frac{s}{s^2 + \omega^2} + \frac{a}{\omega} \cdot \frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} \cdot \frac{\omega}{s^2 + \omega^2}$$

**Step 5: Apply Inverse Laplace Transform**

$$v_o(t) = \underbrace{\frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} e^{-at}}_{\text{Transient}} - \underbrace{\frac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2} \cos(\omega t)}_{\text{Steady-State (Cosine)}} + \underbrace{\frac{V_m a}{\omega^2 + a^2} \sin(\omega t)}_{\text{Steady-State (Sine)}}$$

**Step 6: Combine Steady-State Terms into Single Sinusoid**

Combining cosine and sine into a single sinusoid $A\cos(\omega t) + B\sin(\omega t) = R\cos(\omega t - \phi)$ where $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$:

With $A = -\dfrac{V_m \omega}{\omega^2 + a^2}$ and $B = \dfrac{V_m a}{\omega^2 + a^2}$:

$$R = \frac{V_m}{\omega^2 + a^2}\sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\omega^2 + a^2}}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{B}{A}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{a}{-\omega}\right) = \pi - \tan^{-1}(a/\omega)$$

Substituting $a = 1/RC$:

$$R = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{\omega^2 + 1/(RC)^2}} = \frac{V_m \cdot RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$$

$$\phi = \pi - \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)$$

**Step 7: Final Combined Response**

$$\boxed{v_o(t) = \frac{V_m \omega RC}{1 + (\omega RC)^2} e^{-t/RC} + \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \sin(\omega t - \phi)}$$

where the transient decays as $t \to \infty$, leaving only the steady-state sinusoidal response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The steady-state response has the same frequency $\omega$ as the input, but reduced amplitude and shifted phase. The amplitude ratio $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ is the **frequency response magnitude** of the LP filter.

## 3.4 Derivation of Cutoff Frequency and Bandwidth

For a Low Pass filter, the maximum gain occurs at $\omega = 0$ (DC), where $\vert H_{LP}(0) \vert = 1$.

The cutoff frequency is where the gain drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c RC)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$1 + (\omega_c RC)^2 = 2$$

$$(\omega_c RC)^2 = 1$$

$$\omega_c = \frac{1}{RC}$$

The **bandwidth** of the LP circuit is therefore:

$$BW = \omega_c = \frac{1}{RC} \text{ rad/s} \quad \text{or} \quad f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC} \text{ Hz}$$

## 3.5 Solution with Non-Zero Initial Conditions

If the capacitor has an initial voltage $v_C(0^-) = V_0$, the s-domain capacitor model includes a voltage source $V_0/s$ in series with $1/sC$. The KVL becomes:

$$\frac{V}{s} = R \cdot I(s) + \frac{I(s)}{sC} + \frac{V_0}{s}$$

Solving for $I(s)$:

$$I(s) = \frac{V/s - V_0/s}{R + 1/sC} = \frac{V - V_0}{s(R + 1/sC)} = \frac{V - V_0}{sR + 1/C}$$

The capacitor voltage is:

$$V_C(s) = \frac{I(s)}{sC} + \frac{V_0}{s} = \frac{V - V_0}{s(sRC + 1)} + \frac{V_0}{s}$$

After partial fractions and inverse Laplace transform:

$$v_C(t) = (V - V_0)(1 - e^{-t/RC}) + V_0 = V - (V - V_0)e^{-t/RC}$$

This is the **generalized step response** that accounts for initial conditions.

## 4.1 Series RC Circuit – Physical Topology (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
    nodeA --> R[R Resistor]

    nodeA -.->|Output HP vo t| nodeB
    nodeB -.->|Output LP vo t| nodeG

    style nodeB fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Low Pass RC Step Response – Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step Input V u t] --> B[Apply KVL: vi = VR + VC]
    B --> C[Convert to s-domain: VIs = VCs 1 + sRC]
    C --> D[Transfer Function H s = 1 / 1 + sRC]
    D --> E[Multiply: Vo s = V / s 1 + sRC]
    E --> F[Partial Fraction Expansion]
    F --> G[Inverse Laplace Transform]
    G --> H[Time Domain Response: vo t = V 1 minus e to power minus t over RC]
    H --> I[Transient: e to power minus t over RC decays to 0]
    H --> J[Steady State: vo t approaches V]
    style A fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style H fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style I fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF
    style J fill:#4682B4,color:#FFF
```

## 4.3 High Pass RC Step Response – Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Step Input V u t] --> B[Output across Resistor R]
    B --> C[Transfer Function H s = sRC / 1 + sRC]
    C --> D[Vo s = V RC / 1 + sRC]
    D --> E[Simplify: Vo s = V / s + 1/RC]
    E --> F[Inverse Laplace Transform]
    F --> G[Time Domain: vo t = V e to power minus t over RC]
    G --> H[Initial: vo 0 plus = V maximum]
    G --> I[Final: vo infinity = 0 capacitor fully charged]
```
