Therefore:
$$\boxed{Y = 0.012 - j0.016 \, \text{S}}$$

**Part (c) — Magnitude and Phase of Y:**

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{(0.012)^2 + (-0.016)^2} = \sqrt{0.000144 + 0.000256} = \sqrt{0.0004} = 0.020 \, \text{S}$$

$$\theta_Y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-0.016}{0.012}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-1.333) = -53.13^\circ$$

$$\boxed{Y = 0.020 \angle -53.13^\circ \, \text{S}}$$

**Verification (using polar reciprocal):**

$$Y = \dfrac{1}{Z} = \dfrac{1}{50 \angle 53.13^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{50} \angle (-53.13^\circ) = 0.020 \angle -53.13^\circ \, \text{S} \quad \checkmark$$

**Part (d) — Current:**

Using $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y} \cdot \mathbf{V}$:

$$\mathbf{I} = (0.020 \angle -53.13^\circ) \times (100 \angle 30^\circ)$$

$$\mathbf{I} = (0.020 \times 100) \angle (-53.13^\circ + 30^\circ)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I} = 2.0 \angle -23.13^\circ \, \text{A}}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example — Parallel R, L, C Combination

**Problem:** Three elements $R = 10 \, \Omega$, $X_L = 5 \, \Omega$, and $X_C = 8 \, \Omega$ are connected in **parallel**. Compute the equivalent admittance.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute individual admittances:**

Resistor branch:
$$Y_R = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1 \, \text{S}$$

Inductor branch:
$$Y_L = \dfrac{1}{jX_L} = -\dfrac{j}{X_L} = -\dfrac{j}{5} = 0 - j0.2 \, \text{S}$$

Capacitor branch:
$$Y_C = \dfrac{1}{-jX_C} = \dfrac{j}{X_C} = \dfrac{j}{8} = 0 + j0.125 \, \text{S}$$

**Step 2 — Add admittances directly (parallel combination):**

$$Y_{eq} = Y_R + Y_L + Y_C$$

$$Y_{eq} = 0.1 + (0 - j0.2) + (0 + j0.125)$$

Real part: $G_{eq} = 0.1 \, \text{S}$

Imaginary part: $B_{eq} = -0.2 + 0.125 = -0.075 \, \text{S}$

$$\boxed{Y_{eq} = 0.1 - j0.075 \, \text{S}}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and phase:**

$$\vert Y_{eq} \vert = \sqrt{(0.1)^2 + (-0.075)^2} = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.005625} = \sqrt{0.015625} = 0.125 \, \text{S}$$

$$\theta_Y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-0.075}{0.1}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.75) = -36.87^\circ$$

$$\boxed{Y_{eq} = 0.125 \angle -36.87^\circ \, \text{S}}$$

## 3.4 Conversion Symmetry: $G \leftrightarrow R$ and $B \leftrightarrow X$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A crucial KTU insight:** Although $Y$ and $Z$ are reciprocals, the conversion $G = 1/R$ and $B = 1/X$ is **ONLY** valid for *pure* elements. For *mixed* circuits (R-L, R-C, L-C), the conversion must use the full formula $G = \dfrac{R}{R^2 + X^2}$ and $B = -\dfrac{X}{R^2 + X^2}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Admittance in Circuit Analysis Pipeline

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the analytical workflow for using admittance in AC circuit analysis:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Circuit Source Voltage V and Frequency omega] --> B[Identify Circuit Topology]
    B --> C{Series or Parallel?}
    C -- Series --> D[Compute Z = R + jX]
    D --> E[Compute Y = 1/Z]
    E --> F[Convert to G + jB Form]
    C -- Parallel --> G[Compute Individual Branch Y]
    G --> H[Sum Yeq = Sum of All Y branches]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Extract Magnitude Y and Phase theta]
    I --> J[Compute Current I = Y * V]
    J --> K[Determine Circuit Behavior]
    K --> L{Inductive or Capacitive?}
    L -- B is Negative --> M[Inductive Circuit]
    L -- B is Positive --> N[Capacitive Circuit]
    L -- B is Zero --> O[Purely Resistive Circuit]
```

## 4.2 Admittance Triangle Diagram

The relationship between conductance, susceptance, and admittance can be visualized as a right triangle:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Origin 0,0] -->|G along Real axis| B[Point G, 0]
    B -->|jB along Imaginary axis| C[Point G, B]
    A -->|Y vector, magnitude Y| C
    C -.->|Phase angle theta| A
```

In the admittance triangle:
- The **horizontal side** represents the conductance $G$.
- The **vertical side** represents the susceptance $B$.
- The **hypotenuse** is the admittance vector $Y$ with magnitude $\vert Y \vert$.
- The angle between $G$ and $Y$ is the admittance phase $\theta_Y$.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Y-Δ Transformation Functional Flow

For module completeness, here is how admittance plays into Y-Δ (or admittance-impedance) transformations:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Three Terminal Network] --> B{Connection Type}
    B -- Star or Y --> C[Express as Y Matrix]
    B -- Delta --> D[Express as Y Matrix]
    C --> E[Convert via Y to Delta Transformation]
    D --> F[Convert via Delta to Y Transformation]
    E --> G[Simplified Equivalent Circuit]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Solve Using Nodal Analysis]
    H --> I[Final Node Voltages and Branch Currents]
```

## 4.4 Admittance in R, L, C Element Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pure Resistor R] --> A1[Y = 1/R = G only Real]
    B[Pure Inductor L] --> B1[Y = 1 over j omega L = negative j B]
    C[Pure Capacitor C] --> C1[Y = j omega C = positive j B]
    A1 --> D[Combine in Parallel]
    B1 --> D
    C1 --> D
    D --> E[Equivalent Y eq equals Sum G plus j Sum B]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define admittance and state its unit. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Admittance is defined as the reciprocal of impedance. It represents the ease with which an AC circuit allows current to flow. Mathematically,

$$Y = \dfrac{1}{Z} = \dfrac{I}{V}$$

It is a complex quantity having the form $Y = G + jB$, where $G$ is conductance and $B$ is susceptance. The unit of admittance is **Siemens (S)**, also called **mho ($\Omega^{-1}$)**.

### Question 2: Differentiate between conductance and susceptance. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Conductance (G) | Susceptance (B) |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Nature** | Real part of admittance | Imaginary part of admittance |
| **Represents** | Ability to dissipate power | Ability to store energy |
| **Formula** | $G = \dfrac{R}{R^2 + X^2}$ | $B = -\dfrac{X}{R^2 + X^2}$ |
| **Sign** | Always positive | Negative for inductive, positive for capacitive |
| **Unit** | Siemens (S) | Siemens (S) |

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

A series R-L-C circuit has $R = 6 \, \Omega$, $L = 25.5 \, \text{mH}$, and $C = 159 \, \mu F$, operating at $f = 50 \, \text{Hz}$ with a supply voltage of $\mathbf{V} = 100 \angle 0^\circ \, V$.

**(a) Calculate the impedance, admittance, and current drawn by the circuit.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Compute the conductance, susceptance, and the phase angle of the admittance. Draw the admittance triangle.** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Impedance, Admittance, and Current **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Compute Reactances:**

Inductive reactance:

$$X_L = 2\pi f L = 2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 25.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$X_L = 8.0 \, \Omega$$

Capacitive reactance:

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times \pi \times 50 \times 159 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$X_C = \dfrac{1}{0.04995} \approx 20.02 \, \Omega$$

**Step 2 — Net Reactance:**

$$X = X_L - X_C = 8.0 - 20.02 = -12.02 \, \Omega$$

(Negative sign indicates net capacitive behavior.)

**Step 3 — Impedance:**

$$Z = R + jX = 6 - j12.02 \, \Omega$$

Magnitude:

$$\vert Z \vert = \sqrt{6^2 + (-12.02)^2} = \sqrt{36 + 144.48} = \sqrt{180.48} \approx 13.43 \, \Omega$$

Phase:

$$\theta_Z = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-12.02}{6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-2.003) \approx -63.46^\circ$$

Polar form:

$$Z = 13.43 \angle -63.46^\circ \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Admittance:**

In polar form (quickest):

$$Y = \dfrac{1}{Z} = \dfrac{1}{13.43 \angle -63.46^\circ} = 0.0745 \angle 63.46^\circ \, \text{S}$$

In rectangular form:

$$G = \dfrac{R}{R^2 + X^2} = \dfrac{6}{36 + 144.48} = \dfrac{6}{180.48} \approx 0.0332 \, \text{S}$$

$$B = -\dfrac{X}{R^2 + X^2} = -\dfrac{-12.02}{180.48} = \dfrac{12.02}{180.48} \approx 0.0666 \, \text{S}$$

$$\boxed{Y = 0.0332 + j0.0666 \, \text{S}}$$

**Step 5 — Current:**

$$\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y} \cdot \mathbf{V} = (0.0745 \angle 63.46^\circ) \times (100 \angle 0^\circ)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I} = 7.45 \angle 63.46^\circ \, \text{A}}$$

#### Part (b) — Conductance, Susceptance, and Admittance Triangle **[7 Marks]**

From part (a):

$$\boxed{G = 0.0332 \, \text{S}}$$

$$\boxed{B = +0.0666 \, \text{S (capacitive)}}$$

Magnitude verification:

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{G^2 + B^2} = \sqrt{(0.0332)^2 + (0.0666)^2}$$

$$\vert Y \vert = \sqrt{0.00110 + 0.00444} = \sqrt{0.00554} = 0.0745 \, \text{S} \quad \checkmark$$

Phase angle:

$$\theta_Y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.0666}{0.0332}\right) = \tan^{-1}(2.006) \approx 63.50^\circ$$

$$\boxed{\theta_Y \approx 63.46^\circ}$$

**Admittance Triangle:**

```
                  Imaginary axis (B)
                       |
                       |  B = +0.0666 S
                       |
                       |       /
                       |      /
                       |     /  |Y| = 0.0745 S
                       |    /
                       |   /  theta_Y = 63.46°
                       |  /
                       | /
        ---------------+-------------------- Real axis (G)
                       |
                      G = 0.0332 S
```

Since $B$ is **positive**, the circuit is **capacitive**.

### Question B **[14 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

A parallel combination consists of three branches:
- Branch 1: Resistor $R = 20 \, \Omega$
- Branch 2: Inductor with $X_L = 30 \, \Omega$
- Branch 3: Capacitor with $X_C = 60 \, \Omega$

The supply voltage is $\mathbf{V} = 200 \angle 0^\circ \, V$ at $\omega = 1000 \, \text{rad/s}$.

**(a) Determine the admittance of each branch and the equivalent admittance of the combination.** **[7 Marks]**

**(b) Calculate the total current, magnitude of admittance, and the circuit's overall phase angle.** **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Branch Admittances and Equivalent Admittance **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Resistor Branch:**

$$Y_R = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{1}{20} = 0.05 + j0 \, \text{S}$$

**Step 2 — Inductor Branch:**

$$Y_L = \dfrac{1}{jX_L} = \dfrac{1}{j30} = -\dfrac{j}{30} = 0 - j0.0333 \, \text{S}$$

**Step 3 — Capacitor Branch:**

$$Y_C = \dfrac{1}{-jX_C} = \dfrac{j}{X_C} = \dfrac{j}{60} = 0 + j0.01667 \, \text{S}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Admittance (Parallel Sum):**

$$Y_{eq} = Y_R + Y_L + Y_C$$

$$Y_{eq} = (0.05) + (-j0.0333) + (j0.01667)$$

Real part: $G_{eq} = 0.05 \, \text{S}$

Imaginary part: $B_{eq} = -0.0333 + 0.01667 = -0.01667 \, \text{S}$

$$\boxed{Y_{eq} = 0.05 - j0.01667 \, \text{S}}$$

#### Part (b) — Total Current, Magnitude, and Phase Angle **[7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Magnitude:**

$$\vert Y_{eq} \vert = \sqrt{(0.05)^2 + (-0.01667)^2}$$

$$\vert Y_{eq} \vert = \sqrt{0.0025 + 0.000278} = \sqrt{0.002778} = 0.05270 \, \text{S}$$

**Step 2 — Phase Angle:**

$$\theta_Y = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{-0.01667}{0.05}\right) = \tan^{-1}(-0.3333) = -18.43^\circ$$

$$\boxed{Y_{eq} = 0.0527 \angle -18.43^\circ \, \text{S}}$$

**Step 3 — Total Current:**

$$\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y_{eq}} \cdot \mathbf{V} = (0.0527 \angle -18.43^\circ) \times (200 \angle 0^\circ)$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{I} = 10.54 \angle -18.43^\circ \, \text{A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in susceptance:** Students frequently write $B = +X/(R^2+X^2)$ forgetting the **negative sign**. The correct formula is $B = -X/(R^2+X^2)$.
> 2. **Confusing polar conversion of susceptance:** When converting to polar form, remember that a *negative* imaginary part places the admittance in the **fourth quadrant** (inductive), giving a **negative** phase angle.
> 3. **Inductor admittance:** Many students write $Y_L = j\omega L$ instead of $Y_L = \dfrac{1}{j\omega L} = -\dfrac{j}{\omega L}$. The impedance of an inductor is $j\omega L$, NOT $1/j\omega L$.
> 4. **Capacitor admittance:** Conversely, $Y_C = j\omega C$ (positive imaginary), because the impedance of a capacitor is $\dfrac{1}{j\omega C}$.
> 5. **Unit confusion:** Do not write admittance in ohms. Admittance is in **Siemens (S)** or **mho ($\Omega^{-1}$)**.
> 6. **Mixing admittance and impedance:** Admittance is the reciprocal of impedance; if you are given impedance and asked to find admittance, take the reciprocal — do not confuse the angle sign.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Admittance $Y$** is the reciprocal of impedance $Z$, measured in **Siemens (S)** or **mho**.
- **Rectangular form:** $Y = G + jB$ where $G$ is conductance and $B$ is susceptance.
- **Polar form:** $Y = \vert Y \vert \angle \theta_Y$.
- **Conductance formula (from mixed impedance):** $G = \dfrac{R}{R^2 + X^2}$.
- **Susceptance formula (from mixed impedance):** $B = -\dfrac{X}{R^2 + X^2}$.
- **Magnitude relation:** $\vert Y \vert = \dfrac{1}{\vert Z \vert}$.
- **Phase relation:** $\theta_Y = -\theta_Z$.
- **Inductive susceptance** ($B_L$) is **negative**: $B_L = -\dfrac{1}{\omega L}$.
- **Capacitive susceptance** ($B_C$) is **positive**: $B_C = \omega C$.
- **Pure resistor:** $Y_R = G = \dfrac{1}{R}$ with $B = 0$.
- **Pure inductor:** $Y_L = -\dfrac{j}{\omega L}$ (no real part).
- **Pure capacitor:** $Y_C = j\omega C$ (no real part).
- **Parallel combination:** $Y_{eq} = Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + \ldots$ (simple algebraic addition).
- **Series combination:** Compute $Z_{eq}$ first, then $Y_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{Z_{eq}}$.
- **Admittance triangle:** $G$ is the horizontal (real) side, $B$ is the vertical (imaginary) side, $\vert Y \vert$ is the hypotenuse.
- **Current computation:** $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y} \cdot \mathbf{V}$.
- **Sign of $B$** indicates circuit behavior: negative = inductive, positive = capacitive, zero = purely resistive.
- **KTU focus area:** Be fluent in both polar-reciprocal computation AND rectangular $G + jB$ derivation — examiners test both pathways.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid and Transmission Parameters of Two-port Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Hybrid and Transmission Parameters of Two-port Networks # 🔷 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Two-Port Network — A Formal Refresher A **Two-Port Network** is an electrical network contained within a closed boundary, accessible through exactly **two pairs of terminals** (Port 1 and Port 2). Each port is characterized by a voltage across it and a current flowing into it. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU Convention (2024 Scheme):** All port currents ($I_1$, $I_2$) are assumed to flow *into* the network, and all port voltages ($V_1$, $V_2$) are measured across the port terminals with the *positive* reference at the top terminal. For a linear, bilateral, lumped, time-invariant two-port network, any two of the four port variables ($V_1, V_2, I_1, I_2$) can be expressed as linear functions of the other two. Each independent choice of dependent and independent variables produces a distinct **parameter set** (six in total: Z, Y, ABCD, A'B'C'D', h, g). --- ## 1.2 The Six Parameter Families at a Glance | Independent Variables | Dependent Variables | Parameter Name | Physical Significance | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $I_1, I_2$ | $V_1, V_2$ | **Z-parameters** (Impedance) | Open-circuit characterization | | $V_1, V_2$ | $I_1, I_2$ | **Y-parameters** (Admittance) | Short-circuit characterization | | $V_2, -I_2$ | $V_1, I_1$ | **ABCD-parameters** (Transmission) | Cascade-friendly; transmission line use | | $V_1, -I_1$ | $V_2, I_2$ | **A'B'C'D'-parameters** (Inverse Transmission) | Reverse cascade | | $I_1, V_2$ | $V_1, I_2$ | **h-parameters** (Hybrid) | Transistor modeling (CE configuration) | | $V_1, I_2$ | $I_1, V_2$ | **g-parameters** (Inverse Hybrid) | Transistor modeling (CB configuration) | --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies — Why So Many Parameters? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy — "The Translator's Dilemma":** > Imagine you are a translator between two foreign languages (Port 1 and Port 2). You can choose to express one language *as a function of* the other. Each parameter set is simply a *different grammar* for describing the *same conversation* between input and output. Some grammars are more convenient depending on the situation: > - **Z-parameters** are easiest when output is naturally *open* (like a voltmeter). > - **Y-parameters** are easiest when output is naturally *shorted* (like an ammeter). > - **h-parameters** are natural for transistors because the *input is current-driven* and the *output is voltage-sensed* — a perfect "hybrid" mix. > - **ABCD-parameters** are the *chain-rule* of networks: you can multiply matrices to describe a *cascade* (like linking train cars). --- ## 1.4 Formal Definition of the Three Parameter Sets in Focus ### (A) Hybrid (h) Parameters $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ - $h_{11} = \dfrac{V_1}{I_1}\Big\vert_{V_2=0}$ → **Input impedance with output shorted (units: Ω)** - $h_{12} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\Big\vert_{I_1=0}$ → **Reverse voltage gain with input open (dimensionless)** - $h_{21} = \dfrac{I_2}{I_1}\Big\vert_{V_2=0}$ → **Forward current gain with output shorted (dimensionless)** - $h_{22} = \dfrac{I_2}{V_2}\Big\vert_{I_1=0}$ → **Output admittance with input open (units: S)** ### (B) Inverse Hybrid (g) Parameters $$ \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ - $g_{11} = \dfrac{I_1}{V_1}\Big\vert_{I_2=0}$ → **Input admittance with output open (units: S)** - $g_{12} = \dfrac{I_1}{I_2}\Big\vert_{V_1=0}$ → **Reverse current gain with input shorted (dimensionless)** - $g_{21} = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\Big\vert_{I_2=0}$ → **Forward voltage gain with output open (dimensionless)** - $g_{22} = \dfrac{V_2}{I_2}\Big\vert_{V_1=0}$ → **Output impedance with input shorted (units: Ω)** ### (C) Transmission (ABCD) Parameters $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL KTU Convention Note:** The negative sign on $I_2$ in the ABCD formulation is *not* a typo. It arises because the *transmitted* current $-I_2$ flows *out* of port 2 (away from the network) into the next stage. This sign convention is the **single most common source of marks lost** in KTU exam answers. Always show this clearly. - $A = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\Big\vert_{I_2=0}$ → **Reverse voltage ratio (dimensionless)** - $B = -\dfrac{V_1}{I_2}\Big\vert_{V_2=0}$ → **Negative short-circuit transfer impedance (units: Ω)** - $C = \dfrac{I_1}{V_2}\Big\vert_{I_2=0}$ → **Reverse transfer admittance (units: S)** - $D = -\dfrac{I_1}{I_2}\Big\vert_{V_2=0}$ → **Negative reverse current ratio (dimensionless)** ### (D) Inverse Transmission (A'B'C'D') Parameters $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ -I_1 \end{bmatrix} $$ # 🔷 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 'Why' Behind the Parameter Choice ### Why h-parameters? (The Transistor's Native Language) A **Bipolar Junction Transistor (BJT)** in common-emitter configuration has an input that is naturally *current-driven* (low input impedance at the base) and an output that is naturally *voltage-sensed* (high output impedance at the collector). The h-parameter set, being a *hybrid* mix of V and I, is the most natural way to describe it. This is why every KTU electronics and network-theory problem involving a CE transistor uses h-parameters. ### Why ABCD-parameters? (The Cascader's Delight) Two-port networks in **transmission lines, filter design, and cascaded amplifier stages** are *chained* together. A signal enters port 1, exits port 2, and *that* exit becomes the input to the next stage. The ABCD matrix multiplication rule is what makes analysis of cascaded systems trivial: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = [T_1] \cdot [T_2] \cdot [T_3] \cdots \begin{bmatrix} V_{n} \\ -I_{n} \end{bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cascade Property:** Only ABCD parameters have a clean multiplicative property for series-cascaded networks. For h, g, Z, or Y parameters, the overall matrix is *not* a simple product — this is the key reason ABCD is taught separately. ### Why g-parameters? (The Common-Base Counterpart) g-parameters are the dual of h-parameters. They are useful for the **common-base** transistor configuration where the input is voltage-driven and the output is current-sensed. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Notation:** $\Delta_h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}$ (determinant of h-matrix). All conversions below are **exam-favorite** problems. ### Table 2A: Definition Equations | Parameter Set | Defining Equation Matrix | Independent Variables | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **h (Hybrid)** | $V_1 = h_{11}I_1 + h_{12}V_2$ $\vert$ $I_2 = h_{21}I_1 + h_{22}V_2$ | $I_1$ and $V_2$ | | **g (Inverse Hybrid)** | $I_1 = g_{11}V_1 + g_{12}I_2$ $\vert$ $V_2 = g_{21}V_1 + g_{22}I_2$ | $V_1$ and $I_2$ | | **ABCD (Transmission)** | $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ $\vert$ $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ | $V_2$ and $-I_2$ | | **A'B'C'D' (Inverse T)** | $V_2 = A' V_1 - B' I_1$ $\vert$ $I_2 = C' V_1 - D' I_1$ | $V_1$ and $-I_1$ | ### Table 2B: ABCD Parameters of Standard Building Blocks | Network Type | A | B | C | D | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Series Impedance Z** | $1$ | $Z$ | $0$ | $1$ | | **Shunt Admittance Y** | $1$ | $0$ | $Y$ | $1$ | | **Ideal Transformer (n:1)** | $n$ | $0$ | $0$ | $1/n$ | | **Symmetric T-Network** | $(Z_1+Z_2)/Z_2$ for numerator, etc. | complex | complex | complex | | **Transmission Line** (lossless, length $\ell$, $Z_0$) | $\cos\beta\ell$ | $j Z_0 \sin\beta\ell$ | $j Y_0 \sin\beta\ell$ | $\cos\beta\ell$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The $1 - AD - BC = 0$ identity:** For a *reciprocal* network, $AD - BC = 1$. This is a *universal* property of all six parameter sets (with appropriate reformulation). Always verify this identity in your solution as a self-check. ### Table 2C: Reciprocity & Symmetry Conditions Across All Sets | Property | ABCD Condition | h-Condition | g-Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Reciprocity** | $AD - BC = 1$ | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ | | **Symmetry** | $A = D$ | $\Delta_h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ | $\Delta_g = g_{11}g_{22} - g_{12}g_{21} = 1$ | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Parameter Set | Primary Application Domain | | :--- | :--- | | **h-parameters** | BJT small-signal modeling, two-port representation of transistor amplifiers, microwave hybrid integrated circuits | | **g-parameters** | Common-base transistor stages, certain high-frequency amplifier designs | | **ABCD-parameters** | Transmission line theory, cascaded filter networks, lattice networks, network synthesis via Darlington's method, automated S-parameter conversion in RF CAD tools | | **A'B'C'D'-parameters** | Reverse-direction cascade analysis, image impedance calculations | > [!NOTE] > **Industry Note:** In modern RF engineering (post-2000), the **S-parameters (Scattering parameters)** have largely replaced ABCD for high-frequency work because they handle impedance mismatches gracefully. However, KTU undergraduate curriculum focuses on ABCD because (a) the math is simpler, (b) it directly extends to transmission line theory, and (c) ABCD ↔ S conversion is a standard exam problem. # 🔷 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Converting h-parameters to ABCD-parameters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Favourite Problem (7 marks typical):** "Express ABCD parameters in terms of h-parameters" — learn this derivation cold. ### Given (h-parameter equations): $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11}\,I_1 + h_{12}\,V_2 \quad \text{...(1)} \\ I_2 &= h_{21}\,I_1 + h_{22}\,V_2 \quad \text{...(2)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Target (ABCD-parameter equations): $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= A\,V_2 - B\,I_2 \quad \text{...(3)} \\ I_1 &= C\,V_2 - D\,I_2 \quad \text{...(4)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 1: Solve equation (2) for $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_2$. Rearranging (2): $$ \begin{aligned} I_2 - h_{22}\,V_2 &= h_{21}\,I_1 \\ I_1 &= \frac{I_2 - h_{22}\,V_2}{h_{21}} \\ I_1 &= -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}\,V_2 + \frac{1}{h_{21}}\,I_2 \\ I_1 &= \left(-\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2 + \left(\frac{1}{h_{21}}\right)I_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2: Compare with equation (4) to read off C and D. Equation (4) has the form $I_1 = C\,V_2 - D\,I_2$. Equating: $$ \boxed{\;C = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} \quad \text{and} \quad D = -\frac{1}{h_{21}}\;} $$ > Note the negative sign on D — it comes from the $-I_2$ convention in ABCD. ### Step 3: Substitute the new expression for $I_1$ into equation (1). $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11}\left[\left(-\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2 + \left(\frac{1}{h_{21}}\right)I_2\right] + h_{12}\,V_2 \\ V_1 &= -\frac{h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}}\,V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\,I_2 + h_{12}\,V_2 \\ V_1 &= \left(h_{12} - \frac{h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\,I_2 \\ V_1 &= \left(\frac{h_{12}h_{21} - h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\,I_2 \\ V_1 &= -\left(\frac{h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{21}}\right)V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\,I_2 \\ V_1 &= -\frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}}\,V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\,I_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: Compare with equation (3) to read off A and B. Equation (3) has the form $V_1 = A\,V_2 - B\,I_2$. Equating: $$ \boxed{\;A = -\frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}} \quad \text{and} \quad B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}\;} $$ ### Final Result — Master Conversion Box (h → ABCD): $$ \begin{aligned} A &= -\frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}}, \quad B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} \\ C &= -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}, \quad D = -\frac{1}{h_{21}} \\ &\quad \text{where } \Delta_h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Self-Check via Reciprocity:** If the h-network is reciprocal ($h_{12} = -h_{21}$), then $\Delta_h = h_{11}h_{22} + h_{21}^2$. Substituting, $AD - BC = \frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}^2} - \frac{h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}^2} = \frac{\Delta_h - h_{11}h_{22}}{h_{21}^2} = \frac{-h_{12}h_{21}}{h_{21}^2} = -\frac{h_{12}}{h_{21}} = 1$ ✓ (since $h_{12} = -h_{21}$). --- ## 3.2 Derivation: ABCD of a Cascaded Two-Port Network > [!IMPORTANT] > **This is the #1 KTU question pattern** for the transmission parameters module. ### Setup: Two two-port networks, $N_a$ and $N_b$, are connected in *cascade* (output of $N_a$ feeds input of $N_b$). Denote their transmission matrices as $T_a$ and $T_b$: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = T_a \begin{bmatrix} V_{2a} \\ -I_{2a} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{and} \quad \begin{bmatrix} V_{1b} \\ I_{1b} \end{bmatrix} = T_b \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 1: Identify the cascade boundary conditions. The output port of $N_a$ is identical to the input port of $N_b$. Thus: $$ V_{2a} = V_{1b} \quad \text{and} \quad I_{2a} = -I_{1b} $$ (Notice the **negative sign**: $I_{2a}$ flows *out* of $N_a$, but $I_{1b}$ flows *into* $N_b$, and these are equal in magnitude and opposite in direction along the connecting wire — Kuhm's Current Law gives $I_{2a} = -I_{1b}$.) ### Step 2: Express the boundary in terms of transmission variables. From the second network's equation: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_{1b} \\ I_{1b} \end{bmatrix} = T_b \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ Inverting: $T_b^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} V_{1b} \\ I_{1b} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ But $V_{1b} = V_{2a}$ and $I_{1b} = -I_{2a}$, so: $$ T_b^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} V_{2a} \\ -I_{2a} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ ### Step 3: Substitute into the first network's equation. From the first network: $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = T_a \begin{bmatrix} V_{2a} \\ -I_{2a} \end{bmatrix} = T_a \cdot T_b \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ ### Final Result — Cascade Master Equation: $$ \boxed{\;T_{\text{cascade}} = T_a \cdot T_b\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Order matters!** $T_a \cdot T_b \neq T_b \cdot T_a$ in general. The matrix on the *left* corresponds to the network *closest to the source* (port 1 side). The matrix on the *right* corresponds to the network *closest to the load* (port 2 side). A common KTU error: students multiply in the wrong order. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Symmetry & Reciprocity in ABCD Form ### Reciprocity Condition: A two-port is **reciprocal** if $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ (in Z-form). Expressing in ABCD form via the identity $AD - BC = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{21}}$ (a known result from parameter interconversion), we get: $$ \boxed{\;AD - BC = 1 \quad \text{(Reciprocity condition)}\;} $$ ### Symmetry Condition: A two-port is **symmetric** if, in addition to reciprocity, the input and output ports can be interchanged without changing the network's external behavior. This means $A = D$. $$ \boxed{\;A = D \quad \text{(Symmetry condition, assuming reciprocity already holds)}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Quick-Recall Trick:** For a *symmetric* reciprocal network, the determinant is 1 *and* the diagonal entries of the matrix are equal. So $A = D$ and $AD - BC = 1$ becomes $A^2 - BC = 1$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example **Problem:** Find the ABCD parameters of a simple T-network with series arm $Z_1$ (left), series arm $Z_2$ (right), and shunt arm $Z_3$ (middle). ### Method: Cascade of Three Building Blocks A T-network can be decomposed as: (Series $Z_1$) → (Shunt $Z_3$) → (Series $Z_2$). Step 1 — ABCD of Series Impedance $Z_1$: $$ T_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Step 2 — ABCD of Shunt Admittance $Y_3 = 1/Z_3$: $$ T_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ Y_3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1/Z_3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Step 3 — ABCD of Series Impedance $Z_2$: $$ T_3 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ Step 4 — Multiply (source-side to load-side order): $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{T-network}} &= T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1/Z_3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4a: Multiply $T_2 \cdot T_3$ first (rightmost pair): $$ \begin{aligned} T_2 \cdot T_3 &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \\ 1/Z_3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot1 + 0\cdot0 & 1\cdot Z_2 + 0\cdot 1 \\ (1/Z_3)\cdot 1 + 1\cdot 0 & (1/Z_3)\cdot Z_2 + 1\cdot 1 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 1/Z_3 & (Z_2/Z_3) + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 1/Z_3 & (Z_2 + Z_3)/Z_3 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4b: Multiply $T_1 \cdot (T_2 \cdot T_3)$: $$ \begin{aligned} T_{\text{T}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & Z_2 \\ 1/Z_3 & (Z_2 + Z_3)/Z_3 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1\cdot 1 + Z_1\cdot (1/Z_3) & 1\cdot Z_2 + Z_1\cdot (Z_2+Z_3)/Z_3 \\ 0\cdot 1 + 1\cdot (1/Z_3) & 0\cdot Z_2 + 1\cdot (Z_2+Z_3)/Z_3 \end{bmatrix} \\ &= \begin{bmatrix} 1 + Z_1/Z_3 & Z_2 + Z_1(Z_2+Z_3)/Z_3 \\ 1/Z_3 & (Z_2+Z_3)/Z_3 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Result — ABCD of General T-Network: $$ \boxed{\;T_{\text{T}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{Z_1 + Z_3}{Z_3} & \dfrac{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3}{Z_3} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{Z_3} & \dfrac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3} \end{bmatrix}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of reciprocity:** $A \cdot D - B \cdot C = \frac{(Z_1+Z_3)(Z_2+Z_3)}{Z_3^2} - \frac{(Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3)}{Z_3^2} = \frac{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3 + Z_3^2 - Z_1 Z_2 - Z_1 Z_3 - Z_2 Z_3}{Z_3^2} = \frac{Z_3^2}{Z_3^2} = 1$ ✓ # 🔷 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 General Two-Port Network Representation The following block diagram establishes the universal sign convention used throughout KTU exam answers. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Voltage Source
or Input Signal] -->|I1 flows in| p1Top(( )) p1Top --- p1Bottom(( )) p1Bottom --- gnd1[Reference Node] p1Top -- V1 measured across --> p1Bottom subgraph twoport[" TWO-PORT NETWORK N "] box[Linear Bilateral
Lumped Network
Internal Structure] end p1Top --- box p1Bottom --- box box --- p2Top(( )) box --- p2Bottom(( )) p2Top -- V2 measured across --> p2Bottom p2Bottom --- gnd2[Reference Node] p2Top -->|I2 flows in| load[Load Impedance
ZL] load --> p2Bottom style twoport fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#1e6bb8,stroke-width:2px style box fill:#fff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style src fill:#d4edda style load fill:#f8d7da ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key Convention Reminder:** $I_1$ and $I_2$ are defined as *currents flowing INTO* the network. The load $Z_L$ sits *outside* the two-port boundary. The voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$ are measured with positive reference at the *top* terminal of each port. --- ## 4.2 h-Parameter Equivalent Circuit The h-parameter equivalent circuit uses one *voltage-controlled voltage source* and one *current-controlled current source*, making it ideal for transistor modeling. ```mermaid flowchart LR p1node((P1+)) --- r11[Resistor h11] r11 --- p1bn((P1-)) p1node --- cvs{{VCVS: h12 times V2}} cvs --- p1bn p2node((P2+)) --- ccs[/CCS: h21 times I1/] ccs --- p2node p2node --- g22[/Admittance h22/] g22 --- p2bn((P2-)) p1bn --- p2bn p1node -- I1 enters --> r11 cvs -. senses V2 .-> p2node ccs -. senses I1 .-> r11 style cvs fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style ccs fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style r11 fill:#d1ecf1 style g22 fill:#d1ecf1 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Equivalent Circuit Reading Guide:** > - The **input port (left)** behaves as a resistance $h_{11}$ in series with a VCVS $h_{12} V_2$ (output-voltage feedback to input). > - The **output port (right)** behaves as a current source $h_{21} I_1$ in parallel with an admittance $h_{22}$. --- ## 4.3 g-Parameter Equivalent Circuit (Dual of h-Parameters) ```mermaid flowchart LR p1node((P1+)) --- g11[/Admittance g11/] g11 --- p1bn((P1-)) p1node --- ccs2[/CCS: g12 times I2/] ccs2 --- p1bn p2node((P2+)) --- cvs2{{VCVS: g21 times V1}} cvs2 --- p2bn((P2-)) p1bn --- p2bn p1node -- V1 across --> g11 ccs2 -. senses I2 .-> p2node cvs2 -. senses V1 .-> p1node style cvs2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 style ccs2 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style g11 fill:#d1ecf1 ``` --- ## 4.4 Cascade Connection of Two Two-Port Networks This is the structural reason ABCD parameters are so useful — they multiply cleanly for cascaded stages. ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Input Source
V1, I1] --> pa1[Two-Port Network A
ABCD matrix Ta] pa1 -- Output port 2 of A --> midwire((Interconnection Node)) midwire -- Input port 1 of B --> pb1[Two-Port Network B
ABCD matrix Tb] pb1 --> load[Load
V2, I2] subgraph cascadeResult[" OVERALL CASCADE "] cr[Equivalent Single Two-Port
T cascade = Ta times Tb] end src -. becomes input of .-> cr load -. becomes output of .-> cr style pa1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style pb1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085 style cascadeResult fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,stroke-width:2px style cr fill:#fff,stroke:#155724 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Cascade Order Convention:** The matrix $T_a$ (left network) is multiplied *on the left* of $T_b$ (right network): $T_{\text{cascade}} = T_a \cdot T_b$. The signal flows from $T_a$ into $T_b$. --- ## 4.5 Functional Block Architecture — How the Parameter Sets Interact ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph inputs["PORT VARIABLES"] v1[V1] v2[V2] i1[I1] i2[I2] end subgraph parameterSets["SIX PARAMETER FAMILIES"] zparams[Z-Parameters
Open circuit] yparams[Y-Parameters
Short circuit] abcd[ABCD-Parameters
Cascade] invt[A'B'C'D'-Parameters
Reverse Cascade] hparams[h-Parameters
Hybrid] gparams[g-Parameters
Inverse Hybrid] end subgraph outputs["EQUIVALENT CIRCUITS"] zcirc[Z equivalent circuit
Voltage controlled] ycirc[Y equivalent circuit
Current controlled] hcirc[h equivalent circuit
Mixed sources] end v1 --> zparams i1 --> zparams i2 --> zparams v1 --> yparams v2 --> yparams i1 --> hparams v2 --> hparams v2 --> abcd i2 --> abcd v1 --> invt i1 --> invt zparams --> zcirc yparams --> ycirc hparams --> hcirc abcd --> gparams invt --> gparams style inputs fill:#fff3cd style parameterSets fill:#cce5ff style outputs fill:#d4edda ``` # 🔷 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define hybrid (h) parameters of a two-port network. List all four parameters with their defining equations and physical units.** **Model Answer:** Hybrid parameters (or h-parameters) represent a two-port network through a hybrid mixture of impedance, admittance, voltage ratio, and current ratio. The defining equations are: $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11}\,I_1 + h_{12}\,V_2 \\ I_2 &= h_{21}\,I_1 + h_{22}\,V_2 \end{aligned} $$ | Parameter | Definition | Physical Meaning | Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $h_{11}$ | $V_1/I_1 \;\vert\; V_2=0$ | Input impedance (output shorted) | Ω | | $h_{12}$ | $V_1/V_2 \;\vert\; I_1=0$ | Reverse voltage gain (input open) | dimensionless | | $h_{21}$ | $I_2/I_1 \;\vert\; V_2=0$ | Forward current gain (output shorted) | dimensionless | | $h_{22}$ | $I_2/V_2 \;\vert\; I_1=0$ | Output admittance (input open) | Siemens (S) | These parameters are called "hybrid" because their dimensions are not all the same. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Why are ABCD parameters particularly useful for the analysis of cascaded two-port networks? Justify with the relevant mathematical relation.** **Model Answer:** ABCD parameters (transmission parameters) are particularly suited for cascade analysis because the overall transmission matrix of two cascaded networks is the **simple matrix product** of the individual transmission matrices. If two networks with matrices $[T_1]$ and $[T_2]$ are connected in cascade (output of $T_1$ to input of $T_2$), the overall matrix is: $$ [T] = [T_1] \cdot [T_2] $$ Mathematically, this arises from the boundary conditions: $V_{2a} = V_{1b}$ and $I_{2a} = -I_{1b}$. The first network's output variables become the second network's input variables after accounting for the sign convention on $I_2$. **Significance:** 1. For $N$ cascaded stages, $[T]_{\text{total}} = [T_1] \cdot [T_2] \cdot \ldots \cdot [T_N]$. 2. The order of multiplication matters: leftmost matrix is closest to the source. 3. This property is *unique* to ABCD parameters; Z, Y, h, and g do **not** multiply cleanly for cascades. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question B1 (Choice 1) — Cascaded T-Networks & ABCD Conversion** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Derive the expressions for ABCD parameters in terms of h-parameters of a two-port network. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Two identical T-networks, each with series arm $Z_1 = 10\;\Omega$ and shunt arm $Z_2 = 20\;\Omega$, are connected in cascade. Determine the overall ABCD parameters of the cascade using matrix multiplication. Verify whether the resulting network is reciprocal. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📝 Model Solution for B1(a): **Given (h-parameters):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \quad \cdots (1) \\ I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2 \quad \cdots (2) \end{aligned} $$ **Target (ABCD):** $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= A V_2 - B I_2 \quad \cdots (3) \\ I_1 &= C V_2 - D I_2 \quad \cdots (4) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 1:** From (2), solve for $I_1$: $$ \begin{aligned} h_{21} I_1 &= I_2 - h_{22} V_2 \\ I_1 &= \frac{I_2 - h_{22} V_2}{h_{21}} = \frac{1}{h_{21}} I_2 - \frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2 \end{aligned} $$ Comparing with (4): $I_1 = C V_2 - D I_2$ $$ \Rightarrow C = -\frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}}, \quad D = -\frac{1}{h_{21}} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute $I_1$ into (1): $$ \begin{aligned} V_1 &= h_{11}\left(\frac{1}{h_{21}} I_2 - \frac{h_{22}}{h_{21}} V_2\right) + h_{12} V_2 \\ V_1 &= \left(h_{12} - \frac{h_{11} h_{22}}{h_{21}}\right) V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2 \\ V_1 &= -\frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}} V_2 + \frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}} I_2 \end{aligned} $$ where $\Delta_h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$. Comparing with (3): $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$ $$ \boxed{A = -\frac{\Delta_h}{h_{21}}, \quad B = -\frac{h_{11}}{h_{21}}} $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -\Delta_h/h_{21} & -h_{11}/h_{21} \\ -h_{22}/h_{21} & -1/h_{21} \end{bmatrix}} $$ --- #### 📝 Model Solution for B1(b): **Step 1: ABCD matrix of a single T-network.** From the formula derived in Section 3.4 with $Z_1 = 10\;\Omega$, $Z_3 = 20\;\Omega$, $Z_2 = 10\;\Omega$ (symmetric T): $$ T_{\text{single}} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{Z_1 + Z_3}{Z_3} & \dfrac{Z_1 Z_2 + Z_1 Z_3 + Z_2 Z_3}{Z_3} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{Z_3} & \dfrac{Z_2 + Z_3}{Z_3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{10+20}{20} & \dfrac{100+200+200}{20} \\[8pt] \dfrac{1}{20} & \dfrac{10+20}{20} \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ T_{\text{single}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 25 \\ 0.05 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2: Overall cascade matrix.** Since the two T-networks are identical, $T_{\text{overall}} = T_{\text{single}} \cdot T_{\text{single}}$: $$ T_{\text{overall}} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 25 \\ 0.05 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 25 \\ 0.05 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ Computing each entry: - $A_{\text{ov}} = (1.5)(1.5) + (25)(0.05) = 2.25 + 1.25 = 3.5$ - $B_{\text{ov}} = (1.5)(25) + (25)(1.5) = 37.5 + 37.5 = 75$ - $C_{\text{ov}} = (0.05)(1.5) + (1.5)(0.05) = 0.075 + 0.075 = 0.15$ - $D_{\text{ov}} = (0.05)(25) + (1.5)(1.5) = 1.25 + 2.25 = 3.5$ $$ T_{\text{overall}} = \begin{bmatrix} 3.5 & 75 \\ 0.15 & 3.5 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3: Reciprocity verification.** $$ AD - BC = (3.5)(3.5) - (75)(0.15) = 12.25 - 11.25 = 1.0 \;\checkmark $$ Since $AD - BC = 1$, the cascaded network is **reciprocal**. Furthermore, $A = D = 3.5$, so the network is also **symmetric**. **(7 marks: ABCD derivation step 1 step 2)** --- ### **Question B2 (Choice 2) — g-Parameters & Reciprocity Conditions** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** The h-parameters of a two-port network are given as: $h_{11} = 1000\;\Omega$, $h_{12} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = 50$, $h_{22} = 25\;\mu\text{S}$. Check whether the network is reciprocal and symmetric. Find the corresponding g-parameters. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define transmission (ABCD) parameters. For a two-port network, derive the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry in terms of ABCD parameters. **(7 marks)** --- #### 📝 Model Solution for B2(a): **Given:** $h_{11} = 1000\;\Omega$, $h_{12} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$, $h_{21} = 50$, $h_{22} = 25 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{S}$. **Step 1: Check for reciprocity.** For an h-parameter network, the reciprocity condition is $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. Here, $h_{12} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ and $-h_{21} = -50$. Since $2 \times 10^{-4} \neq -50$, the network is **NOT reciprocal**. **Step 2: Check for symmetry.** A symmetric network must satisfy $\Delta_h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$. $$ \Delta_h = (1000)(25 \times 10^{-6}) - (2 \times 10^{-4})(50) = 0.025 - 0.01 = 0.015 $$ Since $\Delta_h = 0.015 \neq 1$, the network is **NOT symmetric**. **Step 3: Find the g-parameters.** The g-parameter matrix is the inverse of the h-parameter matrix: $$ \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{\Delta_h} \begin{bmatrix} h_{22} & -h_{12} \\ -h_{21} & h_{11} \end{bmatrix} $$ Substituting $\Delta_h = 0.015$: - $g_{11} = h_{22}/\Delta_h = (25 \times 10^{-6})/(0.015) = 1.667 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{S} = 1.667\;\text{mS}$ - $g_{12} = -h_{12}/\Delta_h = -(2 \times 10^{-4})/(0.015) = -1.333 \times 10^{-2}$ - $g_{21} = -h_{21}/\Delta_h = -(50)/(0.015) = -3333.33$ - $g_{22} = h_{11}/\Delta_h = (1000)/(0.015) = 66666.67\;\Omega \approx 66.67\;\text{k}\Omega$ $$ \boxed{\begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.667\;\text{mS} & -0.01333 \\ -3333.33 & 66.67\;\text{k}\Omega \end{bmatrix}} $$ --- #### 📝 Model Solution for B2(b): **Definition of ABCD Parameters:** The transmission (ABCD) parameters of a two-port network relate the input variables $(V_1, I_1)$ to the output variables $(V_2, -I_2)$ as: $$ \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix} $$ where the negative sign on $I_2$ accounts for the direction convention in cascaded networks. **Step 1: Reciprocity Condition.** A two-port is reciprocal if interchanging source and load yields identical transfer characteristics. In ABCD form, this condition is: $$ \boxed{AD - BC = 1} $$ **Derivation sketch:** From parameter interconversion, $Z_{12}/Z_{21} = AD - BC$. Reciprocity in Z-form requires $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, leading to the above. **Step 2: Symmetry Condition.** A two-port is symmetric if its input and output ports can be interchanged without external change. In ABCD form: $$ \boxed{A = D} $$ For a *symmetric and reciprocal* network, both conditions hold simultaneously: $A = D$ and $A^2 - BC = 1$. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **The $-I_2$ sign in ABCD is the #1 marks-loser.** Many students write $V_1 = A V_2 + B I_2$ instead of $V_1 = A V_2 - B I_2$. This propagates through *every* cascade multiplication and gives a wrong answer. **Always draw the $-I_2$ arrow on your diagram.** > > 2. **Cascade matrix order reversal.** Writing $T_{\text{cascade}} = T_b \cdot T_a$ instead of $T_a \cdot T_b$ is the second most common error. Remember: **source-side matrix is on the LEFT.** > > 3. **Confusing symmetry with reciprocity.** A *symmetric* network is automatically *reciprocal* (for passive networks), but the converse is **not** true. Reciprocity only needs $AD - BC = 1$; symmetry additionally needs $A = D$. > > 4. **Forgetting units in h-parameters.** $h_{11}$ is in Ω, $h_{22}$ is in S, $h_{12}$ and $h_{21}$ are dimensionless. Examiners often give 0.5 mark for correct units in part (a) of 3-mark questions. > > 5. **Mixing up the $\Delta_h$ sign.** The determinant is $\Delta_h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21}$ (NOT $+h_{12}h_{21}$). For symmetric h-networks, $\Delta_h = 1$, which means $h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$. > > 6. **Neglecting to verify reciprocity** in numerical problems. After computing ABCD, you MUST check $AD - BC = 1$. It is a 1-mark freebie that 30% of students skip. --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Final-minute reading before exam):** > > - **h-parameters** are *hybrid* — mix V and I. Useful for transistors. Reciprocity: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. Symmetry: $\Delta_h = 1$. > - **g-parameters** are the *inverse* of h-parameters. Useful for common-base transistor stages. > - **ABCD-parameters** are the *transmission* matrix. The defining equation uses **$-I_2$** (NOT $+I_2$). Cascade property: $T_{\text{overall}} = T_1 \cdot T_2$ (source-side matrix on the left). > - **A'B'C'D'-parameters** are the *inverse transmission* matrix. Useful when analyzing from the load side. > - **Reciprocity** condition in ABCD: $AD - BC = 1$. In h: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. In g: $g_{12} = -g_{21}$. > - **Symmetry** condition: $A = D$ in ABCD; $\Delta_h = 1$ in h; $\Delta_g = 1$ in g. > - **Cascade rule**: $T_{\text{cascade}} = T_a \cdot T_b$. Verify with $AD - BC = 1$ after computation. > - **Standard building blocks**: Series $Z$ → $\begin{bmatrix}1 & Z \\ 0 & 1\end{bmatrix}$, Shunt $Y$ → $\begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 \\ Y & 1\end{bmatrix}$, Ideal transformer (n:1) → $\begin{bmatrix}n & 0 \\ 0 & 1/n\end{bmatrix}$. > - **Sign convention:** All currents flow *into* the network at each port. The $-I_2$ in ABCD refers to the *transmitted* current flowing out of port 2. > - **Memory trick for parameter sets:** "**Z**ee and **Y**ou are *opposites*" (Z uses $I$ as input, Y uses $V$ as input). "**H**ybrid is for **H**orizontal transistors (CE)". "**ABCD** is for **A**rrays in **C**ascade".

topics.title: Reciprocity and Symmetry Conditions (Derivation Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reciprocity and Symmetry Conditions (Derivation Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ⚡ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Two-Port Networks** (which form the core of KTU Module 4 of Network Theory), every linear, passive, bilateral network is characterized by the following two fundamental properties:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RECIPROCITY THEOREM (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A two-port network is said to be **reciprocal** if the ratio of excitation (input) to response (output) remains unchanged when the positions of the excitation and response are interchanged. Mathematically, the **transfer impedance** and **transfer admittance** are symmetric in nature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SYMMETRY CONDITION (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> A two-port network is said to be **symmetric** if the input impedance (or admittance) at Port 1 with Port 2 open (or shorted) is equal to the input impedance (or admittance) at Port 2 with Port 1 open (or shorted). In other words, the network is electrically indistinguishable from either end.

### Standard Two-Port Network Schematic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input Port (1)} &\longrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Voltage } V_1 \text{, Current } I_1 \text{)} \\
\text{Output Port (2)} &\longrightarrow \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(Voltage } V_2 \text{, Current } I_2 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

All reciprocity and symmetry conditions are derived from the constitutive parameter matrices of the two-port — namely, **Z-parameters, Y-parameters, h-parameters, g-parameters, ABCD (T) parameters, and A'B'C'D' (t) parameters**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Reciprocity (The Telephone Line):** Imagine two friends, *Rahul* and *Anand*, speaking on a standard copper telephone line. If Rahul speaks softly into his microphone and Anand hears him clearly at a certain volume, then if Anand now speaks at the *same* volume into his microphone, Rahul will hear him at the *same* volume. The transmission path treats both directions **identically** — this is **reciprocity**. (Note: A *carbon microphone* or *amplifier* would break this analogy, because those are active/non-reciprocal devices.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Symmetry (The Uniform Pipe):** Consider a long, uniform water pipe with identical nozzles on both ends. If you measure the water flow resistance from the *left* end (with the right end closed), you get exactly the same value as measuring from the *right* end (with the left end closed). The pipe looks **physically and electrically the same** from either end — this is **symmetry**.

### 1.3 Key Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU)

| Property | What Gets Exchanged? | Conditions Required |
|----------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Reciprocity** | The **excitation** and the **response** are interchanged between the two ports | Network must be **linear, passive, and bilateral** (containing only R, L, M, C — no controlled sources or diodes) |
| **Symmetry** | The **two ports themselves** are interchanged | Network must additionally be **structurally (topologically) identical** at both ports |

> [!WARNING]
> **A reciprocal network is NOT necessarily symmetric, but a symmetric network is ALWAYS reciprocal.** This is a heavily tested KTU concept. The converse is not true in general.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The reciprocity and symmetry conditions are **dimensionless, pure algebraic constraints** on the network parameters.
- The standard **angular frequency** symbol is **$\omega$** (in **rad/s**), and the standard frequency is **$f$** (in **Hz**), with the relationship $\omega = 2\pi f$.
- All parameter conditions are valid in the **phasor domain** (steady-state sinusoidal analysis).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Conceptual "Why" Behind the Conditions

A two-port network is described by six different sets of two equations relating the four variables ($V_1$, $V_2$, $I_1$, $I_2$). Each set corresponds to a different choice of **independent** and **dependent** variables. The reciprocity/symmetry conditions appear differently in each parameter set because the *meaning* of "interchanging ports" is parameter-dependent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Rule (Always Tested):** When deriving conditions for any parameter set, recall that:
> - **Reciprocity** is equivalent to saying: *"Apply a voltage (or current) at Port 1; measure the open-circuit (or short-circuit) current (or voltage) at Port 2." Then "Apply the same excitation at Port 2; measure the response at Port 1."* For a reciprocal network, these two measurements give the same answer.
> - **Symmetry** is equivalent to: *"The driving-point impedance/admittance at Port 1 (with Port 2 appropriately terminated) equals the driving-point impedance/admittance at Port 2 (with Port 1 appropriately terminated)."*

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — All Conditions in One Place

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important revision tool** for this topic in KTU University Exams. Memorize it column by column.

### Table 2.A — Reciprocity & Symmetry Conditions for ALL Two-Port Parameters

| Parameter Set | Defining Matrix | Reciprocity Condition | Symmetry Condition (Reciprocal + Additional) |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|-----------------------------------------------|
| **Z-parameters** (Open-circuit) | $\begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ |
| **Y-parameters** (Short-circuit) | $\begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ |
| **h-parameters** (Hybrid-1) | $\begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1$ |
| **g-parameters** (Hybrid-2) | $\begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix}$ | $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ | $\Delta g = g_{11} g_{22} - g_{12} g_{21} = 1$ |
| **T / ABCD parameters** (Transmission) | $\begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix}$ | $\Delta T = AD - BC = 1$ | $A = D$ (combined with $AD-BC=1$) |
| **t / A'B'C'D' parameters** (Inverse Transmission) | $\begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix}$ | $\Delta t = A'D' - B'C' = 1$ | $A' = D'$ (combined with $A'D'-B'C'=1$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #1:** Students often write "$h_{12} = h_{21}$" for reciprocity in h-parameters. This is **WRONG**. The correct condition is $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ (note the **negative sign**). Similarly, $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ is correct, **not** $g_{12} = g_{21}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall #2:** For T-parameters, reciprocity is **NOT** $A = D$. It is $AD - BC = 1$. The condition $A = D$ is for **symmetry** (and assumes reciprocity is already satisfied). Many students confuse these two and lose easy marks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why These Conditions Matter |
|------------------|----------------------------|
| **Microwave & RF Engineering** | Antennas, waveguides, and most passive microwave junctions are reciprocal. A reciprocal antenna works equally well as a transmitter and receiver. |
| **Telecommunications** | Symmetric two-port models describe uniform transmission lines and balanced filters, which are essential for high-fidelity signal transmission. |
| **Crystal / Piezoelectric Filters** | These are inherently reciprocal. Designers use Z-parameter symmetry ($Z_{11} = Z_{22}$) to simplify filter design equations. |
| **Network Analyzers** | When measuring a Device Under Test (DUT), reciprocity can be used as a **self-check** to validate measurement accuracy. |
| **Control Systems** | Bilateral mechanical linkages and passive fluid systems obey reciprocity, allowing engineers to model them efficiently with two-port parameters. |

---

## 2.4 Logical "How" — Why the Conditions Differ Between Parameter Sets

> [!NOTE]
> The reason the reciprocity condition **changes form** (e.g., $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ vs $h_{12} = -h_{21}$) is because of the **direction of the current arrows** in the port definitions. When we interchange the ports, the current direction at the new Port 1 becomes **opposite** to the current direction at the original Port 2. This sign change is what introduces the negative sign in the h-parameter and g-parameter reciprocity conditions.

For the Z and Y parameters, both port currents flow **into** the network at their respective ports, so the sign change is absorbed by the parameter matrix structure itself — giving the "clean" $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ forms.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Verification

Since the KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly states **"Derivation Not Required"** for this topic, the focus shifts from algebraic derivation to **logical verification** — i.e., understanding *why* the conditions hold and *how* to apply them. Below is a comprehensive logical walkthrough.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Verification for Z-Parameters

### Z-Parameter Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \\
V_2 &= Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Define Reciprocity Test (Z-domain)

**Test A:** Excite Port 1 with current $I_1 = I_s$ (with Port 2 open, i.e., $I_2 = 0$). Measure open-circuit voltage at Port 2.

$$
V_2^{(A)} = Z_{21} \cdot I_s
$$

**Test B:** Excite Port 2 with current $I_2 = I_s$ (with Port 1 open, i.e., $I_1 = 0$). Measure open-circuit voltage at Port 1.

$$
V_1^{(B)} = Z_{12} \cdot I_s
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Reciprocity Definition

For reciprocity, the two responses must be equal:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_2^{(A)} &= V_1^{(B)} \\
Z_{21} \cdot I_s &= Z_{12} \cdot I_s \\
\therefore Z_{12} &= Z_{21} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply Symmetry Definition (Z-domain)

For symmetry, the input impedance at Port 1 (with Port 2 open) must equal the input impedance at Port 2 (with Port 1 open).

$$
Z_{\text{in,1}} = \frac{V_1}{I_1}\bigg\vert_{I_2=0} = Z_{11} \qquad Z_{\text{in,2}} = \frac{V_2}{I_2}\bigg\vert_{I_1=0} = Z_{22}
$$

For symmetry: $Z_{\text{in,1}} = Z_{\text{in,2}}$, which gives:

$$
Z_{11} = Z_{22} \quad \blacksquare
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since Z-parameters are defined with both port currents flowing *into* the network, no sign change appears in the reciprocity condition. This is why we get the symmetric-looking $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Verification for h-Parameters

### h-Parameter Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= h_{11} I_1 + h_{12} V_2 \\
I_2 &= h_{21} I_1 + h_{22} V_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Define Reciprocity Test (h-domain)

**Test A:** Apply input current $I_1 = I_s$ at Port 1, with Port 2 short-circuited ($V_2 = 0$). Measure short-circuit current at Port 2.

$$
I_2^{(A)} = h_{21} \cdot I_s
$$

**Test B:** Apply input voltage $V_2 = V_s$ at Port 2, with Port 1 open-circuited ($I_1 = 0$). Measure open-circuit voltage at Port 1.

$$
V_1^{(B)} = h_{12} \cdot V_s
$$

### Step 2 — Apply Reciprocity Definition

For reciprocity, the ratio of excitation to response must be the same. The "excitation" in Test A is a current, and in Test B is a voltage. To compare, we use the **transfer admittance/impedance ratio concept** — specifically, the reciprocity theorem states that the *transfer response per unit excitation* must match. For h-parameters, this yields:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2^{(A)} / I_s &= V_1^{(B)} / V_s \quad \text{(after dimensional analysis)} \\
h_{21} &= -h_{12} \\
\therefore h_{12} &= -h_{21} \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the negative sign?** When we interchange ports in the h-parameter system, the current at the "new Port 1" flows in the *opposite direction* relative to the original port definition. This sign reversal is the source of the negative sign. The KTU examiner will deduct marks if this sign is missing or written as $h_{12} = h_{21}$.

### Step 3 — Apply Symmetry Definition (h-domain)

For symmetry in h-parameters, the input impedance at Port 1 (with Port 2 shorted) must equal the reciprocal of the input admittance at Port 2 (with Port 1 open). The compact result is:

$$
\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Verification for T (ABCD) Parameters

### T-Parameter Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= A \, V_2 - B \, I_2 \\
I_1 &= C \, V_2 - D \, I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 1 — Define Reciprocity Test (T-domain)

**Test A:** Apply voltage $V_2 = V_s$ at Port 2 (output), with Port 1 shorted ($V_1 = 0$). Measure current at Port 1.

$$
0 = A V_s - B I_2^{(A)} \implies I_2^{(A)} = \frac{A V_s}{B}
$$

**Test B:** Apply voltage $V_1 = V_s$ at Port 1 (input), with Port 2 shorted ($V_2 = 0$). Measure current at Port 2.

$$
V_s = -B I_2^{(B)} \implies I_2^{(B)} = -\frac{V_s}{B}
$$

By combining the relations and using the definition of the reciprocity theorem for the T-parameter system, the condition emerges as the **determinant of the T-matrix equals 1**:

$$
\therefore AD - BC = 1 \quad \blacksquare
$$

### Step 2 — Symmetry for T-Parameters

A symmetric network in T-parameters satisfies **both** the reciprocity condition ($AD - BC = 1$) **and** the additional condition:

$$
A = D \quad \blacksquare
$$

The reason is that symmetry means the network is "the same forwards and backwards," so the relationship between $V_1, V_2$ and $I_1, I_2$ must be unchanged when the indices "1" and "2" are swapped. This swap operation in the T-matrix yields $A \leftrightarrow D$, hence $A = D$.

---

## 3.4 Summary of Reasoning Patterns (Analytical Matrix)

| Parameter | Origin of Negative Sign (if any) | Source of Determinant Condition (if any) |
|-----------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| Z | None (both currents flow in) | Symmetry: $Z_{11}=Z_{22}$ |
| Y | None (both currents flow in) | Symmetry: $Y_{11}=Y_{22}$ |
| h | Current direction reversal at swapped port | Symmetry: $\Delta h = 1$ |
| g | Current direction reversal at swapped port | Symmetry: $\Delta g = 1$ |
| T (ABCD) | Built into the $-B$ and $-D$ signs in defining equations | Reciprocity itself: $AD - BC = 1$; Symmetry adds $A = D$ |
| t (A'B'C'D') | Built into the defining equations (similar to T) | Reciprocity itself: $A'D' - B'C' = 1$; Symmetry adds $A' = D'$ |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to verify whether a given two-port network is reciprocal/symmetric, the *fastest* method is to compute the relevant parameters (Z or Y are usually easiest) and check the equality conditions from the formula sheet in Section 2.
