# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Context (PCECT205 Network Theory):**
> - **Part A (3 marks):** Direct memory-based or short derivation questions
> - **Part B (14 marks):** Two-part analytical questions with internal choice
> - **Module Weightage:** Module 4 (Two-Port Networks) typically carries **15-20%** of the total ESE marks.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]`
**State the conditions for reciprocity and symmetry of a two-port network in terms of Z-parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reciprocity:} \quad & Z_{12} = Z_{21} \\
\text{Symmetry:} \quad & Z_{11} = Z_{22}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A two-port network is **reciprocal** if the off-diagonal elements of its Z-parameter matrix are equal ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$). A **symmetric** two-port network is one that is reciprocal **and** has equal diagonal elements ($Z_{11} = Z_{22}$). All R, L, M, C networks are reciprocal; symmetric networks require equal "diagonal" impedances, e.g., a uniform transmission line.

---

### Question A.2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]`
**A two-port network has the following h-parameter matrix. Is it reciprocal and symmetric? Justify.**
$$
[h] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \, \Omega & 0.5 \\ -0.5 & 0.1 \, \text{S} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Model Answer:**

**Reciprocity Check:** For h-parameters, reciprocity requires $h_{12} = -h_{21}$.

$$
h_{12} = 0.5, \qquad -h_{21} = -(-0.5) = 0.5
$$

Since $h_{12} = -h_{21} = 0.5$, the network **is reciprocal**.

**Symmetry Check:** For h-parameters, symmetry requires $\Delta h = 1$.

$$
\Delta h = (10)(0.1) - (0.5)(-0.5) = 1.0 + 0.25 = 1.25
$$

Since $\Delta h = 1.25 \neq 1$, the network **is NOT symmetric**.

> Hence, the network is **reciprocal but not symmetric**.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Every 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. You are expected to attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Full Model Solution

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** State and explain the conditions for **reciprocity and symmetry** for Z, Y, h, g, and T-parameters of a two-port network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The Z-parameters of a two-port network are given as:
$$
[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 5 \\ 5 & 30 \end{bmatrix} \, \Omega
$$
**Check whether the network is reciprocal and symmetric. Convert to ABCD parameters.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Reciprocity** is the property by which a linear, passive, bilateral network gives the same transmission in both directions. **Symmetry** is the additional property that the input impedance looking into Port 1 (with Port 2 terminated) equals the input impedance looking into Port 2 (with Port 1 terminated).

**Conditions for Reciprocity:**

| Parameter | Reciprocity Condition |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Z | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ |
| Y | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ |
| h | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ |
| g | $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ |
| T (ABCD) | $AD - BC = 1$ |

**Conditions for Symmetry** (additional to reciprocity):

| Parameter | Symmetry Condition |
|-----------|---------------------|
| Z | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ |
| Y | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ |
| h | $\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ |
| g | $\Delta g = g_{11}g_{22} - g_{12}g_{21} = 1$ |
| T (ABCD) | $A = D$ (along with $AD - BC = 1$) |

A passive R-L-C-M network is always reciprocal. For symmetry, the network must also be topologically identical at both ports.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Reciprocity Check:**

From the given matrix: $Z_{12} = 5 \, \Omega$ and $Z_{21} = 5 \, \Omega$.

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, the network **IS reciprocal**.

**Step 2 — Symmetry Check:**

$Z_{11} = 20 \, \Omega$ and $Z_{22} = 30 \, \Omega$.

Since $Z_{11} \neq Z_{22}$, the network is **NOT symmetric**.

> **Conclusion:** The network is **reciprocal but not symmetric**.

**Step 3 — Conversion to ABCD Parameters:**

The standard Z-to-T conversion formulas are:

$$
A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}, \qquad B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}, \qquad C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}}, \qquad D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}
$$

where $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$.

**Compute $\Delta Z$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta Z &= (20)(30) - (5)(5) \\
&= 600 - 25 \\
&= 575 \, \Omega^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compute each ABCD parameter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{20}{5} = 4 \quad \text{(dimensionless)} \\
B &= \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{575}{5} = 115 \, \Omega \\
C &= \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \, \text{S} \\
D &= \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{30}{5} = 6 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify Reciprocity in T-domain:**

$$
AD - BC = (4)(6) - (115)(0.2) = 24 - 23 = 1 \checkmark
$$

The T-parameter determinant equals 1, confirming reciprocity.

**Final Answer:**

$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 115 \, \Omega \\ 0.2 \, \text{S} & 6 \end{bmatrix}
$$

The network is **reciprocal but not symmetric** (note: $A = 4 \neq 6 = D$).

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 ESE Pattern]`

**(a)** With neat derivations of the form, write the **reciprocity conditions** for h, g, and T parameters. State the corresponding **symmetry conditions** also. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-port network has the following **transmission parameters**:
$$
[T] = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 5 \, \Omega \\ 1 \, \text{S} & 2 \end{bmatrix}
$$
**(i)** Test the network for **reciprocity and symmetry**.
**(ii)** Find the corresponding **Y-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to (a):**

The reciprocity and symmetry conditions for the three parameter sets are summarized as follows:

**For h-parameters:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reciprocity:} \quad & h_{12} = -h_{21} \\
\text{Symmetry:} \quad & \Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12} h_{21} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For g-parameters:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reciprocity:} \quad & g_{12} = -g_{21} \\
\text{Symmetry:} \quad & \Delta g = g_{11} g_{22} - g_{12} g_{21} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**For T (ABCD) parameters:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Reciprocity:} \quad & \Delta T = AD - BC = 1 \\
\text{Symmetry:} \quad & A = D \quad \text{(and } AD - BC = 1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

These conditions arise from the principle that, for a reciprocal network, the transfer response per unit excitation must be invariant when the excitation and response ports are interchanged. The negative signs in the h- and g-parameter conditions are due to the reversal of current direction when the ports are swapped.

---

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Test for Reciprocity:**

For T-parameters, the reciprocity condition is $AD - BC = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AD - BC &= (3)(2) - (5)(1) \\
&= 6 - 5 \\
&= 1 \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $AD - BC = 1$, the network **IS reciprocal**.

**Step 2 — Test for Symmetry:**

For T-parameters, the symmetry condition is $A = D$.

$$
A = 3, \qquad D = 2
$$

Since $A \neq D$, the network is **NOT symmetric**.

**Step 3 — Find Y-parameters from T-parameters:**

The T-to-Y conversion formulas are:

$$
Y_{11} = \frac{D}{B}, \qquad Y_{12} = -\frac{\Delta T}{B} = \frac{BC - AD}{B}, \qquad Y_{21} = -\frac{1}{B}, \qquad Y_{22} = \frac{A}{B}
$$

**Compute each Y-parameter:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_{11} &= \frac{D}{B} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \, \text{S} \\
Y_{12} &= -\frac{\Delta T}{B} = -\frac{1}{5} = -0.2 \, \text{S} \\
Y_{21} &= -\frac{1}{B} = -\frac{1}{5} = -0.2 \, \text{S} \\
Y_{22} &= \frac{A}{B} = \frac{3}{5} = 0.6 \, \text{S}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verify Reciprocity in Y-domain:**

$$
Y_{12} = -0.2 = Y_{21} = -0.2 \checkmark
$$

**Final Y-parameter matrix:**

$$
[Y] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & -0.2 \\ -0.2 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} \, \text{S}
$$

Since $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, reciprocity is confirmed. Since $Y_{11} \neq Y_{22}$ ($0.4 \neq 0.6$), the network is not symmetric.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MARK-LOSS PITFALLS IN THIS TOPIC:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in h/g-Parameter Reciprocity:** Writing $h_{12} = h_{21}$ instead of $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. This single error costs **3 marks** out of 7 in Part B (a). Always re-check the sign!
>
> 2. **Confusing T-Parameter Reciprocity with Symmetry:** Writing $A = D$ for reciprocity instead of $AD - BC = 1$. The condition $A = D$ is for **symmetry**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Units:** T-parameter $B$ has units of $\Omega$, and $C$ has units of S. The determinant $AD - BC$ is dimensionless, but students often forget that $BC$ is dimensionless too, while $B$ alone is in ohms. Keep track of units.
>
> 4. **Assuming Every Network is Symmetric:** A network being reciprocal does **NOT** imply it is symmetric. Always check the additional symmetry condition separately.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Justification Step:** In 7-mark questions, simply stating the condition without checking the values against the matrix is treated as **incomplete**. Always show: (i) state condition, (ii) compute LHS, (iii) state conclusion.
>
> 6. **Writing $[\Delta T = AD - BC]$ as $[=0]$:** Some students memorize $AD - BC = 1$ incorrectly as $AD - BC = 0$ (which would actually be the condition for a **singular** T-matrix — i.e., a network with no unique solution). Double-check the sign.

---

## 5.4 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision list for the KTU exam — read this twice before submitting the answer sheet.**

- 🔹 **Reciprocity** = interchange of *excitation* and *response* gives the same result.
- 🔹 **Symmetry** = interchange of *the two ports* gives the same result.
- 🔹 **All passive R-L-C-M networks are reciprocal.** No exceptions.
- 🔹 **Symmetry requires reciprocity + equal diagonal/auxiliary conditions.**
- 🔹 **Z, Y parameter reciprocity:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ (positive equality, no sign flip).
- 🔹 **h, g parameter reciprocity:** $h_{12} = -h_{21}$, $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ (with **NEGATIVE** sign).
- 🔹 **T, t parameter reciprocity:** $AD - BC = 1$, $A'D' - B'C' = 1$ (determinant = 1).
- 🔹 **Symmetry in Z, Y:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$.
- 🔹 **Symmetry in h, g:** $\Delta h = 1$, $\Delta g = 1$.
- 🔹 **Symmetry in T, t:** $A = D$, $A' = D'$ (in addition to reciprocity).
- 🔹 **Negative sign in h/g** comes from current direction reversal during port interchange.
- 🔹 **KTU 2024 Common Test:** "Given a Z/Y matrix, test for reciprocity and symmetry, and convert to ABCD/ABCD-to-Y."
- 🔹 **Conversion formulas to memorize:** $A = Z_{11}/Z_{21}$, $B = \Delta Z / Z_{21}$, $C = 1/Z_{21}$, $D = Z_{22}/Z_{21}$ (for Z → T).
- 🔹 **Always verify** the result by re-checking the reciprocity condition in the new parameter set (e.g., $AD - BC = 1$ for T-parameters).
- 🔹 **Dimensional consistency:** $Z$ in $\Omega$, $Y$ in S, $B$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in S, $A$ and $D$ dimensionless, $\Delta h$ dimensionless.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-relationships between Parameters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Inter-relationships between Parameters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In two-port network analysis, a linear two-port network can be mathematically described by exactly **four** dependent variables: the two port voltages ($V_1$, $V_2$) and the two port currents ($I_1$, $I_2$). However, we can only choose **two** of these as independent (excitations). Depending on which pair we select as independent variables, we obtain **six different sets of two-port parameters**:

| # | Parameter Set | Independent Variables | Dependent Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Z-parameters** (Open-circuit Impedance) | $I_1, I_2$ | $V_1, V_2$ |
| 2 | **Y-parameters** (Short-circuit Admittance) | $V_1, V_2$ | $I_1, I_2$ |
| 3 | **h-parameters** (Hybrid / Series-Shunt) | $I_1, V_2$ | $V_1, I_2$ |
| 4 | **g-parameters** (Inverse Hybrid) | $V_1, I_2$ | $I_1, V_2$ |
| 5 | **ABCD-parameters** (Transmission / Chain) | $V_2, I_2$ | $V_1, I_1$ |
| 6 | **A'B'C'D'-parameters** (Inverse Transmission) | $V_1, I_1$ | $V_2, I_2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inter-relationships between parameters** refers to the systematic conversion formulas that allow an engineer to mathematically translate the description of a single two-port network from one parameter set to any other equivalent parameter set. Each parameter set is a complete, equivalent representation of the same physical network — they merely use different "lenses" to view the same circuit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Many Languages of One Circuit"

Imagine a single human being who speaks **six languages** (English, Hindi, French, German, Spanish, Japanese). The person is the *same*, but the words used to describe him differ in each language. 

In the same way, a two-port network is the *same physical circuit*, but we can describe it using:
- **Z-language**: "How do currents push voltages around?"
- **Y-language**: "How do voltages drive currents?"
- **h-language**: "How does a mix of current and voltage at one port affect the other?"
- **ABCD-language**: "How does a signal *travel through* the network?"

The conversion formulas are like the **dictionary** that translates a description in one language to another — without losing any physical information about the circuit.

## 1.3 Standard General Two-Port Equations

The general linear two-port network obeys the following six representations, all describing the **same** circuit:

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11} & Y_{12} \\ Y_{21} & Y_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} g_{11} & g_{12} \\ g_{21} & g_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A & B \\ C & D \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} A' & B' \\ C' & D' \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ -I_1 \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the **negative sign** convention in ABCD and A'B'C'D' parameters — this is the standard KTU convention. The current $I_2$ is treated as flowing **out** of the receiving port, so when it enters the network, it appears as $-I_2$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic of Conversion

Conversion between two parameter sets follows a **3-step systematic procedure**:

**Step 1 — Identify the target.** Write down the source equations and the destination equations side by side.

**Step 2 — Equate.** Express the destination's independent variables in terms of the source's independent variables (or vice-versa).

**Step 3 — Compare coefficients.** After rearrangement, the coefficients of the source's independent variables on the right-hand side of the destination equation give the destination parameters in terms of the source parameters.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use `Δ` (delta) for the determinant** of any 2×2 parameter matrix throughout. For example, $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$.

### 📋 Conversion Table — Z-Parameters as Source

| Target | Conversion Formulas |
|---|---|
| **Y** | $Y_{11} = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$ |
| **h** | $h_{11} = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{12} = \dfrac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{22} = \dfrac{1}{Z_{22}}$ |
| **g** | $g_{11} = \dfrac{1}{Z_{11}}, \quad g_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{Z_{11}}, \quad g_{21} = \dfrac{Z_{21}}{Z_{11}}, \quad g_{22} = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{11}}$ |
| **ABCD** | $A = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}, \quad B = \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}, \quad C = \dfrac{1}{Z_{21}}, \quad D = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}$ |

### 📋 Conversion Table — Y-Parameters as Source

| Target | Conversion Formulas |
|---|---|
| **Z** | $Z_{11} = \dfrac{Y_{22}}{\Delta Y}, \quad Z_{12} = \dfrac{-Y_{12}}{\Delta Y}, \quad Z_{21} = \dfrac{-Y_{21}}{\Delta Y}, \quad Z_{22} = \dfrac{Y_{11}}{\Delta Y}$ |
| **h** | $h_{11} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{11}}, \quad h_{12} = \dfrac{-Y_{12}}{Y_{11}}, \quad h_{21} = \dfrac{Y_{21}}{Y_{11}}, \quad h_{22} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y_{11}}$ |
| **g** | $g_{11} = \dfrac{\Delta Y}{Y_{22}}, \quad g_{12} = \dfrac{Y_{12}}{Y_{22}}, \quad g_{21} = \dfrac{-Y_{21}}{Y_{22}}, \quad g_{22} = \dfrac{1}{Y_{22}}$ |
| **ABCD** | $A = \dfrac{-Y_{22}}{Y_{21}}, \quad B = \dfrac{-1}{Y_{21}}, \quad C = \dfrac{-\Delta Y}{Y_{21}}, \quad D = \dfrac{-Y_{11}}{Y_{21}}$ |

### 📋 Conversion Table — h-Parameters as Source

| Target | Conversion Formulas |
|---|---|
| **Z** | $Z_{11} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_{22}}, \quad Z_{12} = \dfrac{h_{12}}{h_{22}}, \quad Z_{21} = \dfrac{-h_{21}}{h_{22}}, \quad Z_{22} = \dfrac{1}{h_{22}}$ |
| **Y** | $Y_{11} = \dfrac{1}{h_{11}}, \quad Y_{12} = \dfrac{-h_{12}}{h_{11}}, \quad Y_{21} = \dfrac{h_{21}}{h_{11}}, \quad Y_{22} = \dfrac{\Delta h}{h_{11}}$ |
| **g** | $g_{11} = \dfrac{h_{22}}{\Delta h}, \quad g_{12} = \dfrac{-h_{12}}{\Delta h}, \quad g_{21} = \dfrac{-h_{21}}{\Delta h}, \quad g_{22} = \dfrac{h_{11}}{\Delta h}$ |
| **ABCD** | $A = \dfrac{-\Delta h}{h_{21}}, \quad B = \dfrac{-h_{11}}{h_{21}}, \quad C = \dfrac{-h_{22}}{h_{21}}, \quad D = \dfrac{-1}{h_{21}}$ |

### 📋 Conversion Table — ABCD-Parameters as Source

| Target | Conversion Formulas |
|---|---|
| **Z** | $Z_{11} = \dfrac{A}{C}, \quad Z_{12} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{C}, \quad Z_{21} = \dfrac{1}{C}, \quad Z_{22} = \dfrac{D}{C}$ |
| **Y** | $Y_{11} = \dfrac{D}{B}, \quad Y_{12} = \dfrac{-\Delta T}{B}, \quad Y_{21} = \dfrac{-1}{B}, \quad Y_{22} = \dfrac{A}{B}$ |
| **h** | $h_{11} = \dfrac{B}{D}, \quad h_{12} = \dfrac{\Delta T}{D}, \quad h_{21} = \dfrac{-1}{D}, \quad h_{22} = \dfrac{C}{D}$ |
| **g** | $g_{11} = \dfrac{C}{A}, \quad g_{12} = \dfrac{-\Delta T}{A}, \quad g_{21} = \dfrac{1}{A}, \quad g_{22} = \dfrac{B}{A}$ |

> Where $\Delta T = AD - BC$.

### 📋 Conversion Table — g-Parameters as Source

| Target | Conversion Formulas |
|---|---|
| **Z** | $Z_{11} = \dfrac{1}{g_{11}}, \quad Z_{12} = \dfrac{-g_{12}}{g_{11}}, \quad Z_{21} = \dfrac{g_{21}}{g_{11}}, \quad Z_{22} = \dfrac{\Delta g}{g_{11}}$ |
| **Y** | $Y_{11} = \dfrac{\Delta g}{g_{22}}, \quad Y_{12} = \dfrac{g_{12}}{g_{22}}, \quad Y_{21} = \dfrac{-g_{21}}{g_{22}}, \quad Y_{22} = \dfrac{1}{g_{22}}$ |
| **h** | $h_{11} = \dfrac{g_{22}}{\Delta g}, \quad h_{12} = \dfrac{-g_{12}}{\Delta g}, \quad h_{21} = \dfrac{-g_{21}}{\Delta g}, \quad h_{22} = \dfrac{g_{11}}{\Delta g}$ |
| **ABCD** | $A = \dfrac{1}{g_{21}}, \quad B = \dfrac{g_{11}}{g_{21}}, \quad C = \dfrac{g_{22}}{g_{21}}, \quad D = \dfrac{\Delta g}{g_{21}}$ |

> Where $\Delta g = g_{11}g_{22} - g_{12}g_{21}$.

### 📋 Inverse Transmission (A'B'C'D') Conversion Rules

The A'B'C'D' parameters are obtained simply by **swapping the roles of port 1 and port 2** of ABCD:

$$A' = A, \quad B' = B, \quad C' = C, \quad D' = D \text{ (with ports reversed, and } \Delta T' = A'D' - B'C' = \Delta T)$$

The standard relations are:
$$A = \dfrac{A'}{\Delta T'} = \dfrac{A'}{A'D' - B'C'} \quad ; \quad B = \dfrac{B'}{A'D' - B'C'} \quad ; \quad C = \dfrac{C'}{A'D' - B'C'} \quad ; \quad D = \dfrac{D'}{A'D' - B'C'}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need so many parameter sets?** Each parameter set has a unique engineering advantage:
> - **Z-parameters**: Used for **T-networks** and analysis of **transistor circuits at low frequencies** (exact h-parameter derivation in textbooks).
> - **Y-parameters**: Ideal for **π-networks** and **high-frequency transistor** analysis.
> - **h-parameters**: The de-facto standard for **transistor small-signal modeling** (CE, CB, CC configurations).
> - **ABCD-parameters**: Essential for **cascaded two-port networks** (transmission lines, filters, attenuators) because parameters **multiply**.
> - **A'B'C'D'-parameters**: Used when the cascade order is **reversed**.
> - **g-parameters**: Used in **parallel-parallel cascade** connections.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Z-parameters → Y-parameters

**Given:**
$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2 \quad \quad (1)$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2 \quad \quad (2)$$

**Goal:** Express $I_1$ and $I_2$ in terms of $V_1$ and $V_2$ to identify Y-parameters.

**Step 1:** We can write the above two equations compactly in matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** To find the currents as functions of voltages, take the **inverse** of the Z-matrix:
$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11} & Z_{12} \\ Z_{21} & Z_{22} \end{bmatrix}^{-1} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** Using the 2×2 inverse formula (adjugate divided by determinant):
$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}} \begin{bmatrix} Z_{22} & -Z_{12} \\ -Z_{21} & Z_{11} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4:** Expanding matrix multiplication:
$$I_1 = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} V_1 + \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} V_2$$

$$I_2 = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} V_1 + \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} V_2$$

**Step 5:** Comparing coefficients with the Y-parameter definition ($I_1 = Y_{11}V_1 + Y_{12}V_2$ and $I_2 = Y_{21}V_1 + Y_{22}V_2$):
$$\boxed{Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}}$$

> **Deduction summary:** Y-parameters are simply the elements of $[Z]^{-1}$. This is the most fundamental inter-relationship: **Y = Z⁻¹**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Z-parameters → h-parameters

**Given:** Equations (1) and (2) from above.
**Goal:** Express $V_1$ and $I_2$ in terms of $I_1$ and $V_2$.

**Step 1:** From equation (2), solve for $I_1$:
$$Z_{21} I_1 = V_2 - Z_{22} I_2 \implies I_1 = \frac{V_2}{Z_{21}} - \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} I_2$$

Wait, let me use a cleaner route. Solve for $I_2$ in terms of $V_2$ and $I_1$ from equation (2):

$$Z_{22} I_2 = V_2 - Z_{21} I_1 \implies I_2 = -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{1}{Z_{22}} V_2$$

**Step 2:** This already gives us $h_{21}$ and $h_{22}$:
$$\boxed{h_{21} = -\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{22} = \frac{1}{Z_{22}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute this $I_2$ expression into equation (1):
$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} \left[-\frac{Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{1}{Z_{22}} V_2 \right]$$

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 - \frac{Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} V_2$$

$$V_1 = \left(\frac{Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{22}}\right) I_1 + \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} V_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}} I_1 + \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} V_2$$

**Step 4:** Reading off the coefficients:
$$\boxed{h_{11} = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}}, \quad h_{12} = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Z-parameters → ABCD-Parameters

**Given:** Equations (1) and (2). 
**Goal:** Express $V_1$ and $I_1$ in terms of $V_2$ and $-I_2$.

**Step 1:** Solve equation (2) for $I_1$:
$$Z_{21} I_1 = V_2 - Z_{22} I_2$$
$$I_1 = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} V_2 - \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} I_2$$

Multiplying by $-1$ on the term with $I_2$:
$$I_1 = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}(-I_2)$$

**Step 2:** Reading off $C$ and $D$:
$$\boxed{C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}}, \quad D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $I_1$ into equation (1):
$$V_1 = Z_{11} \left[\frac{1}{Z_{21}} V_2 - \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} I_2\right] + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 - \frac{Z_{11} Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} I_2 + Z_{12} I_2$$

$$V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \left(\frac{-Z_{11} Z_{22} + Z_{12} Z_{21}}{Z_{21}}\right) I_2$$

Note: $-Z_{11} Z_{22} + Z_{12} Z_{21} = -(Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}) = -\Delta Z$

$$V_1 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \left(\frac{-\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}\right) I_2 = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} V_2 + \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}(-I_2)$$

**Step 4:** Reading off $A$ and $B$:
$$\boxed{A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}}, \quad B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Verification Example

**Problem:** Given the T-network with $Z_a = 1\,\Omega$, $Z_b = 2\,\Omega$, $Z_c = 3\,\Omega$ (series-shunt-series), find Z, Y, h, and ABCD parameters. Verify that conversions are consistent.

**Step 1 — Compute Z-parameters directly:**

For a T-network: 
$$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_b = 1 + 2 = 3\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{22} = Z_c + Z_b = 3 + 2 = 5\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_b = 2\,\Omega$$

$$\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21} = 3 \times 5 - 2 \times 2 = 15 - 4 = 11\,\Omega^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute Y-parameters using conversion:**
$$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{5}{11}\,\text{S}$$
$$Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{3}{11}\,\text{S}$$
$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-2}{11}\,\text{S}$$

**Step 3 — Verify by direct computation for T-network:**
For a T-network, $Y_{11} = \frac{Z_b + Z_c}{Z_a Z_b + Z_b Z_c + Z_c Z_a} = \frac{2 + 3}{1 \cdot 2 + 2 \cdot 3 + 3 \cdot 1} = \frac{5}{11}$ ✓

**Step 4 — Compute h-parameters using conversion:**
$$h_{11} = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{22}} = \frac{11}{5} = 2.2\,\Omega$$
$$h_{12} = \frac{Z_{12}}{Z_{22}} = \frac{2}{5} = 0.4 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$
$$h_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{Z_{22}} = \frac{-2}{5} = -0.4 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$
$$h_{22} = \frac{1}{Z_{22}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2\,\text{S}$$

**Step 5 — Compute ABCD parameters using conversion:**
$$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$
$$B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{11}{2} = 5.5\,\Omega$$
$$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5\,\text{S}$$
$$D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 6 — Consistency check using $AD - BC$:**
$$AD - BC = (1.5)(2.5) - (5.5)(0.5) = 3.75 - 2.75 = 1.0 \checkmark$$

The result $AD - BC = 1$ is consistent with the T-network being **reciprocal** (symmetric). ✓

## 3.5 Symmetry / Reciprocity Conditions (Summary)

| Property | Z | Y | h | g | ABCD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocal** ($Z_{12}=Z_{21}$) | $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ | $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ | $h_{12} = -h_{21}$ | $g_{12} = -g_{21}$ | $AD - BC = 1$ |
| **Symmetric** ($Z_{11}=Z_{22}$) | $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ | $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$ | $\Delta h = h_{11} h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = 1$ | $\Delta g = 1$ | $A = D$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Two-Port Network Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1L[Port 1 Left Terminal] --> N1((Node 1a))
    N1 --> NET[Two-Port Network Black Box]
    NET --> N2((Node 2a))
    N2 --> P2L[Port 2 Left Terminal]
    P1R[Port 1 Right Terminal] --> N3((Node 1b))
    NET -.-> N3
    N3 --> P2R[Port 2 Right Terminal]
    P1L ---|V1 I1| P1R
    P2L ---|V2 I2| P2R
```

## 4.2 Parameter Conversion Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Given Parameter Set] --> B1{Choose Conversion Target}
    B1 --> T1[Target Z parameters]
    B1 --> T2[Target Y parameters]
    B1 --> T3[Target h parameters]
    B1 --> T4[Target g parameters]
    B1 --> T5[Target ABCD parameters]
    B1 --> T6[Target A prime B prime C prime D prime parameters]
    A0 --> S1[Step 1 Write Source Matrix Equations]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Algebraic Substitution of Independent Variables]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Compare Coefficients with Target Form]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Apply Determinant Identities Delta]
    S4 --> OUT[Converted Parameter Matrix]
    OUT --> V[Verification by Reciprocity Condition]
```

## 4.3 Six Parameter Set Relationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z[Z matrix] <-->|Matrix Inverse Y equals Z inverse| Y[Y matrix]
    Z <-->|Hybrid h conversion| H[h matrix]
    Z <-->|Inverse Hybrid g conversion| G[g matrix]
    Z <-->|Transmission T conversion| T[ABCD matrix]
    Y <-->|h conversion| H
    Y <-->|g conversion| G
    Y <-->|ABCD conversion| T
    H <-->|g conversion| G
    H <-->|ABCD conversion| T
    G <-->|ABCD conversion| T
    T <-->|Swap ports| TP[A prime B prime C prime D prime matrix]
```

## 4.4 Cascaded Two-Port Network using ABCD

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source] --> N1((Node In 1))
    N1 --> NET1[Two Port Network 1 with Matrix T1]
    NET1 --> N2((Node Mid 1))
    N2 --> NET2[Two Port Network 2 with Matrix T2]
    NET2 --> N3((Node Mid 2))
    N3 --> NET3[Two Port Network N with Matrix Tn]
    NET3 --> LOAD[Load ZL]
    NET1 -.->|Product T1 times T2 times Tn| NET3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Architectural insight:** The reason ABCD parameters are favored for cascaded networks is that the overall transmission matrix is simply the matrix product $T_{overall} = T_1 \cdot T_2 \cdot T_3 \cdots T_n$, while all other parameter sets require **more complex combination rules** (series, parallel, etc.).

## 4.5 Comparison Table — Independent Variable Selection

| Parameter Set | $V_1$ | $I_1$ | $V_2$ | $I_2$ | Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Z | Dependent | Independent | Dependent | Independent | Open-circuit Impedance |
| Y | Independent | Dependent | Independent | Dependent | Short-circuit Admittance |
| h | Dependent | Independent | Independent | Dependent | Series input Shunt output |
| g | Independent | Dependent | Dependent | Independent | Shunt input Series output |
| ABCD | Dependent | Dependent | Independent | Independent with sign | Transmission |
| A prime B prime C prime D prime | Independent | Independent | Dependent | Dependent | Inverse Transmission |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**"Define Z-parameters of a two-port network. Why are they also called open-circuit impedance parameters?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Z-parameters are the open-circuit impedance parameters of a two-port network. They are defined by the equations:
$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

Each Z-parameter is found by setting one of the port currents to zero (i.e., leaving the corresponding port **open-circuited**):
- $Z_{11} = V_1/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ — driving-point impedance at port 1 with port 2 open.
- $Z_{12} = V_1/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ — reverse transfer impedance.
- $Z_{21} = V_2/I_1 \big\vert_{I_2=0}$ — forward transfer impedance.
- $Z_{22} = V_2/I_2 \big\vert_{I_1=0}$ — driving-point impedance at port 2 with port 1 open.

Since each is measured under an **open-circuit** condition, they are called **open-circuit impedance parameters**. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**"State the relationship between Z-parameters and Y-parameters. Justify why Y = Z⁻¹."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Z-parameter equations can be written compactly as:
$$\mathbf{V} = \mathbf{Z}\,\mathbf{I}$$

Rearranging for the currents:
$$\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Z}^{-1}\,\mathbf{V}$$

By definition, $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{Y}\,\mathbf{V}$, hence:
$$\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{Z}^{-1}$$

This means the Y-parameter matrix is the **matrix inverse** of the Z-parameter matrix. Element-wise:
$$Y_{11} = \dfrac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{12} = \dfrac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{21} = \dfrac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z}, \quad Y_{22} = \dfrac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z}$$
where $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. KTU provides **internal choice** — any ONE of two alternatives is to be answered.

---

### **Question A — Option 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For the given two-port network, the Z-parameters are:
$$[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 4 \\ 4 & 8 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega$$

Determine the **Y-parameters** and **ABCD-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-port network has the following **h-parameters**:
$$h_{11} = 1\,\text{k}\Omega, \quad h_{12} = 2 \times 10^{-4}, \quad h_{21} = 100, \quad h_{22} = 50\,\mu\text{S}$$

Calculate the **Z-parameters** and **Y-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify Z-matrix elements:
$$Z_{11} = 10, \quad Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 4, \quad Z_{22} = 8$$
$$\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21} = (10)(8) - (4)(4) = 80 - 16 = 64\,\Omega^2$$

**Step 2:** Compute Y-parameters using conversion formulas:
$$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{8}{64} = 0.125\,\text{S} = 125\,\text{mS}$$
$$Y_{12} = \frac{-Z_{12}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-4}{64} = -0.0625\,\text{S} = -62.5\,\text{mS}$$
$$Y_{21} = \frac{-Z_{21}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{-4}{64} = -0.0625\,\text{S} = -62.5\,\text{mS}$$
$$Y_{22} = \frac{Z_{11}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{10}{64} = 0.15625\,\text{S} = 156.25\,\text{mS}$$

$$[Y] = \begin{bmatrix} 0.125 & -0.0625 \\ -0.0625 & 0.15625 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}$$

**Step 3:** Compute ABCD parameters:
$$A = \frac{Z_{11}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{10}{4} = 2.5$$
$$B = \frac{\Delta Z}{Z_{21}} = \frac{64}{4} = 16\,\Omega$$
$$C = \frac{1}{Z_{21}} = \frac{1}{4} = 0.25\,\text{S}$$
$$D = \frac{Z_{22}}{Z_{21}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2$$

$$\boxed{[ABCD] = \begin{bmatrix} 2.5 & 16 \\ 0.25 & 2 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:** $AD - BC = (2.5)(2) - (16)(0.25) = 5 - 4 = 1$ ✓ (Reciprocal) **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Identify h-parameter values and compute determinant:
$$h_{11} = 1000\,\Omega, \quad h_{12} = 2 \times 10^{-4}, \quad h_{21} = 100, \quad h_{22} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{S}$$
$$\Delta h = h_{11}h_{22} - h_{12}h_{21} = (1000)(50 \times 10^{-6}) - (2 \times 10^{-4})(100)$$
$$= 0.05 - 0.02 = 0.03 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

**Step 2:** Convert h → Z:
$$Z_{11} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_{22}} = \frac{0.03}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{0.03}{0.00005} = 600\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12} = \frac{h_{12}}{h_{22}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{5 \times 10^{-5}} = 4\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{21} = \frac{-h_{21}}{h_{22}} = \frac{-100}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = -2 \times 10^{6}\,\Omega = -2\,\text{M}\Omega$$
$$Z_{22} = \frac{1}{h_{22}} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6}} = 20{,}000\,\Omega = 20\,\text{k}\Omega$$

$$\boxed{[Z] = \begin{bmatrix} 600 & 4 \\ -2 \times 10^{6} & 20 \times 10^{3} \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega}$$

**Step 3:** Convert h → Y:
$$Y_{11} = \frac{1}{h_{11}} = \frac{1}{1000} = 10^{-3}\,\text{S} = 1\,\text{mS}$$
$$Y_{12} = \frac{-h_{12}}{h_{11}} = \frac{-2 \times 10^{-4}}{1000} = -2 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{S}$$
$$Y_{21} = \frac{h_{21}}{h_{11}} = \frac{100}{1000} = 0.1\,\text{S}$$
$$Y_{22} = \frac{\Delta h}{h_{11}} = \frac{0.03}{1000} = 3 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{S} = 30\,\mu\text{S}$$

$$\boxed{[Y] = \begin{bmatrix} 10^{-3} & -2 \times 10^{-7} \\ 0.1 & 3 \times 10^{-5} \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}}$$

**Verification using $Y = Z^{-1}$:** 
$$\Delta Z = (600)(20000) - (4)(-2 \times 10^6) = 12 \times 10^6 + 8 \times 10^6 = 20 \times 10^6$$
$$Y_{11} = \frac{Z_{22}}{\Delta Z} = \frac{20000}{20 \times 10^6} = 10^{-3}\,\text{S} \checkmark$$
**(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A two-port network is characterized by the **Y-parameters**:
$$[Y] = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & -2 \\ -2 & 3 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{S}$$

Compute the corresponding **Z-parameters** and **ABCD-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The **ABCD-parameters** of a two-port network are:
$$[T] = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 10 \\ 0.5 & 1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Find the **h-parameters** and **g-parameters**. **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1:** Identify Y-matrix elements and determinant:
$$Y_{11} = 5, \quad Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -2, \quad Y_{22} = 3$$
$$\Delta Y = Y_{11}Y_{22} - Y_{12}Y_{21} = (5)(3) - (-2)(-2) = 15 - 4 = 11$$

**Step 2:** Compute Z-parameters (Z = Y⁻¹):
$$Z_{11} = \frac{Y_{22}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{3}{11} \approx 0.2727\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12} = \frac{-Y_{12}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{2}{11} \approx 0.1818\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{21} = \frac{-Y_{21}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{2}{11} \approx 0.1818\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{22} = \frac{Y_{11}}{\Delta Y} = \frac{5}{11} \approx 0.4545\,\Omega$$

$$\boxed{[Z] = \frac{1}{11}\begin{bmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 2 & 5 \end{bmatrix}\,\Omega}$$

**Step 3:** Compute ABCD parameters using Y → ABCD formulas:
$$A = \frac{-Y_{22}}{Y_{21}} = \frac{-3}{-2} = 1.5$$
$$B = \frac{-1}{Y_{21}} = \frac{-1}{-2} = 0.5\,\Omega$$
$$C = \frac{-\Delta Y}{Y_{21}} = \frac{-11}{-2} = 5.5\,\text{S}$$
$$D = \frac{-Y_{11}}{Y_{21}} = \frac{-5}{-2} = 2.5$$

$$\boxed{[ABCD] = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0.5 \\ 5.5 & 2.5 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:** $AD - BC = (1.5)(2.5) - (0.5)(5.5) = 3.75 - 2.75 = 1$ ✓ **(7 marks)**

#### 📝 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Identify ABCD values and determinant:
$$A = 1.5, \quad B = 10, \quad C = 0.5, \quad D = 1.5$$
$$\Delta T = AD - BC = (1.5)(1.5) - (10)(0.5) = 2.25 - 5 = -2.75$$

**Step 2:** Compute h-parameters (ABCD → h):
$$h_{11} = \frac{B}{D} = \frac{10}{1.5} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.667\,\Omega$$
$$h_{12} = \frac{\Delta T}{D} = \frac{-2.75}{1.5} = \frac{-11}{6} \approx -1.833$$
$$h_{21} = \frac{-1}{D} = \frac{-1}{1.5} = -\frac{2}{3} \approx -0.6667$$
$$h_{22} = \frac{C}{D} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333\,\text{S}$$

$$\boxed{[h] = \begin{bmatrix} 20/3 & -11/6 \\ -2/3 & 1/3 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute g-parameters (ABCD → g):
$$g_{11} = \frac{C}{A} = \frac{0.5}{1.5} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333\,\text{S}$$
$$g_{12} = \frac{-\Delta T}{A} = \frac{2.75}{1.5} = \frac{11}{6} \approx 1.833$$
$$g_{21} = \frac{1}{A} = \frac{1}{1.5} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.6667$$
$$g_{22} = \frac{B}{A} = \frac{10}{1.5} = \frac{20}{3} \approx 6.667\,\Omega$$

$$\boxed{[g] = \begin{bmatrix} 1/3 & 11/6 \\ 2/3 & 20/3 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Observation:** Note that $h_{11} = g_{22}$, $h_{22} = g_{11}$, $h_{12} = -g_{12}$, $h_{21} = -g_{21}$. This **anti-symmetry** between h and g is a universal property for reciprocal networks. ✓ **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign Errors in ABCD**: KTU strictly uses the **negative sign** convention. The output current is $-I_2$ (current *leaving* port 2). Many students mistakenly drop the minus sign in the conversion formulas, leading to wrong signs throughout.
> 2. **Forgetting the Determinant**: When computing $[Z]^{-1}$, students often use only the formula $Y_{11} = 1/Z_{11}$ instead of the correct $Y_{11} = Z_{22}/\Delta Z$. **Always use the cofactor matrix divided by the determinant**.
> 3. **Unit Confusion**: When $h_{11}$ is given in kΩ, convert to Ω before computing $Y_{11} = 1/h_{11}$ — KTU deducts marks for unit inconsistency.
> 4. **Reciprocity Check**: Always verify $AD - BC = 1$ (for reciprocal networks). If your answer doesn't give 1, you have an algebraic error — **this is your built-in check**.
> 5. **Hybrid h-reciprocity**: For reciprocal networks in h-parameters, $h_{12} = -h_{21}$. Many students write $h_{12} = h_{21}$, losing marks.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical facts to memorize before the exam:**

- ⭐ **Six parameter sets** describe the same two-port: Z, Y, h, g, ABCD, A'B'C'D'.
- ⭐ **The most fundamental inter-relationship**: $\mathbf{Y} = \mathbf{Z}^{-1}$ (and equivalently $\mathbf{Z} = \mathbf{Y}^{-1}$).
- ⭐ **Determinant shorthand**: Always denote the determinant of a 2×2 matrix with $\Delta$ (e.g., $\Delta Z = Z_{11}Z_{22} - Z_{12}Z_{21}$).
- ⭐ **Reciprocity conditions**:
  - In Z: $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$
  - In Y: $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$
  - In h: $h_{12} = -h_{21}$
  - In g: $g_{12} = -g_{21}$
  - In ABCD: $AD - BC = 1$
- ⭐ **Symmetry condition** (also reciprocal): $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, $A = D$, $\Delta h = 1$, $\Delta g = 1$.
- ⭐ **ABCD cascade advantage**: Cascaded networks' ABCD matrix is the **product** $T_{total} = T_1 T_2 T_3 \cdots$
- ⭐ **Sign convention**: ABCD and A'B'C'D' use $-I_2$ and $-I_1$ respectively (current leaving the port).
- ⭐ **Dimensions to remember**: $Z \to \Omega$, $Y \to \text{S}$, $h_{11}, g_{22} \to \Omega$, $h_{22}, g_{11} \to \text{S}$, $h_{12}, h_{21}, g_{12}, g_{21}$ are dimensionless, $A, D$ dimensionless, $B \to \Omega$, $C \to \text{S}$.
- ⭐ **Conversion "shortcut" rule**: When asked to convert, always identify the **target equation's independent variables** first, then solve for them using the source equations.
- ⭐ **Verification step**: After conversion, verify reciprocity and symmetry. If your converted network violates the expected symmetry condition, redo the conversion.
- ⭐ **Common T-network values**: $Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_b$, $Z_{22} = Z_b + Z_c$, $Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_b$.
- ⭐ **Common π-network values**: $Y_{11} = Y_a + Y_c$, $Y_{22} = Y_b + Y_c$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -Y_c$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Series and Parallel Connections of Two-port Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCECT205 - NETWORK THEORY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Series and Parallel Connections of Two-port Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition — Two-Port Network Interconnection

A **two-port network** is an electrical network enclosed in a black box with two distinct pairs of access terminals: an **input port** (Port 1: terminals 1–1') where an independent signal enters, and an **output port** (Port 2: terminals 2–2') where the processed/load-side signal exists. When two such networks are combined, the manner in which their ports are joined defines the overall system behavior.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCECT205)**, interconnections of two-port networks are classified into four canonical types:

1. **Cascade (Chain) Connection** — output port of one feeds the input port of the next.
2. **Series (Cascade in Z-domain) Connection** — input ports in series, output ports in series.
3. **Parallel Connection** — input ports in parallel, output ports in parallel.
4. **Hybrid (Series–Parallel / Parallel–Series) Connection** — mixed topology.

This module focuses on **Series and Parallel Connections**, which are governed by the **Z-parameters (Impedance parameters)** and **Y-parameters (Admittance parameters)**, respectively.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Train Coach Analogy:**
> Imagine a long train as one large transportation system (overall two-port network). Each **coach** is a **smaller two-port network** with its own entry door (input port) and exit door (output port). When we couple coaches end-to-end, a passenger walks straight through — this is a **cascade** connection.
> Now, suppose two parallel tracks are merged into one: passengers from Track A and Track B must add their flow together before exiting. This merging represents a **parallel** connection. Conversely, if two thin wires are twisted to form a single thicker wire, the impedances add up — this is a **series** connection of input (and output) ports.

- **Series Connection** → Currents in the two networks at the joined terminals are the *same*; voltages **add**.
- **Parallel Connection** → Voltages across the joined terminals are the *same*; currents **add**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCECT205, Module 4):**
> Students must understand that **only Z-parameters add for series**, and **only Y-parameters add for parallel**. Misapplying parameters (e.g., using ABCD-parameters for a parallel connection) is a common valuation error and leads to **loss of 3–5 marks** in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- Standard two-port port conditions: **Port currents** $I_1, I_2$ enter the positive terminals; **Port voltages** $V_1, V_2$ are measured across the port with respect to the reference (common ground for series/parallel).
- **Reciprocity condition:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ for reciprocal networks.
- **Symmetry condition:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$ for symmetric networks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Series Connection — Z-Parameter Justification

The standard two-port Z-parameter representation is:

$$V_1 = Z_{11} I_1 + Z_{12} I_2$$
$$V_2 = Z_{21} I_1 + Z_{22} I_2$$

For **two networks A and B connected in series**, the boundary conditions are:

- The **port currents** are common: $I_{1A} = I_{1B} = I_1$ and $I_{2A} = I_{2B} = I_2$.
- The **port voltages add**: $V_1 = V_{1A} + V_{1B}$ and $V_2 = V_{2A} + V_{2B}$.

Because of the **linearity of the impedance representation**, we can directly add the individual Z-matrices. This is the fundamental reason why the **Z-parameters are natural for series connection** — they are linear in voltage when currents are held common.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Parallel Connection — Y-Parameter Justification

The standard two-port Y-parameter representation is:

$$I_1 = Y_{11} V_1 + Y_{12} V_2$$
$$I_2 = Y_{21} V_1 + Y_{22} V_2$$

For **two networks A and B connected in parallel**, the boundary conditions are:

- The **port voltages** are common: $V_{1A} = V_{1B} = V_1$ and $V_{2A} = V_{2B} = V_2$.
- The **port currents add**: $I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$ and $I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$.

Since the Y-parameter equations are **linear in current** when voltages are held common, the individual Y-matrices add directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Conceptual Hook:**
> - Series connection → **common currents** → Z-parameters add (voltages superpose).
> - Parallel connection → **common voltages** → Y-parameters add (currents superpose).
> This is a direct consequence of the **Superposition Theorem** applied to linear bilateral networks.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Connection Type | Parameter that Adds | Resulting Matrix Equation | Validity Condition |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Series** | **Z-parameters** | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = (Z_A + Z_B) \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Port currents of A and B must be **identically equal** (no external loading at junction). |
| **Parallel** | **Y-parameters** | $\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = (Y_A + Y_B) \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Port voltages of A and B must be **identically equal**; no internal short-circuit between the two reference nodes. |
| **Cascade** | **ABCD-parameters** | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_1 \end{bmatrix} = T_A \cdot T_B \begin{bmatrix} V_2 \\ -I_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Output port of A directly feeds input port of B. |
| **Series–Parallel (Hybrid-g)** | **g-parameters** | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = (g_A + g_B) \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Input in series, output in parallel. |
| **Parallel–Series (Hybrid-h)** | **h-parameters** | $\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = (h_A + h_B) \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$ | Input in parallel, output in series. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Convention for Z and Y:**
> - $Z_{ij}$ units: **Ohms ($\Omega$)**.
> - $Y_{ij}$ units: **Siemens (S)**.
> - Off-diagonal terms satisfy $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ and $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ for **reciprocal** networks.

### 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic for Solving Series/Parallel Problems

1. **Identify the topology** — Check if currents are common (series) or voltages are common (parallel).
2. **Select the appropriate parameter set** — Z for series, Y for parallel.
3. **Compute individual matrices** — Derive $Z_A, Z_B$ or $Y_A, Y_B$ using standard test-source methods ($I_1 = 0, I_2 = 0$ for Z; $V_1 = 0, V_2 = 0$ for Y).
4. **Add the matrices** element-by-element to obtain the overall matrix.
5. **Verify** reciprocity ($Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$) and check units.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Filter Design:** Cascaded and parallel-connected two-port networks model **lattice filters**, **lumped-element filters**, and **matching networks** in RF/microwave engineering.
- **Transmission Line Modeling:** Long transmission lines are often analyzed as a **cascade of identical two-port sections** using ABCD-parameters.
- **Transistor Amplifier Analysis:** **Hybrid-$\pi$ and h-parameter models** of BJTs use parallel–series connections extensively.
- **Operational Amplifier Networks:** Feedback amplifiers use interconnected two-port building blocks to compute loop gain and stability margins.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Series-Connected Two-Port Z-Parameter Addition

Consider two two-port networks **A** and **B**, both **reciprocal and linear**, with their input ports connected in series and their output ports connected in series. Let the common (mesh-like) currents be $I_1$ and $I_2$ flowing through both networks.

**For Network A (Z-parameter form):**

$$V_{1A} = Z_{11}^A I_1 + Z_{12}^A I_2$$
$$V_{2A} = Z_{21}^A I_1 + Z_{22}^A I_2$$

**For Network B (Z-parameter form):**

$$V_{1B} = Z_{11}^B I_1 + Z_{12}^B I_2$$
$$V_{2B} = Z_{21}^B I_1 + Z_{22}^B I_2$$

**Applying the series connection constraint (voltages add):**

$$V_1 = V_{1A} + V_{1B}$$
$$V_2 = V_{2A} + V_{2B}$$

**Substituting:**

$$V_1 = (Z_{11}^A + Z_{11}^B) I_1 + (Z_{12}^A + Z_{12}^B) I_2$$

$$V_2 = (Z_{21}^A + Z_{21}^B) I_1 + (Z_{22}^A + Z_{22}^B) I_2$$

**Final result in matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Z_{11}^A + Z_{11}^B & Z_{12}^A + Z_{12}^B \\ Z_{21}^A + Z_{21}^B & Z_{22}^A + Z_{22}^B \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> This matrix equation is the **proof that the overall Z-matrix equals the sum of the individual Z-matrices** when two networks are connected in series.

### 3.2 Derivation: Parallel-Connected Two-Port Y-Parameter Addition

Consider two two-port networks **A** and **B** connected in parallel at both ports. Let the common (node) voltages be $V_1$ and $V_2$.

**For Network A (Y-parameter form):**

$$I_{1A} = Y_{11}^A V_1 + Y_{12}^A V_2$$
$$I_{2A} = Y_{21}^A V_1 + Y_{22}^A V_2$$

**For Network B (Y-parameter form):**

$$I_{1B} = Y_{11}^B V_1 + Y_{12}^B V_2$$
$$I_{2B} = Y_{21}^B V_1 + Y_{22}^B V_2$$

**Applying the parallel connection constraint (currents add by KCL):**

$$I_1 = I_{1A} + I_{1B}$$
$$I_2 = I_{2A} + I_{2B}$$

**Substituting:**

$$I_1 = (Y_{11}^A + Y_{11}^B) V_1 + (Y_{12}^A + Y_{12}^B) V_2$$

$$I_2 = (Y_{21}^A + Y_{21}^B) V_1 + (Y_{22}^A + Y_{22}^B) V_2$$

**Final result in matrix form:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} I_1 \\ I_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} Y_{11}^A + Y_{11}^B & Y_{12}^A + Y_{12}^B \\ Y_{21}^A + Y_{21}^B & Y_{22}^A + Y_{22}^B \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} V_1 \\ V_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

> This is the **proof that the overall Y-matrix equals the sum of the individual Y-matrices** when two networks are connected in parallel.

### 3.3 Numerical Example: Two T-Networks in Series

> **Problem:** Two identical T-networks are connected in series. Each T-network has series arm $Z_a = 2\,\Omega$ and shunt arm $Z_b = 4\,\Omega$. Find the overall Z-matrix.

**Step 1 — Find the Z-matrix of a single T-network.**

For a T-network with series arm $Z_a$ (each side) and shunt arm $Z_b$ (middle branch):

$$Z_{11} = Z_a + Z_b = 2 + 4 = 6\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{22} = Z_a + Z_b = 2 + 4 = 6\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12} = Z_{21} = Z_b = 4\,\Omega$$

**Step 2 — Identify the connection type.**

The two T-networks are in **series** at both ports. Hence, the **Z-matrices add**.

**Step 3 — Add element-wise.**

$$Z_{11}^{total} = Z_{11}^A + Z_{11}^B = 6 + 6 = 12\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12}^{total} = Z_{12}^A + Z_{12}^B = 4 + 4 = 8\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{21}^{total} = Z_{21}^A + Z_{21}^B = 4 + 4 = 8\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{22}^{total} = Z_{22}^A + Z_{22}^B = 6 + 6 = 12\,\Omega$$

**Step 4 — Final overall Z-matrix:**

$$Z_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & 8 \\ 8 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \,\Omega$$

**Verification:** The result is symmetric ($Z_{12} = Z_{21} = 8\,\Omega$), as expected for reciprocal, lossless T-networks.

### 3.4 Numerical Example: Two $\pi$-Networks in Parallel

> **Problem:** Two identical $\pi$-networks are connected in parallel. Each $\pi$-network has series arm $Z = 6\,\Omega$ and shunt admittance $Y = 0.25\,S$. Find the overall Y-matrix.

**Step 1 — Find the Y-matrix of a single $\pi$-network.**

For a $\pi$-network with series arm $Z$ and shunt admittances $Y$ on each side:

$$Y_{11} = Y + \frac{1}{Z} = 0.25 + \frac{1}{6} = 0.25 + 0.1667 = 0.4167\,S$$
$$Y_{22} = Y + \frac{1}{Z} = 0.4167\,S$$
$$Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -\frac{1}{Z} = -0.1667\,S$$

**Step 2 — Add the two Y-matrices (parallel connection).**

$$Y_{11}^{total} = 0.4167 + 0.4167 = 0.8333\,S$$
$$Y_{22}^{total} = 0.4167 + 0.4167 = 0.8333\,S$$
$$Y_{12}^{total} = -0.1667 + (-0.1667) = -0.3333\,S$$
$$Y_{21}^{total} = -0.1667 + (-0.1667) = -0.3333\,S$$

**Step 3 — Final overall Y-matrix:**

$$Y_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8333 & -0.3333 \\ -0.3333 & 0.8333 \end{bmatrix} S$$

**Verification:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, confirming reciprocity.

### 3.5 Conversion Between Parameter Sets (Useful Cross-Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme Module 4, students are expected to **convert** between parameter sets. The relationships between Z and Y are:

$$Y = Z^{-1} \quad \text{and} \quad Z = Y^{-1}$$

This means: **if you know the overall Z-matrix of a series connection, you can invert it to get the Y-matrix of an equivalent parallel-connection model** — and vice versa. This is useful when the problem specifies a topology but asks for the parameter set in a different form.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Two Two-Port Networks in Series

The diagram below illustrates the canonical **series-series connection**: input ports of A and B are joined in series, output ports of A and B are joined in series. The common currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ flow through both networks.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1((Terminal 1)) --- NA1[Network A Port 1]
    NB1[Network B Port 1] --- A2((Terminal 1 Prime))
    NA1 --- nodeJ1((Junction 1))
    NB1 --- nodeJ1
    nodeJ1 --- C1[Common Current I1]
    C1 --- nodeI1[Shared I1]
    nodeI1 --- NA2[Network A]
    NA2 --- nodeJ2((Junction 2))
    nodeJ2 --- NB2[Network B]
    NB2 --- A3((Terminal 2))
    A4((Terminal 2 Prime)) --- nodeJ2
    C2[Common Current I2] --- nodeJ2

    style NA1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style NA2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style NB1 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style NB2 fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style C1 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style C2 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
```

**Key takeaway from the diagram:**
- The **node labeled Junction 1** indicates the series connection of the input ports.
- The **node labeled Junction 2** indicates the series connection of the output ports.
- $I_1$ and $I_2$ are the **common currents** shared by both networks.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Two Two-Port Networks in Parallel

The diagram below shows the **parallel–parallel connection**: input ports of A and B share the same node voltage $V_1$, output ports share $V_2$. Currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ **add** at the junction by KCL.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph parallelBlock [Parallel Connected Two-Port System]
        direction TB
        NA[Network A] --- nodeV1((Node Voltage V1))
        NB[Network B] --- nodeV1
        nodeV1 --- PA1((Port 1 Top))
        nodeV1 --- PA2((Port 1 Bottom))
        NA --- nodeV2((Node Voltage V2))
        NB --- nodeV2
        nodeV2 --- PA3((Port 2 Top))
        nodeV2 --- PA4((Port 2 Bottom))
    end
    I1Sum[Total Input Current I1 equals IA1 plus IB1] --- nodeV1
    I2Sum[Total Output Current I2 equals IA2 plus IB2] --- nodeV2

    style NA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style NB fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style I1Sum fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style I2Sum fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style parallelBlock fill:#f9f9f9,stroke:#333333
```

**Key takeaway from the diagram:**
- The **node voltages $V_1$ and $V_2$** are the same for both networks.
- The **port currents add** at each node, validating the use of Y-parameter superposition.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Two Two-Port Networks A and B]) --> idTopo{Identify Topology}
    idTopo -- Currents Common --> seriesPath[Series Connection Path]
    idTopo -- Voltages Common --> parallelPath[Parallel Connection Path]
    seriesPath --> compZ[Compute ZA and ZB]
    parallelPath --> compY[Compute YA and YB]
    compZ --> addZ[Add Matrices: Ztotal = ZA plus ZB]
    compY --> addY[Add Matrices: Ytotal = YA plus YB]
    addZ --> verifyZ{Reciprocity Check: Z12 equals Z21}
    addY --> verifyY{Reciprocity Check: Y12 equals Y21}
    verifyZ -- Yes --> resZ[Output: Ztotal Matrix]
    verifyZ -- No --> warnZ[Revaluate Network Topology]
    verifyY -- Yes --> resY[Output: Ytotal Matrix]
    verifyY -- No --> warnY[Revaluate Network Topology]
    resZ --> endFlow([End: Combined Two-Port Network Model])
    resY --> endFlow

    style start fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style endFlow fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724
    style seriesPath fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style parallelPath fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300
    style warnZ fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style warnY fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

**Interpretation of the flow:**
- The **decision diamond** routes the student to the correct analysis path (Z for series, Y for parallel).
- A **reciprocity check** is a built-in validation step that catches common student errors.
- The final output is a single, unified matrix representing the combined two-port network.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> When two two-port networks are connected in series, which two-port parameters add up directly? Justify your answer in one sentence.

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
The **Z-parameters (impedance parameters)** add up directly when two two-port networks are connected in series. This is because, in a series connection, the **port currents $I_1$ and $I_2$ are common** to both networks, making the Z-parameter representation (which expresses voltages as linear functions of currents) directly additive by the Superposition Theorem.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> State the condition under which two two-port networks can be validly connected in parallel.

**Model Answer (2 marks):**
Two two-port networks can be validly connected in parallel if and only if the **port voltages of both networks are identical** at the input ($V_{1A} = V_{1B}$) and output ($V_{2A} = V_{2B}$) ports, with **no external element or load present at the common reference nodes** that would cause a short circuit or break the KCL summation of currents.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam — Module 4, 14 Marks]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Two two-port networks A and B have the following Z-parameter matrices:

$$Z_A = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 2 \\ 2 & 7 \end{bmatrix} \,\Omega, \quad Z_B = \begin{bmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \,\Omega$$

If the two networks are connected in **series**, determine the **overall Z-matrix** of the combined network and verify the reciprocity condition.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The same two networks are now reconnected in **parallel**. Compute the **overall Y-matrix** of the combined system. Show all conversion steps clearly.

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Series Connection:**

Since the networks are in series, the Z-matrices add element-wise:

$$Z_{total} = Z_A + Z_B = \begin{bmatrix} 5 + 3 & 2 + 1 \\ 2 + 1 & 7 + 4 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 & 3 \\ 3 & 11 \end{bmatrix} \,\Omega$$

**Reciprocity Check:**

$$Z_{12} = 3\,\Omega \quad \text{and} \quad Z_{21} = 3\,\Omega$$

Since $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, the **reciprocity condition is satisfied**. The combined network is reciprocal.

**Part (b) — Parallel Connection:**

For parallel connection, we need the **Y-matrices** of A and B. Since $Z$ and $Y$ are inverses:

$$Y_A = Z_A^{-1} = \frac{1}{(5 \cdot 7 - 2 \cdot 2)} \begin{bmatrix} 7 & -2 \\ -2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{31} \begin{bmatrix} 7 & -2 \\ -2 & 5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2258 & -0.0645 \\ -0.0645 & 0.1613 \end{bmatrix} S$$

$$Y_B = Z_B^{-1} = \frac{1}{(3 \cdot 4 - 1 \cdot 1)} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{11} \begin{bmatrix} 4 & -1 \\ -1 & 3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.3636 & -0.0909 \\ -0.0909 & 0.2727 \end{bmatrix} S$$

**Overall Y-matrix (parallel addition):**

$$Y_{total} = Y_A + Y_B = \begin{bmatrix} 0.2258 + 0.3636 & -0.0645 + (-0.0909) \\ -0.0645 + (-0.0909) & 0.1613 + 0.2727 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$Y_{total} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5894 & -0.1554 \\ -0.1554 & 0.4340 \end{bmatrix} S$$

**Verification of Reciprocity:** $Y_{12} = Y_{21} = -0.1554\,S$ ✓

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the condition under which the **Z-parameters of two two-port networks in series** can be added directly to obtain the overall Z-matrix. State the equivalent condition for the **Y-parameter addition in parallel connection**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A T-network with series arms $Z_a = 3\,\Omega$ and shunt arm $Z_b = 6\,\Omega$ is connected in **series** with another identical T-network. Compute the **overall Z-matrix** and comment on the symmetry of the resulting network.

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Conditions:**

*Series connection validity:*
- **Condition 1 (current continuity):** The port current entering the input port of network A must equal the port current leaving the input port of network B (i.e., $I_{1A} = I_{1B} = I_1$). Similarly for the output port.
- **Condition 2 (no external loading at junctions):** No external element should be connected between the series-joined terminals, otherwise it would violate the pure two-port structure.
- **Condition 3 (linearity and reciprocity preserved):** Both networks must be linear, bilateral, and passive (LTI).

*Parallel connection validity:*
- **Condition 1 (voltage equality):** The voltages across the input ports of A and B must be identical ($V_{1A} = V_{1B} = V_1$), and similarly at the output.
- **Condition 2 (no internal short-circuit):** The reference terminals of the two networks must be joined without any element in between, otherwise KCL at the common node would be violated.
- **Condition 3 (linearity and reciprocity preserved):** Same as for series.

*Mathematical Implication:*
- For series, **Z-parameters add** because the KVL superposition of voltages is linear in the common currents.
- For parallel, **Y-parameters add** because the KCL superposition of currents is linear in the common voltages.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

For a single T-network with $Z_a = 3\,\Omega$ and $Z_b = 6\,\Omega$:

$$Z_{11}^A = Z_a + Z_b = 3 + 6 = 9\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{22}^A = Z_a + Z_b = 3 + 6 = 9\,\Omega$$
$$Z_{12}^A = Z_{21}^A = Z_b = 6\,\Omega$$

So, $Z_A = \begin{bmatrix} 9 & 6 \\ 6 & 9 \end{bmatrix} \Omega$ and $Z_B = Z_A$ (identical networks).

For series connection, the overall Z-matrix is:

$$Z_{total} = Z_A + Z_B = \begin{bmatrix} 9 + 9 & 6 + 6 \\ 6 + 6 & 9 + 9 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 18 & 12 \\ 12 & 18 \end{bmatrix} \Omega$$

**Comment on Symmetry:** Since $Z_{11} = Z_{22} = 18\,\Omega$, the combined network is **symmetric** in addition to being **reciprocal**. The T-network topology with identical series arms automatically produces a symmetric structure when cascaded with its twin.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often use **ABCD-parameters for parallel** or **Y-parameters for series**. This is fundamentally wrong and will lead to a **complete loss of 7 marks** in a sub-part.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** When converting between Z and Y, students **forget to take the matrix inverse**, instead performing scalar division. This will lead to a **loss of 3–4 marks** for incorrect matrix operations.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Students often **skip the reciprocity check**. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards **1 mark** for verifying $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$ or $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$ as a self-check step.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** When two networks are connected in parallel, students must ensure the **reference nodes are joined directly**. If the problem diagram shows a resistor between the two reference terminals, the parallel-addition rule **does not apply directly**, and KCL must be re-applied from scratch.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Series and Parallel Connections of Two-Port Networks:**

- **Series Connection Rule:** Use **Z-parameters**. Z-matrices **add element-by-element**: $Z_{total} = Z_A + Z_B$.
- **Parallel Connection Rule:** Use **Y-parameters**. Y-matrices **add element-by-element**: $Y_{total} = Y_A + Y_B$.
- **Series–Parallel (Hybrid-g) Connection:** Use **g-parameters**. g-matrices add.
- **Parallel–Series (Hybrid-h) Connection:** Use **h-parameters**. h-matrices add.
- **Cascade Connection Rule:** Use **ABCD-parameters**. T-matrices **multiply in order**: $T_{total} = T_A \cdot T_B$.
- **Conversion Formulas:** $Y = Z^{-1}$ and $Z = Y^{-1}$. Remember the **determinant of Z** is $\Delta Z = Z_{11} Z_{22} - Z_{12} Z_{21}$.
- **Reciprocity Conditions:** $Z_{12} = Z_{21}$, $Y_{12} = Y_{21}$, $AD - BC = 1$ (for ABCD), and $h_{12} = -h_{21}$.
- **Symmetry Conditions:** $Z_{11} = Z_{22}$, $Y_{11} = Y_{22}$, and $A = D$ (for ABCD).
- **Unit Awareness:** Z-parameters are in **Ohms ($\Omega$)**; Y-parameters are in **Siemens (S)**. Mixing units in matrix entries is a frequent error.
- **Test-Source Recall:** To find Z-parameters, set $I_1 = 0$ (open-circuit Port 1) and $I_2 = 0$ (open-circuit Port 2). To find Y-parameters, set $V_1 = 0$ and $V_2 = 0$ (short-circuit ports).
- **KTU Exam Weightage:** This topic typically carries **7–14 marks** in the End Semester Examination, often as a full Part-B question with internal choice. It is also frequently combined with **network functions and stability** in composite problems.
- **Common Pitfall:** When the problem mentions "lattice network," "bridge network," or "T-to-$\pi$ conversion," ensure the **port identification** is correct before applying the series/parallel addition rule. A misidentified port can completely invalidate the analysis.
